Now I want to go deeper into what measurement

where measurement sits in the framework — on the physical modeling branch. Now I want to go deeper into what measurement actually is. Not how to press buttons on a sensor, but what a sensor is really doing: what it reports, what it infers, and what it silently assumes. And in the second half of this lecture, I'll introduce the formal vocabulary of experiment design — the language you will use in your Phase 1 design deliverable this week when you commit to a hypothesis and a procedure for your assigned canned experiment before any data collection begins. ================================================================================ slide 2: Measurement Is Model Realization (Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical Reality" shows a schematic of a

ion (Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical Reality" shows a schematic of a physical system — a rotating wheel with angular velocity arrows, labeled "True Angular Velocity: ω." Bottom layer labeled "Sensor Output" shows a voltage-vs-time trace with visible noise and a slight baseline offset, labeled "Recorded Signal: 2.34 V ± noise." Between the two layers, a downward arrow passes through a gray box labeled with four stages: Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Analog-to-Digital Conversion → Calibration Equation. A red annotation at the bottom reads "This is not the physical quantity — it is a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early:

ized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early: measurement is not data collection. Measurement is model realization. When you place a sensor on a structure or a system, you are not directly recording a physical truth — you are producing an inference about the physical world through a chain of transduction, conditioning, digitization, and calibration assumptions. Every step in that chain introduces error. Every step reflects a modeling choice. The voltage you read on your screen is not the angular velocity or the force or the temperature — it is the output of a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling

esponds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling you. The physical modeling branch of the AMVF starts here — in this chain. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Transducer Chain (Image: A horizontal five-box chain diagram on a light background, boxes connected by right-pointing arrows. Box 1: "Physical Phenomenon" — icon of a force arrow on a surface. Box 2: "Transducer Element" — icon of a sensor element converting mechanical energy to electrical, labeled "e.g., strain gauge → resistance change." Box 3: "Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital

abeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital levels, with arrow labeled "sampling rate matters here." Box 5: "Recorded Value" — a number in a spreadsheet cell. A red callout below Box 2 reads "Each stage introduces its own error source." A separate callout at Box 4 reads "Sampling and aliasing: Phase II, Week 7." The chain runs left to right across the full width of the slide.) Talking Point: The transducer chain is the sequence of physical and electronic processes that converts a real-world quantity into a recorded number. It starts at the phenomenon you care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every

angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every transformation can introduce error. The transducer converts energy from one form to another: a piezoelectric crystal produces voltage in response to strain, a thermocouple generates voltage proportional to temperature difference, a MEMS accelerometer changes capacitance in response to inertial force. Signal conditioning amplifies, filters, and excites. The analog-to-digital converter discretizes the continuous signal. I will not go deep into sampling theory today — that is Week 7 in Phase II. But I want you to see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause.

see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause. ================================================================================ slide 4: What We Measure vs. What We Infer (Image: A two-column diagram. Left column header: "What the Sensor Reports" — a blue box showing "Voltage: 2.34 V" with a voltmeter icon. Right column header: "What We Care About" — a gold box showing "Force: 14.7 ± 0.4 N" with a force arrow icon. Between the columns, a downward-pointing conversion block contains: "Calibration Equation: F = k·V + b" and below it "Assumptions: linear response, stable calibration constants, constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the

constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the assumptions in the conversion." An upward arrow from the right column back to the left with a dashed line asks: "If one assumption breaks — which column is wrong?") Talking Point: Sensors almost never directly measure the quantity you care about. A strain gauge measures resistance change. A thermocouple measures voltage. A pressure transducer measures membrane deflection. What you actually want is strain, temperature, or pressure. The conversion from what you measure to what you infer is not automatic — it is a calibration equation built on physical assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the

assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the electrical excitation fluctuates — the inference breaks too, even though the raw voltage reading is perfectly accurate. This is why calibration is not optional, and it is why I will ask you in every lab report to state explicitly what your sensor measured versus what you inferred from that measurement. The distinction is the beginning of experimental honesty. ================================================================================ slide 5: Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies (Image: A four-quadrant target diagram. Each quadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight

uadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster shifted far from center, labeled "consistent but wrong." Top-right: "Low Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster near center, labeled "ideal." Bottom-left: "High Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster far from center, labeled "wrong and inconsistent." Bottom-right: "Low Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster near center, labeled "inconsistent but not systematically wrong." Below each quadrant: the remedy — "Calibration" (top-left), "No action needed" (top-right), "Calibration + more measurements" (bottom-left), "More measurements + isolation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.)

lation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.) Talking Point: Bias and precision are two distinct properties of a measurement system, and they require completely different interventions. Bias is a systematic offset — every reading is shifted by a consistent amount in the same direction. A poorly zeroed load cell, a thermocouple with a bad reference junction, a sensor mounted with mechanical preload — these all introduce bias. Bias can sometimes be corrected by calibration against a known reference, but only if you have that reference. Precision is about scatter — how much do repeated measurements of the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all

f the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all biased. Low precision means your measurements scatter widely. Knowing which problem you have determines the solution: bias problems call for better calibration; precision problems call for more repetition, better isolation, and better experimental control. You will characterize both in your Phase 1 experiment. ================================================================================ slide 6: Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source (Image: A cross-sectional engineering sketch of a sensor mounted in a physical system. Three annotation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point,

tation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point, thermocouple reads lower than true fluid temperature." (2) "Mounting stress — over-tightening changes the mechanical boundary condition, altering strain at the gauge location." (3) "Electromagnetic interference — signal wire runs near a motor power lead, inducing 60 Hz noise into the measurement channel." The sketch is schematic and black-and-white with red callout arrows. A text note at the bottom: "Installation errors often exceed the sensor specification error. They are design choices, not random events.") Talking Point: A sensor that has been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field.

s been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field. Installation effects are the category of errors introduced by how and where a sensor is placed in the physical environment, and they are chronically underestimated by students and sometimes by professionals. Conduction error occurs when the sensor body conducts thermal energy away from the measurement point, pulling the reading toward ambient temperature. Mounting stress changes the mechanical state at the sensor location — a classic problem with strain gauges on thin or flexible structures. Electromagnetic interference is a signal contamination problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in

problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in aerospace laboratory work, and your experimental design this week must anticipate them before you take the first reading. If you discover an installation effect after the data is collected, you have a confound — not a measurement. ================================================================================ slide 7: Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A calibration curve graph on a clean white background. X-axis labeled "Known Reference Input (True Value)" with regular tick marks. Y-axis labeled "Sensor Output (Raw Units)." Two curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual

wo curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual Measured Response." The region between the curves is highlighted in yellow and labeled "Calibration Residual." Below the graph, a best-fit equation: "Output = a·Input + b, R² = 0.9992." In the bottom-right corner, a red text box: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known reference. It corrects bias. It does NOT validate a model.") Talking Point: Calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output to a known reference standard and using that comparison to derive a characterization equation — a scale factor, an offset, a polynomial fit — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If

t — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If your force sensor reads five percent high across its full range, calibration corrects that. Calibration is a measurement activity — it improves the fidelity of what the sensor reports about the physical world. I want to be explicit about what calibration is not: it is not validation. Calibration tells you the sensor is correctly reporting the physical quantity. Validation tells you whether your model accurately predicts system behavior. These are different claims about different things, and we will develop this distinction carefully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model.

efully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model. ================================================================================ slide 8: Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference (Image: A fishbone cause-and-effect diagram. Each branch

"Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch is clean and labeled in a consistent font. A red callout at the center node reads "Confounders produce apparent effects that are not caused by the independent variable.") A red annotation at the bottom reads "This is not the physical quantity — it is a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this

a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early: measurement is not data collection. Measurement is model realization. When you place a sensor on a structure or a system, you are not directly recording a physical truth — you are producing an inference about the physical world through a chain of transduction, conditioning, digitization, and calibration assumptions. Every step in that chain introduces error. Every step reflects a modeling choice. The voltage you read on your screen is not the angular velocity or the force or the temperature — it is the output of a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what

a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling you. The physical modeling branch of the AMVF starts here — in this chain. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Transducer Chain (Image: A horizontal five-box chain diagram on a light background, boxes connected by right-pointing arrows. Box 1: "Physical Phenomenon" — icon of a force arrow on a surface. Box 2: "Transducer Element" — icon of a sensor element converting mechanical energy to electrical, labeled "e.g., strain gauge → resistance change." Box 3: "Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform

"Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital levels, with arrow labeled "sampling rate matters here." Box 5: "Recorded Value" — a number in a spreadsheet cell. A red callout below Box 2 reads "Each stage introduces its own error source." A separate callout at Box 4 reads "Sampling and aliasing: Phase II, Week 7." The chain runs left to right across the full width of the slide.) Talking Point: The transducer chain is the sequence of physical and electronic processes that converts a real-world quantity into a recorded number. It starts at the phenomenon you care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in

care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every transformation can introduce error. The transducer converts energy from one form to another: a piezoelectric crystal produces voltage in response to strain, a thermocouple generates voltage proportional to temperature difference, a MEMS accelerometer changes capacitance in response to inertial force. Signal conditioning amplifies, filters, and excites. The analog-to-digital converter discretizes the continuous signal. I will not go deep into sampling theory today — that is Week 7 in Phase II. But I want you to see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause.

see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause. ================================================================================ slide 4: What We Measure vs. What We Infer (Image: A two-column diagram. Left column header: "What the Sensor Reports" — a blue box showing "Voltage: 2.34 V" with a voltmeter icon. Right column header: "What We Care About" — a gold box showing "Force: 14.7 ± 0.4 N" with a force arrow icon. Between the columns, a downward-pointing conversion block contains: "Calibration Equation: F = k·V + b" and below it "Assumptions: linear response, stable calibration constants, constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the

constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the assumptions in the conversion." An upward arrow from the right column back to the left with a dashed line asks: "If one assumption breaks — which column is wrong?") Talking Point: Sensors almost never directly measure the quantity you care about. A strain gauge measures resistance change. A thermocouple measures voltage. A pressure transducer measures membrane deflection. What you actually want is strain, temperature, or pressure. The conversion from what you measure to what you infer is not automatic — it is a calibration equation built on physical assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the

assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the electrical excitation fluctuates — the inference breaks too, even though the raw voltage reading is perfectly accurate. This is why calibration is not optional, and it is why I will ask you in every lab report to state explicitly what your sensor measured versus what you inferred from that measurement. The distinction is the beginning of experimental honesty. ================================================================================ slide 5: Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies (Image: A four-quadrant target diagram. Each quadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight

uadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster shifted far from center, labeled "consistent but wrong." Top-right: "Low Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster near center, labeled "ideal." Bottom-left: "High Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster far from center, labeled "wrong and inconsistent." Bottom-right: "Low Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster near center, labeled "inconsistent but not systematically wrong." Below each quadrant: the remedy — "Calibration" (top-left), "No action needed" (top-right), "Calibration + more measurements" (bottom-left), "More measurements + isolation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.)

lation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.) Talking Point: Bias and precision are two distinct properties of a measurement system, and they require completely different interventions. Bias is a systematic offset — every reading is shifted by a consistent amount in the same direction. A poorly zeroed load cell, a thermocouple with a bad reference junction, a sensor mounted with mechanical preload — these all introduce bias. Bias can sometimes be corrected by calibration against a known reference, but only if you have that reference. Precision is about scatter — how much do repeated measurements of the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all

f the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all biased. Low precision means your measurements scatter widely. Knowing which problem you have determines the solution: bias problems call for better calibration; precision problems call for more repetition, better isolation, and better experimental control. You will characterize both in your Phase 1 experiment. ================================================================================ slide 6: Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source (Image: A cross-sectional engineering sketch of a sensor mounted in a physical system. Three annotation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point,

tation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point, thermocouple reads lower than true fluid temperature." (2) "Mounting stress — over-tightening changes the mechanical boundary condition, altering strain at the gauge location." (3) "Electromagnetic interference — signal wire runs near a motor power lead, inducing 60 Hz noise into the measurement channel." The sketch is schematic and black-and-white with red callout arrows. A text note at the bottom: "Installation errors often exceed the sensor specification error. They are design choices, not random events.") Talking Point: A sensor that has been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field.

s been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field. Installation effects are the category of errors introduced by how and where a sensor is placed in the physical environment, and they are chronically underestimated by students and sometimes by professionals. Conduction error occurs when the sensor body conducts thermal energy away from the measurement point, pulling the reading toward ambient temperature. Mounting stress changes the mechanical state at the sensor location — a classic problem with strain gauges on thin or flexible structures. Electromagnetic interference is a signal contamination problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in

problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in aerospace laboratory work, and your experimental design this week must anticipate them before you take the first reading. If you discover an installation effect after the data is collected, you have a confound — not a measurement. ================================================================================ slide 7: Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A calibration curve graph on a clean white background. X-axis labeled "Known Reference Input (True Value)" with regular tick marks. Y-axis labeled "Sensor Output (Raw Units)." Two curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual

wo curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual Measured Response." The region between the curves is highlighted in yellow and labeled "Calibration Residual." Below the graph, a best-fit equation: "Output = a·Input + b, R² = 0.9992." In the bottom-right corner, a red text box: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known reference. It corrects bias. It does NOT validate a model.") Talking Point: Calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output to a known reference standard and using that comparison to derive a characterization equation — a scale factor, an offset, a polynomial fit — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If

t — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If your force sensor reads five percent high across its full range, calibration corrects that. Calibration is a measurement activity — it improves the fidelity of what the sensor reports about the physical world. I want to be explicit about what calibration is not: it is not validation. Calibration tells you the sensor is correctly reporting the physical quantity. Validation tells you whether your model accurately predicts system behavior. These are different claims about different things, and we will develop this distinction carefully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model.

efully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model. ================================================================================ slide 8: Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference (Image: A fishbone cause-and-effect diagram. The central horizontal spine points to a box labeled "Measured Outcome." Four angled branches extend from the spine, each labeled: "Environment" (sub-causes: temperature drift, humidity, ambient vibration), "Instrumentation" (sub-causes: sensor drift, noise floor, calibration age), "Operator" (sub-causes: positioning variability, recording inconsistency, timing errors), "Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch

"Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch is clean and labeled in a consistent font. A red callout at the center node reads "Confounders produce apparent effects that are not caused by the independent variable.") Talking Point: A confounding factor is anything that causes your dependent variable to change for reasons other than the independent variable you're manipulating. If you're measuring the period of a pendulum while someone opens a window, and the airflow changes the drag on the pendulum bob, that's a confound. If you're measuring structural deflection and the temperature in the lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. Confounders are dangerous because they can

lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. Confounders are dangerous because they can produce real, repeatable, statistically consistent apparent effects that have nothing to do with the phenomenon you are studying. The antidote to confounding is experimental design: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order, and careful isolation of the variable you are trying to manipulate. One of the required elements of your Phase 1 design deliverable this week is to identify at least one confounding factor in your assigned experiment before you run it — because after you have collected the data is the wrong time to remember them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment

member them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary (Image: A structured three-section diagram on a clean white background, each section a labeled card. Card 1: "Hypothesis" — template text: "If [independent variable] is changed by [specific amount or direction], then [dependent variable] will [specific expected response], because [physical reasoning in one sentence]." Card 2: "Variables" — a three-column table with column headers: Independent Variable (what you change), Dependent Variable (what you measure), Controlled Variables (what you hold fixed). Card 3: "Confounders" — a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note

a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note reading "All three elements are required in the Phase 1 design deliverable this week." The cards are visually distinct with subtle color coding.) Talking Point: Formal experiment design requires specific vocabulary, and I want to give you that vocabulary precisely. A hypothesis is a specific, falsifiable, causal statement — it is not a question, it is not a guess, and it is not a vague expectation. An independent variable is the quantity you intentionally change. A dependent variable is the quantity you measure in response. Controlled variables are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three

are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three elements define the scope of the experiment — they tell you and everyone who reads your report what you were actually testing. This week's Phase 1 design deliverable requires all three, along with a hypothesis and at least one identified confounder. Come to the lab session this week ready to write these down for your assigned experiment, because that design work is what drives everything that follows. ================================================================================ slide 10: Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X:

ide comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X: "If we change the input voltage to the circuit, the measured power will change." Below, three red annotations: "Direction unspecified," "No physical reasoning," "Not falsifiable in a useful sense." Right box, labeled "Strong Hypothesis" with a green checkmark: "If input voltage is increased from 3V to 9V while resistance is held constant, measured electrical power will increase by a factor of nine, because power is proportional to voltage squared (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance." Below, three green annotations: "Specific quantitative prediction," "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual

" "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual comparison.) Talking Point: The quality of your hypothesis determines the quality of your experiment. Let me show you exactly what I mean. The weak hypothesis — "if we change the input voltage, the power will change" — is technically true but scientifically useless. It predicts a directional change without specifying how much, and it gives no physical reasoning. If the power changes by five percent or by nine hundred percent, both confirm the weak hypothesis. The strong hypothesis specifies a quantitative prediction, states the physical mechanism, and is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of

nd is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of three-fold instead of nine-fold, I have a discrepancy I need to explain — and explaining it will teach me something. The strong hypothesis is the one worth testing. In your experiment design this week, your hypothesis must commit to a mechanism, not just a direction. ================================================================================ slide 11: Connecting Your Design to the AMVF (Image: The AMVF flowchart from Lecture 1, now with five sticky-note style annotations attached to specific nodes. At "Physical Realization": "Your hypothesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and

hesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and calibration procedure." At "Data Reduction": "Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation." At "Experimental Outcomes": "Your measured values with uncertainty stated." At "Quantitative Comparison": "Your validation metric applied to outcomes vs. model." The rest of the diagram is lightly grayed. A label below the figure reads "Phase 1 design deliverable: annotate this diagram with your actual decisions.") Talking Point: Before you leave this lecture, I want to draw the explicit connection between everything we just discussed and the AMVF from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection,

from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection, installation decisions, and calibration plan map to the instrumentation node. Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation map to data reduction. Your final reported values with uncertainty map to experimental outcomes. And your comparison to the predictive model maps to the quantitative comparison node. The Phase 1 design deliverable this week asks you to annotate an AMVF diagram with your actual design decisions at each node. That exercise is not bureaucratic — it is the most direct way to check whether every measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate.

measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Hypothesis Testable? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's submitted hypothesis: "The motor will produce more torque at higher input voltage." Below the quote, a single prompt in bold: "What makes this hypothesis not testable as stated? How would you fix it to make it defensible?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this hypothesis. [Pause — allow student responses.] The claim is technically true but scientifically useless: "more torque" could mean 0.001% more or 500% more — both would confirm it. There is no

true but scientifically useless: "more torque" could mean 0.001% more or 500% more — both would confirm it. There is no quantitative prediction, no stated physical mechanism, and no threshold that a measurement could clearly contradict. A defensible version commits to all three: "If input voltage is increased from 12V to 24V while load resistance is held constant, motor output torque will increase by a factor of four, because in a DC motor under constant winding resistance and no magnetic saturation, torque scales as the square of the applied voltage." That version names the mechanism, commits to a specific ratio, and can be clearly contradicted by a measurement showing a factor of 1.8 or a factor of 10. Notice: the strong hypothesis taught you something even before the experiment ran —

g a factor of 1.8 or a factor of 10. Notice: the strong hypothesis taught you something even before the experiment ran — you had to know the physics to write it. That is the point. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are

ore you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - References "assigned canned experiment" and "Phase 1 design deliverable" per Iteration 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example. - P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example,

Iteration 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example. - P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting naturally to the W2 Lecture 2 Lab 1 activity without introducing it early. - Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced lightly in Slide 7 — tagged as "Week 3" for the deeper treatment, per blueprint continuity. - Slide 11 explicitly connects to the annotated AMVF deliverable from the W1 lab session. - Confounder identification requirement in Slide 8 and 9 matches the Phase 1 required elements listed in the W1 lab activity blueprint. - Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 12 (Evaluator Moment — hypothesis falsifiability). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 7 talking

d slide 12 (Evaluator Moment — hypothesis falsifiability). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 7 talking point — replaced "correction equation" with "characterization equation" to avoid implying the sensor was broken (characterization describes the sensor's mapping, not corrects a defect).

pages.title: R01
pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Reading R01 # AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation ## ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework Every claim an engineer makes about... AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework Every claim an engineer makes about a system is only as credible as the framework used to make it. Professional experimentalists have spent decades building that framework already. The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework introduced in Week 1's lecture is not an invention of this course. It is a synthesis of engineering practice that has been codified over decades in standards documents, measurement handbooks, and validation guides produced by professional organizations whose job is to decide how engineers should argue for results. This reading maps those resources. It's not a reading list to complete right now, but a set of pointers that will become required references as the course progresses. The most important of these documents is ASME V&V 10-2006 , the primary engineering standard anchoring the AMVF framework used throughout ASEN 3501. Published by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, V&V 10-2006 establishes the conceptual and procedural framework for verification and validation in computational engineering. Its full title, Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer , signals that its procedural requirements are specific to computational modeling. Its conceptual vocabulary (verification, validation, model adequacy, uncertainty) has been widely adopted as the standard framework language across both computational and experimental engineering, and that's the role it plays in this course. It defines those terms in the precise, technical sense that practicing engineers use. When the Week 1 lecture distinguished verification (did you solve the equations correctly?) from validation (are your equations the right ones for this problem?), those definitions trace directly to this document. When the AMVF flowchart separates mathematical modeling from physical modeling and places quantitative comparison at the intersection, that architecture reflects the V&V 10-2006 framework. This standard is the language primer for everything that follows in this course. Beyond V&V 10-2006, practicing aerospace experimentalists draw on a cluster of related documents. The AIAA measurement standards , produced by the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, address measurement methodology specific to aerospace applications: wind tunnel testing, flight measurements, and propulsion characterization. These standards specify how uncertainty should be treated in measurement-intensive aerospace environments and will surface explicitly when E2's more complex experimental setups are introduced. Measurement standards aren't generic. Different engineering domains have their own standards that define acceptable practice, and knowing which standard applies to your experiment is part of experimental design. For uncertainty analysis specifically, the ASME PTC (Performance Test Codes) standards provide procedural requirements for measurement uncertainty in engineering performance testing. These standards require that every reported result carry explicit uncertainty bounds derived by methods consistent with the standard's prescribed framework. If you're ever asked whether your measurement result is credible in a professional or regulatory context, the answer lives in whether your uncertainty analysis conforms to a recognized standard. The reference that bridges all of these to classroom practice is Coleman & Steele , Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers (4th edition). This book is the accessible bridge between the formal standards and the methods you'll use in this course, beginning in Week 2, when partial derivative error propagation and Monte Carlo simulation are introduced. Relevant chapters will be assigned as the course progresses; having the book as a reference from Week 1 means you can follow the lecture directly into its theoretical foundations whenever you want to go deeper. This is easy to miss on first encounter: when you apply the AMVF in a lab report, you're not following a course-specific convention. You're operating within the same framework that engineers at NASA, aerospace contractors, and national labs use when they document that a test was conducted correctly. Standards documents are the mechanism by which that framework is enforced across organizations and over time. ASME V&V 10-2006 will be a required reference in E3 when you develop your own V&V plan. The AIAA and ASME PTC standards will surface in E2. Knowing they exist, and having a sense of what they contain, changes how you read your own results. The Takeaway: ASME V&V 10-2006 is the professional anchor for the framework this course builds on: not a textbook chapter, but the actual document that practicing engineers cite when arguing that a test was done right. From Week 1, you're working within that professional tradition, not just a course format.

pages.title: R02
pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Reading R02 # GitHub Basics for Course Documentation ## Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened. In professional aerospace practi... GitHub Basics for Course Documentation Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened. In professional aerospace practice, an experiment that exists only in memory is worth nothing to the next engineer who needs to reproduce, extend, or audit your work. Engineering documentation has two jobs: it preserves what you did and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain meticulous records not because they enjoy paperwork, but because the value of a measurement result depends entirely on the ability of a third party to evaluate it independently. A voltage reading without context is noise. A voltage reading with a documented procedure, sensor specification, calibration record, and uncertainty estimate is evidence. GitHub is a platform for hosting and sharing documents using version control, a system that tracks every change you make to a file over time, along with who made the change and when. In software development, this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you'll use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio where each experiment you conduct has a documented home and a traceable history. The mechanics are straightforward. A repository (repo) is a folder with version history: a project folder that remembers every saved version. A README.md is a text file written in Markdown , a lightweight formatting language, that appears on the repository's front page and describes what the project is. You'll write your experiment documentation in Markdown: plain text with simple syntax for headers ( # Title ), bold ( **term** ), and lists ( - item ) that renders cleanly in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser. The workflow for adding files has three steps: stage (select which files to include in this snapshot), commit (save a labeled snapshot with a short note about what changed and why), and push (send that snapshot to GitHub's servers so it is accessible online). For this course you won't need the full depth of version control (branches, pull requests, merge conflicts), but understanding the commit as "a labeled save with a note about what changed" is the fundamental concept. Your commit messages are part of your documentation narrative; write them as though you're explaining your lab session to a colleague six months from now. Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new repository for ASEN 3501, and uploading your first file, which will be the pre-lab design work from this week's Tier 1 Phase 1 design session. That first file should include your experimental hypothesis, your identified variables, and a description of your annotated AMVF diagram. The repository structure you establish now will carry forward through all subsequent experiment deliverables. Use AI tools freely to help with syntax questions, Markdown formatting, or workflow steps. This is exactly the kind of mechanical task where AI augmentation is efficient and appropriate. One habit worth building from the start: GitHub is a record of your process, not a drop box for final products. A repository with one massive commit at the end of the semester tells no story. Frequent, small commits with clear messages create a record that shows how your thinking developed. That process record is a professional artifact. Engineers and employers who look at your GitHub portfolio are reading your technical judgment over time, not just the last thing you submitted. The Takeaway: The Tier 1 Phase 1 pre-lab work from this week's design session is your first portfolio entry. Commit it to your repository with a clear description, and begin the documentation habit that every subsequent lab deliverable will extend.

In professional aerospace practi... GitHub Basics for Course Documentation Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened. In professional aerospace practice, an experiment that exists only in memory is worth nothing to the next engineer who needs to reproduce, extend, or audit your work. Engineering documentation has two jobs: it preserves what you did and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain

and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain meticulous records not because they enjoy paperwork, but because the value of a measurement result depends entirely on the ability of a third party to evaluate it independently. A voltage reading without context is noise. A voltage reading with a documented procedure, sensor specification, calibration record, and uncertainty estimate is evidence. GitHub is a platform for hosting and sharing documents using version control, a system that tracks every change you make to a file over time, along with who made the change and when. In software development, this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you'll use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio

this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you'll use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio where each experiment you conduct has a documented home and a traceable history. The mechanics are straightforward. A repository (repo) is a folder with version history: a project folder that remembers every saved version. A README.md is a text file written in Markdown , a lightweight formatting language, that appears on the repository's front page and describes what the project is. You'll write your experiment documentation in Markdown: plain text with simple syntax for headers ( # Title ), bold ( **term** ), and lists ( - item ) that renders cleanly in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser. The workflow for adding files has three

in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser. The workflow for adding files has three steps: stage (select which files to include in this snapshot), commit (save a labeled snapshot with a short note about what changed and why), and push (send that snapshot to GitHub's servers so it is accessible online). For this course you won't need the full depth of version control (branches, pull requests, merge conflicts), but understanding the commit as "a labeled save with a note about what changed" is the fundamental concept. Your commit messages are part of your documentation narrative; write them as though you're explaining your lab session to a colleague six months from now. Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new

ssion to a colleague six months from now. Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new repository for ASEN 3501, and uploading your first file, which will be the pre-lab design work from this week's Tier 1 Phase 1 design session. That first file should include your experimental hypothesis, your identified variables, and a description of your annotated AMVF diagram. The repository structure you establish now will carry forward through all subsequent experiment deliverables. Use AI tools freely to help with syntax questions, Markdown formatting, or workflow steps. This is exactly the kind of mechanical task where AI augmentation is efficient and appropriate. One habit worth building from the start: GitHub is a record of your process, not a drop box for

pages.title: L01
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Lecture L01 E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ========================================================... E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data. ================================================================================ slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same (Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.") Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester. ================================================================================ slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise (Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.") Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions. ================================================================================ slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random (Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.) Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected. ================================================================================ slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim (Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed vertical arrow spanning ±0.4 N, labeled "Uncertainty Interval." To the right: a formal statement box reading "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Below the bar: a contrast pair. Left: a bare number "14.7 N" with a red label "Not an engineering claim — cannot be used for comparison." Right: the same number with the uncertainty statement, with a green label "Engineering claim — can be compared to a model prediction." A definition box at the bottom: "Uncertainty: a quantified bound on how large the error could plausibly be, expressed as a probability interval.") Talking Point: Uncertainty is the number that converts a raw measurement into an engineering claim. Here is the key insight: you almost never know the actual error in your measurement. If you knew the error exactly, you would have corrected for it. What you can do is bound the plausible magnitude of the error, and express that bound as a probability interval. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my instrument, my calibration procedure, and my measurement process, I am 95% confident the true value lies within 0.4 N of 14.7 N. That is an engineering claim. The bare number 14.7 N, without the interval, is just an observation — it cannot be meaningfully compared to a model prediction, because I cannot determine whether agreement is real or coincidental. Every result in this course must carry its uncertainty, stated explicitly with a confidence level and a method. ASME PTC 19.1 and professional measurement standards require this — it is not a course-specific convention but the professional standard for experimental uncertainty reporting, separate from and complementary to the modeling framework of ASME V&V 10-2006. ================================================================================ slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions (Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.) Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size. ================================================================================ slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean (Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.") Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================ slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows (Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.) Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — What Is Missing from This Conclusion? (Image: A bar graph showing three side-by-side bars on a white background. Blue bars labeled "Measured Power" at three voltage levels (3V, 6V, 9V) with values approximately 1.8 W, 7.1 W, 16.4 W. Orange bars labeled "Model Prediction (P = V²/R)" at the same voltage levels. The bars appear close in height. No error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is missing?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this figure and caption. [Pause — allow student responses.] The conclusion is not defensible. Without uncertainty bounds on the bars, you cannot determine whether the visual agreement is meaningful or coincidental. The gap between each blue and orange bar might exceed the measurement uncertainty — in which case you have a discrepancy requiring explanation. Or the gap might be negligible — in which case agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty intervals on every bar, the conclusion has no engineering basis. A professional report that made this claim would be returned immediately for revision. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Opening quote "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything" — Carl Richards — incorporated in the title slide talking point per the W2 L1 blueprint. Three-way distinction (variability / measurement error / uncertainty) is the organizing spine of the lecture, as specified in the blueprint Purpose statement. Confidence interval interpretation (slide 7) uses the correct frequentist framing — students need this for the W3 comparison work. Repeatability vs. reproducibility (slide 8) references ASME and ISO standards, consistent with the professional standards thread from Week 1. Slide 10 explicitly connects this lecture forward to W3, W4, and W5 — AMVF continuity maintained without forcing it. Monte Carlo content deferred to W2 L2 per blueprint structure. Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — missing uncertainty in bar graph claim). V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 9): Slide 5 talking point — added sentence attributing experimental uncertainty requirements to ASME PTC 19.1, distinguishing from ASME V&V 10-2006 which is the computational modeling standard. V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 6 talking point — added t-distribution note for N < 30 with MATLAB reference. Slide 7 talking point — added one sentence on Bayesian credible interval as an alternative framework, noting course uses frequentist CI per ASME/ISO standards.
