E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities (Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.") Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively. ================================================================================ slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method (Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.) Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point. ================================================================================ slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms (Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.") Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one. ================================================================================ slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest (Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational.

y, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible.

ch is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities (Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small

m: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.") Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest

ters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively. ================================================================================ slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method (Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled

· δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.) Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by

uares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a

he course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point. ================================================================================ slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms (Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V

x, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.") Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your

tity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one.

uation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one. ================================================================================ slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest (Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't

maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.") Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your

are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation

dingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break (Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full

laid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.") Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of

ose distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding. ================================================================================ slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions (Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian

====== slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions (Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be

input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.") Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. Uniform is the honest choice when all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no information about how errors are distributed within that range. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you

distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. The output distributions in both cases will look roughly Gaussian by the central limit theorem, but their widths will differ. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where Multiple Models Converge (Image: A single probability density plot showing three overlapping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from

apping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from I²R." The curves have different widths and are slightly offset. The region where all three curves substantially overlap is shaded green and labeled "Region of maximum overlap — best estimate of true P." A vertical dashed line marks the "True Value" falling within the overlap region. An annotation: "Overlap is evidence of consistency. Non-overlap is a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get three output distributions. Because all three equations are valid models for the same physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap

ame physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap region is where multiple independent lines of evidence converge, and that convergence is more convincing than any single model alone. If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward.

e these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward. ================================================================================ slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework (Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the

certainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.") Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step

TLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you

: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward. ================================================================================ slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example (Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars,

solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like

re, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature

voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the

ing. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.") Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current

n P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than

============= slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured,

framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control

ig sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.") Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those

quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain

h input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break (Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve

ulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.") Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples

pt is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding.

e partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding. ================================================================================ slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions (Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P

et) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.") Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. Uniform is the honest choice when all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no

all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no information about how errors are distributed within that range. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. The output distributions in both cases will look roughly Gaussian by the central limit theorem, but their widths will differ. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where

================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where Multiple Models Converge (Image: A single probability density plot showing three overlapping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from I²R." The curves have different widths and are slightly offset. The region where all three curves substantially overlap is shaded green and labeled "Region of maximum overlap — best estimate of true P." A vertical dashed line marks the "True Value" falling within the overlap region. An annotation: "Overlap is evidence of consistency. Non-overlap is a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get

s a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get three output distributions. Because all three equations are valid models for the same physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap region is where multiple independent lines of evidence converge, and that convergence is more convincing than any single model alone. If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the

measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward. ================================================================================ slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework (Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?"

t is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.") Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you

present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to

ncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward. ================================================================================ slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example (Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars

vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System:

id line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model

ted by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments:

ator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.") Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the

appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for.

t only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source

a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a

from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a major new element in Iteration 5 not present in the prior version. Blueprint specifies this framework should be in W2 L2 and provided as a reading in W3. V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Slides 9-10 retained per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS; motivating transition sentence added to slide 9 talking point: "Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive." - P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction. - Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint. - Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method,

s. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint. - Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method, overlap region as key output — per blueprint. - "Where would you spend your money/budget" framing embedded in slide 5 sensitivity matrix. - Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — formula choice based on sensor uncertainties). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 3 talking point — added worked partial derivative calculation step: ∂P/∂V = 2V/R for P = V²/R, evaluated at V = 12 V, R = 4.8 Ω, showing sensitivity coefficient of 5 W/V.

pages.title: R01
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Reading R01 # Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition ## A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution, not a number, your entir... Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution, not a number, your entire relationship with experimental data changes. This reading introduces a reference text you'll use repeatedly throughout this course: Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers by Hugh W. Coleman and W. Glenn Steele, Fourth Edition. It's not a cover-to-cover assignment. It's a field guide: different chapters address different problems you'll encounter, and this reading orients you to the book's structure so you can find what you need when you need it. Coleman & Steele belongs to a tradition of engineering texts that treat measurement uncertainty not as an afterthought (the error bar you add before submitting a figure) but as the primary product of an experiment. The core thesis of the book matches the core thesis of this course: the goal of an experiment is not to produce a number, it is to produce a defensible claim. A defensible claim requires not just a result but a quantified bound on how uncertain you are about that result, derived by methods that a critical reader can evaluate and reproduce. Part I of Coleman & Steele (Chapters 1–4) is the section most relevant to E1. Chapter 1 establishes the conceptual framework: the three-way distinction between elemental error sources (individual contributors to uncertainty), random errors (scatter that reduces with repeated measurements), and systematic errors (bias that does not reduce with repetition). This structure and its vocabulary are exactly what Week 2's lectures develop. If a concept from lecture feels imprecise, Chapter 1 is the first place to check; it provides the careful definitions and worked examples that lecture time does not always allow. Chapter 3 is where the partial derivative propagation method is fully derived. If you have seen the formula for propagating uncertainty through a derived quantity (summing the squared products of partial derivatives and input uncertainties in a root-sum-of-squares combination), that formula is the central result of this chapter. Coleman & Steele derive it carefully, identify the assumptions it requires (first-order Taylor series expansion, uncorrelated error sources), and work through engineering examples of the same form you'll encounter in Lab 1. The three power equations (P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R) are precisely the kind of derived-quantity example this chapter addresses. Different equation forms for the same physical quantity yield different partial derivatives and therefore different sensitivities to the same input errors. Chapter 3 is where that result is grounded in theory. Chapter 4 introduces Monte Carlo simulation as a complement to the partial derivative method. Where partial derivatives give you a closed-form estimate of output uncertainty given input distributions, Monte Carlo simulation draws many samples from those input distributions and propagates them through the full equation, revealing the output distribution directly. The two methods agree when the assumptions of partial derivative propagation hold well; they diverge when the output function is highly nonlinear or when input distributions are non-Gaussian. Week 2 develops both methods in the context of Lab 1. Coleman & Steele Chapter 4 is the theoretical grounding. If the lecture derivation moved quickly, Chapter 4 is where to find the slower version: the assumptions are spelled out, the edge cases are discussed, and worked examples show exactly where the two methods converge and where they diverge. Part II of Coleman & Steele addresses validation, comparing experimental outcomes to model predictions within uncertainty bounds. This section becomes more relevant in E2 and E3, but the same reference you use for uncertainty propagation in Week 2 also develops the conceptual framework for the quantitative comparison methods you'll use in Week 3 and beyond. The book carries you through the full measurement-to-validation pipeline. The relationship between Coleman & Steele and the ASME standards from Week 1 is deliberate. The book's methodology is consistent with ASME PTC 19.1 (the measurement uncertainty standard for performance testing) and with the uncertainty quantification framework assumed by ASME V&V 10-2006. The international reference for measurement uncertainty is the ISO GUM ( Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement ). Coleman & Steele's methods are consistent with the GUM framework, making the book an accessible entry point into what is otherwise a dense international standard. Applying Coleman & Steele's methods in your lab reports means applying an approach that satisfies recognized professional standards, which matters when engineering results are submitted for design decisions, regulatory review, or publication. The Takeaway: Coleman & Steele is the methodological backbone behind every uncertainty propagation exercise in this course. Keep it as a reference, not a reading, and use it to ground your understanding in the professional standards that this course's framework is built on. Chapters 1 and 3 are your Week 2 anchors; Chapter 4 supports Monte Carlo; Part II becomes relevant in Week 3 and beyond.

pages.title: R02
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Reading R02 # Technical Writing in Engineering ## Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping, without hedging or over-e... Technical Writing in Engineering Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping, without hedging or over-explaining, and letting the evidence speak for itself. You have been writing for years. You know how to tell a story, argue a point, describe something you observed. Technical writing uses none of those skills in quite the same way. It's a different genre with different rules, and the rules exist for a specific reason: engineering writing isn't read for enjoyment. It's read by someone who needs to make a decision, verify a result, reproduce a measurement, or check your work. Every sentence exists to support that purpose, or it should not exist at all. This reading gives you the practical framework for engineering writing that you'll apply throughout this course, starting with the Lab 1 brief report this week. The Reader You Are Writing For Before writing a single sentence, ask yourself: who reads this, and what do they need from it? In professional engineering, your reader is a technical person who has never seen your specific work before. They are competent; they understand the physics, the methods, the vocabulary. They are not, however, clairvoyant. They cannot infer what you measured from how you describe your setup. They cannot reconstruct your uncertainty calculation from a result with no error bars. They cannot evaluate your conclusion without knowing how you got there. Write for the skeptical expert, not the sympathetic friend. The sympathetic friend fills in gaps; the skeptical expert points at them. The goal is a document that survives the skeptical expert: one where every claim is supported, every result is reproducible, and no reader has to guess at anything essential. A second audience worth naming explicitly: the non-engineer. Several assignments in this course ask you to explain a technical result to someone without an engineering background. This is not dumbing down. It's a different technical skill. Plain-language communication requires you to identify the essential idea underneath the technical apparatus and express it without jargon. If you cannot do that, it is a signal that your own understanding is not yet complete. Worked Example: From Engineering Form to Plain Language Here is the same result written two ways. Engineering form (for the skeptical expert): > "The measured electrical power dissipated in a 5 Ω resistor with a 12 V input is 25.7 ± 1.3 W at 95% confidence, derived using partial derivative uncertainty propagation from voltage and current measurements with a ±0.2 V voltmeter and ±0.05 A ammeter." This version is correct and complete. Every number is supported. Every bound is traceable to a method. A colleague can reproduce the result. Plain-language version (for the non-engineer): > "We measured how much electrical power a small resistor was consuming when connected to a 12-volt source — similar to a standard lantern battery. The result was about 26 watts, roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses. Our measurement has an uncertainty of about 5%, which means the true value is almost certainly somewhere between 24 and 27 watts." What changed and why: Uncertainty expressed as consequence, not as a statistical bound. "±1.3 W at 95% confidence" is meaningless to a non-engineer; "about 5%" gives a sense of scale, and "almost certainly between 24 and 27 watts" states the consequence in plain language. Physical result grounded in an everyday reference. "25.7 W" is a number; "roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses" is a physical anchor that makes the magnitude interpretable. Technical method removed. The non-engineer reader does not need to know about partial derivative propagation; they need to trust that the measurement was done carefully. That trust is built through the framing ("our measurement has an uncertainty of about 5%"), not through the method details. The skeptical expert version and the plain-language version contain the same core result. Writing both isn't redundant. It's evidence that you understand the result well enough to translate it. Several deliverables in this course require both: the Lab 1 brief report, the Phase 1 report (Section 9), and the Phase 3 presentation. The Architecture of a Technical Document Every technical document (lab report, memo, technical note) is a narrative. Not a story with plot and character, but a logical argument that has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Readers orient by structure. A document without clear structure wastes the reader's time. The standard structure for an engineering report maps directly onto the logical arc of a technical argument: Introduction — Three things, in this order: (1) what this document is about and why it was written; (2) what new information it contains that the reader did not have before; (3) why the results matter. The introduction is not background for its own sake; it is a contract with the reader about what they are about to receive. Body — The evidence: your method, your measurements, your analysis. Sufficient detail that someone else could reproduce your results. This is where your figures, tables, and equations live, each one integrated into the narrative, not appended to it. Conclusion — Not a summary of the introduction. A conclusion states: (1) what was found; (2) what that finding implies; (3) what remains uncertain or unresolved; (4) what comes next. Conclusions that merely restate the introduction have done nothing. If a section of your report does not serve one of these three purposes, it probably should not be there. Figures: The Core Unit of Technical Communication Most technical writing errors are not in the prose. They are in the figures and in the prose that surrounds them. A figure in an engineering report is not a visual aid. It is an argument. It exists to support a specific claim, and every element of the figure must serve that claim. When you produce any figure in this course, the following elements are required, not optional: Labeled axes with units. "Force (N)" not "Force." "Time (s)" not "seconds." No axis is unlabeled, no unit is omitted. A caption that identifies and explains. The caption must name every plotted element and explain what the figure shows. "Figure 2. Measured power vs. voltage for all three configurations" is a label. "Figure 2. Measured power as a function of applied voltage for the P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R configurations. Error bars represent ±1σ from partial-derivative uncertainty propagation. The three configurations converge at low voltages but diverge above 5V, consistent with resistance variation as a function of temperature." That is a caption. Reference in the text. Every figure is referenced before it appears. "Figure 2 shows..." or "as shown in Figure 2..." Never let a figure float in the document without a textual anchor that explains why it is there. Conclusions drawn explicitly. Do not leave the reader to extract the point. After referencing the figure, state what it shows, what it implies, and whether any anomalies require explanation. The Language of Technical Writing Engineering writing has a distinctive voice, and it is not stuffy or passive by accident. The conventions exist because precision matters more than personality. No contractions. "Do not" not "don't." "Cannot" not "can't." This isn't pedantry; contractions signal informality and undermine technical credibility in a professional document. Define before you use. Every acronym is spelled out on first use: "National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)." Every non-obvious symbol is defined. Assume the reader can look up a word; do not assume they will know what your variable names mean. Be specific. Vague claims invite skepticism. "The measurement was close to the predicted value" tells the reader nothing useful. "The measured power (4.32 W) fell within the 95% confidence interval of the propagated uncertainty estimate (4.18 to 4.51 W)" makes a falsifiable claim. Every quantitative result should be stated with its uncertainty in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level). Active voice, lean sentences. "The apparatus was connected, and three sets of data were collected" is cleaner than "I plugged in the apparatus and successfully collected three sets of data." The passive voice is appropriate when the actor is irrelevant or unknown, not as a default. The goal is directness, not impersonality. Remove what does not earn its place. Technical writing is edited ruthlessly. Words like "very," "basically," "actually," "in order to," "it should be noted that," and "as previously mentioned" add length without adding meaning. The revision process is not about polishing prose. It's about cutting everything that does not serve the reader's need to understand your result. Numbers, Equations, and Units Numbers and equations follow conventions that are enforced uniformly in professional engineering writing, and they will be enforced in this course. Space between number and unit. Write 9.81 m/s² not 9.81m/s² . Write 2.4 mm not 2.4mm . No exceptions. Consistent units. Do not switch between metric and imperial mid-document. If your data is in N, your results are in N. Variables are italicized; numbers and units are not. The variable P represents power; the value 4.32 W is a measurement. Only variables are italicized. Equations are part of the text. Introduce every equation in a sentence: "Power dissipated in the resistor is given by..." Equations aren't standalone objects; they are statements that the surrounding prose must set up and interpret. Every result carries uncertainty. A number without uncertainty bounds is not an engineering result; it is a reading. State method, confidence level, and bounds every time. The Revision Mindset Good technical writing isn't written; it's revised. The document you produce in the first pass is a draft that organizes your thinking, not a product that communicates it. Every professional communicator, engineers included, treats revision as the actual work. A practical three-pass approach: First pass , check structure. Does the document have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion that form a complete logical argument? Second pass , check claims. Is every quantitative statement supported? Is every figure correctly referenced and captioned? Is every conclusion drawn explicitly from evidence? Third pass , check language. Are there contractions? Vague terms? Redundant words? Sentences that could be cut in half without losing meaning? This course's use of AI for drafting does not change this framework. It intensifies it. An AI-generated draft may have perfect grammar and confident tone while being subtly wrong about the physics, imprecise about the uncertainty, or logically disconnected in its conclusions. Your job isn't to produce the draft; it is to know what right looks like so you can recognize when the draft is wrong. The revision mindset is the professional skill this course is building. A Checklist for Every Deliverable Before submitting any written work in this course, confirm: [ ] Every figure has labeled axes with units, a complete caption, and an explicit reference in the text [ ] Every quantitative result is stated in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level) [ ] The introduction states purpose, new information, and significance [ ] The conclusion states findings, implications, uncertainties, and next steps, not a restatement of the introduction [ ] No contractions appear anywhere in the document [ ] Every acronym is defined on first use; every symbol is defined before use [ ] There is a space between every number and its unit [ ] Units are consistent throughout [ ] Every claim that needs evidence has it [ ] The document has been read aloud at least once; sentences that are hard to say aloud are hard to read The Takeaway: Technical writing is not a soft skill bolted onto engineering. It is the mechanism by which your results become claims that others can evaluate, reproduce, and build on. A result that cannot be communicated clearly is a result that does not yet exist as engineering knowledge. CLO Alignment: CLO 5 — Communicate experimental results effectively using standard technical writing conventions Assigned: E1, Week 2 | Companion deliverable: Lab 1 Brief Report Sources: Write More Good Club Technical Writing Checklist (UTEP/Rumpf Lab); Technical Writer's Checklist, TSC 2007; ASEN 2003 Lab Report Guide (Scheeres/Park, CU Boulder)

chunks.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Reading R02 # Technical Writing in Engineering ## Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping, without hedging or over-e... Technical Writing in Engineering Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping, without hedging or over-explaining, and letting the evidence speak for itself. You have been writing for years. You know how to tell a story, argue a point, describe something you observed. Technical writing uses none of those skills in quite the same way. It's a different genre with different rules, and the rules exist for a specific reason:

se skills in quite the same way. It's a different genre with different rules, and the rules exist for a specific reason: engineering writing isn't read for enjoyment. It's read by someone who needs to make a decision, verify a result, reproduce a measurement, or check your work. Every sentence exists to support that purpose, or it should not exist at all. This reading gives you the practical framework for engineering writing that you'll apply throughout this course, starting with the Lab 1 brief report this week. The Reader You Are Writing For Before writing a single sentence, ask yourself: who reads this, and what do they need from it? In professional engineering, your reader is a technical person who has never seen your specific work before. They are competent; they understand the

your reader is a technical person who has never seen your specific work before. They are competent; they understand the physics, the methods, the vocabulary. They are not, however, clairvoyant. They cannot infer what you measured from how you describe your setup. They cannot reconstruct your uncertainty calculation from a result with no error bars. They cannot evaluate your conclusion without knowing how you got there. Write for the skeptical expert, not the sympathetic friend. The sympathetic friend fills in gaps; the skeptical expert points at them. The goal is a document that survives the skeptical expert: one where every claim is supported, every result is reproducible, and no reader has to guess at anything essential. A second audience worth naming explicitly: the non-engineer.

oducible, and no reader has to guess at anything essential. A second audience worth naming explicitly: the non-engineer. Several assignments in this course ask you to explain a technical result to someone without an engineering background. This is not dumbing down. It's a different technical skill. Plain-language communication requires you to identify the essential idea underneath the technical apparatus and express it without jargon. If you cannot do that, it is a signal that your own understanding is not yet complete. Worked Example: From Engineering Form to Plain Language Here is the same result written two ways. Engineering form (for the skeptical expert): > "The measured electrical power dissipated in a 5 Ω resistor with a 12 V input is 25.7 ± 1.3 W at 95% confidence, derived using

easured electrical power dissipated in a 5 Ω resistor with a 12 V input is 25.7 ± 1.3 W at 95% confidence, derived using partial derivative uncertainty propagation from voltage and current measurements with a ±0.2 V voltmeter and ±0.05 A ammeter." This version is correct and complete. Every number is supported. Every bound is traceable to a method. A colleague can reproduce the result. Plain-language version (for the non-engineer): > "We measured how much electrical power a small resistor was consuming when connected to a 12-volt source — similar to a standard lantern battery. The result was about 26 watts, roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses. Our measurement has an uncertainty of about 5%, which means the true value is almost certainly somewhere between 24 and 27 watts." What

has an uncertainty of about 5%, which means the true value is almost certainly somewhere between 24 and 27 watts." What changed and why: Uncertainty expressed as consequence, not as a statistical bound. "±1.3 W at 95% confidence" is meaningless to a non-engineer; "about 5%" gives a sense of scale, and "almost certainly between 24 and 27 watts" states the consequence in plain language. Physical result grounded in an everyday reference. "25.7 W" is a number; "roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses" is a physical anchor that makes the magnitude interpretable. Technical method removed. The non-engineer reader does not need to know about partial derivative propagation; they need to trust that the measurement was done carefully. That trust is built through the framing ("our

e propagation; they need to trust that the measurement was done carefully. That trust is built through the framing ("our measurement has an uncertainty of about 5%"), not through the method details. The skeptical expert version and the plain-language version contain the same core result. Writing both isn't redundant. It's evidence that you understand the result well enough to translate it. Several deliverables in this course require both: the Lab 1 brief report, the Phase 1 report (Section 9), and the Phase 3 presentation. The Architecture of a Technical Document Every technical document (lab report, memo, technical note) is a narrative. Not a story with plot and character, but a logical argument that has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Readers orient by structure. A document without

cter, but a logical argument that has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Readers orient by structure. A document without clear structure wastes the reader's time. The standard structure for an engineering report maps directly onto the logical arc of a technical argument: Introduction — Three things, in this order: (1) what this document is about and why it was written; (2) what new information it contains that the reader did not have before; (3) why the results matter. The introduction is not background for its own sake; it is a contract with the reader about what they are about to receive. Body — The evidence: your method, your measurements, your analysis. Sufficient detail that someone else could reproduce your results. This is where your figures, tables, and equations live, each one

detail that someone else could reproduce your results. This is where your figures, tables, and equations live, each one integrated into the narrative, not appended to it. Conclusion — Not a summary of the introduction. A conclusion states: (1) what was found; (2) what that finding implies; (3) what remains uncertain or unresolved; (4) what comes next. Conclusions that merely restate the introduction have done nothing. If a section of your report does not serve one of these three purposes, it probably should not be there. Figures: The Core Unit of Technical Communication Most technical writing errors are not in the prose. They are in the figures and in the prose that surrounds them. A figure in an engineering report is not a visual aid. It is an argument. It exists to support a specific

urrounds them. A figure in an engineering report is not a visual aid. It is an argument. It exists to support a specific claim, and every element of the figure must serve that claim. When you produce any figure in this course, the following elements are required, not optional: Labeled axes with units. "Force (N)" not "Force." "Time (s)" not "seconds." No axis is unlabeled, no unit is omitted. A caption that identifies and explains. The caption must name every plotted element and explain what the figure shows. "Figure 2. Measured power vs. voltage for all three configurations" is a label. "Figure 2. Measured power as a function of applied voltage for the P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R configurations. Error bars represent ±1σ from partial-derivative uncertainty propagation. The three

= V²/R, and P = I²R configurations. Error bars represent ±1σ from partial-derivative uncertainty propagation. The three configurations converge at low voltages but diverge above 5V, consistent with resistance variation as a function of temperature." That is a caption. Reference in the text. Every figure is referenced before it appears. "Figure 2 shows..." or "as shown in Figure 2..." Never let a figure float in the document without a textual anchor that explains why it is there. Conclusions drawn explicitly. Do not leave the reader to extract the point. After referencing the figure, state what it shows, what it implies, and whether any anomalies require explanation. The Language of Technical Writing Engineering writing has a distinctive voice, and it is not stuffy or passive by accident.
