pages.title: lab1 PIV
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Assessment lab1 PIV # ASEN 3501 — Lab 1: Power, Voltage, and the Language of Uncertainty ## Student-Facing Assignment **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Week:** 2 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 5 **Assessment:** Brief l... ASEN 3501 — Lab 1: Power, Voltage, and the Language of Uncertainty Student-Facing Assignment Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Week: 2 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 5 Assessment: Brief lab report (support document) + 3-minute oral interview Estimated time: 3–5 hours total (in-lab activity: ~1 hour; report preparation: 2–4 hours) Why This Lab Exists Every engineering measurement you will ever take comes with a number and an uncertainty. The number without the uncertainty is incomplete — it tells you what the instrument displayed, not what the physical quantity actually is or how confident you should be in that display. This lab is your first chance to practice treating uncertainty as a required output, not an afterthought. The physical setup is deliberately simple: a resistor, a power supply, and a multimeter. The simplicity is intentional. There is nothing here to distract you from the core question: given that your instruments have limits, what can you actually claim about the power dissipated in this circuit? That question — and the rigorous way to answer it — is the foundation on which everything else in this course is built. Learning Goals By the end of this lab and its associated activities, you should be able to: Measure voltage and current using a digital multimeter and correctly read and record the result — including an appropriate uncertainty assigned from the instrument datasheet. Apply first-order uncertainty propagation (the general method using partial derivatives) to compute the uncertainty in a derived quantity — electrical power — from measurements of voltage and current. Explain why different algebraic forms of the same physical law (P = IV, P = V²/R, P = I²R) produce different uncertainty estimates for the same quantity, and articulate which form is most sensitive to which measured input. Run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to estimate uncertainty using the same manufacturer specifications, and compare the Monte Carlo result to the partial-derivative result. State a result in the correct engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, confidence level or method) . Explain the core concepts of this lab clearly and concisely to someone who has not taken this course. Background: The Circuit You will build a simple series circuit: a DC power supply, a known resistor, and a multimeter. You will measure: V — voltage across the resistor (voltmeter, in parallel) I — current through the resistor (ammeter, in series) From these measurements you will compute electrical power three ways: Equation Measured inputs What you derive P = IV I and V directly Power from both measurements P = V²/R V and R (nominal) Power using voltage and resistance P = I²R I and R (nominal) Power using current and resistance Each form uses different measured inputs. Different inputs have different uncertainties. Different forms therefore produce different uncertainty estimates for the same quantity P. This is not an error — it is the physics of error propagation. The Instrument — Reading Your Multimeter What the display is telling you A digital multimeter display shows a numerical reading that is updated at a fixed rate. On most benchtop multimeters, the least significant digit — the rightmost digit — will fluctuate during a measurement. This is not noise in the measurement. It is the instrument's resolution limit combined with real variation in the signal. The rule for recording measurements from a fluctuating display: > Cover the fluctuating digit with your finger. Read and record the stable digits. Assign > an uncertainty of ±½ of the last stable digit's place value. Example: Your voltmeter reads 4.9_3_, 4.9_2_, 4.9_4_ — the units digit in the tenths place is stable (4.9) but the hundredths digit fluctuates (2, 3, 4). You record 4.9 V and assign an uncertainty of ±0.05 V (half of one unit in the tenths place). This is a conservative but not overcautious recording strategy. You are not ignoring variation — you are bounding it at the resolution of the instrument. The tradeoff matters: if you assign unnecessarily large uncertainties, the systems designed around your measurements will be over-engineered and expensive. If you assign uncertainties that are too small, those systems will fail. The correct answer is the smallest defensible bound. Using the datasheet The instrument datasheet specifies accuracy in the form: ± (% of reading + % of range) For example: ± (0.5% of reading + 0.1% of range). This is the manufacturer specification for the maximum systematic error. You will use this specification — not the fluctuating digit alone — as your formal uncertainty estimate for the partial-derivative and Monte Carlo analyses. You will receive the multimeter datasheet at the start of the lab session. Before you begin any measurements, locate the specification for: DC voltage accuracy in the range you are using DC current accuracy in the range you are using Write these down. They are your primary uncertainty inputs. In-Lab Procedure The procedure has three parts: building and measuring (steps 1–4), uncertainty analysis from the datasheet (steps 5–7), and Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB (steps 8–9). Build the series circuit using the provided resistor, power supply, and multimeter. Record the nominal resistor value and tolerance from its label. Set the power supply to a target voltage (specified by your lab instructor). Read and record V and I from the multimeter displays using the fluctuating-digit procedure above. Record each reading three times to check for repeatability. Compute P three ways from your recorded values. Note the differences. Using the manufacturer specifications from the datasheet, compute the uncertainty in V and the uncertainty in I. Apply first-order error propagation (partial derivatives) to compute the uncertainty in P for each of the three forms. Show your work: write out the partial derivatives, substitute values, and state the result as P ± u_P. Answer: which form of the power equation is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Where would you spend money on a better instrument if minimizing uncertainty in P was the goal? Using the same manufacturer specifications as probability distribution parameters, run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB for each of the three power equation forms. Model V and I as independent random variables drawn from distributions consistent with the datasheet. Two defensible choices are: (a) a uniform distribution spanning [measured value − u, measured value + u], which treats the spec as a hard tolerance bound with no assumed shape; or (b) a Gaussian distribution with σ = u/2, which treats the spec as a ±2σ (approximately 95%) coverage bound. See W2_L2 slide 7 for the reasoning behind each choice. State your distribution choice explicitly in your report and justify it in one sentence. Use at least 10,000 samples. Plot the resulting distribution of P for each form on the same figure with appropriate labels and captions. Compare the Monte Carlo result to your partial-derivative result. Are they consistent? If not, explain why they might differ. Note: if you used σ = u/2 in your Gaussian simulation (treating the spec as a ±2σ bound), expect your Monte Carlo output standard deviation to be approximately half your partial-derivative δP. This is not an error — it reflects the σ-convention you chose. State your convention explicitly and check consistency in Section 6 of your report. Deliverable: Brief Lab Report You will prepare a brief lab report to bring to your oral interview. This document is your support material for the interview — you may refer to it during the conversation, but the interview questions will probe your understanding, not your ability to read your own document. The report is not graded independently. It is evidence that you have prepared. Required sections 1. Circuit description and measurement record One paragraph and a simple schematic or sketch. What did you build? What did you measure? Include your recorded V and I values with units. Include the datasheet specification you used. 2. Fluctuating digit — what you did and why Describe the fluctuating digit procedure in your own words. What did the display show? What did you record? What uncertainty did you assign from the display, and why? 3. Uncertainty propagation — partial derivatives Show the three partial derivative derivations for u_P under each power equation form. Present the final result for each as P ± u_P with units. One table or three clearly labeled calculations is acceptable. 4. Sensitivity analysis Which form is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Answer with numbers, not just words. Where would you invest in a better instrument, and why? 5. Monte Carlo simulation Include your MATLAB figure(s) showing the distributions of P for each equation form. Each figure must have a title, labeled axes with units, a legend, and a caption that states the key takeaway in one sentence. Include the key MATLAB code (the simulation loop and distribution plot — not every setup line). State what distribution you used for V and I (Gaussian or uniform), and justify your choice in one sentence. State the σ-convention you applied (e.g., σ = u or σ = u/2) so the comparison in Section 6 is unambiguous. 6. Comparison: partial derivatives vs. Monte Carlo Are the results consistent? State the comparison numerically — do not just say "yes they agree." What does the comparison tell you about when you might prefer one method over the other? 7. Result statement State your best estimate of P in the correct engineering form: P = [value] ± [uncertainty] [units] ([confidence level or method, e.g., 95% CI or manufacturer spec bound]) 8. Plain-language explanation In 3–5 sentences, explain what you did in this lab and what your result means to someone who has never taken an engineering course. Assume your audience is a curious high school student. This section should be in your introduction or conclusion — not appended as an afterthought. Oral Interview The oral interview is a 3-minute, one-on-one conversation with a member of the teaching team. You may bring your lab report. No other materials are permitted. The interview is a pass/fail assessment. You must pass this interview to satisfy the Lab 1 requirement. See the separate file LAB1_PIV_rubric_student.md for the question categories and what a passing response looks like. Prepare by reviewing that document before your interview. Preparing with the course AI tutor The course AI tutor persona is described in the E0 supplemental materials. You can upload this assignment document and the student rubric to a Claude or ChatGPT session configured with the tutor persona, and practice your oral responses before the interview. Example prompts: *"Ask me an oral interview question about why P = IV and P = V²/R give different uncertainty estimates."* "Pretend you are the instructor and ask me to explain the fluctuating digit procedure." *"Give me a harder version of a question about when Monte Carlo is preferable to the partial derivative method."* The AI tutor cannot predict the exact questions you will be asked, but it can give you targeted practice on the core concepts. Use it. Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 2 Lecture: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Coleman & Steele, Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers , 4th ed. — Chapter on uncertainty propagation (reference copy on course page) Multimeter datasheet — provided at lab session MATLAB starter script — available on Canvas (sets up the Monte Carlo loop structure; you complete the equation forms and plotting) E0 Supplemental: Virtual Multimeter Simulator (optional bonus activity) E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice For multimeter to measure current and voltage simultaneous: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y6xnLkiUMn8 ASEN 3501 — Lab 1 Assignment | E1 Week 2 Evergreen — physics content; Update-friendly — multimeter datasheet specifications, MATLAB starter script, power supply target voltage

lay shows a numerical reading that is updated at a fixed rate. On most benchtop multimeters, the least significant digit — the rightmost digit — will fluctuate during a measurement. This is not noise in the measurement. It is the instrument's resolution limit combined with real variation in the signal. The rule for recording measurements from a fluctuating display: > Cover the fluctuating digit with your finger. Read and record the stable digits. Assign > an uncertainty of ±½ of the last stable digit's place value. Example: Your voltmeter reads 4.9_3_, 4.9_2_, 4.9_4_ — the units digit in the tenths place is stable (4.9) but the hundredths digit fluctuates (2, 3, 4). You record 4.9 V and assign an uncertainty of ±0.05 V (half of one unit in the tenths place). This is a conservative but not

record 4.9 V and assign an uncertainty of ±0.05 V (half of one unit in the tenths place). This is a conservative but not overcautious recording strategy. You are not ignoring variation — you are bounding it at the resolution of the instrument. The tradeoff matters: if you assign unnecessarily large uncertainties, the systems designed around your measurements will be over-engineered and expensive. If you assign uncertainties that are too small, those systems will fail. The correct answer is the smallest defensible bound. Using the datasheet The instrument datasheet specifies accuracy in the form: ± (% of reading + % of range) For example: ± (0.5% of reading + 0.1% of range). This is the manufacturer specification for the maximum systematic error. You will use this specification — not the

nge). This is the manufacturer specification for the maximum systematic error. You will use this specification — not the fluctuating digit alone — as your formal uncertainty estimate for the partial-derivative and Monte Carlo analyses. You will receive the multimeter datasheet at the start of the lab session. Before you begin any measurements, locate the specification for: DC voltage accuracy in the range you are using DC current accuracy in the range you are using Write these down. They are your primary uncertainty inputs. In-Lab Procedure The procedure has three parts: building and measuring (steps 1–4), uncertainty analysis from the datasheet (steps 5–7), and Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB (steps 8–9). Build the series circuit using the provided resistor, power supply, and

nd Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB (steps 8–9). Build the series circuit using the provided resistor, power supply, and multimeter. Record the nominal resistor value and tolerance from its label. Set the power supply to a target voltage (specified by your lab instructor). Read and record V and I from the multimeter displays using the fluctuating-digit procedure above. Record each reading three times to check for repeatability. Compute P three ways from your recorded values. Note the differences. Using the manufacturer specifications from the datasheet, compute the uncertainty in V and the uncertainty in I. Apply first-order error propagation (partial derivatives) to compute the uncertainty in P for each of the three forms. Show your work: write out the partial derivatives, substitute

compute the uncertainty in P for each of the three forms. Show your work: write out the partial derivatives, substitute values, and state the result as P ± u_P. Answer: which form of the power equation is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Where would you spend money on a better instrument if minimizing uncertainty in P was the goal? Using the same manufacturer specifications as probability distribution parameters, run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB for each of the three power equation forms. Model V and I as independent random variables drawn from distributions consistent with the datasheet. Two defensible choices are: (a) a uniform distribution spanning [measured value − u, measured value + u], which treats the spec as a hard tolerance bound with no assumed shape; or (b) a

measured value − u, measured value + u], which treats the spec as a hard tolerance bound with no assumed shape; or (b) a Gaussian distribution with σ = u/2, which treats the spec as a ±2σ (approximately 95%) coverage bound. See W2_L2 slide 7 for the reasoning behind each choice. State your distribution choice explicitly in your report and justify it in one sentence. Use at least 10,000 samples. Plot the resulting distribution of P for each form on the same figure with appropriate labels and captions. Compare the Monte Carlo result to your partial-derivative result. Are they consistent? If not, explain why they might differ. Note: if you used σ = u/2 in your Gaussian simulation (treating the spec as a ±2σ bound), expect your Monte Carlo output standard deviation to be approximately half

imulation (treating the spec as a ±2σ bound), expect your Monte Carlo output standard deviation to be approximately half your partial-derivative δP. This is not an error — it reflects the σ-convention you chose. State your convention explicitly and check consistency in Section 6 of your report. Deliverable: Brief Lab Report You will prepare a brief lab report to bring to your oral interview. This document is your support material for the interview — you may refer to it during the conversation, but the interview questions will probe your understanding, not your ability to read your own document. The report is not graded independently. It is evidence that you have prepared. Required sections 1. Circuit description and measurement record One paragraph and a simple schematic or sketch. What

ed. Required sections 1. Circuit description and measurement record One paragraph and a simple schematic or sketch. What did you build? What did you measure? Include your recorded V and I values with units. Include the datasheet specification you used. 2. Fluctuating digit — what you did and why Describe the fluctuating digit procedure in your own words. What did the display show? What did you record? What uncertainty did you assign from the display, and why? 3. Uncertainty propagation — partial derivatives Show the three partial derivative derivations for u_P under each power equation form. Present the final result for each as P ± u_P with units. One table or three clearly labeled calculations is acceptable. 4. Sensitivity analysis Which form is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I?

arly labeled calculations is acceptable. 4. Sensitivity analysis Which form is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Answer with numbers, not just words. Where would you invest in a better instrument, and why? 5. Monte Carlo simulation Include your MATLAB figure(s) showing the distributions of P for each equation form. Each figure must have a title, labeled axes with units, a legend, and a caption that states the key takeaway in one sentence. Include the key MATLAB code (the simulation loop and distribution plot — not every setup line). State what distribution you used for V and I (Gaussian or uniform), and justify your choice in one sentence. State the σ-convention you applied (e.g., σ = u or σ = u/2) so the comparison in Section 6 is unambiguous. 6. Comparison: partial derivatives

n you applied (e.g., σ = u or σ = u/2) so the comparison in Section 6 is unambiguous. 6. Comparison: partial derivatives vs. Monte Carlo Are the results consistent? State the comparison numerically — do not just say "yes they agree." What does the comparison tell you about when you might prefer one method over the other? 7. Result statement State your best estimate of P in the correct engineering form: P = [value] ± [uncertainty] [units] ([confidence level or method, e.g., 95% CI or manufacturer spec bound]) 8. Plain-language explanation In 3–5 sentences, explain what you did in this lab and what your result means to someone who has never taken an engineering course. Assume your audience is a curious high school student. This section should be in your introduction or conclusion — not

. Assume your audience is a curious high school student. This section should be in your introduction or conclusion — not appended as an afterthought. Oral Interview The oral interview is a 3-minute, one-on-one conversation with a member of the teaching team. You may bring your lab report. No other materials are permitted. The interview is a pass/fail assessment. You must pass this interview to satisfy the Lab 1 requirement. See the separate file LAB1_PIV_rubric_student.md for the question categories and what a passing response looks like. Prepare by reviewing that document before your interview. Preparing with the course AI tutor The course AI tutor persona is described in the E0 supplemental materials. You can upload this assignment document and the student rubric to a Claude or ChatGPT

in the E0 supplemental materials. You can upload this assignment document and the student rubric to a Claude or ChatGPT session configured with the tutor persona, and practice your oral responses before the interview. Example prompts: *"Ask me an oral interview question about why P = IV and P = V²/R give different uncertainty estimates."* "Pretend you are the instructor and ask me to explain the fluctuating digit procedure." *"Give me a harder version of a question about when Monte Carlo is preferable to the partial derivative method."* The AI tutor cannot predict the exact questions you will be asked, but it can give you targeted practice on the core concepts. Use it. Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 2 Lecture: Variability,

e it. Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 2 Lecture: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Coleman & Steele, Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers , 4th ed. — Chapter on uncertainty propagation (reference copy on course page) Multimeter datasheet — provided at lab session MATLAB starter script — available on Canvas (sets up the Monte Carlo loop structure; you complete the equation forms and plotting) E0 Supplemental: Virtual Multimeter Simulator (optional bonus activity) E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice For multimeter to measure current and voltage simultaneous: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y6xnLkiUMn8 ASEN 3501 — Lab 1 Assignment | E1 Week 2 Evergreen — physics content; Update-friendly —

.youtube.com/watch?v=Y6xnLkiUMn8 ASEN 3501 — Lab 1 Assignment | E1 Week 2 Evergreen — physics content; Update-friendly — multimeter datasheet specifications, MATLAB starter script, power supply target voltage

pages.title: lab1 PIV faq
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Assessment lab1 PIV faq # Lab 1 (P=IV) — Frequently Asked Questions ## ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods *This document answers questions that come up frequently during Lab 1 preparation, execution, and report writing. If your questi... Lab 1 (P=IV) — Frequently Asked Questions ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods This document answers questions that come up frequently during Lab 1 preparation, execution, and report writing. If your question isn't here, ask your TA or the course AI tutor. Before the lab Should I use the nominal resistance value (from the label) or the measured resistance (from a pre-assembly DMM reading) when I compute power and propagate uncertainty? Use the measured value with u_R propagated from the DMM voltage accuracy specification. The measured value is more accurate than the nominal ±5% label. The nominal value is still useful for a sanity check — your measured value should fall within the stated tolerance. If it doesn't, something is wrong with the measurement or the component. Do I need to memorize my report for the oral interview? No. You may bring your lab report and refer to it freely. What the interviewer is assessing is whether you can explain the reasoning behind a result, not whether you can recall a formula from memory. If asked to walk through the partial derivative computation, it is completely fine to look at the formula in your report. The question is whether you can explain what each term means and why the form matters. The rubric requires you to demonstrate understanding in 3 of 5 categories. See LAB1_PIV_rubric_student.md for what "understanding" looks like in each category. During the lab Should I average the three voltage and current readings, or use each trial separately? Use the mean of your three readings as your measured value of V and I. The standard error of the mean from three readings (σ/√3) is typically much smaller than the manufacturer specification uncertainty, so the spec will dominate your formal uncertainty estimate. Record all three individual readings in your report to demonstrate repeatability — but compute P and propagate uncertainty using the mean values. Monte Carlo simulation Which distribution should I use for V and I in the Monte Carlo simulation — Gaussian or uniform? Both are defensible. The choice depends on what you know about the error source: Uniform distribution [measured value − u, measured value + u]: use this when all you know is a tolerance bound from the datasheet, with no information about the distribution shape. This is the more conservative and technically honest choice for a manufacturer specification. Gaussian distribution with σ = u/2: use this when you are treating the manufacturer spec as an approximately 95% confidence bound (k=2 coverage factor), which is common for precision instrument specifications. The factor of 2 means σ = u/2, not σ = u. See W2_L2 slide 7 for the full reasoning. Either choice is acceptable — what matters is that you state your choice explicitly in Section 5 of your report and justify it in one sentence. If you want a single defensible default: use uniform . It makes fewer assumptions. Why does my Monte Carlo standard deviation look like it's about half of my partial-derivative uncertainty? Did I do something wrong? Probably not — this is a σ-convention issue. Here's what's happening: The partial-derivative method computes δP using the full manufacturer spec value u as the input uncertainty. The result δP represents the maximum bound. If you set σ = u/2 in your Gaussian Monte Carlo (treating u as a ±2σ bound), then your simulation input standard deviation is half the spec value. The output distribution standard deviation will therefore also be approximately half of δP. This is not an error — it's a consistent consequence of your σ-convention. To make the two methods give comparable magnitudes directly, either: Use uniform distributions (the bounds match the spec value directly), or Use Gaussian with σ = u (treating the spec as a 1σ bound — less standard but makes the comparison numerically direct) The key requirement is to state your convention explicitly in Section 5 and check consistency when writing Section 6. The comparison should explain why the numbers relate the way they do, not just assert that they agree or disagree. Report writing I computed three different values of P — 0.2425 W, 0.2447 W, and 0.2404 W. They're close but not the same. Which one is my "best estimate" for Section 7? Report the result from the form with the smallest propagated uncertainty. In this lab that is typically P = IV, because both V and I are directly measured inputs and the sensitivity coefficients are smaller than in the forms that involve R² terms. Your choice should be consistent with your Section 4 sensitivity analysis. If you concluded in Section 4 that P = IV has the smallest uncertainty, your Section 7 result should use that form. The point of the sensitivity analysis is not just arithmetic — it is to make a reasoned choice about which result to report. You may report all three forms for completeness, but designate one as your primary result and explain why. Should I say "95% CI" or "manufacturer spec bound" when stating my result in engineering form in Section 7? For Lab 1, use "manufacturer spec bound" as your default. Here's the distinction: "Manufacturer spec bound" means the uncertainty was derived directly from the instrument datasheet specification, without a statistical sampling argument. This is accurate for what you did. "95% CI" means you can trace the bound to a specific probability statement — either from repeated measurements and a t-distribution, or from a manufacturer spec that explicitly states it corresponds to 95% coverage. Some multimeter datasheets do state that their accuracy specification corresponds to a 95% or 99% confidence level. If yours does, you may write "95% CI (manufacturer spec)." If it doesn't state the confidence level explicitly, write "manufacturer spec bound." The general format is: P = [value] ± [uncertainty] W (manufacturer spec bound, partial derivative propagation) Lab 1 FAQ | ASEN 3501 | E1 Week 2 Generated from simulated student gap analysis — 2026-05-26 Questions or corrections: contact the teaching team

pages.title: L01
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Lecture L01 E1_W3_L1 — Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 =================================================... E1_W3_L1 — Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics Talking Point: Weeks 1 and 2 gave you the tools to measure a quantity and express your confidence in it as an uncertainty interval. Week 3 asks the next question: now that you have a measurement with quantified uncertainty and a model prediction, how do you decide whether they agree? Not visually — not by eyeballing a plot and saying "they look close" — but with a structured, defensible, numerical argument. That shift, from visual judgment to structured comparison, is one of the most important cognitive moves in this course. This lecture gives you the tools to make it, including how to put your comparison criteria directly on the figure where anyone reading the report can see them. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem with Visual Inspection (Image: Three side-by-side plots showing identical data and model, differing only in axis scale. Plot 1: axes zoomed far out — data points and model line appear to coincide perfectly. Plot 2: axes zoomed tightly — data and model appear to diverge dramatically. Plot 3: intermediate scale — judgment is genuinely ambiguous. All three plots have the same axis labels and the same data values; only the scale differs. A caption below: "Same data. Same model. Three different visual impressions. Which one represents agreement?" A red annotation: "Agreement cannot depend on how you made the figure.") Talking Point: I want you to feel this problem before we solve it. I have shown you the same dataset and the same model prediction three times, with three different axis scales. In the first plot, they look identical. In the second, they look like significant disagreement. In the third, you are genuinely unsure. But the data and model have not changed — only the axis scale has. This is the fundamental problem with visual inspection as the basis for an engineering conclusion. A plot is a presentation choice, and presentation choices are not physics. If your conclusion about agreement changes depending on how you drew the figure, then your conclusion is not grounded in the data — it is grounded in aesthetics. The rest of this lecture is about replacing aesthetic judgment with a defined numerical protocol. ================================================================================ slide 3: What Does "Agreement" Mean Technically? (Image: A horizontal number line diagram. A vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction μ_model" is shown at a fixed position, with a light shaded band around it labeled "Model Uncertainty ±U_model." A measurement point labeled "Experimental Outcome μ_exp" is shown with a horizontal two-headed arrow labeled "±U_exp." Three cases are stacked vertically below the number line. Case A: the two uncertainty bands overlap broadly — green label "Overlap — agreement plausible." Case B: the bands just barely touch — yellow label "Marginal — quantify carefully." Case C: the bands are fully separated — red label "No overlap — genuine discrepancy requires explanation." A note: "Agreement depends on both uncertainties, not just one.") Talking Point: Agreement in the engineering sense means that the difference between your measurement and your model prediction is not larger than what can be explained by the combined uncertainty of both quantities. Not just the measurement uncertainty — both. A model prediction carries uncertainty too, from approximations, parameter assumptions, and model-form limitations. The question is not "is the measurement equal to the prediction?" but "is the difference between measurement and prediction consistent with zero, given the combined uncertainty of both?" When uncertainty intervals overlap, agreement is plausible. When they do not overlap at all, you have a genuine discrepancy that requires explanation — either the model is wrong, the measurement is wrong, or your uncertainty estimates are wrong. And "plausible" is not enough — you still need to quantify it with a specific metric. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation Metrics — Name Your Criterion Before You Look (Image: Three labeled formula boxes arranged vertically on a clean white background. Box 1: "Relative Difference: d_rel = |Q_exp − Q_model| / Q_model × 100%. Interprets as: how far apart are the values as a percentage?" Box 2: "Within Combined Uncertainty: |Q_exp − Q_model| < √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: is the difference smaller than the combined uncertainty at the stated confidence level?" Box 3: "Normalized Agreement Index: NAI = |Q_exp − Q_model| / √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: how many combined standard deviations apart are the two values? NAI < 1 suggests agreement." A red text box at the bottom: "REQUIRED: state your metric and acceptance threshold BEFORE computing the comparison. Post-hoc metric selection is not defensible.") Talking Point: A validation metric is a defined, reproducible procedure for quantifying the distance between a measurement and a model prediction in units that account for the relevant uncertainties. Three representative forms are shown here. Relative difference is the simplest — it gives you the percentage gap — but it does not account for uncertainty. The combined-uncertainty check adds the critical test: is the gap smaller than what your uncertainties can explain? The normalized agreement index generalizes this by expressing disagreement in units of combined standard uncertainty — values below one suggest agreement. The critical rule is at the bottom: you must state your metric and acceptance threshold before you look at the result. If you choose your metric after seeing the data — post-hoc, meaning after the fact — you can always find one that confirms what you hoped to find. That is a well-documented bias in experimental reporting, and it is why pre-commitment is required in every deliverable in this course. The term "post-hoc" is worth knowing: it appears in academic literature, review comments, and professional engineering standards, and recognizing it will help you identify this specific failure mode when you see it in your own work or someone else's. ================================================================================ slide 5: A Worked Comparison — Full Numerical Example (Image: A structured step-by-step worked example box. Measurement: "Q_exp = 14.7 N, U_exp = ±0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Model prediction: "Q_model = 15.3 N, U_model = ±0.6 N (from model parameter uncertainty)." Step 1: "Relative difference: |14.7 − 15.3| / 15.3 × 100% = 3.9%." Step 2: "Combined uncertainty: U_comb = √(0.4² + 0.6²) = 0.72 N = 4.7% of model prediction." Step 3: "Comparison: 3.9% < 4.7% — the difference is within combined uncertainty." Step 4 (highlighted in gold): "Conclusion: Agreement is supported at 95% confidence using the relative-difference-within-combined-uncertainty metric, pre-committed before this calculation." A note at the bottom: "All steps must appear in your report in this order.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a complete worked example so you see exactly what the report section looks like. I have a force measurement of 14.7 N with a 95% uncertainty interval of plus or minus 0.4 N, and a model prediction of 15.3 N with an estimated model uncertainty of plus or minus 0.6 N. Step one: compute the relative difference — 3.9%. Step two: compute the combined uncertainty as the quadrature sum of the two individual uncertainties — 0.72 N, or 4.7% of the model value. Step three: compare — 3.9% is less than 4.7%, so the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. Step four: state the conclusion, tied to the metric and the confidence level. Every step must appear in your report, in this order. Skipping steps, or stating the conclusion without showing the calculation, is insufficient documentation for an engineering comparison. ================================================================================ slide 6: When Agreement Fails — Disagreement Is Diagnostic (Image: A two-branch flowchart starting from "Comparison Metric Exceeds Threshold." Branch 1: "Measurement problem?" — sub-nodes: "Calibration error? Installation effect? Uncontrolled confounder?" Branch 2: "Model problem?" — sub-nodes: "Wrong equation form? Invalid assumption? Missing physical effect?" Both branches converge at "Investigate root cause — then classify the discrepancy type." Below: two residual plots. Left: residuals randomly scattered around zero — labeled "Random residuals: consistent with measurement noise." Right: residuals trending upward with the independent variable — labeled "Structured residuals: evidence of missing physics, not noise.") Talking Point: When your validation metric tells you that measurement and model do not agree within your defined criterion, that is not a failed experiment — it is a finding. The disagreement is diagnostic: it tells you that either your measurement has an unaccounted-for problem or your model is missing something. Your job is to diagnose which. The residuals — defined as: residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point — carry that diagnostic information. Random residuals that scatter around zero with no pattern suggest measurement noise. Residuals that show a systematic trend — always positive, or increasing with the independent variable — suggest a model-form limitation: the model is missing a physical effect that grows with that variable. Structured disagreement is more useful than agreement, because it tells you something specific about what the model is missing. ================================================================================ slide 7: Comparing Two Measurements — The Multi-Sensor Case (Image: Two side-by-side numerical scenarios. Scenario A: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 14.2 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 14.2| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 0.5 / 0.64 = 0.78 — within 1 combined σ. Agreement supported." Below: green checkmark. Scenario B: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 16.1 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 16.1| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 1.4 / 0.64 = 2.19 — exceeds 2 combined σ. Disagreement: investigate sensor calibration or installation." Below: red annotation. A caption: "The same metric applies whether you are comparing measurement to model or measurement to measurement.") Talking Point: The same validation metric framework applies when you are comparing two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors — which is exactly what the lab activity this week requires. The calculation is identical: compute the difference between the two sensor readings, compute the combined uncertainty, ask whether the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. If yes, the two sensors are internally consistent — you have cross-validated one against the other. If no, at least one sensor has a problem: calibration error, installation effect, or a systematic difference in what the two sensors are physically responding to. Using two independent sensors to measure the same quantity is one of the most powerful tools in experimental practice, because independent evidence is more convincing than a single instrument, and this lab is designed specifically around that. ================================================================================ slide 8: Showing Margins and Requirements on Plots (Image: A clean figure showing experimental data (blue dots with error bars) and a model prediction (solid red curve) on a plot with labeled axes. In addition to the data and model, three horizontal reference lines are added: a solid gold line labeled "Requirement: P ≤ 35 W," a dashed green line labeled "Design Margin: P ≤ 30 W," and a light gray shaded band between 30 and 35 W labeled "Margin Zone." Several data points fall below 30 W (in the "pass" region), one falls in the margin zone, and none exceed 35 W. A caption: "The plot itself communicates whether the result passes or fails — no text narrative required.") Talking Point: A brief but important practical skill: when your experiment is connected to a design requirement — a load limit, a temperature bound, an efficiency threshold — you can and should annotate your figure with that requirement directly. Put a horizontal line on the plot at the requirement value. Add a second line at the design margin if one exists. Now the figure communicates the conclusion without requiring the reader to cross-reference a separate document: every data point is visually classified as passing or failing. This technique connects your experimental result to the engineering decision the experiment was designed to inform. It is not a deep dive into systems design — that is for other courses — but it is a practical communication skill that will appear in every technical report you produce in industry. In this course, when your experiment has a defined acceptance criterion, that criterion should appear on the figure. ================================================================================ slide 9: Building a Defensible Comparison Argument — The Required Structure (Image: A structured six-step outline formatted as a numbered list, styled like a report section template. "(1) State validation metric — name it, explain why it is appropriate for this data type." "(2) State acceptance threshold — defined before the comparison." "(3) Apply metric numerically — show all intermediate calculations." "(4) State conclusion — agreement or disagreement — tied to the confidence level and the stated metric." "(5) If disagreement: describe residual structure — random or systematic?" "(6) If agreement: note the conditions under which agreement holds and any caveats about range or extrapolation." A red annotation at steps 1 and 2: "Pre-commitment required — these two steps must precede step 3.") Talking Point: I want to give you the required structure for a defensible comparison argument, because this structure will appear in every deliverable from this point forward. First: name your validation metric and justify why it is appropriate. Different data types and different modeling claims call for different metrics. Second: state your acceptance threshold before computing. Third: show the full numerical calculation with all intermediate steps. Fourth: state the conclusion explicitly — agreement or disagreement — tied to the metric and the confidence level. Fifth: if the comparison reveals disagreement, classify the residual structure. And sixth: if the comparison supports agreement, note the conditions and limits of that agreement. This is the argument structure for the Comparative Analysis Memo due this week, and it is the same structure I will look for in every subsequent lab report. ================================================================================ slide 10: The AMVF Comparison Node — What You Are Doing (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the "Quantitative Comparison" node highlighted prominently in gold. Arrows entering the node from the left are labeled "Simulation Outcomes (with model uncertainty)" and from the right "Experimental Outcomes (with measurement uncertainty)." Arrows leaving the node: one labeled "Agreement within criterion → Validation supported" pointing upward-left toward the Mathematical Model node, and one labeled "Disagreement → Investigate model-form or measurement problem" pointing downward. A bracket below the node reads "This is the activity you are executing in the Week 3 lab and memo. This is where the two branches of the AMVF meet.") Talking Point: I want to locate everything in this lecture within the AMVF, because that connection is important. The quantitative comparison node is where the two branches of the AMVF meet — where the simulation output from the mathematical branch and the experimental outcome from the physical branch are evaluated against each other. Everything you have learned in the last two weeks — measurement, uncertainty propagation, confidence intervals — has been preparation for this node. The structured comparison you are now able to execute is not a report-writing exercise. It is the central activity of the validation branch of the AMVF. When you apply your validation metric in the lab this week, you are executing a step that professional engineers perform every time they need to argue that a model is credible for a specific use case and a specific set of conditions. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluating Another's Work — The Four-Part Critique Structure (Image: A clean four-box layout on a white background, each box color-coded. Box 1 (green): "(1) What is correct and well-supported? — Name specific evidence. Identify where the report does what it claims." Box 2 (red): "(2) What is incorrect or unsupported? — Identify claims that are wrong, or conclusions that go beyond what the data shows." Box 3 (gold): "(3) What is missing? — Identify required elements that are absent: uncertainty quantification, pre-committed metric, stated confidence level, or required calculation steps." Box 4 (blue): "(4) What specific improvements would most strengthen this report? — Be actionable. Not 'add more analysis' but 'state the validation metric before applying it and show the intermediate calculation.'" A red caption below: "Name the criteria before you read — for the same reason you commit to a metric before you compute." A note: "This structure applies to the in-class comparative analysis exercise and to every peer evaluation in this course.") Talking Point: In a moment, we are going to apply everything from today's lecture to a sample lab report. Before you open it, I want to give you the four questions that organize a defensible critique — the same way I asked you to commit to a validation metric before computing. First: what is correct and well-supported? Identify what the report does right, with specific evidence. This matters because vague praise is useless — if you cannot say why something is correct, you have not evaluated it. Second: what is incorrect or unsupported? Find claims that are wrong, or conclusions that extend beyond what the data actually shows. Third: what is missing? This is the category most students underuse. Missing elements — no uncertainty quantification, no pre-committed metric, no confidence level stated — are not vague weaknesses; they are specific absences that a checklist can identify. Fourth: what specific improvements would most strengthen the report? Actionable, specific, tied to the assignment requirements. "Add more analysis" is not a critique — "state the validation metric before applying it and show the propagation calculation" is. You are committing to these four criteria now, before you read — for the same reason we commit to a metric before we compute. The quality of your critique will be evaluated on how specifically and accurately you apply all four. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Comparison Defensible? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's lab report excerpt: "After computing the data, I tried both the relative difference metric and the combined-uncertainty metric. The relative difference showed agreement (4.2% < 5.0% threshold). I used that metric for my conclusion." A bold prompt below: "Is this comparison defensible? What, if anything, is wrong with the approach?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause — allow student responses.] The approach is not defensible. The student computed the data first, tried multiple metrics, and then chose the one that gave agreement. This is post-hoc metric selection — a form of motivated reasoning that allows any dataset to be made to "agree" by selecting the metric that best suits the outcome. The rule is explicit: the metric and acceptance threshold must be stated before the comparison is computed. If the student genuinely believes relative difference is the right metric for this data type, they should be able to explain why on first principles, independent of which metric produces the more favorable answer. The fact that they tried both and chose the favorable one is the problem — not the metric itself. Real credibility requires that the comparison could have shown disagreement, and the student was committed to reporting it regardless. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure Structured comparison is not complicated, but it requires discipline at a moment when most people are tempted to eyeball the result and call it good. The discipline is this: define the metric and commit to the threshold, compute it numerically, show your work, and state the conclusion clearly — including the conditions under which it holds. When the comparison reveals disagreement, read the residuals and diagnose before you conclude. When it supports agreement, annotate your figure with the acceptance criterion so the result is visible to anyone who reads the report. This week's Comparative Analysis Memo is your first practice of this full argument structure. What you build this week is the foundational skill for every validation activity in Phase II and Phase III — and for every engineering report you write after this course is over. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Kept explicitly scoped as a communication skill, not a systems design topic, per the blueprint instruction. Pre-commitment to metric and threshold emphasized in slides 4 and 9 — matches course assessment philosophy from instructor_clarification_notes. Multi-sensor comparison in slide 7 connects to the W3 lab activity. Residual structure diagnostic introduced in slides 6 and 9 — seeds W3 L2 (calibration vs. validation, anomalous data patterns) without front-loading that content. AMVF comparison node made explicit in slide 10. Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). E1_W3_L1 — Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics Talking Point: Weeks 1 and 2 gave you the tools to measure a quantity and express your confidence in it as an uncertainty interval. Week 3 asks the next question: now that you have a measurement with quantified uncertainty and a model prediction, how do you decide whether they agree? Not visually — not by eyeballing a plot and saying "they look close" — but with a structured, defensible, numerical argument. That shift, from visual judgment to structured comparison, is one of the most important cognitive moves in this course. This lecture gives you the tools to make it, including how to put your comparison criteria directly on the figure where anyone reading the report can see them. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem with Visual Inspection (Image: Three side-by-side plots showing identical data and model, differing only in axis scale. Plot 1: axes zoomed far out — data points and model line appear to coincide perfectly. Plot 2: axes zoomed tightly — data and model appear to diverge dramatically. Plot 3: intermediate scale — judgment is genuinely ambiguous. All three plots have the same axis labels and the same data values; only the scale differs. A caption below: "Same data. Same model. Three different visual impressions. Which one represents agreement?" A red annotation: "Agreement cannot depend on how you made the figure.") Talking Point: I want you to feel this problem before we solve it. I have shown you the same dataset and the same model prediction three times, with three different axis scales. In the first plot, they look identical. In the second, they look like significant disagreement. In the third, you are genuinely unsure. But the data and model have not changed — only the axis scale has. This is the fundamental problem with visual inspection as the basis for an engineering conclusion. A plot is a presentation choice, and presentation choices are not physics. If your conclusion about agreement changes depending on how you drew the figure, then your conclusion is not grounded in the data — it is grounded in aesthetics. The rest of this lecture is about replacing aesthetic judgment with a defined numerical protocol. ================================================================================ slide 3: What Does "Agreement" Mean Technically? (Image: A horizontal number line diagram. A vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction μ_model" is shown at a fixed position, with a light shaded band around it labeled "Model Uncertainty ±U_model." A measurement point labeled "Experimental Outcome μ_exp" is shown with a horizontal two-headed arrow labeled "±U_exp." Three cases are stacked vertically below the number line. Case A: the two uncertainty bands overlap broadly — green label "Overlap — agreement plausible." Case B: the bands just barely touch — yellow label "Marginal — quantify carefully." Case C: the bands are fully separated — red label "No overlap — genuine discrepancy requires explanation." A note: "Agreement depends on both uncertainties, not just one.") Talking Point: Agreement in the engineering sense means that the difference between your measurement and your model prediction is not larger than what can be explained by the combined uncertainty of both quantities. Not just the measurement uncertainty — both. A model prediction carries uncertainty too, from approximations, parameter assumptions, and model-form limitations. The question is not "is the measurement equal to the prediction?" but "is the difference between measurement and prediction consistent with zero, given the combined uncertainty of both?" When uncertainty intervals overlap, agreement is plausible. When they do not overlap at all, you have a genuine discrepancy that requires explanation — either the model is wrong, the measurement is wrong, or your uncertainty estimates are wrong. And "plausible" is not enough — you still need to quantify it with a specific metric. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation Metrics — Name Your Criterion Before You Look (Image: Three labeled formula boxes arranged vertically on a clean white background. Box 1: "Relative Difference: d_rel = |Q_exp − Q_model| / Q_model × 100%. Interprets as: how far apart are the values as a percentage?" Box 2: "Within Combined Uncertainty: |Q_exp − Q_model| < √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: is the difference smaller than the combined uncertainty at the stated confidence level?" Box 3: "Normalized Agreement Index: NAI = |Q_exp − Q_model| / √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: how many combined standard deviations apart are the two values? NAI < 1 suggests agreement." A red text box at the bottom: "REQUIRED: state your metric and acceptance threshold BEFORE computing the comparison. Post-hoc metric selection is not defensible.") Talking Point: A validation metric is a defined, reproducible procedure for quantifying the distance between a measurement and a model prediction in units that account for the relevant uncertainties. Three representative forms are shown here. Relative difference is the simplest — it gives you the percentage gap — but it does not account for uncertainty. The combined-uncertainty check adds the critical test: is the gap smaller than what your uncertainties can explain? The normalized agreement index generalizes this by expressing disagreement in units of combined standard uncertainty — values below one suggest agreement. The critical rule is at the bottom: you must state your metric and acceptance threshold before you look at the result. If you choose your metric after seeing the data — post-hoc, meaning after the fact — you can always find one that confirms what you hoped to find. That is a well-documented bias in experimental reporting, and it is why pre-commitment is required in every deliverable in this course. The term "post-hoc" is worth knowing: it appears in academic literature, review comments, and professional engineering standards, and recognizing it will help you identify this specific failure mode when you see it in your own work or someone else's. ================================================================================ slide 5: A Worked Comparison — Full Numerical Example (Image: A structured step-by-step worked example box. Measurement: "Q_exp = 14.7 N, U_exp = ±0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Model prediction: "Q_model = 15.3 N, U_model = ±0.6 N (from model parameter uncertainty)." Step 1: "Relative difference: |14.7 − 15.3| / 15.3 × 100% = 3.9%." Step 2: "Combined uncertainty: U_comb = √(0.4² + 0.6²) = 0.72 N = 4.7% of model prediction." Step 3: "Comparison: 3.9% < 4.7% — the difference is within combined uncertainty." Step 4 (highlighted in gold): "Conclusion: Agreement is supported at 95% confidence using the relative-difference-within-combined-uncertainty metric, pre-committed before this calculation." A note at the bottom: "All steps must appear in your report in this order.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a complete worked example so you see exactly what the report section looks like. I have a force measurement of 14.7 N with a 95% uncertainty interval of plus or minus 0.4 N, and a model prediction of 15.3 N with an estimated model uncertainty of plus or minus 0.6 N. Step one: compute the relative difference — 3.9%. Step two: compute the combined uncertainty as the quadrature sum of the two individual uncertainties — 0.72 N, or 4.7% of the model value. Step three: compare — 3.9% is less than 4.7%, so the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. Step four: state the conclusion, tied to the metric and the confidence level. Every step must appear in your report, in this order. Skipping steps, or stating the conclusion without showing the calculation, is insufficient documentation for an engineering comparison. ================================================================================ slide 6: When Agreement Fails — Disagreement Is Diagnostic (Image: A two-branch flowchart starting from "Comparison Metric Exceeds Threshold." Branch 1: "Measurement problem?" — sub-nodes: "Calibration error? Installation effect? Uncontrolled confounder?" Branch 2: "Model problem?" — sub-nodes: "Wrong equation form? Invalid assumption? Missing physical effect?" Both branches converge at "Investigate root cause — then classify the discrepancy type." Below: two residual plots. Left: residuals randomly scattered around zero — labeled "Random residuals: consistent with measurement noise." Right: residuals trending upward with the independent variable — labeled "Structured residuals: evidence of missing physics, not noise.") Talking Point: When your validation metric tells you that measurement and model do not agree within your defined criterion, that is not a failed experiment — it is a finding. Below: red annotation. A caption: "The same metric applies whether you are comparing measurement to model or measurement to measurement.") Several data points fall below 30 W (in the "pass" region), one falls in the margin zone, and none exceed 35 W. A caption: "The plot itself communicates whether the result passes or fails — no text narrative required.") Talking Point: A brief but important practical skill: when your experiment is connected to a design requirement — a load limit, a temperature bound, an efficiency threshold — you can and should annotate your figure with that requirement directly. "(1) State validation metric — name it, explain why it is appropriate for this data type." "(2) State acceptance threshold — defined before the comparison." "(3) Apply metric numerically — show all intermediate calculations." "(4) State conclusion — agreement or disagreement — tied to the confidence level and the stated metric." "(5) If disagreement: describe residual structure — random or systematic?" "(6) If agreement: note the conditions under which agreement holds and any caveats about range or extrapolation." A red annotation at steps 1 and 2: "Pre-commitment required — these two steps must precede step 3.") Talking Point: I want to give you the required structure for a defensible comparison argument, because this structure will appear in every deliverable from this point forward. And sixth: if the comparison supports agreement, note the conditions and limits of that agreement. This is the argument structure for the Comparative Analysis Memo due this week, and it is the same structure I will look for in every subsequent lab report. ================================================================================ slide 10: The AMVF Comparison Node — What You Are Doing (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the "Quantitative Comparison" node highlighted prominently in gold. Arrows leaving the node: one labeled "Agreement within criterion → Validation supported" pointing upward-left toward the Mathematical Model node, and one labeled "Disagreement → Investigate model-form or measurement problem" pointing downward. A bracket below the node reads "This is the activity you are executing in the Week 3 lab and memo. This is where the two branches of the AMVF meet.") fidence in it as an uncertainty interval. Week 3 asks the next question: now that you have a measurement with quantified uncertainty and a model prediction, how do you decide whether they agree? Not visually — not by eyeballing a plot and saying "they look close" — but with a structured, defensible, numerical argument. That shift, from visual judgment to structured comparison, is one of the most important cognitive moves in this course. This lecture gives you the tools to make it, including how to put your comparison criteria directly on the figure where anyone reading the report can see them. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem with Visual Inspection (Image: Three side-by-side plots showing identical data and model,

========= slide 2: The Problem with Visual Inspection (Image: Three side-by-side plots showing identical data and model, differing only in axis scale. Plot 1: axes zoomed far out — data points and model line appear to coincide perfectly. Plot 2: axes zoomed tightly — data and model appear to diverge dramatically. Plot 3: intermediate scale — judgment is genuinely ambiguous. All three plots have the same axis labels and the same data values; only the scale differs. A caption below: "Same data. Same model. Three different visual impressions. Which one represents agreement?" A red annotation: "Agreement cannot depend on how you made the figure.") Talking Point: I want you to feel this problem before we solve it. I have shown you the same dataset and the same model prediction three times,

ou to feel this problem before we solve it. I have shown you the same dataset and the same model prediction three times, with three different axis scales. In the first plot, they look identical. In the second, they look like significant disagreement. In the third, you are genuinely unsure. But the data and model have not changed — only the axis scale has. This is the fundamental problem with visual inspection as the basis for an engineering conclusion. A plot is a presentation choice, and presentation choices are not physics. If your conclusion about agreement changes depending on how you drew the figure, then your conclusion is not grounded in the data — it is grounded in aesthetics. The rest of this lecture is about replacing aesthetic judgment with a defined numerical protocol.

rounded in aesthetics. The rest of this lecture is about replacing aesthetic judgment with a defined numerical protocol. ================================================================================ slide 3: What Does "Agreement" Mean Technically? (Image: A horizontal number line diagram. A vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction μ_model" is shown at a fixed position, with a light shaded band around it labeled "Model Uncertainty ±U_model." A measurement point labeled "Experimental Outcome μ_exp" is shown with a horizontal two-headed arrow labeled "±U_exp." Three cases are stacked vertically below the number line. Case A: the two uncertainty bands overlap broadly — green label "Overlap — agreement plausible." Case B: the bands just barely touch — yellow label "Marginal — quantify

y — green label "Overlap — agreement plausible." Case B: the bands just barely touch — yellow label "Marginal — quantify carefully." Case C: the bands are fully separated — red label "No overlap — genuine discrepancy requires explanation." A note: "Agreement depends on both uncertainties, not just one.") Talking Point: Agreement in the engineering sense means that the difference between your measurement and your model prediction is not larger than what can be explained by the combined uncertainty of both quantities. Not just the measurement uncertainty — both. A model prediction carries uncertainty too, from approximations, parameter assumptions, and model-form limitations. The question is not "is the measurement equal to the prediction?" but "is the difference between measurement and

ations. The question is not "is the measurement equal to the prediction?" but "is the difference between measurement and prediction consistent with zero, given the combined uncertainty of both?" When uncertainty intervals overlap, agreement is plausible. When they do not overlap at all, you have a genuine discrepancy that requires explanation — either the model is wrong, the measurement is wrong, or your uncertainty estimates are wrong. And "plausible" is not enough — you still need to quantify it with a specific metric. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation Metrics — Name Your Criterion Before You Look (Image: Three labeled formula boxes arranged vertically on a clean white background. Box 1: "Relative Difference: d_rel =

Image: Three labeled formula boxes arranged vertically on a clean white background. Box 1: "Relative Difference: d_rel = |Q_exp − Q_model| / Q_model × 100%. Interprets as: how far apart are the values as a percentage?" Box 2: "Within Combined Uncertainty: |Q_exp − Q_model| < √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: is the difference smaller than the combined uncertainty at the stated confidence level?" Box 3: "Normalized Agreement Index: NAI = |Q_exp − Q_model| / √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: how many combined standard deviations apart are the two values? NAI < 1 suggests agreement." A red text box at the bottom: "REQUIRED: state your metric and acceptance threshold BEFORE computing the comparison. Post-hoc metric selection is not defensible.") Talking Point: A validation metric is a

BEFORE computing the comparison. Post-hoc metric selection is not defensible.") Talking Point: A validation metric is a defined, reproducible procedure for quantifying the distance between a measurement and a model prediction in units that account for the relevant uncertainties. Three representative forms are shown here. Relative difference is the simplest — it gives you the percentage gap — but it does not account for uncertainty. The combined-uncertainty check adds the critical test: is the gap smaller than what your uncertainties can explain? The normalized agreement index generalizes this by expressing disagreement in units of combined standard uncertainty — values below one suggest agreement. The critical rule is at the bottom: you must state your metric and acceptance threshold

ues below one suggest agreement. The critical rule is at the bottom: you must state your metric and acceptance threshold before you look at the result. If you choose your metric after seeing the data — post-hoc, meaning after the fact — you can always find one that confirms what you hoped to find. That is a well-documented bias in experimental reporting, and it is why pre-commitment is required in every deliverable in this course. The term "post-hoc" is worth knowing: it appears in academic literature, review comments, and professional engineering standards, and recognizing it will help you identify this specific failure mode when you see it in your own work or someone else's. ================================================================================ slide 5: A Worked Comparison —

Measurement: "Q_exp = 14.7 N, U_exp = ±0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Model prediction: "Q_model = 15.3 N, U_model = ±0.6 N (from model parameter uncertainty)." Step 1: "Relative difference: |14.7 − 15.3| / 15.3 × 100% = 3.9%." Step 2: "Combined uncertainty: U_comb = √(0.4² + 0.6²) = 0.72 N = 4.7% of model prediction." Step 3: "Comparison: 3.9% < 4.7% — the difference is within combined uncertainty." Step 4 (highlighted in gold): "Conclusion: Agreement is supported at 95% confidence using the relative-difference-within-combined-uncertainty metric, pre-committed before this

supported at 95% confidence using the relative-difference-within-combined-uncertainty metric, pre-committed before this calculation." A note at the bottom: "All steps must appear in your report in this order.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a complete worked example so you see exactly what the report section looks like. I have a force measurement of 14.7 N with a 95% uncertainty interval of plus or minus 0.4 N, and a model prediction of 15.3 N with an estimated model uncertainty of plus or minus 0.6 N. Step one: compute the relative difference — 3.9%. Step two: compute the combined uncertainty as the quadrature sum of the two individual uncertainties — 0.72 N, or 4.7% of the model value. Step three: compare — 3.9% is less than 4.7%, so the difference falls within the combined

N, or 4.7% of the model value. Step three: compare — 3.9% is less than 4.7%, so the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. Step four: state the conclusion, tied to the metric and the confidence level. Every step must appear in your report, in this order. Skipping steps, or stating the conclusion without showing the calculation, is insufficient documentation for an engineering comparison. ================================================================================ slide 6: When Agreement Fails — Disagreement Is Diagnostic (Image: A two-branch flowchart starting from "Comparison Metric Exceeds Threshold." Branch 1: "Measurement problem?" — sub-nodes: "Calibration error? Installation effect? Uncontrolled confounder?" Branch 2: "Model problem?" — sub-nodes: "Wrong equation

alibration error? Installation effect? Uncontrolled confounder?" Branch 2: "Model problem?" — sub-nodes: "Wrong equation form? Invalid assumption? Missing physical effect?" Both branches converge at "Investigate root cause — then classify the discrepancy type." Below: two residual plots. Left: residuals randomly scattered around zero — labeled "Random residuals: consistent with measurement noise." Right: residuals trending upward with the independent variable — labeled "Structured residuals: evidence of missing physics, not noise.") Talking Point: When your validation metric tells you that measurement and model do not agree within your defined criterion, that is not a failed experiment — it is a finding. The disagreement is diagnostic: it tells you that either your measurement has an

a failed experiment — it is a finding. The disagreement is diagnostic: it tells you that either your measurement has an unaccounted-for problem or your model is missing something. Your job is to diagnose which. The residuals — defined as: residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point — carry that diagnostic information. Random residuals that scatter around zero with no pattern suggest measurement noise. Residuals that show a systematic trend — always positive, or increasing with the independent variable — suggest a model-form limitation: the model is missing a physical effect that grows with that variable. Structured disagreement is more useful than agreement, because it tells you something specific about what the model is missing.

red disagreement is more useful than agreement, because it tells you something specific about what the model is missing. ================================================================================ slide 7: Comparing Two Measurements — The Multi-Sensor Case (Image: Two side-by-side numerical scenarios. Scenario A: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 14.2 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 14.2| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 0.5 / 0.64 = 0.78 — within 1 combined σ. Agreement supported." Below: green checkmark. Scenario B: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 16.1 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 16.1| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 1.4 / 0.64 = 2.19 — exceeds 2 combined σ. Disagreement: investigate sensor calibration or installation." Below: red annotation. A caption: "The same metric applies whether you are comparing measurement to model or

tallation." Below: red annotation. A caption: "The same metric applies whether you are comparing measurement to model or measurement to measurement.") Talking Point: The same validation metric framework applies when you are comparing two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors — which is exactly what the lab activity this week requires. The calculation is identical: compute the difference between the two sensor readings, compute the combined uncertainty, ask whether the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. If yes, the two sensors are internally consistent — you have cross-validated one against the other. If no, at least one sensor has a problem: calibration error, installation effect, or a systematic difference in what the two sensors are physically

has a problem: calibration error, installation effect, or a systematic difference in what the two sensors are physically responding to. Using two independent sensors to measure the same quantity is one of the most powerful tools in experimental practice, because independent evidence is more convincing than a single instrument, and this lab is designed specifically around that. ================================================================================ slide 8: Showing Margins and Requirements on Plots (Image: A clean figure showing experimental data (blue dots with error bars) and a model prediction (solid red curve) on a plot with labeled axes. In addition to the data and model, three horizontal reference lines are added: a solid gold line labeled "Requirement: P ≤ 35 W," a dashed

data and model, three horizontal reference lines are added: a solid gold line labeled "Requirement: P ≤ 35 W," a dashed green line labeled "Design Margin: P ≤ 30 W," and a light gray shaded band between 30 and 35 W labeled "Margin Zone." Several data points fall below 30 W (in the "pass" region), one falls in the margin zone, and none exceed 35 W. A caption: "The plot itself communicates whether the result passes or fails — no text narrative required.") Talking Point: A brief but important practical skill: when your experiment is connected to a design requirement — a load limit, a temperature bound, an efficiency threshold — you can and should annotate your figure with that requirement directly. Put a horizontal line on the plot at the requirement value. Add a second line at the design

h that requirement directly. Put a horizontal line on the plot at the requirement value. Add a second line at the design margin if one exists. Now the figure communicates the conclusion without requiring the reader to cross-reference a separate document: every data point is visually classified as passing or failing. This technique connects your experimental result to the engineering decision the experiment was designed to inform. It is not a deep dive into systems design — that is for other courses — but it is a practical communication skill that will appear in every technical report you produce in industry. In this course, when your experiment has a defined acceptance criterion, that criterion should appear on the figure.

"(1) State validation metric — name it, explain why it is appropriate for this data type." "(2) State acceptance threshold — defined before the comparison." "(3) Apply metric numerically — show all intermediate calculations." "(4) State conclusion — agreement or disagreement — tied to the confidence level and the stated metric." "(5) If disagreement: describe residual structure — random or systematic?" "(6) If

fidence level and the stated metric." "(5) If disagreement: describe residual structure — random or systematic?"
