pages.title: R01
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Reading R01 # How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework ## Eight Questions Every Engineering Figure Must Answer A graph that requires a verbal explanation has already failed. A figure in an engineering report must stand alone,... How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework Eight Questions Every Engineering Figure Must Answer A graph that requires a verbal explanation has already failed. A figure in an engineering report must stand alone, answering every question a critical reader could raise without requiring the author's interpretation. Engineering figures are the primary medium for arguing from data. A well-constructed figure makes a claim, shows the evidence for that claim, and gives the reader the tools to evaluate both, without assistance from the surrounding text. A poorly constructed figure forces the reader to work to extract meaning, often arriving at the wrong conclusion or no conclusion at all. The gap between these two outcomes is not artistic talent; it is a checklist. The following eight-question framework provides a sequential checklist for evaluating any engineering figure you produce, or any figure you encounter in someone else's report. Apply these questions in order: each one checks a necessary element of a complete engineering argument from data. A figure that passes all eight can stand alone. A figure that fails any one of them is incomplete. 1. Motivation — Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer? The figure caption or title should state this explicitly in one sentence. A graph without a stated purpose forces the reader to guess, and readers guess wrong. Example: "Figure 3. Measured vs. predicted oscillation amplitude — evaluating whether the undamped model agrees with measured decay within propagated uncertainty bounds." If you cannot write this sentence, the graph does not belong in the report yet. 2. Axes — What is plotted on each axis, with units, at an appropriate scale? Axis labels without units are incomplete. Scale choices that compress variation make disagreement invisible; scale choices that amplify noise make agreement look worse than it is. Both axes should span the physically meaningful range of the quantity, not the range of convenience. 3. Data source — Where did the data come from, and how was it collected? The reader should be able to identify the sensor, the acquisition method, and the conditions under which the data was taken without reading the body of the report. This information belongs in the caption or the legend. If your figure uses data from two different sensors or collection sessions, both must be identified. 4. Uncertainties — Are error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and explicitly labeled? A data point without uncertainty bounds isn't an engineering claim; it is a raw observation. Uncertainty bounds must appear on the figure, be derived by the method described in the report, and be labeled with their basis (for example: "95% CI, partial derivative propagation from manufacturer spec"). Unless otherwise specified, error bars in this course represent ±1 standard uncertainty (k=1), not a 95% confidence interval; if you use a different coverage factor, state it explicitly. A figure with no error bars says only "here is a number." A figure with labeled uncertainty bounds says "here is a defensible claim." 5. Trend — What pattern does the data show? Is the relationship linear, exponential, oscillatory, saturating? The trend is the signal your measurement is characterizing. State it explicitly, either in the caption or as an annotated line on the figure. A reader should be able to identify the pattern from the figure alone, without searching the prose. 6. Model comparison — How does the measured trend compare to the predictive model? The model should appear on the same figure as the data, as a line, band, or point estimate, with its own label. Placing model and data on the same axes makes the comparison visible and makes the argument spatial rather than verbal. A figure that shows only data, with the model discussed in the text, requires the reader to perform the comparison in their head. Do not outsource the argument. 7. Discrepancy — If there is a gap between measured data and model prediction, what are the possible causes? This is one of the most frequently omitted elements in student reports and one of the most important in professional practice. A gap that falls within the uncertainty band is consistent with measurement noise; note that explicitly. A gap with systematic structure (increasing with the independent variable, or consistently signed) suggests a model-form limitation; name the physical effect that is likely missing. Annotate the figure directly when the discrepancy is specific to a region of the plot. 8. System-level implication — What does this graph tell you about the overall system? The figure is evidence for a conclusion about the system under study. State that conclusion. A figure that ends at "the data shows X" without connecting X to a claim about the system has done half the work. The closing sentence of the caption should complete the argument: "Result implies that the measured damping ratio (ζ = 0.12 ± 0.01) is 20% higher than the undamped model predicts, suggesting an aerodynamic drag contribution not captured by the model." Building a figure from scratch: Before opening MATLAB, start by answering question 1: what question does this figure answer? If you cannot state the question, you do not have a figure yet; you have a data-plotting exercise. Once the question is stated, the remaining seven elements follow naturally: the question determines what belongs on the axes, what comparison is needed, and what the closing sentence of the caption must say. This sequence (question first, figure second) is the difference between a figure that makes an argument and one that merely displays data. The Takeaway: Apply this eight-question checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course. It is the minimum standard for an engineering argument from data. The quality of your figures against this checklist will be evaluated in every subsequent deliverable, beginning with the comparative analysis memo due this week.

pages.title: R02
pages.text: E1 Week 3 (Course Week 3) Reading R02 # Comparative Analysis — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work ## Why the ability to evaluate a report matters as much as the ability to write one The most important thing a reviewer can tell you is... Comparative Analysis — The Meta-Skill of Evaluating Another's Engineering Work Why the ability to evaluate a report matters as much as the ability to write one The most important thing a reviewer can tell you is not what you got wrong. It's what you assumed was obvious but left out. Engineers spend years learning to produce: to design experiments, collect data, propagate uncertainty, and write the result. Far less time is spent learning the complementary skill: reading another engineer's work and determining, precisely and charitably, whether the argument holds. Yet comparative analysis is not a peripheral academic exercise. It is the mechanism by which engineering knowledge is verified before it is trusted. Every technical report that crosses a program manager's desk, every analysis that feeds a design decision, passes through someone's judgment of: does this argument hold, and what is missing? Comparative analysis is the practice of evaluating another person's engineering argument, not for aesthetic quality or writing style, but for structural validity: are the claims correct, are they supported by the evidence shown, does the work meet the requirements of the assignment, and what specifically could be improved? The four-part framework below is the structure that makes this evaluation rigorous rather than impressionistic. You are committing to it before you read, for exactly the same reason you commit to a validation metric before you compute: naming your evaluation criteria in advance prevents you from finding what you hoped to find rather than what is actually there. Part 1 — What is correct and well-supported. Every critique must begin here. Identifying what is done well is not a formality or a softener; it's a calibration exercise. If you cannot point to specific evidence that supports a claim, you have not evaluated it; you have reacted to it. A claim is well-supported when the calculation is shown, the intermediate steps are traceable, and the conclusion follows from the numbers. "The uncertainty analysis is complete" is not a valid observation unless you can cite the specific propagation steps and confirm they are present. Part 2 — What is incorrect or unsupported. A claim is incorrect when the calculation is wrong or the conclusion contradicts the data shown. A claim is unsupported when the conclusion is present but the calculation that would support it is absent. These are different failure modes and require different responses. An incorrect claim needs correction; an unsupported claim needs the missing work to be shown. A common unsupported claim in validation work: "The measurement agrees with the model," stated without showing the numerical comparison at all. Agreement is a conclusion that requires an argument, not just an assertion. Part 3 — What is missing. This is the category most evaluators underuse, and it is where the deepest value in a critique lies. Missing elements are structural absences: the required calculation is not there, the confidence level is not stated, the validation metric is not pre-committed, the residual analysis is not shown. Unlike an incorrect claim, a missing element cannot be corrected by revising what is written; it requires adding work that was never done. Identifying missing elements requires knowing the assignment requirements well enough to notice their absence. This is why the course requires pre-committed criteria in every deliverable: both for the student writing and for the student evaluating. Part 4 — What specific improvements would most strengthen the report. Critique that ends at diagnosis is incomplete. The fourth part of a defensible evaluation asks: given the errors and absences identified, what specific actions would most improve this work? Not "add more analysis," but "state the validation metric and acceptance threshold before presenting the numerical comparison, and show the intermediate calculation steps." The test of a specific improvement is whether the author could act on it without asking a clarifying question. Vague feedback is not useful feedback; it is a signal that the reviewer stopped before finishing the evaluation. Peer evaluation is comparative analysis of a live presentation. The Week 3 exercise asks you to evaluate a written report; the Phase 3 lightning talk asks you to evaluate a peer's spoken presentation. The same four-part structure applies, with one adaptation: the evidence you cite must be specific to what was said, shown, or omitted in the talk, not a general impression. "The uncertainty analysis was unclear" is not an observation; "the presenter stated a final result without showing how the sensor spec uncertainty was propagated into the derived quantity, which the rubric requires" is one. Vague praise ("the talk was well-organized") does not meet the standard; specific praise does ("the risk assessment named two physically motivated anomalous patterns before describing the outcome, which is the required structure"). The grading standard for a peer evaluation is the same as for any other deliverable: a claim without specific evidence is not a defensible claim. The Takeaway: Comparative analysis is a skill, and like all skills it must be practiced against defined criteria. Name the four questions before you read or listen, apply them specifically, and your critique becomes the kind of evidence-based argument the course requires of every deliverable. The comparative analysis exercise in Week 3 is your first graded practice, and the same four-part structure will appear in every peer evaluation and segment debrief through Phase III.

pages.title: L01
pages.text: E1 Week 4 (Course Week 4) Lecture L01 E1_W4_L1 — From Model to Experiment: Reading the Equations to Design the Test ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 4, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 Revised by V&V Executor — 2... E1_W4_L1 — From Model to Experiment: Reading the Equations to Design the Test ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 4, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 Revised by V&V Executor — 2026-05-19 (added 3-slide opening sequence: Phase 1 report orientation, calibration uncertainty propagation, predict-from-specs procedure) ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test Talking Point: You have spent three weeks learning to execute the physical modeling branch of the AMVF — how to measure, how to propagate uncertainty, how to argue for agreement. This week I want to change the direction of the question. Instead of asking "given this measurement, what does it tell us?" I want to ask "given this model, what does it tell us about how to design the experiment?" Because the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document. If you can read a model's equations and extract the sensitivity structure, you will make better instrumentation choices, select more informative test conditions, and write more defensible reports. That is what this lecture is about. But before we get into the design logic, I want to orient you to the Phase 1 report — the deliverable due at the end of this week — because this lecture connects directly to several of its required sections, and one of them you will execute in today's lab session. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Phase 1 Report — A 10-Section Integrated Argument (Image: A structured numbered list of all 10 required sections of the Phase 1 report, displayed in two columns. Left column (sections 1–5): (1) Experimental Design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors; (2) AMVF Annotation — which pipeline steps you executed and what each established; (3) Measurement Record — calibrated values with documented uncertainty; (4) Multi-Sensor Comparison — do independent measurement approaches agree within mutual uncertainty bounds?; (5) Uncertainty Propagation — partial derivative analysis, dominant error source ranking. Right column (sections 6–10): (6) Model Comparison — validation metric and residual structure classification; (7) Experimentally Informed Model — how would you update the model given the comparison?; (8) MATLAB Figures — required plots; (9) Plain-Language Summary — the result stated for a non-engineer; (10) Predict-from-Specs Exercise — uncertainty prediction from an alternative sensor spec sheet, executed in today's lab session. Sections 5, 6, and 10 are highlighted in gold. A gold banner: "These 10 sections are not a checklist — they are a single argument. Each section depends on the ones before it.") Talking Point: The Phase 1 report has 10 required sections. I want you to see them as a single integrated argument, not a to-do list, because each section depends on the analysis in the sections before it. Section 1 is your experimental design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors. Section 2 annotates your workflow against the AMVF — where each step sits in the verification and validation pipeline. Section 3 is your measurement record — calibrated data values with their documented uncertainties. Section 4 is your multi-sensor comparison — do your independent measurement approaches agree within their mutual uncertainty bounds? Section 5 is uncertainty propagation — partial derivative sensitivity analysis leading to a ranked list of error sources. Section 6 is model comparison — does the data agree with the prediction, and what does the residual structure tell you? Section 7 is the experimentally informed model — given the comparison, how would you update the model? Section 8 is your MATLAB figures. Section 9 is your plain-language summary — the same result, stated for a non-engineer. And Section 10 is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, which you will execute today during the lab session using an alternative sensor spec sheet provided by the instructor. Sections 1 through 9 form a single analytical thread from setup to conclusion. Section 10 is a separate procedure that previews Phase 2 — I am going to explain it explicitly in a moment. ================================================================================ slide 3: Calibration Uncertainty Propagation — A Worked Step (Image: A four-step workflow diagram labeled "How Calibration Uncertainty Enters Your Total Error Budget." Step 1: "Record sensor output at known reference values" — a small table showing reference input values (e.g., known masses) in one column and measured sensor outputs in a second column, with the label "known standards → recorded sensor response." Step 2: "Fit calibration curve" — a scatter plot of the reference data with a linear fit line through it, slope and intercept labeled. Step 3: "Compute calibration residuals" — a residual plot showing (measured output − curve prediction) at each reference point, with the spread of residuals annotated as "calibration uncertainty u_cal." Step 4: "Propagate as one term in the quadrature sum" — the full quadrature propagation equation displayed: u_total = √[(∂Q/∂x₁ · u₁)² + (∂Q/∂x₂ · u₂)² + u_cal²], with u_cal labeled in gold as "calibration uncertainty enters here." A gold annotation: "Calibration uncertainty is not separate from your error budget — it enters through the same propagation machinery you have been using since Week 2." A caption below: "For the centripetal force experiment: the calibration residuals of your force transducer are the u_cal term. They propagate exactly like any other input uncertainty.") Talking Point: One required component of your Phase 1 report — Section 5 — is a calibration uncertainty term in your total error budget. I want to make the procedure explicit because it is not always obvious how calibration connects to propagation. Here is the four-step chain. Step 1: record the sensor output at a set of known reference values. For a force transducer, these might be known dead-weight standards. For a temperature sensor, a precision bath. These are the calibration data points. Step 2: fit a calibration curve to those reference points — typically a linear regression, giving you a slope and an intercept. This curve is how you convert sensor voltage or count to physical units. Step 3: compute the calibration residuals — the difference between the curve's prediction and the actual measured output at each reference point. The spread of those residuals is your calibration uncertainty, u_cal. Step 4: propagate u_cal through the partial derivative framework as one additive term inside the quadrature sum, alongside your random measurement uncertainty and any systematic instrument uncertainty from the spec sheet. It enters through the same machinery you have been using since Week 2 — it is not a separate accounting system. For your Tier 1 experiment, the calibration documentation for each sensor you used tells you whether calibration was performed recently and with what precision. That documentation feeds directly into this step. If calibration records are not available, that absence is itself a reportable uncertainty. ================================================================================ slide 4: Predict-from-Specs — A Labeled Procedure (Image: A three-step labeled workflow box titled "Predict-from-Specs — Section 10 Procedure." Step 1: "Receive alternative sensor spec sheet" — icon of a one-page document labeled "Instructor-provided in today's lab session." Step 2: "Propagate specs forward" — the uncertainty propagation equation with the new sensor's specified accuracy values substituted in; the output is labeled "predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity for this sensor configuration." Step 3: "Sketch the prediction" — a hand-drawn-style sketch of the primary derived quantity plotted against the independent variable, with a shaded uncertainty band. Required annotation labels point to: x-axis (independent variable name and units), y-axis (derived quantity name and units), shaded band (±u at 95% CI), and a legend entry for the prediction curve. A gold annotation: "This is Phase 2 in miniature — predict, without data, what a different sensor configuration would produce." A note at the bottom: "No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs.") Talking Point: Section 10 of the Phase 1 report is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, and I want to name it explicitly as a procedure so you know exactly what to do when the spec sheet arrives during today's lab session. Here are the three steps. Step 1: you will receive a one-page alternative sensor spec sheet from the instructor. It describes a different sensor configuration — a different accuracy class, a different range, or a different transducer type — that could hypothetically be used to run the same experiment. Step 2: using that spec sheet, propagate the stated sensor accuracies through the partial derivative framework from Section 5 — but now with the alternative sensor's numbers substituted in. The output is a predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity if that sensor had been used instead. Step 3: sketch that prediction — a plot of the derived quantity versus the independent variable with labeled axes and an uncertainty band shown as a shaded region or error bars. I want the sketch to include labeled axes with units, the uncertainty bound quantified numerically, and a clear indication that this is a prediction, not a measurement. No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs. This procedure is Phase 2 in miniature — you are predicting, from specifications alone, what a different sensor configuration would produce. That skill is the central task of the Phase 2 prediction memo. Today is your first practice run at it. ================================================================================ slide 5: The Model as a Design Document (Image: A simple schematic showing a mathematical model equation on the left — for example, a damped oscillation equation "x(t) = A·e^(-ζωₙt)·cos(ωd·t + φ)" — with labeled parameters: A (initial amplitude), ζ (damping ratio), ωₙ (natural frequency), ωd (damped frequency). On the right, a test article diagram showing a spring-mass-damper on a shaker platform with accelerometers attached. Bidirectional arrows labeled with three questions: "What must I measure?" pointing from equation to hardware; "How precisely?" pointing from equation to hardware; "Under what conditions?" pointing from equation to hardware. A gold banner across the bottom: "Every term in the model is an experimental design requirement — if you know how to read it.") Talking Point: The connection I want you to see is this: every term in a physical model is a measurement requirement in disguise. Look at the damped oscillation equation. It has four parameters — amplitude, damping ratio, natural frequency, and phase. Each of those parameters needs to be either measured, inferred, or held constant in the experiment. The natural frequency can be estimated from the mass and stiffness — but both mass and stiffness must be measured, and if either has uncertainty, that uncertainty appears in the natural frequency estimate. The damping ratio requires the decay envelope to be resolved — which means your time resolution must be fine enough to track the amplitude decrease over several cycles. The model tells you all of this before you touch hardware — if you know how to read it. The model is the first instrumentation document. Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — whether it is centripetal force, pendulum period, or electrical power — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor. ================================================================================ slide 6: The P=IV Lesson Generalized (Image: A two-panel figure. Left panel shows the P=IV power equation example from Week 2 with the sensitivity breakdown: "P = I²R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error because current appears squared." Annotation: "You learned this pattern in Week 2." Right panel generalizes: a generic derived quantity equation Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) with a table showing three columns: "Input," "Partial Derivative ∂Q/∂xᵢ," and "Sensitivity Magnitude |∂Q/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ|." The table has three filled rows with placeholder values showing one input dominating the sensitivity. A gold arrow connects the two panels with the label "Same logic — any model, any derived quantity." Caption: "The power equation was the training problem. Now you apply the same logic to your actual experiment.") Talking Point: You learned this pattern in Week 2 with the power equations. P equals I-squared times R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error, because current appears squared and the partial derivative amplifies its contribution. That was not a special property of the power equation — it is a property of any derived quantity that depends on measured inputs. For any model Q equals f of x₁, x₂, and so on, the partial derivatives are the sensitivity coefficients, and the product of a large sensitivity coefficient and a large input uncertainty is the signature of a dominant error source. The power equation was the training example. Your Tier 1 canned experiment is the real problem. Right now, you should be taking the governing equations from your experiment — whatever physical model connects your inputs to your derived output — and computing the partial derivatives to see which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget. That calculation should have been in your Phase 1 report. If it was not, it will be. ================================================================================ slide 7: Worked Example — An Unbalanced Wheel (Tier 1 Pool) (Image: A diagram of a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass attached at radius r from the center. The disk rotates at angular velocity ω, and the centripetal force on the eccentric mass is labeled F_c = m·r·ω². Below the diagram, a partial derivative sensitivity analysis table. Column 1: "Parameter." Column 2: "Partial Derivative of F_c." Column 3: "Value (for m = 0.05 kg, r = 0.08 m, ω = 120 rad/s, typical sensor spec uncertainties)." Rows: m — ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²; r — ∂F_c/∂r = m·ω²; ω — ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. The sensitivity table shows that angular velocity dominates — the factor of 2m·r·ω, evaluated at 120 rad/s, is much larger than the others. A gold box: "Dominant uncertainty source: angular velocity measurement." This example is from the Tier 1 pool and is distinct from any experiment a student in the room is running.) Talking Point: Let me use an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that is not your assigned experiment — to walk through this analysis concretely. Consider a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass at radius r. The centripetal force is F_c equals m times r times omega squared. Taking partial derivatives: the sensitivity to mass is r times omega squared, to radius is m times omega squared, and to angular velocity is 2 times m times r times omega. At typical lab conditions — 120 radians per second, for example — the angular velocity term is much larger than the others. And there is an additional amplification: angular velocity appears squared in the model, so a 2% uncertainty in omega contributes 4% to the uncertainty in force. This immediately tells you three things: first, angular velocity measurement is the highest-priority sensor selection. Second, if your tachometer has poor precision, improve it before improving the mass or ruler. Third, the most informative test conditions are those where omega variation is detectable and measurable, not just those where force is conveniently large. ================================================================================ slide 8: Four Questions the Model Answers (Image: A clean four-question framework formatted as a 2x2 grid of boxes. Box 1 (top-left): "Which input dominates the uncertainty budget?" — icon of a ranked bar chart. Box 2 (top-right): "What sensor spec do I need?" — icon of a sensor with a tolerance specification label. Box 3 (bottom-left): "What test conditions are most informative?" — icon of a plot with a region highlighted. Box 4 (bottom-right): "What anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong?" — icon of a plot with a systematic deviation from the expected curve. A gold border surrounds all four boxes with the label: "Read these from the partial derivative structure — before you touch hardware.") Talking Point: Every physical model, when you apply partial derivative sensitivity analysis, answers four questions that directly inform your experimental design. First: which input dominates the uncertainty budget? The answer tells you which sensor needs the tightest spec. Second: what sensor precision do I need? The answer comes from working backward from the acceptable output uncertainty — what maximum input uncertainty is tolerable given the sensitivity coefficients? Third: what test conditions are most informative? Models are often most sensitive to the parameter of interest — the one you are trying to estimate — at specific values of the independent variable. Testing there maximizes the information you extract. Fourth: what anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong? If the model assumes linearity, the anomalous signature is curvature or hysteresis. If the model ignores a coupling term, the anomalous signature is a dependence on a variable you held constant. Commit to all four answers before you run the experiment. ================================================================================ slide 9: Working Backward — What Precision Do I Need? (Image: A worked example diagram. Top: "Acceptable output uncertainty goal: δF_c < 5% at 95% CI." Below: the F_c = m·r·ω² equation with partial derivative terms shown. A table computes: "Given δm from a lab scale (±0.1 g), δr from a caliper (±0.5 mm), what δω is acceptable?" Using the quadrature sum requirement and the dominant sensitivity coefficient for ω, the calculation shows that δω must be less than approximately 0.8 rad/s to meet the 5% goal. Below the table: "This means your tachometer or speed controller must have precision better than 0.8 rad/s at 120 rad/s — about 0.67% of full scale." A gold annotation: "Working backward from an output requirement to an input spec is called an accuracy requirement — it is the sensor selection criterion.") Talking Point: Here is the reverse problem, and it is the one that shows up constantly in actual test design. Suppose I have a requirement: the uncertainty in my centripetal force estimate must be less than 5% at 95% confidence. I can compute, from the partial derivative structure, what that requirement implies for each of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the angular velocity term. Inverting the propagation equation tells me the maximum allowable angular velocity uncertainty — say, 0.8 radians per second at 120 radians per second — which translates to a tachometer precision requirement of less than 0.67% of full scale. Now I can go to an instrument catalog with a specific requirement, not just a vague desire for a "good" tachometer. This is what sensor selection looks like when it is grounded in the model rather than in habit or convenience. ================================================================================ slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative? (Image: A sensitivity versus independent variable plot. X-axis: "Angular Velocity ω (rad/s)" ranging from 0 to 200. Y-axis: "Sensitivity of F_c to mass uncertainty ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²." The curve rises steeply with ω, labeled "Sensitivity increases as ω²." Three vertical dashed lines mark: "Low ω region — small sensitivity, hard to distinguish F_c from noise." "Mid ω region — moderate sensitivity, informative but not extreme." "High ω region — large sensitivity, best signal-to-noise for model comparison, but hardware constraints apply." A shaded green band marks the "most informative region" around mid-to-high ω. A note: "The model tells you where the comparison is most discriminating — test there.") Talking Point: The model also tells you where to run the experiment for maximum information. In the centripetal force example, the force depends on omega squared — which means the sensitivity of the comparison between model and data is much higher at large omega. At low rotation rates, the forces are small and hard to measure above the noise floor. At high rotation rates, the forces are large and the comparison between model and data is highly discriminating — small disagreements are detectable. Of course, hardware constraints — bearing loads, motor speed limits, safety margins — set the upper bound. But within those constraints, the model tells you that testing at moderate-to-high omega gives you the most informative comparison. This is not just intuition — it comes directly from the partial derivative structure. Testing at conditions where the model is most sensitive to the quantity of interest is how you design a meaningful experiment rather than one that could agree or disagree for no informative reason. ================================================================================ slide 11: What Anomalous Behavior Would Tell Me the Model Is Wrong? (Image: Three side-by-side plots, each showing the centripetal force model prediction (dashed line, F_c = m·r·ω²) against possible data patterns. Plot A: "Expected — scatter around the model curve within the uncertainty band." Dots scatter around the curve within gray bounds. Label: "Consistent with measurement noise — model is adequate." Plot B: "Anomalous — nonlinear deviation at high ω." Data follows the model at low ω but falls below it at high ω. Label: "Possible aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass — not in the model. Model is missing a velocity-dependent force." Plot C: "Anomalous — constant offset across all ω." Data runs parallel to the model but consistently below. Label: "Possible systematic bias in mass or radius measurement — calibration issue, not model-form error." Caption: "Anomalous signatures are predictable from model assumptions — commit to them before the experiment.") Talking Point: From the model, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would tell you the model is wrong. For the centripetal force model, if the data shows scatter within the uncertainty band, the model is adequate for this experiment. But if the data shows a systematic deviation that grows with omega — falling below the model at high rotation rates — that is a signature of an additional velocity-dependent force, like aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass, that the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega values, that is not a model-form error — it is a systematic measurement bias in mass or radius, which is a calibration issue, not a physics issue. These three patterns are distinguishable, and you can predict which ones are physically plausible before running a single trial. That predictive commitment is what I want to see in the Phase 1 report's model comparison section. ================================================================================ slide 12: The Phase 1 Report — Connecting This Lecture to the Deliverable (Image: A structured outline of the Phase 1 report's required sections, with specific sections highlighted. The full list: (1) Experimental design, (2) AMVF annotation, (3) Measurement record, (4) Multi-sensor comparison, (5) Uncertainty propagation, (6) Model comparison, (7) Experimentally informed model, (8) MATLAB figures, (9) Plain-language summary, (10) Predict-from-specs exercise. Sections 5 and 6 are highlighted in gold with annotations: Section 5 — "Dominant error source ranking: use partial derivative sensitivity analysis — which input contributes most to output uncertainty?" Section 6 — "Model comparison: validation metric + residual analysis — is disagreement random scatter or structured pattern?" A note: "Sections 5 and 6 are where this lecture lives.") Talking Point: Let me connect this lecture to what you are producing for the Phase 1 report, which is due at the end of this week. Section five — uncertainty propagation — requires dominant error source ranking. That ranking comes from the partial derivative sensitivity analysis you now know how to do: compute the sensitivity coefficients, multiply by the input uncertainties, and rank the contributions. The dominant source is the one with the largest product. Section six — model comparison — requires not just a validation metric but also a classification of the residual structure. Is the disagreement between your data and your model random scatter within the uncertainty band, or is it structured — showing a trend, a curvature, a consistent offset? That classification comes from examining the residuals the way we discussed in Week 3 Lecture 2. Sections five and six are where everything in this lecture appears in your deliverable. Use the analysis, not just the vocabulary. ================================================================================ slide 13: The Model Is the First Instrumentation Document (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column header: "Intuitive Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "I'll use the sensor we have in the lab. I'll measure everything I can reach. I'll run as many trials as time allows." A red annotation: "Decisions not connected to model structure — no basis for evaluating if they are sufficient." Right column header: "Model-Driven Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "Angular velocity measurement is the dominant source: spec the tachometer first. Test at high omega where comparison is discriminating. Take enough angular velocity readings to reduce the dominant uncertainty to below 1%." A green annotation: "Decisions derived from partial derivative analysis — directly connected to output uncertainty requirement." A gold banner: "The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you touch hardware.") Talking Point: I want to close with a contrast that I hope makes the central point of this lecture vivid. The left column is how instrumentation decisions get made when they are not connected to the model — you use what's available, measure what's accessible, and run as many trials as time allows. These are not bad choices necessarily, but they have no principled basis. You do not know if they are sufficient. The right column is what model-driven instrumentation design looks like — specific sensor choices with specific precision requirements derived from specific sensitivity coefficients, test conditions selected because the model is most discriminating there, trial count determined by the output uncertainty requirement. The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you write a sensor list. Read it before you design the test matrix. Read it before you finalize your lab setup. If you do, your instrumentation choices become defensible, your comparison becomes meaningful, and your report becomes an engineering argument rather than a data summary. ================================================================================ slide 14: Evaluator Moment — Find the Error in This Ranking (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's Phase 1 report excerpt: "Uncertainty propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total uncertainty. This is because our lab scale has the largest absolute uncertainty of all three inputs at ±0.5 kg. Therefore, improving the scale would most reduce total uncertainty in F_c." A bold prompt below: "Is this ranking correct? If not, what is the error in the reasoning?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause.] The ranking is wrong. The student identified the input with the largest absolute uncertainty but forgot to multiply by the sensitivity coefficient. Dominant error source is determined by the product |∂F_c/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity coefficient is orders of magnitude larger than ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω². Even if the tachometer has a smaller absolute uncertainty than the scale, multiplying by the large sensitivity coefficient may still give a larger contribution to total force uncertainty. The correct procedure is to compute all three products and rank them. The student skipped the sensitivity analysis and went straight to comparing raw uncertainties — which is exactly the error the partial derivative method is designed to prevent. If you are nodding right now, you understand why this lecture exists. ================================================================================ slide 15: Closure The physical modeling branch of the AMVF is not a passive receiver of whatever data a measurement produces — it is an active design space that the mathematical model constrains from the beginning. The partial derivative sensitivity analysis you have practiced since Week 2 is not just a bookkeeping tool for uncertainty propagation — it is a design tool that tells you which sensors matter most, what precision they need, where to run the experiment for maximum information, and what anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure. The Phase 1 report asks you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline — from hypothesis through sensor selection through calibration through comparison — as a single traceable workflow grounded in the model you were given. That traceable workflow is what test engineering looks like. That is what this week asks you to demonstrate. ================================================================================ Agent Notes New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). E1_W4_L1 — From Model to Experiment: Reading the Equations to Design the Test ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 4, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 Revised by V&V Executor — 2026-05-19 (added 3-slide opening sequence: Phase 1 report orientation, calibration uncertainty propagation, predict-from-specs procedure) ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test Talking Point: You have spent three weeks learning to execute the physical modeling branch of the AMVF — how to measure, how to propagate uncertainty, how to argue for agreement. This week I want to change the direction of the question. Instead of asking "given this measurement, what does it tell us?" I want to ask "given this model, what does it tell us about how to design the experiment?" Because the model is not just a thing you compare data to — it is a design document. If you can read a model's equations and extract the sensitivity structure, you will make better instrumentation choices, select more informative test conditions, and write more defensible reports. That is what this lecture is about. But before we get into the design logic, I want to orient you to the Phase 1 report — the deliverable due at the end of this week — because this lecture connects directly to several of its required sections, and one of them you will execute in today's lab session. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Phase 1 Report — A 10-Section Integrated Argument (Image: A structured numbered list of all 10 required sections of the Phase 1 report, displayed in two columns. Left column (sections 1–5): (1) Experimental Design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors; (2) AMVF Annotation — which pipeline steps you executed and what each established; (3) Measurement Record — calibrated values with documented uncertainty; (4) Multi-Sensor Comparison — do independent measurement approaches agree within mutual uncertainty bounds?; (5) Uncertainty Propagation — partial derivative analysis, dominant error source ranking. Right column (sections 6–10): (6) Model Comparison — validation metric and residual structure classification; (7) Experimentally Informed Model — how would you update the model given the comparison?; (8) MATLAB Figures — required plots; (9) Plain-Language Summary — the result stated for a non-engineer; (10) Predict-from-Specs Exercise — uncertainty prediction from an alternative sensor spec sheet, executed in today's lab session. Sections 5, 6, and 10 are highlighted in gold. A gold banner: "These 10 sections are not a checklist — they are a single argument. Each section depends on the ones before it.") Talking Point: The Phase 1 report has 10 required sections. I want you to see them as a single integrated argument, not a to-do list, because each section depends on the analysis in the sections before it. Section 1 is your experimental design — what you measured, why, and with what sensors. Section 2 annotates your workflow against the AMVF — where each step sits in the verification and validation pipeline. Section 3 is your measurement record — calibrated data values with their documented uncertainties. Section 4 is your multi-sensor comparison — do your independent measurement approaches agree within their mutual uncertainty bounds? Section 5 is uncertainty propagation — partial derivative sensitivity analysis leading to a ranked list of error sources. Section 6 is model comparison — does the data agree with the prediction, and what does the residual structure tell you? Section 7 is the experimentally informed model — given the comparison, how would you update the model? Section 8 is your MATLAB figures. Section 9 is your plain-language summary — the same result, stated for a non-engineer. And Section 10 is a predict-from-specs sub-exercise, which you will execute today during the lab session using an alternative sensor spec sheet provided by the instructor. Sections 1 through 9 form a single analytical thread from setup to conclusion. Section 10 is a separate procedure that previews Phase 2 — I am going to explain it explicitly in a moment. ================================================================================ slide 3: Calibration Uncertainty Propagation — A Worked Step (Image: A four-step workflow diagram labeled "How Calibration Uncertainty Enters Your Total Error Budget." Step 2: using that spec sheet, propagate the stated sensor accuracies through the partial derivative framework from Section 5 — but now with the alternative sensor's numbers substituted in. The output is a predicted uncertainty bound on the primary derived quantity if that sensor had been used instead. Step 3: sketch that prediction — a plot of the derived quantity versus the independent variable with labeled axes and an uncertainty band shown as a shaded region or error bars. I want the sketch to include labeled axes with units, the uncertainty bound quantified numerically, and a clear indication that this is a prediction, not a measurement. No data. No hardware. Just the model and the specs. This procedure is Phase 2 in miniature — you are predicting, from specifications alone, what a different sensor configuration would produce. That skill is the central task of the Phase 2 prediction memo. Today is your first practice run at it. ================================================================================ slide 5: The Model as a Design Document (Image: A simple schematic showing a mathematical model equation on the left — for example, a damped oscillation equation "x(t) = A·e^(-ζωₙt)·cos(ωd·t + φ)" — with labeled parameters: A (initial amplitude), ζ (damping ratio), ωₙ (natural frequency), ωd (damped frequency). On the right, a test article diagram showing a spring-mass-damper on a shaker platform with accelerometers attached. Bidirectional arrows labeled with three questions: "What must I measure?" pointing from equation to hardware; "How precisely?" pointing from equation to hardware; "Under what conditions?" pointing from equation to hardware. A gold banner across the bottom: "Every term in the model is an experimental design requirement — if you know how to read it.") Talking Point: The connection I want you to see is this: every term in a physical model is a measurement requirement in disguise. Look at the damped oscillation equation. It has four parameters — amplitude, damping ratio, natural frequency, and phase. Each of those parameters needs to be either measured, inferred, or held constant in the experiment. The natural frequency can be estimated from the mass and stiffness — but both mass and stiffness must be measured, and if either has uncertainty, that uncertainty appears in the natural frequency estimate. The damping ratio requires the decay envelope to be resolved — which means your time resolution must be fine enough to track the amplitude decrease over several cycles. The model tells you all of this before you touch hardware — if you know how to read it. The model is the first instrumentation document. Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — whether it is centripetal force, pendulum period, or electrical power — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor. ================================================================================ slide 6: The P=IV Lesson Generalized (Image: A two-panel figure. Left panel shows the P=IV power equation example from Week 2 with the sensitivity breakdown: "P = I²R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error because current appears squared." Annotation: "You learned this pattern in Week 2." Right panel generalizes: a generic derived quantity equation Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) with a table showing three columns: "Input," "Partial Derivative ∂Q/∂xᵢ," and "Sensitivity Magnitude |∂Q/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ|." The table has three filled rows with placeholder values showing one input dominating the sensitivity. A gold arrow connects the two panels with the label "Same logic — any model, any derived quantity." Caption: "The power equation was the training problem. Now you apply the same logic to your actual experiment.") Talking Point: You learned this pattern in Week 2 with the power equations. P equals I-squared times R is more sensitive to current error than to resistance error, because current appears squared and the partial derivative amplifies its contribution. That was not a special property of the power equation — it is a property of any derived quantity that depends on measured inputs. For any model Q equals f of x₁, x₂, and so on, the partial derivatives are the sensitivity coefficients, and the product of a large sensitivity coefficient and a large input uncertainty is the signature of a dominant error source. The power equation was the training example. Your Tier 1 canned experiment is the real problem. Right now, you should be taking the governing equations from your experiment — whatever physical model connects your inputs to your derived output — and computing the partial derivatives to see which measured quantity dominates the uncertainty budget. That calculation should have been in your Phase 1 report. If it was not, it will be. ================================================================================ slide 7: Worked Example — An Unbalanced Wheel (Tier 1 Pool) (Image: A diagram of a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass attached at radius r from the center. The disk rotates at angular velocity ω, and the centripetal force on the eccentric mass is labeled F_c = m·r·ω². Below the diagram, a partial derivative sensitivity analysis table. Column 1: "Parameter." Column 2: "Partial Derivative of F_c." Column 3: "Value (for m = 0.05 kg, r = 0.08 m, ω = 120 rad/s, typical sensor spec uncertainties)." Rows: m — ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²; r — ∂F_c/∂r = m·ω²; ω — ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. The sensitivity table shows that angular velocity dominates — the factor of 2m·r·ω, evaluated at 120 rad/s, is much larger than the others. A gold box: "Dominant uncertainty source: angular velocity measurement." This example is from the Tier 1 pool and is distinct from any experiment a student in the room is running.) Talking Point: Let me use an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that is not your assigned experiment — to walk through this analysis concretely. Consider a rotating disk with a small eccentric mass at radius r. The centripetal force is F_c equals m times r times omega squared. Taking partial derivatives: the sensitivity to mass is r times omega squared, to radius is m times omega squared, and to angular velocity is 2 times m times r times omega. At typical lab conditions — 120 radians per second, for example — the angular velocity term is much larger than the others. And there is an additional amplification: angular velocity appears squared in the model, so a 2% uncertainty in omega contributes 4% to the uncertainty in force. This immediately tells you three things: first, angular velocity measurement is the highest-priority sensor selection. Second, if your tachometer has poor precision, improve it before improving the mass or ruler. Third, the most informative test conditions are those where omega variation is detectable and measurable, not just those where force is conveniently large. ================================================================================ slide 8: Four Questions the Model Answers (Image: A clean four-question framework formatted as a 2x2 grid of boxes. Box 1 (top-left): "Which input dominates the uncertainty budget?" — icon of a ranked bar chart. Box 2 (top-right): "What sensor spec do I need?" — icon of a sensor with a tolerance specification label. Box 3 (bottom-left): "What test conditions are most informative?" — icon of a plot with a region highlighted. Box 4 (bottom-right): "What anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong?" — icon of a plot with a systematic deviation from the expected curve. A gold border surrounds all four boxes with the label: "Read these from the partial derivative structure — before you touch hardware.") Talking Point: Every physical model, when you apply partial derivative sensitivity analysis, answers four questions that directly inform your experimental design. First: which input dominates the uncertainty budget? The answer tells you which sensor needs the tightest spec. Second: what sensor precision do I need? The answer comes from working backward from the acceptable output uncertainty — what maximum input uncertainty is tolerable given the sensitivity coefficients? Third: what test conditions are most informative? Models are often most sensitive to the parameter of interest — the one you are trying to estimate — at specific values of the independent variable. Testing there maximizes the information you extract. Fourth: what anomalous behavior would tell me the model is wrong? If the model assumes linearity, the anomalous signature is curvature or hysteresis. If the model ignores a coupling term, the anomalous signature is a dependence on a variable you held constant. Commit to all four answers before you run the experiment. ================================================================================ slide 9: Working Backward — What Precision Do I Need? (Image: A worked example diagram. Top: "Acceptable output uncertainty goal: δF_c < 5% at 95% CI." Below: the F_c = m·r·ω² equation with partial derivative terms shown. A table computes: "Given δm from a lab scale (±0.1 g), δr from a caliper (±0.5 mm), what δω is acceptable?" Using the quadrature sum requirement and the dominant sensitivity coefficient for ω, the calculation shows that δω must be less than approximately 0.8 rad/s to meet the 5% goal. Below the table: "This means your tachometer or speed controller must have precision better than 0.8 rad/s at 120 rad/s — about 0.67% of full scale." A gold annotation: "Working backward from an output requirement to an input spec is called an accuracy requirement — it is the sensor selection criterion.") Talking Point: Here is the reverse problem, and it is the one that shows up constantly in actual test design. Suppose I have a requirement: the uncertainty in my centripetal force estimate must be less than 5% at 95% confidence. I can compute, from the partial derivative structure, what that requirement implies for each of my input sensors. If my mass scale and calipers meet their specs, then the entire remaining uncertainty budget flows through the angular velocity term. Inverting the propagation equation tells me the maximum allowable angular velocity uncertainty — say, 0.8 radians per second at 120 radians per second — which translates to a tachometer precision requirement of less than 0.67% of full scale. Now I can go to an instrument catalog with a specific requirement, not just a vague desire for a "good" tachometer. This is what sensor selection looks like when it is grounded in the model rather than in habit or convenience. ================================================================================ slide 10: What Test Conditions Are Most Informative? (Image: A sensitivity versus independent variable plot. X-axis: "Angular Velocity ω (rad/s)" ranging from 0 to 200. Y-axis: "Sensitivity of F_c to mass uncertainty ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω²." The curve rises steeply with ω, labeled "Sensitivity increases as ω²." Three vertical dashed lines mark: "Low ω region — small sensitivity, hard to distinguish F_c from noise." "Mid ω region — moderate sensitivity, informative but not extreme." "High ω region — large sensitivity, best signal-to-noise for model comparison, but hardware constraints apply." A shaded green band marks the "most informative region" around mid-to-high ω. A note: "The model tells you where the comparison is most discriminating — test there.") Talking Point: The model also tells you where to run the experiment for maximum information. In the centripetal force example, the force depends on omega squared — which means the sensitivity of the comparison between model and data is much higher at large omega. At low rotation rates, the forces are small and hard to measure above the noise floor. At high rotation rates, the forces are large and the comparison between model and data is highly discriminating — small disagreements are detectable. Of course, hardware constraints — bearing loads, motor speed limits, safety margins — set the upper bound. But within those constraints, the model tells you that testing at moderate-to-high omega gives you the most informative comparison. This is not just intuition — it comes directly from the partial derivative structure. Testing at conditions where the model is most sensitive to the quantity of interest is how you design a meaningful experiment rather than one that could agree or disagree for no informative reason. ================================================================================ slide 11: What Anomalous Behavior Would Tell Me the Model Is Wrong? (Image: Three side-by-side plots, each showing the centripetal force model prediction (dashed line, F_c = m·r·ω²) against possible data patterns. Plot A: "Expected — scatter around the model curve within the uncertainty band." Dots scatter around the curve within gray bounds. Label: "Consistent with measurement noise — model is adequate." Plot B: "Anomalous — nonlinear deviation at high ω." Data follows the model at low ω but falls below it at high ω. Label: "Possible aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass — not in the model. Model is missing a velocity-dependent force." Plot C: "Anomalous — constant offset across all ω." Data runs parallel to the model but consistently below. Label: "Possible systematic bias in mass or radius measurement — calibration issue, not model-form error." Caption: "Anomalous signatures are predictable from model assumptions — commit to them before the experiment.") Talking Point: From the model, you can predict not just what the expected data looks like, but what specific anomalous patterns would tell you the model is wrong. For the centripetal force model, if the data shows scatter within the uncertainty band, the model is adequate for this experiment. But if the data shows a systematic deviation that grows with omega — falling below the model at high rotation rates — that is a signature of an additional velocity-dependent force, like aerodynamic drag on the eccentric mass, that the simple F equals m-r-omega-squared model does not include. And if the data shows a constant offset across all omega values, that is not a model-form error — it is a systematic measurement bias in mass or radius, which is a calibration issue, not a physics issue. These three patterns are distinguishable, and you can predict which ones are physically plausible before running a single trial. That predictive commitment is what I want to see in the Phase 1 report's model comparison section. ================================================================================ slide 12: The Phase 1 Report — Connecting This Lecture to the Deliverable (Image: A structured outline of the Phase 1 report's required sections, with specific sections highlighted. The full list: (1) Experimental design, (2) AMVF annotation, (3) Measurement record, (4) Multi-sensor comparison, (5) Uncertainty propagation, (6) Model comparison, (7) Experimentally informed model, (8) MATLAB figures, (9) Plain-language summary, (10) Predict-from-specs exercise. Sections 5 and 6 are highlighted in gold with annotations: Section 5 — "Dominant error source ranking: use partial derivative sensitivity analysis — which input contributes most to output uncertainty?" Section 6 — "Model comparison: validation metric + residual analysis — is disagreement random scatter or structured pattern?" A note: "Sections 5 and 6 are where this lecture lives.") Talking Point: Let me connect this lecture to what you are producing for the Phase 1 report, which is due at the end of this week. Section five — uncertainty propagation — requires dominant error source ranking. That ranking comes from the partial derivative sensitivity analysis you now know how to do: compute the sensitivity coefficients, multiply by the input uncertainties, and rank the contributions. The dominant source is the one with the largest product. Section six — model comparison — requires not just a validation metric but also a classification of the residual structure. Is the disagreement between your data and your model random scatter within the uncertainty band, or is it structured — showing a trend, a curvature, a consistent offset? That classification comes from examining the residuals the way we discussed in Week 3 Lecture 2. Sections five and six are where everything in this lecture appears in your deliverable. Use the analysis, not just the vocabulary. ================================================================================ slide 13: The Model Is the First Instrumentation Document (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column header: "Intuitive Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "I'll use the sensor we have in the lab. I'll measure everything I can reach. I'll run as many trials as time allows." A red annotation: "Decisions not connected to model structure — no basis for evaluating if they are sufficient." Right column header: "Model-Driven Instrumentation Choice" — a list: "Angular velocity measurement is the dominant source: spec the tachometer first. Test at high omega where comparison is discriminating. Take enough angular velocity readings to reduce the dominant uncertainty to below 1%." A green annotation: "Decisions derived from partial derivative analysis — directly connected to output uncertainty requirement." A gold banner: "The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you touch hardware.") Talking Point: I want to close with a contrast that I hope makes the central point of this lecture vivid. The left column is how instrumentation decisions get made when they are not connected to the model — you use what's available, measure what's accessible, and run as many trials as time allows. These are not bad choices necessarily, but they have no principled basis. You do not know if they are sufficient. The right column is what model-driven instrumentation design looks like — specific sensor choices with specific precision requirements derived from specific sensitivity coefficients, test conditions selected because the model is most discriminating there, trial count determined by the output uncertainty requirement. The model is the first instrumentation document. Read it before you write a sensor list. Read it before you design the test matrix. Read it before you finalize your lab setup. If you do, your instrumentation choices become defensible, your comparison becomes meaningful, and your report becomes an engineering argument rather than a data summary. ================================================================================ slide 14: Evaluator Moment — Find the Error in This Ranking (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's Phase 1 report excerpt: "Uncertainty propagation analysis: The dominant error source in the centripetal force measurement is mass, contributing 68% of total uncertainty. This is because our lab scale has the largest absolute uncertainty of all three inputs at ±0.5 kg. Therefore, improving the scale would most reduce total uncertainty in F_c." A bold prompt below: "Is this ranking correct? If not, what is the error in the reasoning?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause.] The ranking is wrong. The student identified the input with the largest absolute uncertainty but forgot to multiply by the sensitivity coefficient. Dominant error source is determined by the product |∂F_c/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ| — not by δxᵢ alone. The sensitivity to angular velocity is ∂F_c/∂ω = 2m·r·ω. At ω = 100 rad/s, that sensitivity coefficient is orders of magnitude larger than ∂F_c/∂m = r·ω². Even if the tachometer has a smaller absolute uncertainty than the scale, multiplying by the large sensitivity coefficient may still give a larger contribution to total force uncertainty. The correct procedure is to compute all three products and rank them. The student skipped the sensitivity analysis and went straight to comparing raw uncertainties — which is exactly the error the partial derivative method is designed to prevent. If you are nodding right now, you understand why this lecture exists. ================================================================================ slide 15: Closure The physical modeling branch of the AMVF is not a passive receiver of whatever data a measurement produces — it is an active design space that the mathematical model constrains from the beginning. The partial derivative sensitivity analysis you have practiced since Week 2 is not just a bookkeeping tool for uncertainty propagation — it is a design tool that tells you which sensors matter most, what precision they need, where to run the experiment for maximum information, and what anomalous patterns would indicate model-form failure. The Phase 1 report asks you to demonstrate this analysis explicitly. By the time you complete it, you will have walked the full Phase I pipeline — from hypothesis through sensor selection through calibration through comparison — as a single traceable workflow grounded in the model you were given. That traceable workflow is what test engineering looks like. That is what this week asks you to demonstrate. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W4 Lecture 1: "From Model to Experiment — Reading the Equations to Design the Test" (CLO 1, 2, 3). - Combines the blueprint's two options: dominant error source ranking (Option A) and model-to-experiment reading (Option B). Opens with partial derivative sensitivity as the bridge between model structure and measurement design. - Worked example uses centripetal force for an unbalanced wheel — an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that is distinct from what students in the room would be running (per blueprint instruction). Not a pendulum. Not P=IV (those are already used). - Four-question framework (slide 8) captures the blueprint's stated scope: which quantity dominates, sensor selection implications, most informative test conditions, anomalous behavior signature. - Backward propagation (sensor spec from output requirement) is in slide 9 — a practical skill that supports the Phase 1 report's sensor selection rationale section. - Phase 1 report connection made explicit in slides 2 and 12: slide 2 orients students to all 10 sections as an integrated argument; slide 12 drills into sections 5 and 6 specifically. - V&V Executor revision (2026-05-19): Added slides 2, 3, 4 as an opening sequence per approved action item [HIGH] W4 L1 — Three critical content gaps. Slides 2–11 (original) renumbered to 5–14. New slides cover: (a) Phase 1 report as 10-section integrated argument; (b) calibration uncertainty propagation 4-step procedure using centripetal force example; (c) predict-from-specs workflow as a labeled 3-step procedure for Section 10. - No E2 or E3 concepts introduced. W3 L2 calibration vs. validation distinction referenced but not re-taught. - Slide count: 15 (title + 12 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 14 (Evaluator Moment — wrong partial derivative ranking). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 5 talking point — added one-sentence bridge to student's own experiment: "Whatever your Tier 1 experiment's governing equation — use these same three questions to read it as a design document before you set up a single sensor."
