pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Lecture L01 E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ===============================... E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be direct about what a prediction actually is, why it matters that you commit to it before seeing any data, and what distinguishes a prediction that a test engineer would respect from a guess dressed in engineering vocabulary. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the subject of this lecture. By the end, you will know exactly what the Phase 2 memo requires — and more importantly, you will understand why each element exists. ================================================================================ slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters (Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The output will probably go up. It should be around 30 watts or so. I think it will match the model pretty well." Red annotations: "No committed value," "No physical reasoning," "No mechanism," "Cannot be evaluated after the fact." Right panel header: "Prediction" — icon of a test engineer at a whiteboard. Text: "Based on F_c = m·r·ω², I predict the centripetal force will reach 28.4 ± 3.2 N at ω = 100 rad/s, with current uncertainty dominant at 11.3% of output. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption: "The distinguishing feature is falsifiability: can the prediction be clearly contradicted by the data?") Talking Point: A prediction and a guess are not different by degree — they are different by structure. A guess is directional and vague: "it will probably go up," "it should be around 30," "it will match pretty well." None of those statements can be clearly falsified by a measurement. If the value is 38 instead of 30, a person who said "around 30" can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so that a measurement can clearly confirm it, approximately confirm it, or clearly contradict it. The ability for the prediction to be contradicted is not a weakness — it is the entire point. A prediction that cannot be contradicted does not say anything about the physics. If you learn nothing when the prediction is right, you also learn nothing when it is wrong. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Six Elements of a Defensible Prediction (Image: A vertical numbered list with six labeled items, each in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it increases' but 'it reaches 28.4 N at ω = 100 rad/s.'" Card 2 (green): "Propagated Uncertainty — derived from sensor specs using partial derivatives or Monte Carlo. Stated as estimate ± U at confidence level." Card 3 (gold): "Committed Sketch — a hand-drawn or MATLAB plot of the expected output with labeled axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before data is seen." Card 4 (purple): "Ranked Uncertainty Sources — which input contributes most? In what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Card 6 (orange): "Risk Assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern identified with physical basis. What would 'wrong' look like, and what physical effect would cause it?" Below all six: "All six are required in the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Missing any one makes the prediction incomplete.") Talking Point: The Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires all six of these elements — and I want to walk through why each one exists. System response features commit you to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you know from Week 2. The committed sketch forces you to visualize what the data should look like before you see it — axes, units, scale, and uncertainty bounds drawn by hand or in MATLAB. The ranked uncertainty sources tell me what you understand about the dominant error in this measurement. The named acceptance criterion commits you to the standard you will use for evaluation — before you see whether your prediction was close or not. And element six: risk assessment. Name at least one specific anomalous data pattern that would indicate the model is wrong — not just "the data might be noisy," but a specific physically-grounded signature: "if the output shows amplitude-dependent behavior, the linear model is missing a nonlinear term." The risk assessment is what connects your prediction to the anomalous data thinking from Week 3. Six elements. All six are required. ================================================================================ slide 4: Element 1 — System Response Features (Image: A comparison of two prediction statements for the same experiment. Statement A: "The measured temperature will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT with h = 35 W/m²K from the correlation for natural convection from a horizontal surface." Green annotations: "Specific value with units," "Physical mechanism (energy balance) stated," "Parameter source identified (convection correlation)," "Falsifiable: a reading of 110°C would clearly contradict this." Caption: "A system response feature is a committed, specific, mechanism-grounded prediction — not a directional tendency.") Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach 87 plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius at 25 Watts of heater power, based on the steady-state energy balance with a natural convection coefficient derived from the correlation for a horizontal surface" — that is a system response feature. It commits to a value. It names the physical mechanism behind the prediction. It gives me a basis for evaluating whether the prediction was right or wrong, and more importantly, for understanding what a discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable." ================================================================================ slide 5: Element 2 — Propagated Uncertainty from Sensor Specs (Image: A three-step workflow diagram. Step 1: "Read the spec sheet — extract sensor accuracy, resolution, and measurement range." A sample spec card shows: "Thermocouple accuracy: ±0.5°C. Data acquisition resolution: ±0.2°C. Combined standard uncertainty: √(0.5² + 0.2²) = 0.54°C." Step 2: "Apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source: uncertainty in convection coefficient h, contributing 78% of total variance)." A gold callout: "This is predict-from-specs — estimating uncertainty before you have data, using only the sensor spec sheet.") Talking Point: The uncertainty estimate in your prediction is not a guess — it is a calculation from the sensor spec. This is the predict-from-specs workflow you practiced at the end of the Week 4 lab: given a spec sheet, apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation, and report the predicted output uncertainty. You are doing exactly the same thing for the Phase 2 memo, but now you have not run the experiment yourself — you are working from the spec sheet included in the experiment packet. The calculation is the same: take the sensor accuracies and resolutions from the spec, propagate them through the governing equation, rank the contributions, and report the dominant source. The fact that you have not seen the physical hardware does not change the calculation. If you can read a spec sheet and apply the propagation formula, you can estimate the uncertainty before the experiment runs. ================================================================================ slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch (Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a time-series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 60. Y-axis labeled "Temperature (°C)" from 20 to 100. A smooth S-shaped curve rises from ambient temperature to an asymptote around 87°C, labeled "Expected steady-state: 87°C." Two dashed lines parallel to the curve form an uncertainty band labeled "±4°C (propagated from sensor spec)." A horizontal target line at 87°C is labeled "Predicted steady-state." Hand-drawn annotations in a different style: "First 10s: rapid rise." "30-40s: rate slows." "After 50s: near steady-state." "Scatter within this band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and bounds committed in advance.") Talking Point: The committed sketch is not a formality. It is the most direct test of whether your prediction is concrete. If you cannot draw the expected output — with labeled axes, units, approximate scale, and an uncertainty band — you do not yet have a prediction. You have a direction. The sketch forces you to make a sequence of commitments: what quantity is on each axis, at what scale, over what range. The shape of the curve commits you to the physical mechanism — linear growth, exponential approach, oscillation. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your sketch. If it falls within the band, you have learned something positive. If it falls outside, you have a discrepancy to explain. But none of that analysis is possible if you did not draw the sketch before you saw the data. Draw it first. ================================================================================ slide 7: Elements 4 and 5 — Ranked Sources and Acceptance Criterion (Image: A two-panel slide. Left panel: "Element 4 — Ranked Uncertainty Sources." A horizontal bar chart showing three inputs, with bars proportional to their contribution to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C (DAQ resolution) — 8%. Annotations: "Input A dominates — improving the convection correlation would most reduce uncertainty." "Inputs B and C are secondary — better sensors would help marginally." Right panel: "Element 5 — Acceptance Criterion." A text box: "Pre-committed criterion: the prediction is considered successful if the measured steady-state temperature falls within the propagated uncertainty interval of 87 ± 4°C. If the measured value falls outside this band, the discrepancy exceeds measurement noise and requires a physical explanation." A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.") Talking Point: Element four — ranked uncertainty sources — is the output of the sensitivity analysis from this week's lecture. Once you have propagated uncertainty through the governing equation, you know which input contributes the most. State it explicitly, with estimated magnitudes. This is not just bookkeeping — it tells me whether you understand the dominant error in the measurement and whether your prediction accounts for the right sources of uncertainty. Element five — the named acceptance criterion — is the pre-commitment that makes the comparison defensible. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction holds; if it falls outside, the discrepancy requires a physical explanation. Pre-committing to the criterion means you cannot move the goalposts after seeing the result. That is not a bureaucratic rule — it is the logical requirement that makes validation meaningful. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Grading Philosophy — Right Prediction, Wrong Reasoning Scores Lower (Image: A 2x2 matrix with "Prediction Correctness" on one axis (Right / Wrong) and "Quality of Reasoning" on the other axis (Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal outcome." Top-right "Right prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Suspicious — you may have gotten lucky. Scores lower than bottom-left." Bottom-left "Wrong prediction, sound reasoning" — green label "Good — the analysis was correct; the discrepancy teaches you something." Bottom-right "Wrong prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Poor — both the analysis and the outcome fail." A caption: "A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right from vague intuition.") Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not the grading standard you may be used to. A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and a complete, committed analysis scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition with no mechanism, no calculation, and no committed uncertainty. The goal of Phase 2 is not to produce a correct prediction. The goal is to demonstrate that you understood the physics of the experiment before you saw the data — well enough to build a specific, mechanistic, falsifiable prediction. If your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons, you have learned something real. If your prediction was right for the wrong reasons — if you got lucky — you have learned nothing and the grading reflects that. This philosophy is not idiosyncratic — it is the professional norm for test engineers, and you should hear it clearly before Phase 2 begins. ================================================================================ slide 9: Worked Example — A Complete Prediction for an Unfamiliar Experiment (Image: A structured layout walking through a Phase 2 memo structure for a simple heat conduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with temperature sensors at two points along the rod. Headers and one-line summaries for each section: "System Response Features: steady-state temperature at sensor 2 predicted to be 54 ± 5°C based on one-dimensional heat conduction equation Q = k·A·ΔT/L." "Uncertainty: dominant source is thermal conductivity k — literature value uncertainty contributes 68% of total output variance." "Sketch: temperature profile along rod shown as linear gradient with ±5°C band." "Acceptance Criterion: prediction is successful if measured temperature at sensor 2 is within 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod surface fails — radiation or convection losses not included." The layout is clean and reads as a professional memo excerpt.) Talking Point: Let me walk you through what a complete Phase 2 prediction looks like, using a heat conduction experiment that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I read the theory primer, identify the governing equation — one-dimensional Fourier conduction — and extract the system response feature: steady-state temperature at the second sensor, predicted to be 54 degrees Celsius. I propagate uncertainty using the conductivity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the expected linear temperature gradient along the rod with the uncertainty band. I commit to an acceptance criterion: within 54 plus or minus 5 degrees. And in the risk assessment — element six, which is required for Phase 2 — I identify the anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure: if the measured temperature profile is nonlinear along the rod, the model's assumption of no lateral heat loss is wrong, and the one-dimensional model is inadequate. That is a complete prediction. ================================================================================ slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session (Image: A structured timeline for today's lab session. Step 1 (30 min): "Receive your Phase 2 experiment packet — theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, original hypothesis from the student who ran Phase 1." Step 2 (45 min): "Extract system response features, propagate uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch." Step 3 (15 min): "Write the acceptance criterion and rank the uncertainty sources." Step 4: "Submit the Prediction Memo before data is released — this is the gate." A red annotation at Step 4: "Data will not be released until the memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received Experiment B's packet; the student who ran Experiment B received Experiment A's packet. You will present both in the Phase 3 lightning talk.") Talking Point: In today's lab session, you will receive the experiment packet for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that you did not run as your Phase 1. The packet includes the theory primer, sensor specifications, DAQ configuration, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran that experiment. You will not receive the data until after you submit the Prediction Memo. That submission is the gate — data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all six elements of the prediction: extract system response features from the theory primer, propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch, rank the uncertainty sources, write the acceptance criterion, and write the risk assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern with physical basis that would indicate model-form failure. Submit before data is released. That commitment is what makes the subsequent comparison honest. The Phase 3 lightning talk — where you present your cross-prediction results — also includes a graded peer evaluation. A strong peer evaluation names specific evidence and explains the reasoning: not "the uncertainty analysis was incomplete" but "the uncertainty in the derived quantity was not propagated from the sensor spec, which is required by the rubric." The Week 3 reading on comparative analysis gives you the four-part structure; apply it to a live talk the same way you apply it to a written report. ================================================================================ slide 11: Closure Pre-commitment is not a bureaucratic requirement. It is the logical structure that makes experimental learning honest. If you construct your interpretation of the data after seeing it, you will inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic, falsifiable, with an uncertainty estimate and an acceptance criterion — is your promise to yourself and to the reader that your interpretation of the data was not reverse-engineered from the outcome. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the six components of that promise. In the Phase 2 memo you are writing today, every one of them is required. And the grading philosophy is explicit: sound reasoning in the wrong direction is more valuable than vague intuition in the right one. That is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now. ================================================================================ Agent Notes New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W5 Lecture 1: "What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look" (CLO 1, 2, 3). All six elements of the Phase 2 Prediction Memo are explicitly walked through in slides 3–7, per blueprint: system response features (committed values), uncertainty from spec propagation, committed sketch, ranked uncertainty sources, named acceptance criterion, risk assessment. Slide 3 updated from "Five Elements" to "Six Elements" by V&V Executor 2026-05-19 to resolve mismatch with Phase 2 rubric which requires six sections. The grading philosophy — wrong prediction from sound reasoning scores higher than right prediction from vague intuition — is communicated explicitly in slide 8, per the blueprint instruction that this philosophy "must be communicated explicitly in the rubric and in class before Phase 2 begins." Worked example (slide 9) uses a heat conduction experiment, distinct from the centripetal force example in W4 L1 and not a pendulum. Slide 10 describes the lab session structure, including the "memo as gate" requirement. Risk assessment element (anomalous signature) mentioned in slides 9 and 10 — bridges to W3 L2's anomalous data signature content. Slide count: 11 (title + 9 content + closure). Within spec (10-12). V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 10): Slide 10 talking point — added two sentences orienting students to the Phase 3 peer evaluation rubric: name specific evidence + explained reasoning vs. vague critique. Forward pointer to W3 R2 (comparative analysis reading) as the four-part framework to apply to live talks. A smooth S-shaped curve rises from ambient temperature to an asymptote around 87°C, labeled "Expected steady-state: 87°C." Two dashed lines parallel to the curve form an uncertainty band labeled "±4°C (propagated from sensor spec)." A horizontal target line at 87°C is labeled "Predicted steady-state." Hand-drawn annotations in a different style: "First 10s: rapid rise." "30-40s: rate slows." "After 50s: near steady-state." "Scatter within this band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and bounds committed in advance.") Talking Point: The committed sketch is not a formality. It is the most direct test of whether your prediction is concrete. If you cannot draw the expected output — with labeled axes, units, approximate scale, and an uncertainty band — you do not yet have a prediction. You have a direction. The sketch forces you to make a sequence of commitments: what quantity is on each axis, at what scale, over what range. The shape of the curve commits you to the physical mechanism — linear growth, exponential approach, oscillation. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your sketch. If it falls within the band, you have learned something positive. If it falls outside, you have a discrepancy to explain. But none of that analysis is possible if you did not draw the sketch before you saw the data. Draw it first. ================================================================================ slide 7: Elements 4 and 5 — Ranked Sources and Acceptance Criterion (Image: A two-panel slide. Left panel: "Element 4 — Ranked Uncertainty Sources." A horizontal bar chart showing three inputs, with bars proportional to their contribution to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C (DAQ resolution) — 8%. Annotations: "Input A dominates — improving the convection correlation would most reduce uncertainty." "Inputs B and C are secondary — better sensors would help marginally." Right panel: "Element 5 — Acceptance Criterion." A text box: "Pre-committed criterion: the prediction is considered successful if the measured steady-state temperature falls within the propagated uncertainty interval of 87 ± 4°C. If the measured value falls outside this band, the discrepancy exceeds measurement noise and requires a physical explanation." A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.") Talking Point: Element four — ranked uncertainty sources — is the output of the sensitivity analysis from this week's lecture. Once you have propagated uncertainty through the governing equation, you know which input contributes the most. State it explicitly, with estimated magnitudes. This is not just bookkeeping — it tells me whether you understand the dominant error in the measurement and whether your prediction accounts for the right sources of uncertainty. Element five — the named acceptance criterion — is the pre-commitment that makes the comparison defensible. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction holds; if it falls outside, the discrepancy requires a physical explanation. Pre-committing to the criterion means you cannot move the goalposts after seeing the result. That is not a bureaucratic rule — it is the logical requirement that makes validation meaningful. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Grading Philosophy — Right Prediction, Wrong Reasoning Scores Lower (Image: A 2x2 matrix with "Prediction Correctness" on one axis (Right / Wrong) and "Quality of Reasoning" on the other axis (Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal outcome." Top-right "Right prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Suspicious — you may have gotten lucky. Scores lower than bottom-left." Bottom-left "Wrong prediction, sound reasoning" — green label "Good — the analysis was correct; the discrepancy teaches you something." Bottom-right "Wrong prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Poor — both the analysis and the outcome fail." A caption: "A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right from vague intuition.") Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not the grading standard you may be used to. A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and a complete, committed analysis scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition with no mechanism, no calculation, and no committed uncertainty. The goal of Phase 2 is not to produce a correct prediction. The goal is to demonstrate that you understood the physics of the experiment before you saw the data — well enough to build a specific, mechanistic, falsifiable prediction. If your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons, you have learned something real. If your prediction was right for the wrong reasons — if you got lucky — you have learned nothing and the grading reflects that. This philosophy is not idiosyncratic — it is the professional norm for test engineers, and you should hear it clearly before Phase 2 begins. ================================================================================ slide 9: Worked Example — A Complete Prediction for an Unfamiliar Experiment (Image: A structured layout walking through a Phase 2 memo structure for a simple heat conduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with temperature sensors at two points along the rod. Headers and one-line summaries for each section: "System Response Features: steady-state temperature at sensor 2 predicted to be 54 ± 5°C based on one-dimensional heat conduction equation Q = k·A·ΔT/L." "Uncertainty: dominant source is thermal conductivity k — literature value uncertainty contributes 68% of total output variance." "Sketch: temperature profile along rod shown as linear gradient with ±5°C band." "Acceptance Criterion: prediction is successful if measured temperature at sensor 2 is within 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod surface fails — radiation or convection losses not included." The layout is clean and reads as a professional memo excerpt.) Talking Point: Let me walk you through what a complete Phase 2 prediction looks like, using a heat conduction experiment that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I read the theory primer, identify the governing equation — one-dimensional Fourier conduction — and extract the system response feature: steady-state temperature at the second sensor, predicted to be 54 degrees Celsius. I propagate uncertainty using the conductivity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the expected linear temperature gradient along the rod with the uncertainty band. I commit to an acceptance criterion: within 54 plus or minus 5 degrees. And in the risk assessment — element six, which is required for Phase 2 — I identify the anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure: if the measured temperature profile is nonlinear along the rod, the model's assumption of no lateral heat loss is wrong, and the one-dimensional model is inadequate. That is a complete prediction. ================================================================================ slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session (Image: A structured timeline for today's lab session. Step 1 (30 min): "Receive your Phase 2 experiment packet — theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, original hypothesis from the student who ran Phase 1." Step 2 (45 min): "Extract system response features, propagate uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch." Step 3 (15 min): "Write the acceptance criterion and rank the uncertainty sources." Step 4: "Submit the Prediction Memo before data is released — this is the gate." A red annotation at Step 4: "Data will not be released until the memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received Experiment B's packet; the student who ran Experiment B received Experiment A's packet. You will present both in the Phase 3 lightning talk.") Talking Point: In today's lab session, you will receive the experiment packet for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that you did not run as your Phase 1. The packet includes the theory primer, sensor specifications, DAQ configuration, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran that experiment. You will not receive the data until after you submit the Prediction Memo. That submission is the gate — data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all six elements of the prediction: extract system response features from the theory primer, propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch, rank the uncertainty sources, write the acceptance criterion, and write the risk assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern with physical basis that would indicate model-form failure. Submit before data is released. That commitment is what makes the subsequent comparison honest. The Phase 3 lightning talk — where you present your cross-prediction results — also includes a graded peer evaluation. A strong peer evaluation names specific evidence and explains the reasoning: not "the uncertainty analysis was incomplete" but "the uncertainty in the derived quantity was not propagated from the sensor spec, which is required by the rubric." The Week 3 reading on comparative analysis gives you the four-part structure; apply it to a live talk the same way you apply it to a written report. ================================================================================ slide 11: Closure Pre-commitment is not a bureaucratic requirement. It is the logical structure that makes experimental learning honest. If you construct your interpretation of the data after seeing it, you will inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic, falsifiable, with an uncertainty estimate and an acceptance criterion — is your promise to yourself and to the reader that your interpretation of the data was not reverse-engineered from the outcome. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the six components of that promise. In the Phase 2 memo you are writing today, every one of them is required. And the grading philosophy is explicit: sound reasoning in the wrong direction is more valuable than vague intuition in the right one. That is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W5 Lecture 1: "What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look" (CLO 1, 2, 3). - All six elements of the Phase 2 Prediction Memo are explicitly walked through in slides 3–7, per blueprint: system response features (committed values), uncertainty from spec propagation, committed sketch, ranked uncertainty sources, named acceptance criterion, risk assessment. Slide 3 updated from "Five Elements" to "Six Elements" by V&V Executor 2026-05-19 to resolve mismatch with Phase 2 rubric which requires six sections. - The grading philosophy — wrong prediction from sound reasoning scores higher than right prediction from vague intuition — is communicated explicitly in slide 8, per the blueprint instruction that this philosophy "must be communicated explicitly in the rubric and in class before Phase 2 begins." - Worked example (slide 9) uses a heat conduction experiment, distinct from the centripetal force example in W4 L1 and not a pendulum. - Slide 10 describes the lab session structure, including the "memo as gate" requirement. - Risk assessment element (anomalous signature) mentioned in slides 9 and 10 — bridges to W3 L2's anomalous data signature content. - Slide count: 11 (title + 9 content + closure). Within spec (10-12). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 10): Slide 10 talking point — added two sentences orienting students to the Phase 3 peer evaluation rubric: name specific evidence + explained reasoning vs. vague critique. Forward pointer to W3 R2 (comparative analysis reading) as the four-part framework to apply to live talks.

E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be

hing specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be direct about what a prediction actually is, why it matters that you commit to it before seeing any data, and what distinguishes a prediction that a test engineer would respect from a guess dressed in engineering vocabulary. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the subject of this lecture. By the end, you will know exactly what the Phase 2 memo requires — and more importantly, you will understand why each element exists. ================================================================================ slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters (Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The

rence That Matters (Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The output will probably go up. It should be around 30 watts or so. I think it will match the model pretty well." Red annotations: "No committed value," "No physical reasoning," "No mechanism," "Cannot be evaluated after the fact." Right panel header: "Prediction" — icon of a test engineer at a whiteboard. Text: "Based on F_c = m·r·ω², I predict the centripetal force will reach 28.4 ± 3.2 N at ω = 100 rad/s, with current uncertainty dominant at 11.3% of output. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption:

ed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption: "The distinguishing feature is falsifiability: can the prediction be clearly contradicted by the data?") Talking Point: A prediction and a guess are not different by degree — they are different by structure. A guess is directional and vague: "it will probably go up," "it should be around 30," "it will match pretty well." None of those statements can be clearly falsified by a measurement. If the value is 38 instead of 30, a person who said "around 30" can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so

mmits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so that a measurement can clearly confirm it, approximately confirm it, or clearly contradict it. The ability for the prediction to be contradicted is not a weakness — it is the entire point. A prediction that cannot be contradicted does not say anything about the physics. If you learn nothing when the prediction is right, you also learn nothing when it is wrong. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Six Elements of a Defensible Prediction (Image: A vertical numbered list with six labeled items, each in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it

in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it increases' but 'it reaches 28.4 N at ω = 100 rad/s.'" Card 2 (green): "Propagated Uncertainty — derived from sensor specs using partial derivatives or Monte Carlo. Stated as estimate ± U at confidence level." Card 3 (gold): "Committed Sketch — a hand-drawn or MATLAB plot of the expected output with labeled axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before data is seen." Card 4 (purple): "Ranked Uncertainty Sources — which input contributes most? In what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Card 6 (orange): "Risk

old, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Card 6 (orange): "Risk Assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern identified with physical basis. What would 'wrong' look like, and what physical effect would cause it?" Below all six: "All six are required in the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Missing any one makes the prediction incomplete.") Talking Point: The Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires all six of these elements — and I want to walk through why each one exists. System response features commit you to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you

ects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you know from Week 2. The committed sketch forces you to visualize what the data should look like before you see it — axes, units, scale, and uncertainty bounds drawn by hand or in MATLAB. The ranked uncertainty sources tell me what you understand about the dominant error in this measurement. The named acceptance criterion commits you to the standard you will use for evaluation — before you see whether your prediction was close or not. And element six: risk assessment. Name at least one specific anomalous data pattern that would indicate the model is wrong — not just "the data might be noisy," but a specific physically-grounded signature: "if the output shows

model is wrong — not just "the data might be noisy," but a specific physically-grounded signature: "if the output shows amplitude-dependent behavior, the linear model is missing a nonlinear term." The risk assessment is what connects your prediction to the anomalous data thinking from Week 3. Six elements. All six are required. ================================================================================ slide 4: Element 1 — System Response Features (Image: A comparison of two prediction statements for the same experiment. Statement A: "The measured temperature will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT

t B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT with h = 35 W/m²K from the correlation for natural convection from a horizontal surface." Green annotations: "Specific value with units," "Physical mechanism (energy balance) stated," "Parameter source identified (convection correlation)," "Falsifiable: a reading of 110°C would clearly contradict this." Caption: "A system response feature is a committed, specific, mechanism-grounded prediction — not a directional tendency.") Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach

ve as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach 87 plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius at 25 Watts of heater power, based on the steady-state energy balance with a natural convection coefficient derived from the correlation for a horizontal surface" — that is a system response feature. It commits to a value. It names the physical mechanism behind the prediction. It gives me a basis for evaluating whether the prediction was right or wrong, and more importantly, for understanding what a discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable."

m response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable." ================================================================================ slide 5: Element 2 — Propagated Uncertainty from Sensor Specs (Image: A three-step workflow diagram. Step 1: "Read the spec sheet — extract sensor accuracy, resolution, and measurement range." A sample spec card shows: "Thermocouple accuracy: ±0.5°C. Data acquisition resolution: ±0.2°C. Combined standard uncertainty: √(0.5² + 0.2²) = 0.54°C." Step 2: "Apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source:

rgy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source: uncertainty in convection coefficient h, contributing 78% of total variance)." A gold callout: "This is predict-from-specs — estimating uncertainty before you have data, using only the sensor spec sheet.") Talking Point: The uncertainty estimate in your prediction is not a guess — it is a calculation from the sensor spec. The fact that you have not seen the physical hardware does not change the calculation. If you can read a spec sheet and apply the propagation formula, you can estimate the uncertainty before the experiment runs. ================================================================================ slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch (Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a time-series plot. All six elements of the Phase 2 Prediction Memo are explicitly walked through in slides 3–7, per blueprint: system response features (committed values), uncertainty from spec propagation, committed sketch, ranked uncertainty sources, named acceptance criterion, risk assessment. Slide 3 updated from "Five Elements" to "Six Elements" by V&V Executor 2026-05-19 to resolve mismatch with Phase 2 rubric which requires six sections. E1_W5_L1 — What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 1 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2

Like — Committing Before You Look Talking Point: This lecture exists to prepare you for something specific: the Phase 2 Prediction Memo, which you will begin in today's lab session. Before we do that, I want to be direct about what a prediction actually is, why it matters that you commit to it before seeing any data, and what distinguishes a prediction that a test engineer would respect from a guess dressed in engineering vocabulary. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the subject of this lecture. By the end, you will know exactly what the Phase 2 memo requires — and more importantly, you will understand why each element exists. ================================================================================ slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters (Image:

==================================================== slide 2: Prediction vs. Guess — The Difference That Matters (Image: A split-panel comparison. Left panel header: "Guess" — icon of a shrugging figure. Text: "The output will probably go up. It should be around 30 watts or so. I think it will match the model pretty well." Red annotations: "No committed value," "No physical reasoning," "No mechanism," "Cannot be evaluated after the fact." Right panel header: "Prediction" — icon of a test engineer at a whiteboard. Text: "Based on F_c = m·r·ω², I predict the centripetal force will reach 28.4 ± 3.2 N at ω = 100 rad/s, with current uncertainty dominant at 11.3% of output. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value,"

tput. If the data shows hysteresis, the model is missing a damping term." Green annotations: "Committed specific value," "Physical mechanism stated," "Uncertainty estimated from specs," "Anomalous pattern named." Caption: "The distinguishing feature is falsifiability: can the prediction be clearly contradicted by the data?") Talking Point: A prediction and a guess are not different by degree — they are different by structure. A guess is directional and vague: "it will probably go up," "it should be around 30," "it will match pretty well." None of those statements can be clearly falsified by a measurement. If the value is 38 instead of 30, a person who said "around 30" can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific

can always reinterpret their prior statement. A prediction is falsifiable in the precise sense: it commits to a specific value or range, derived from a specific physical mechanism, with a specific uncertainty estimate, so that a measurement can clearly confirm it, approximately confirm it, or clearly contradict it. The ability for the prediction to be contradicted is not a weakness — it is the entire point. A prediction that cannot be contradicted does not say anything about the physics. If you learn nothing when the prediction is right, you also learn nothing when it is wrong. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Six Elements of a Defensible Prediction (Image: A vertical numbered list with six labeled items, each in a distinct

The Six Elements of a Defensible Prediction (Image: A vertical numbered list with six labeled items, each in a distinct color-coded card. Card 1 (blue): "System Response Features — specific committed values or ranges. Not 'it increases' but 'it reaches 28.4 N at ω = 100 rad/s.'" Card 2 (green): "Propagated Uncertainty — derived from sensor specs using partial derivatives or Monte Carlo. Stated as estimate ± U at confidence level." Card 3 (gold): "Committed Sketch — a hand-drawn or MATLAB plot of the expected output with labeled axes, units, and uncertainty bounds drawn before data is seen." Card 4 (purple): "Ranked Uncertainty Sources — which input contributes most? In what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as

what order? Specific estimated magnitudes." Card 5 (red): "Named Acceptance Criterion — a numerical threshold, stated as a specific inequality or metric value, defined before the comparison is computed." Card 6 (orange): "Risk Assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern identified with physical basis. What would 'wrong' look like, and what physical effect would cause it?" Below all six: "All six are required in the Phase 2 Prediction Memo. Missing any one makes the prediction incomplete.") Talking Point: The Phase 2 Prediction Memo requires all six of these elements — and I want to walk through why each one exists. System response features commit you to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your

to something specific: a number, a range, a shape, a direction with a magnitude. The uncertainty estimate connects your prediction to the measurement apparatus — it is derived from the sensor spec using the propagation methods you know from Week 2. The committed sketch forces you to visualize what the data should look like before you see it — axes, units, scale, and uncertainty bounds drawn by hand or in MATLAB. The ranked uncertainty sources tell me what you understand about the dominant error in this measurement. The named acceptance criterion commits you to the standard you will use for evaluation — before you see whether your prediction was close or not. And element six: risk assessment. Name at least one specific anomalous data pattern that would indicate the model is wrong — not

ent six: risk assessment. Name at least one specific anomalous data pattern that would indicate the model is wrong — not just "the data might be noisy," but a specific physically-grounded signature: "if the output shows amplitude-dependent behavior, the linear model is missing a nonlinear term." The risk assessment is what connects your prediction to the anomalous data thinking from Week 3. Six elements. All six are required. ================================================================================ slide 4: Element 1 — System Response Features (Image: A comparison of two prediction statements for the same experiment. Statement A: "The measured temperature will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The

ure will be high." Red annotations: "No units," "No direction," "No mechanism," "Cannot be falsified." Statement B: "The thermocouple will read 87 ± 4°C at a heater power of 25 W, based on the steady-state energy balance Q = h·A·ΔT with h = 35 W/m²K from the correlation for natural convection from a horizontal surface." Green annotations: "Specific value with units," "Physical mechanism (energy balance) stated," "Parameter source identified (convection correlation)," "Falsifiable: a reading of 110°C would clearly contradict this." Caption: "A system response feature is a committed, specific, mechanism-grounded prediction — not a directional tendency.") Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the

system response feature is a specific prediction about what the measured quantity will be, or how it will behave as the independent variable changes. "It will increase" is not a system response feature. "The temperature will reach 87 plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius at 25 Watts of heater power, based on the steady-state energy balance with a natural convection coefficient derived from the correlation for a horizontal surface" — that is a system response feature. It commits to a value. It names the physical mechanism behind the prediction. It gives me a basis for evaluating whether the prediction was right or wrong, and more importantly, for understanding what a discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or

discrepancy means physically. In the Phase 2 memo, every system response feature must be tied to a physical equation or correlation — not to intuition, not to "it seemed reasonable." ================================================================================ slide 5: Element 2 — Propagated Uncertainty from Sensor Specs (Image: A three-step workflow diagram. Step 1: "Read the spec sheet — extract sensor accuracy, resolution, and measurement range." A sample spec card shows: "Thermocouple accuracy: ±0.5°C. Data acquisition resolution: ±0.2°C. Combined standard uncertainty: √(0.5² + 0.2²) = 0.54°C." Step 2: "Apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result:

ing equation." Shows a partial derivative expression for ΔT from the energy balance equation. Step 3: "State the result: Uncertainty in predicted temperature = ±4°C at 95% CI (dominant source: uncertainty in convection coefficient h, contributing 78% of total variance)." A gold callout: "This is predict-from-specs — estimating uncertainty before you have data, using only the sensor spec sheet.") Talking Point: The uncertainty estimate in your prediction is not a guess — it is a calculation from the sensor spec. This is the predict-from-specs workflow you practiced at the end of the Week 4 lab: given a spec sheet, apply partial derivative propagation to the governing equation, and report the predicted output uncertainty. You are doing exactly the same thing for the Phase 2 memo, but now

quation, and report the predicted output uncertainty. You are doing exactly the same thing for the Phase 2 memo, but now you have not run the experiment yourself — you are working from the spec sheet included in the experiment packet. The calculation is the same: take the sensor accuracies and resolutions from the spec, propagate them through the governing equation, rank the contributions, and report the dominant source. The fact that you have not seen the physical hardware does not change the calculation. If you can read a spec sheet and apply the propagation formula, you can estimate the uncertainty before the experiment runs. ================================================================================ slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch (Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a

======================================= slide 6: Element 3 — The Committed Sketch (Image: A hand-drawn style sketch of a time-series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 60. Y-axis labeled "Temperature (°C)" from 20 to 100. A smooth S-shaped curve rises from ambient temperature to an asymptote around 87°C, labeled "Expected steady-state: 87°C." Two dashed lines parallel to the curve form an uncertainty band labeled "±4°C (propagated from sensor spec)." A horizontal target line at 87°C is labeled "Predicted steady-state." Hand-drawn annotations in a different style: "First 10s: rapid rise." "30-40s: rate slows." "After 50s: near steady-state." "Scatter within this band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and

his band is measurement noise — consistent with sensor spec." Caption: "Drawn before data is released — axes, units, and bounds committed in advance.") Talking Point: The committed sketch is not a formality. It is the most direct test of whether your prediction is concrete. If you cannot draw the expected output — with labeled axes, units, approximate scale, and an uncertainty band — you do not yet have a prediction. You have a direction. The sketch forces you to make a sequence of commitments: what quantity is on each axis, at what scale, over what range. The shape of the curve commits you to the physical mechanism — linear growth, exponential approach, oscillation. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your

n. The uncertainty band commits you to the magnitude of expected scatter. When the data comes in, you overlay it on your sketch. If it falls within the band, you have learned something positive. If it falls outside, you have a discrepancy to explain. But none of that analysis is possible if you did not draw the sketch before you saw the data. Draw it first. ================================================================================ slide 7: Elements 4 and 5 — Ranked Sources and Acceptance Criterion (Image: A two-panel slide. Left panel: "Element 4 — Ranked Uncertainty Sources." A horizontal bar chart showing three inputs, with bars proportional to their contribution to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C

on to total output uncertainty: Input A (convection coefficient h) — 78%. Input B (thermocouple accuracy) — 14%. Input C (DAQ resolution) — 8%. Annotations: "Input A dominates — improving the convection correlation would most reduce uncertainty." "Inputs B and C are secondary — better sensors would help marginally." Right panel: "Element 5 — Acceptance Criterion." A text box: "Pre-committed criterion: the prediction is considered successful if the measured steady-state temperature falls within the propagated uncertainty interval of 87 ± 4°C. If the measured value falls outside this band, the discrepancy exceeds measurement noise and requires a physical explanation." A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.") Talking Point:

A red annotation: "This criterion was stated before data release — it cannot be revised retroactively.") Talking Point: Element four — ranked uncertainty sources — is the output of the sensitivity analysis from this week's lecture. Once you have propagated uncertainty through the governing equation, you know which input contributes the most. State it explicitly, with estimated magnitudes. This is not just bookkeeping — it tells me whether you understand the dominant error in the measurement and whether your prediction accounts for the right sources of uncertainty. Element five — the named acceptance criterion — is the pre-commitment that makes the comparison defensible. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction

e. You state a specific threshold before you see the data: if the measured value falls within this range, the prediction holds; if it falls outside, the discrepancy requires a physical explanation. Pre-committing to the criterion means you cannot move the goalposts after seeing the result. That is not a bureaucratic rule — it is the logical requirement that makes validation meaningful. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Grading Philosophy — Right Prediction, Wrong Reasoning Scores Lower (Image: A 2x2 matrix with "Prediction Correctness" on one axis (Right / Wrong) and "Quality of Reasoning" on the other axis (Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal

(Sound / Flawed). Four quadrants: Top-left "Right prediction, sound reasoning" — gold star label "Excellent — the ideal outcome." Top-right "Right prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Suspicious — you may have gotten lucky. Scores lower than bottom-left." Bottom-left "Wrong prediction, sound reasoning" — green label "Good — the analysis was correct; the discrepancy teaches you something." Bottom-right "Wrong prediction, flawed reasoning" — red label "Poor — both the analysis and the outcome fail." A caption: "A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right from vague intuition.") Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not

intuition.") Talking Point: I want to communicate the Phase 2 grading standard as explicitly as I can, because it is not the grading standard you may be used to. A prediction that turns out to be wrong but was derived from sound physical reasoning and a complete, committed analysis scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition with no mechanism, no calculation, and no committed uncertainty. The goal of Phase 2 is not to produce a correct prediction. The goal is to demonstrate that you understood the physics of the experiment before you saw the data — well enough to build a specific, mechanistic, falsifiable prediction. If your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons,

your prediction was right for the right reasons, that is excellent. If your prediction was wrong for the right reasons, you have learned something real. If your prediction was right for the wrong reasons — if you got lucky — you have learned nothing and the grading reflects that. This philosophy is not idiosyncratic — it is the professional norm for test engineers, and you should hear it clearly before Phase 2 begins. ================================================================================ slide 9: Worked Example — A Complete Prediction for an Unfamiliar Experiment (Image: A structured layout walking through a Phase 2 memo structure for a simple heat conduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with

onduction experiment — one the class has not run as Phase 1. The experiment involves a metal rod heated at one end, with temperature sensors at two points along the rod. Headers and one-line summaries for each section: "System Response Features: steady-state temperature at sensor 2 predicted to be 54 ± 5°C based on one-dimensional heat conduction equation Q = k·A·ΔT/L." "Uncertainty: dominant source is thermal conductivity k — literature value uncertainty contributes 68% of total output variance." "Sketch: temperature profile along rod shown as linear gradient with ±5°C band." "Acceptance Criterion: prediction is successful if measured temperature at sensor 2 is within 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod

in 54 ± 5°C." "Risk Assessment: if temperature profile is nonlinear, the model's assumption of no heat loss from the rod surface fails — radiation or convection losses not included." The layout is clean and reads as a professional memo excerpt.) Talking Point: Let me walk you through what a complete Phase 2 prediction looks like, using a heat conduction experiment that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I read the theory primer, identify the governing equation — one-dimensional Fourier conduction — and extract the system response feature: steady-state temperature at the second sensor, predicted to be 54 degrees Celsius. I propagate uncertainty using the conductivity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the

ity value from the literature and the sensor specs from the experiment packet — the conductivity dominates. I sketch the expected linear temperature gradient along the rod with the uncertainty band. I commit to an acceptance criterion: within 54 plus or minus 5 degrees. And in the risk assessment — element six, which is required for Phase 2 — I identify the anomalous signature that would indicate model-form failure: if the measured temperature profile is nonlinear along the rod, the model's assumption of no lateral heat loss is wrong, and the one-dimensional model is inadequate. That is a complete prediction. ================================================================================ slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session (Image: A structured timeline for today's lab

======================== slide 10: What You Will Do in Today's Lab Session (Image: A structured timeline for today's lab session. Step 1 (30 min): "Receive your Phase 2 experiment packet — theory primer, sensor specs, DAQ configuration, physical setup description, original hypothesis from the student who ran Phase 1." Step 2 (45 min): "Extract system response features, propagate uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch." Step 3 (15 min): "Write the acceptance criterion and rank the uncertainty sources." Step 4: "Submit the Prediction Memo before data is released — this is the gate." A red annotation at Step 4: "Data will not be released until the memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received

memo is submitted. The memo is the gate, not a deliverable alongside the data." A gold note: "The pairing: you received Experiment B's packet; the student who ran Experiment B received Experiment A's packet. You will present both in the Phase 3 lightning talk.") Talking Point: In today's lab session, you will receive the experiment packet for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that you did not run as your Phase 1. The packet includes the theory primer, sensor specifications, DAQ configuration, and the original hypothesis from the student who ran that experiment. You will not receive the data until after you submit the Prediction Memo. That submission is the gate — data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all six elements of the prediction:

data is released after the memo, not alongside it. Spend the session working through all six elements of the prediction: extract system response features from the theory primer, propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet, draw the committed sketch, rank the uncertainty sources, write the acceptance criterion, and write the risk assessment — at least one specific anomalous data pattern with physical basis that would indicate model-form failure. Submit before data is released. That commitment is what makes the subsequent comparison honest. The Phase 3 lightning talk — where you present your cross-prediction results — also includes a graded peer evaluation. A strong peer evaluation names specific evidence and explains the reasoning: not "the uncertainty analysis was incomplete" but "the

er evaluation names specific evidence and explains the reasoning: not "the uncertainty analysis was incomplete" but "the uncertainty in the derived quantity was not propagated from the sensor spec, which is required by the rubric." The Week 3 reading on comparative analysis gives you the four-part structure; apply it to a live talk the same way you apply it to a written report. ================================================================================ slide 11: Closure Pre-commitment is not a bureaucratic requirement. It is the logical structure that makes experimental learning honest. If you construct your interpretation of the data after seeing it, you will inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic,

ill inevitably see what you expected to see, or what you hoped to see. The committed prediction — specific, mechanistic, falsifiable, with an uncertainty estimate and an acceptance criterion — is your promise to yourself and to the reader that your interpretation of the data was not reverse-engineered from the outcome. The six elements of a defensible prediction are the six components of that promise. In the Phase 2 memo you are writing today, every one of them is required. And the grading philosophy is explicit: sound reasoning in the wrong direction is more valuable than vague intuition in the right one. That is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now.

at is the standard this course holds you to. That is the standard your profession will hold you to. Build the habit now. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W5 Lecture 1: "What a Good Prediction Looks Like — Committing Before You Look" (CLO 1, 2, 3). - All six elements of the Phase 2 Prediction Memo are explicitly walked through in slides 3–7, per blueprint: system response features (committed values), uncertainty from spec propagation, committed sketch, ranked uncertainty sources, named acceptance criterion, risk assessment.
