pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Lecture L02 E1_W5_L2 — The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 2 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 =======================... E1_W5_L2 — The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 5, Lecture 2 Generated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough Talking Point: This lecture is not a slide lecture in the usual sense. What I am going to do is walk through a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I am going to do it transparently, in real time, narrating every decision I make — including the ones I am uncertain about. I will not polish this demonstration. I will show you the thinking, not just the result, because the thinking is what you are learning to do. When I reveal the data and we compare it to the prediction, I will tell you exactly what the discrepancy means and which category of disagreement it falls into. By the end of this lecture, you will have seen the complete Phase 2 workflow executed once — and E1 will be closed. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Experiment — A Torsional Pendulum (Image: A schematic diagram of a torsional pendulum. A horizontal disk is suspended by a thin metal rod or wire attached at its center, which is fixed at the top to a rigid support. The disk is shown mid-oscillation with angular displacement θ indicated by an arrow. Labels: "Disk: moment of inertia I (kg·m²)," "Suspension wire: torsional stiffness κ (N·m/rad)," "Angular position sensor: encoder at top of wire." A second label identifies the equipment as an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that no student in the room ran as Phase 1. A text note: "No one in this room ran this experiment. I am working from the theory primer and the sensor spec sheet — exactly what you have for your Phase 2 packet.") Talking Point: The experiment is a torsional pendulum. A disk is suspended by a thin rod with known torsional stiffness. When the disk is displaced angularly and released, it oscillates — and the oscillation frequency depends on the torsional stiffness of the rod and the moment of inertia of the disk. This is an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that none of you ran as your Phase 1. I have the same materials you have for Phase 2: a theory primer that describes the experiment and gives the governing equation, and a sensor spec sheet for the angular encoder used to measure the oscillation. I have not seen the data. What I am about to do is walk through the five elements of a prediction using those materials alone — exactly the workflow from this morning's lecture. ================================================================================ slide 3: Step 1 — Extract the Governing Equation and System Response Features (Image: A derivation slide. The governing equation: "I·θ̈ + κ·θ = 0," leading to the natural frequency expression: "ωₙ = √(κ/I)" and the period "T = 2π√(I/κ)." Below: a table showing the values from the theory primer: "I = 0.0248 kg·m² (disk geometry, measured with calipers and a scale)," "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad (from wire calibration data in the packet)." Calculation: "T = 2π√(0.0248/0.0315) = 2π × 0.887 = 5.57 s." A gold box: "System Response Feature: The torsional pendulum period is predicted to be 5.57 s. The oscillation amplitude should decay due to air damping — but the model assumes undamped oscillation. Flag this assumption explicitly.") Talking Point: Step one: read the theory primer and extract the governing equation. For a torsional pendulum, the equation of motion is I times theta double-dot plus kappa times theta equals zero, giving a natural frequency of the square root of kappa over I. With the values from the primer — moment of inertia 0.0248 kilogram-meter-squared, torsional stiffness 0.0315 Newton-meters per radian — the predicted period is 5.57 seconds. That is the system response feature I am committing to. I also note immediately that the model assumes undamped oscillation — there is no damping term. The actual disk oscillates in air, which has some drag. Air damping will cause amplitude decay but minimally affects the period for light damping (ζ ≪ 1). The period deviation I observe later is a different effect — amplitude-dependent nonlinearity. For now, my undamped period prediction stands, and I flag the amplitude decay as an expected but unmodeled feature. ================================================================================ slide 4: Step 2 — Propagate Uncertainty from the Spec Sheet (Image: A partial derivative propagation table. Column headers: "Parameter," "Value," "Partial Derivative ∂T/∂(param)," "Uncertainty δ(param)," "Contribution |∂T/∂(param) × δ(param)|." Rows: "I = 0.0248 kg·m²" — partial derivative = π/√(κI) — δI = ±5% from geometry measurement — contribution = ±0.14 s. "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad" — partial derivative = −π√(I)/κ^(3/2) — δκ = ±8% from wire calibration uncertainty — contribution = ±0.22 s. "Combined uncertainty (quadrature sum): δT = √(0.14² + 0.22²) = ±0.26 s." A gold box: "Predicted period: 5.57 ± 0.26 s (95% CI, partial derivative method). Dominant source: torsional stiffness κ, contributing 72% of total output variance.") Talking Point: Step two: propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet values. I take the partial derivative of the period with respect to I and with respect to kappa. The stiffness kappa has larger uncertainty than the moment of inertia — the wire calibration in the packet carries 8% uncertainty, while the geometry measurement for I carries about 5%. Evaluating the propagation, the kappa term dominates: it contributes 72% of the total output variance. The combined uncertainty in the predicted period is plus or minus 0.26 seconds at 95% confidence. So my prediction is: the period is 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. That is a specific, defensible claim derived from the spec sheet — without having seen the angular encoder measure a single oscillation. ================================================================================ slide 5: Step 3 — The Committed Sketch (Image: A hand-drawn-style time series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 30. Y-axis labeled "Angular Displacement θ (rad)" from −0.8 to 0.8. The expected oscillation is shown as a sinusoidal wave with a period of approximately 5.57 seconds. An uncertainty band shades the region within ±0.26 s of the expected period — shown as a slight smearing of the zero crossings. A separate pair of dashed lines shows gradual amplitude decay due to air damping: "Expected amplitude envelope — decays slowly due to air drag (not modeled, but physically expected)." Axes are labeled with units. A gold annotation at the first period: "T = 5.57 ± 0.26 s — committed before data release." The plot is clearly labeled as a pre-data sketch.) Talking Point: Step three: draw the committed sketch. Here is the expected oscillation — sinusoidal, period 5.57 seconds, amplitude starting near the initial displacement and decaying slowly. I have drawn the amplitude envelope with a slight decay because I know air drag will be present even though the model ignores it. The shaded region at the zero crossings represents the uncertainty in the period — if the measured zero crossings are within the shaded band, the period is consistent with the prediction. If they are systematically outside, I have a discrepancy. Notice that I have committed to the shape, the period, the approximate initial amplitude, and the expected slow decay — all before seeing a single data point. This is the sketch that will go into my Phase 2 memo, and it is the first thing I will overlay the data on when it comes in. ================================================================================ slide 6: Step 4 — Acceptance Criterion and Risk Assessment (Image: Two clearly separated text boxes. Box 1 — "Acceptance Criterion": "The prediction is successful if the measured oscillation period is within 5.57 ± 0.26 s (the propagated uncertainty interval at 95% CI). A measured period outside this interval requires a physical explanation — either an unaccounted-for uncertainty source or a model-form limitation." Box 2 — "Risk Assessment — Two Anomalous Signatures to Watch": "(1) If the period decreases significantly with amplitude (nonlinearity), the small-angle approximation in the model has failed. The linear model is invalid at large angular displacements. (2) If the period is significantly shorter than predicted — less than 5.2 s — the torsional stiffness in the spec may be miscalibrated, or the wire has hardened since the calibration was performed." Both boxes use consistent formatting. Caption: "Commit to what wrong looks like before you see the data.") Talking Point: Step four: state the acceptance criterion and the risk assessment. The acceptance criterion is numerical and pre-committed — if the measured period is within 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds, I will classify the comparison as supporting agreement. If it falls outside, I need a physical explanation. The risk assessment names two specific anomalous patterns I should watch for. First: if the measured period decreases with amplitude — if early oscillations are shorter than later ones as the amplitude decays — the small-angle approximation in the linearized model has failed. The linear model is only valid for small angular displacements, and this failure is predictable in advance. Second: if the measured period is significantly shorter than predicted — say, below 5.2 seconds — the torsional stiffness in the packet may be wrong, or the wire properties have changed since calibration. Both anomalous signatures are physically motivated, and both are commitable before the data is released. ================================================================================ slide 7: Data Release — What Did We Measure? (Image: A plot of actual measured angular displacement vs. time for the torsional pendulum experiment. The time axis runs from 0 to 30 seconds. The oscillation is clearly sinusoidal, and the amplitude decays slowly as expected. A highlighted annotation marks the measured period at several zero crossings — averaging to approximately 5.83 seconds. The committed prediction range (5.31 to 5.83 s) is shown as a vertical shaded band at the boundary — 5.83 is at the outer edge of the prediction interval. The amplitude decay matches the sketched envelope approximately. A gold annotation at the measured period: "Measured: T = 5.83 ± 0.05 s (from zero-crossing analysis, 8 oscillations)." The figure is clearly labeled as the actual data, in contrast to the sketch from slide 5.) Talking Point: Here is the data. The measured period, averaged over eight complete oscillations using zero-crossing analysis, is 5.83 seconds. My prediction was 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. The measured value is at the very edge of my prediction interval — 5.83 is the upper bound of 5.57 plus 0.26. So: does this constitute agreement? Using my pre-committed criterion — agreement if the measured period is within the uncertainty interval — the answer is marginal. 5.83 is exactly at the boundary. I need to apply the validation metric carefully. The relative difference is (5.83 minus 5.57) divided by 5.57 — about 4.7%. The combined uncertainty is 0.26 s, which is 4.7% of the model prediction. So the difference is just barely within the combined uncertainty. I will classify this as marginal agreement — but I should investigate the discrepancy rather than simply accepting agreement. ================================================================================ slide 8: What Does the Discrepancy Tell Us? (Image: A residual analysis plot. X-axis: "Oscillation Number" from 1 to 8. Y-axis: "Period (s) for each oscillation." The individual measured periods are plotted as dots. A horizontal dashed line marks the predicted value of 5.57 s. All measured periods are above the prediction line, and there is a slight trend: earlier oscillations (when amplitude is large) have slightly longer periods — around 5.90 s — while later oscillations (when amplitude is small) converge toward 5.65 s. A gold annotation: "Systematic trend: period decreases as amplitude decreases — consistent with nonlinear stiffness at large displacement." A second annotation: "Residuals are NOT random. The small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement — this was the first risk assessment item.") Talking Point: Look at the residuals. The measured period is not just slightly high — it shows a systematic trend. The first few oscillations, when the amplitude is large, have longer periods. The later oscillations, as amplitude decays, have shorter periods approaching the linear model prediction. That is not random scatter. That is the small-angle approximation failing at large displacement — exactly the first risk assessment item I committed to before seeing the data. The linear model predicts a constant period regardless of amplitude. The physical system shows amplitude-dependent period because the linearization I·θ-double-dot plus kappa·theta is only valid for small theta. The linear torsional pendulum model uses the approximation sin(θ) ≈ θ for small angles. At large angular amplitudes, the exact sin(θ) restoring force is weaker than the linear approximation, reducing the effective stiffness and increasing the period. At large theta, the nonlinear sine term in the full restoring force equation matters. The anomalous signature I predicted is the one I see. This is what it looks like when a model-form failure is predicted in advance and then confirmed: you learn something specific about the physics of the system, not just that the model was approximately right. ================================================================================ slide 9: Classifying the Discrepancy (Image: A three-category taxonomy table, referencing the taxonomy introduced in the W5 Reading "Discrepancy Is Data." Three rows: Category 1: "Within propagated uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. No model revision required." Status for this experiment: "Not applicable — residuals show systematic trend, not random scatter." Category 2: "Larger than uncertainty band, no systematic structure — suggests underestimated uncertainty." Status for this experiment: "Partially applicable — period is at the edge of the uncertainty band." Category 3: "Systematic trend in residuals — model-form limitation. Model missing a physical effect." Status for this experiment: "PRIMARY CATEGORY — amplitude-dependent period is consistent with small-angle approximation failure." A gold box: "Classification: Category 3 — model-form limitation. The model is missing the nonlinear restoring force term active at large displacement." Caption: "Category 3 is the most informative discrepancy — it tells you what physics to add to the model.") Talking Point: Let me classify this discrepancy using the taxonomy from the reading assigned this week. Category 1 is scatter within the uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. That does not apply here: the residuals are structured, not random. Category 2 is a gap larger than the uncertainty band with no systematic structure — suggesting underestimated uncertainty. That is partially applicable, since the measured period is at the edge of our interval. But the primary classification is Category 3: a systematic trend in the residuals that reveals a model-form limitation. The amplitude-dependent period is direct evidence that the small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement. The correct physical model would use the full nonlinear equation of motion — or at minimum, the perturbation correction for finite amplitude oscillations. Category 3 is the most informative kind of discrepancy because it tells you specifically what physics to add to the model. The experimentally informed model revision is clear: include the amplitude correction. ================================================================================ slide 10: What This Closes — And What It Opens (Image: A split slide. Left half: "What Phase 1 built." A horizontal list: "AMVF literacy," "Measurement fundamentals," "Uncertainty propagation," "Quantitative comparison," "Full pipeline execution," "Peer critique." Label: "You can walk the physical modeling branch." Right half: "What Phase 2 revealed." A horizontal list: "Prediction from specs alone," "Pre-commitment," "Anomalous signature prediction," "Discrepancy classification." Label: "You can engage both branches." Below both halves, a bridge spanning the full width labeled "Transition to Phase II — E2." Text: "In E2, prediction before measurement is not an exercise — it is the required gate before any hardware access. The models are more complex. The stakes are higher. The cognitive move is the same." A closing question centered at the bottom: "What would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?") Talking Point: Let me close E1 by making explicit what E2 will demand. In Phase 1, you walked the physical modeling branch — you measured, you propagated uncertainty, you compared. Phase 2 added the pre-commitment step — before seeing the data, you built a prediction from equations and specs alone. What you practiced in Phase 2 is the same skill E2 requires, applied under higher stakes. In E2, the canned experiments are more complex aerospace systems — more parameters, more coupled physical effects, more places for model assumptions to fail. The models require more careful reading. The uncertainty budgets are harder to construct from a spec sheet alone. And crucially, prediction before hardware access is not optional in E2 — it is the gate. You submit your prediction memo, and only after submission does the experiment begin. The question that opens E2 is the same question we just answered for the torsional pendulum: what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend? That is the question of a test engineer. That is the question you are now equipped to answer. ================================================================================ slide 11: Closure E1 is complete. Over the last five weeks, you walked the right branch of the AMVF — from hypothesis through measurement through uncertainty propagation through quantitative comparison — and you finished by committing to a prediction before seeing data and evaluating that prediction against the outcome. The torsional pendulum example in this lecture showed you what that looks like when it is done transparently: every decision narrated, every assumption named, every discrepancy classified and physically interpreted. The discrepancy was not a failure. It was the most valuable outcome of the experiment — a specific, physically interpretable model-form limitation that tells you exactly what to improve. That is what a test engineer produces. That is what experimental methods is for. The rest of this course builds that skill into more complex systems, more demanding modeling requirements, and eventually your own experimental design from scratch. You are ready for what comes next. ================================================================================ Agent Notes New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). What I am going to do is walk through a complete prediction-to-outcome comparison for an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that no one in this room ran as their Phase 1. I am going to do it transparently, in real time, narrating every decision I make — including the ones I am uncertain about. I will not polish this demonstration. I will show you the thinking, not just the result, because the thinking is what you are learning to do. When I reveal the data and we compare it to the prediction, I will tell you exactly what the discrepancy means and which category of disagreement it falls into. By the end of this lecture, you will have seen the complete Phase 2 workflow executed once — and E1 will be closed. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Experiment — A Torsional Pendulum (Image: A schematic diagram of a torsional pendulum. A horizontal disk is suspended by a thin metal rod or wire attached at its center, which is fixed at the top to a rigid support. The disk is shown mid-oscillation with angular displacement θ indicated by an arrow. Labels: "Disk: moment of inertia I (kg·m²)," "Suspension wire: torsional stiffness κ (N·m/rad)," "Angular position sensor: encoder at top of wire." A second label identifies the equipment as an experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one that no student in the room ran as Phase 1. A text note: "No one in this room ran this experiment. I am working from the theory primer and the sensor spec sheet — exactly what you have for your Phase 2 packet.") Talking Point: The experiment is a torsional pendulum. A disk is suspended by a thin rod with known torsional stiffness. When the disk is displaced angularly and released, it oscillates — and the oscillation frequency depends on the torsional stiffness of the rod and the moment of inertia of the disk. This is an experiment from the Tier 1 pool that none of you ran as your Phase 1. I have the same materials you have for Phase 2: a theory primer that describes the experiment and gives the governing equation, and a sensor spec sheet for the angular encoder used to measure the oscillation. I have not seen the data. What I am about to do is walk through the five elements of a prediction using those materials alone — exactly the workflow from this morning's lecture. ================================================================================ slide 3: Step 1 — Extract the Governing Equation and System Response Features (Image: A derivation slide. The governing equation: "I·θ̈ + κ·θ = 0," leading to the natural frequency expression: "ωₙ = √(κ/I)" and the period "T = 2π√(I/κ)." Below: a table showing the values from the theory primer: "I = 0.0248 kg·m² (disk geometry, measured with calipers and a scale)," "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad (from wire calibration data in the packet)." Calculation: "T = 2π√(0.0248/0.0315) = 2π × 0.887 = 5.57 s." A gold box: "System Response Feature: The torsional pendulum period is predicted to be 5.57 s. The oscillation amplitude should decay due to air damping — but the model assumes undamped oscillation. Flag this assumption explicitly.") Talking Point: Step one: read the theory primer and extract the governing equation. For a torsional pendulum, the equation of motion is I times theta double-dot plus kappa times theta equals zero, giving a natural frequency of the square root of kappa over I. With the values from the primer — moment of inertia 0.0248 kilogram-meter-squared, torsional stiffness 0.0315 Newton-meters per radian — the predicted period is 5.57 seconds. That is the system response feature I am committing to. I also note immediately that the model assumes undamped oscillation — there is no damping term. The actual disk oscillates in air, which has some drag. Air damping will cause amplitude decay but minimally affects the period for light damping (ζ ≪ 1). The period deviation I observe later is a different effect — amplitude-dependent nonlinearity. For now, my undamped period prediction stands, and I flag the amplitude decay as an expected but unmodeled feature. ================================================================================ slide 4: Step 2 — Propagate Uncertainty from the Spec Sheet (Image: A partial derivative propagation table. Column headers: "Parameter," "Value," "Partial Derivative ∂T/∂(param)," "Uncertainty δ(param)," "Contribution |∂T/∂(param) × δ(param)|." Rows: "I = 0.0248 kg·m²" — partial derivative = π/√(κI) — δI = ±5% from geometry measurement — contribution = ±0.14 s. "κ = 0.0315 N·m/rad" — partial derivative = −π√(I)/κ^(3/2) — δκ = ±8% from wire calibration uncertainty — contribution = ±0.22 s. "Combined uncertainty (quadrature sum): δT = √(0.14² + 0.22²) = ±0.26 s." A gold box: "Predicted period: 5.57 ± 0.26 s (95% CI, partial derivative method). Dominant source: torsional stiffness κ, contributing 72% of total output variance.") Talking Point: Step two: propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet values. I take the partial derivative of the period with respect to I and with respect to kappa. The stiffness kappa has larger uncertainty than the moment of inertia — the wire calibration in the packet carries 8% uncertainty, while the geometry measurement for I carries about 5%. Evaluating the propagation, the kappa term dominates: it contributes 72% of the total output variance. The combined uncertainty in the predicted period is plus or minus 0.26 seconds at 95% confidence. So my prediction is: the period is 5.57 plus or minus 0.26 seconds. That is a specific, defensible claim derived from the spec sheet — without having seen the angular encoder measure a single oscillation. ================================================================================ slide 5: Step 3 — The Committed Sketch (Image: A hand-drawn-style time series plot. X-axis labeled "Time (s)" from 0 to 30. Y-axis labeled "Angular Displacement θ (rad)" from −0.8 to 0.8. The expected oscillation is shown as a sinusoidal wave with a period of approximately 5.57 seconds. An uncertainty band shades the region within ±0.26 s of the expected period — shown as a slight smearing of the zero crossings. A separate pair of dashed lines shows gradual amplitude decay due to air damping: "Expected amplitude envelope — decays slowly due to air drag (not modeled, but physically expected)." Axes are labeled with units. A gold annotation at the first period: "T = 5.57 ± 0.26 s — committed before data release." The plot is clearly labeled as a pre-data sketch.) Talking Point: Step three: draw the committed sketch. Here is the expected oscillation — sinusoidal, period 5.57 seconds, amplitude starting near the initial displacement and decaying slowly. I have drawn the amplitude envelope with a slight decay because I know air drag will be present even though the model ignores it. The shaded region at the zero crossings represents the uncertainty in the period — if the measured zero crossings are within the shaded band, the period is consistent with the prediction. If they are systematically outside, I have a discrepancy. Notice that I have committed to the shape, the period, the approximate initial amplitude, and the expected slow decay — all before seeing a single data point. This is the sketch that will go into my Phase 2 memo, and it is the first thing I will overlay the data on when it comes in. ================================================================================ slide 6: Step 4 — Acceptance Criterion and Risk Assessment (Image: Two clearly separated text boxes. epancy Tell Us? (Image: A residual analysis plot. X-axis: "Oscillation Number" from 1 to 8. Y-axis: "Period (s) for each oscillation." The individual measured periods are plotted as dots. T random. The small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement — this was the first risk assessment item.") Talking Point: Look at the residuals. The measured period is not just slightly high — it shows a systematic trend. The first few oscillations, when the amplitude is large, have longer periods. The later oscillations, as amplitude decays, have shorter periods approaching the linear model prediction. That is not random scatter. That is the small-angle approximation failing at large displacement — exactly the first risk assessment item I committed to before seeing the data. The linear model predicts a constant period regardless of amplitude. The physical system shows amplitude-dependent period because the linearization I·θ-double-dot plus kappa·theta is only valid for

ical system shows amplitude-dependent period because the linearization I·θ-double-dot plus kappa·theta is only valid for small theta. The linear torsional pendulum model uses the approximation sin(θ) ≈ θ for small angles. At large angular amplitudes, the exact sin(θ) restoring force is weaker than the linear approximation, reducing the effective stiffness and increasing the period. At large theta, the nonlinear sine term in the full restoring force equation matters. The anomalous signature I predicted is the one I see. This is what it looks like when a model-form failure is predicted in advance and then confirmed: you learn something specific about the physics of the system, not just that the model was approximately right.

metric carefully. The relative difference is (5.83 minus 5.57) divided by 5.57 — about 4.7%. The combined uncertainty is 0.26 s, which is 4.7% of the model prediction. So the difference is just barely within the combined uncertainty. I will classify this as marginal agreement — but I should investigate the discrepancy rather than simply accepting agreement. ================================================================================ slide 8: What Does the Discrepancy Tell Us? (Image: A residual analysis plot. X-axis: "Oscillation Number" from 1 to 8. Y-axis: "Period (s) for each oscillation." The individual measured periods are plotted as dots. A horizontal dashed line marks the predicted value of 5.57 s. All measured periods are above the prediction line, and there is a slight trend:

ne marks the predicted value of 5.57 s. All measured periods are above the prediction line, and there is a slight trend: earlier oscillations (when amplitude is large) have slightly longer periods — around 5.90 s — while later oscillations (when amplitude is small) converge toward 5.65 s. A gold annotation: "Systematic trend: period decreases as amplitude decreases — consistent with nonlinear stiffness at large displacement." A second annotation: "Residuals are NOT random. The small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement — this was the first risk assessment item.") Talking Point: Look at the residuals. The measured period is not just slightly high — it shows a systematic trend. The first few oscillations, when the amplitude is large, have longer periods. The later

h — it shows a systematic trend. The first few oscillations, when the amplitude is large, have longer periods. The later oscillations, as amplitude decays, have shorter periods approaching the linear model prediction. That is not random scatter. That is the small-angle approximation failing at large displacement — exactly the first risk assessment item I committed to before seeing the data. The linear model predicts a constant period regardless of amplitude. The physical system shows amplitude-dependent period because the linearization I·θ-double-dot plus kappa·theta is only valid for small theta. The linear torsional pendulum model uses the approximation sin(θ) ≈ θ for small angles. At large angular amplitudes, the exact sin(θ) restoring force is weaker than the linear approximation,

for small angles. At large angular amplitudes, the exact sin(θ) restoring force is weaker than the linear approximation, reducing the effective stiffness and increasing the period. At large theta, the nonlinear sine term in the full restoring force equation matters. The anomalous signature I predicted is the one I see. This is what it looks like when a model-form failure is predicted in advance and then confirmed: you learn something specific about the physics of the system, not just that the model was approximately right. ================================================================================ slide 9: Classifying the Discrepancy (Image: A three-category taxonomy table, referencing the taxonomy introduced in the W5 Reading "Discrepancy Is Data." Three rows: Category 1: "Within

nomy table, referencing the taxonomy introduced in the W5 Reading "Discrepancy Is Data." Three rows: Category 1: "Within propagated uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. No model revision required." Status for this experiment: "Not applicable — residuals show systematic trend, not random scatter." Category 2: "Larger than uncertainty band, no systematic structure — suggests underestimated uncertainty." Status for this experiment: "Partially applicable — period is at the edge of the uncertainty band." Category 3: "Systematic trend in residuals — model-form limitation. Model missing a physical effect." Status for this experiment: "PRIMARY CATEGORY — amplitude-dependent period is consistent with small-angle approximation failure." A gold box: "Classification: Category 3 —

litude-dependent period is consistent with small-angle approximation failure." A gold box: "Classification: Category 3 — model-form limitation. The model is missing the nonlinear restoring force term active at large displacement." Caption: "Category 3 is the most informative discrepancy — it tells you what physics to add to the model.") Talking Point: Let me classify this discrepancy using the taxonomy from the reading assigned this week. Category 1 is scatter within the uncertainty band — consistent with measurement noise. That does not apply here: the residuals are structured, not random. Category 2 is a gap larger than the uncertainty band with no systematic structure — suggesting underestimated uncertainty. That is partially applicable, since the measured period is at the edge of our

e — suggesting underestimated uncertainty. That is partially applicable, since the measured period is at the edge of our interval. But the primary classification is Category 3: a systematic trend in the residuals that reveals a model-form limitation. The amplitude-dependent period is direct evidence that the small-angle approximation fails at large initial displacement. The correct physical model would use the full nonlinear equation of motion — or at minimum, the perturbation correction for finite amplitude oscillations. Category 3 is the most informative kind of discrepancy because it tells you specifically what physics to add to the model. The experimentally informed model revision is clear: include the amplitude correction.

what physics to add to the model. The experimentally informed model revision is clear: include the amplitude correction. ================================================================================ slide 10: What This Closes — And What It Opens (Image: A split slide. Left half: "What Phase 1 built." A horizontal list: "AMVF literacy," "Measurement fundamentals," "Uncertainty propagation," "Quantitative comparison," "Full pipeline execution," "Peer critique." Label: "You can walk the physical modeling branch." Right half: "What Phase 2 revealed." A horizontal list: "Prediction from specs alone," "Pre-commitment," "Anomalous signature prediction," "Discrepancy classification." Label: "You can engage both branches." Below both halves, a bridge spanning the full width labeled "Transition

ication." Label: "You can engage both branches." Below both halves, a bridge spanning the full width labeled "Transition to Phase II — E2." Text: "In E2, prediction before measurement is not an exercise — it is the required gate before any hardware access. The models are more complex. The stakes are higher. The cognitive move is the same." A closing question centered at the bottom: "What would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?") Talking Point: Let me close E1 by making explicit what E2 will demand. In Phase 1, you walked the physical modeling branch — you measured, you propagated uncertainty, you compared. Phase 2 added the pre-commitment step — before seeing the data, you built a prediction from equations and specs alone. What you

added the pre-commitment step — before seeing the data, you built a prediction from equations and specs alone. What you practiced in Phase 2 is the same skill E2 requires, applied under higher stakes. In E2, the canned experiments are more complex aerospace systems — more parameters, more coupled physical effects, more places for model assumptions to fail. The models require more careful reading. The uncertainty budgets are harder to construct from a spec sheet alone. And crucially, prediction before hardware access is not optional in E2 — it is the gate. You submit your prediction memo, and only after submission does the experiment begin. The question that opens E2 is the same question we just answered for the torsional pendulum: what would you need to know, before touching any

ns E2 is the same question we just answered for the torsional pendulum: what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend? That is the question of a test engineer. That is the question you are now equipped to answer. ================================================================================ slide 11: Closure E1 is complete. Over the last five weeks, you walked the right branch of the AMVF — from hypothesis through measurement through uncertainty propagation through quantitative comparison — and you finished by committing to a prediction before seeing data and evaluating that prediction against the outcome. The torsional pendulum example in this lecture showed you what that looks like when it is done transparently: every decision

torsional pendulum example in this lecture showed you what that looks like when it is done transparently: every decision narrated, every assumption named, every discrepancy classified and physically interpreted. The discrepancy was not a failure. It was the most valuable outcome of the experiment — a specific, physically interpretable model-form limitation that tells you exactly what to improve. That is what a test engineer produces. That is what experimental methods is for. The rest of this course builds that skill into more complex systems, more demanding modeling requirements, and eventually your own experimental design from scratch. You are ready for what comes next. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - New lecture,

mes next. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - New lecture, generated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - This lecture was added in Iteration 5 as W5 Lecture 2: "The Exploratory Example — A Complete Prediction-to-Outcome Walkthrough" (CLO 1, 2, 3, 5). - The experiment used is a torsional pendulum — from the Tier 1 pool, distinct from any Phase 1 assignment, not previously used as a worked example in any earlier lecture. - The lecture follows the exact sequence specified in the blueprint: theory primer → identify output → partial derivative propagation → committed sketch → acceptance criterion → data reveal → discrepancy classification → bridge to E2. - Discrepancy in the worked example is classified as Category 3

data reveal → discrepancy classification → bridge to E2. - Discrepancy in the worked example is classified as Category 3 (systematic trend → model-form limitation) per the reading taxonomy assigned in W5. The small-angle approximation failure is physically specific and predictable in advance. - E2 bridge is made explicit in slide 10, with the closing question verbatim from the blueprint: "what would you need to know, before touching any hardware, to make a prediction you could defend?" - This lecture is marked "Update-friendly" in the blueprint (the specific experiment should be rotated annually). The torsional pendulum example should be swapped for a different Tier 1 pool experiment in subsequent course offerings. - Slide count: 11 (title + 9 content + closure). Within spec (10-12). -

pool experiment in subsequent course offerings. - Slide count: 11 (title + 9 content + closure). Within spec (10-12). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Corrected slide 3 talking point — replaced incorrect claim that damping lengthens the period with correct physics: air damping causes amplitude decay but minimally affects period for ζ ≪ 1; the period deviation is amplitude-dependent nonlinearity, not a damping effect. Added two sentences to slide 8 explicitly stating the sin(θ) ≈ θ approximation and its failure at large amplitude. Absorbed not-consolidated approved item (W5 L2 slide 8 — add two sentences on amplitude-dependent period physics) into this same commit.

pages.title: R01
pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Reading R01 # Discrepancy Is Data — Interpreting What the Gap Tells You ## Three Categories of Mismatch, and What Each One Reveals About Your Model The most instructive moment in any experiment is not when your result matches the... Discrepancy Is Data — Interpreting What the Gap Tells You Three Categories of Mismatch, and What Each One Reveals About Your Model The most instructive moment in any experiment is not when your result matches the prediction. It's when it doesn't, and you're forced to explain why. Every experiment ends with a comparison: measured result on one side, model prediction on the other. When the two agree, confidence builds. When they don't, something more interesting happens: the disagreement carries information that the agreement never could. A result that matches your model tells you the model is not obviously wrong, for this experiment, at this scale, under these conditions. A result that doesn't match tells you something specific about what the model is missing or what the measurement got wrong. But only an engineer who knows how to read the gap can extract that information. The discipline of discrepancy interpretation begins with classification. Not all gaps between measurement and prediction are the same, and the same physical magnitude of gap can mean fundamentally different things depending on its relationship to the propagated uncertainty bounds and the structure of the residuals. Case 1: The gap falls within the propagated uncertainty band. The measured result and the model prediction are statistically consistent; the scatter is explained by measurement noise, and no model revision is required by this result alone. This is the resolution you hoped for when you designed the experiment. Note carefully what it does not mean: consistency within uncertainty says only that you cannot distinguish the model from noise with this experiment, this instrumentation, and this level of precision. A tighter measurement (fewer sources of random error, a better-characterized sensor) might reveal a real gap that the current uncertainty band was hiding. Consider measuring the natural frequency of a spring-mass system: if the scatter in your repeated frequency measurements is larger than the difference between measured and predicted frequency, the two are statistically consistent. Tighten the measurement, and a real discrepancy may emerge. Case 2: The gap is larger than the uncertainty band, but has no systematic structure. The data points scatter above and below the model, but their distribution is consistently displaced; the mean of the measured values does not fall within the predicted range, even accounting for propagated uncertainty. The residuals show no trend; they are randomly scattered but uniformly offset. This is a signal that your uncertainty analysis is incomplete: a significant error source was omitted from the propagation. The diagnostic question is which source. Review the measurement chain: are there sensor specifications you rough-estimated? An installation effect you assumed was negligible? A calibration offset you did not propagate? Consider a pendulum period measurement where you identified reaction time as the dominant uncertainty source but forgot that the pendulum's initial release angle affects the linearization assumption. That systematic physical effect, omitted from the uncertainty model, produces exactly this pattern: a gap larger than the stated bounds that has no systematic structure. Case 3: The gap shows systematic structure. The residuals are not random; they grow monotonically with the independent variable, or they are consistently positive over one range and negative over another, or they follow a pattern that mirrors the shape of a physical effect your model ignores. This is model-form error : the model's mathematical structure is wrong, not just its parameter values. Recalibrating the sensor or tightening the uncertainty bounds will not close this gap, because the gap is not caused by measurement variability at all. Consider a ball launched at an angle, where the model ignores air resistance: the predicted trajectory is systematically too long and too high at late times. The residuals grow with time, and they have a consistent sign, positive in the height direction throughout the flight. That is the fingerprint of the missing drag term. The pattern in the residuals is not noise. It is a physical signal, and it points to exactly what the model is missing. The value of this taxonomy isn't just retrospective; it shapes how you design experiments and write predictions before any data is seen. When you commit to a prediction for the Tier 1 Phase 2 Prediction Memo, understanding which category of discrepancy is plausible helps you define your acceptance criterion precisely. If you expect only Case 1 (noise within uncertainty), a tight propagated uncertainty band is your pass/fail gate. If you suspect Case 3 (model-form error), your acceptance criterion should explicitly describe what systematic structure in the residuals would constitute evidence of a model-form failure, and your risk assessment should name the physical effect you think the model might be missing. This taxonomy carries directly into E2, where residual plots are formalized as a required analytical tool and where the consequences of misclassifying a Case 3 gap as Case 1 (ignoring systematic model-form error as "just noise") become more consequential as the models grow more complex. The verbal decision rule is simple to apply in practice: residuals within the uncertainty band → Case 1, no model revision required; mean outside the band but no trend in the residuals → Case 2, find the missing uncertainty source; consistent trend in the residuals → Case 3, find the missing physics. Apply this rule before reaching for a statistical test or a more complex analysis. The qualitative pattern is usually diagnostic enough to identify the category and point toward the appropriate response. The Takeaway: Discrepancy between prediction and measurement isn't a grade penalty; it's an opportunity to identify which part of your model or measurement chain failed. The answer depends entirely on whether the gap is within your uncertainty bounds (Case 1), uniformly displaced beyond them (Case 2), or systematically structured (Case 3), and each category points to a different corrective action.
