pages.title: tier1 assignment
pages.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Assessment tier1 assignment # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** 2–5 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 **Structure... ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Weeks: 2–5 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 Structure: Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4) What This Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical experiments differ — you will be assigned a specific experiment for each phase — but the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are identical for all students regardless of which experiment they are assigned. Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional, and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive. Why Three Phases? Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1. It is the natural order. It is also a good starting point — but it is only the beginning. A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it on top of a prediction. Before a test ever runs, a test engineer asks: what should the data look like? They build a model of the experiment itself — accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, the signal chain — and commit to a prediction. That prediction is not decoration. It is used to design the experiment: to determine whether the sensors are capable of resolving the quantity of interest, to anticipate failure modes, and to define in advance what a meaningful result looks like. The ability to watch data come in during a live test and immediately know whether it matches expectation — and to act on that in real time — is one of the most important skills a test engineer develops. This course will get there. The full experimental design workflow — where your prediction shapes the design of the test — is the focus of E3. For now, in E1 and E2, you are building the foundational mindset: the habit of pre-committing to a prediction before you see the data. This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model before you saw the data, you cannot dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is real, and you have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one experiment. Your partner ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data — an experiment you never ran, using only the setup description and sensor specs. This is deliberate. The goal is not to mimic the full test engineer workflow, but to develop the prediction-first mindset by working through it in the most challenging form: predicting an experiment you have no direct experience with. Think of Phase 3 as reverse-engineering the experiment. You are reconstructing what the data should look like from the physics and the instrument chain alone — without the shortcut of having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset — building a forward model before you have the answer — is exactly what you will need when you design your own experiment from scratch in E3. The three-phase structure is not the actual workflow of a test engineer. It is a scaffold for developing the mindset of one. Assignment Overview Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual) What you are assigned: One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool. What you do: Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates). Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind) What you are assigned: A different experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one you did not run in Phase 1. What you do: Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup description for this unfamiliar experiment — predict what the data should look like. You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware. What you produce: A Phase 2 Prediction Memo — submitted before data is released. When: Week 4 (prediction submitted; data released after the due date). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You will be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment — they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. What you do: Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments. What you produce: A combined oral presentation (lightning talk) — you and your partner present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4 presentation. When: Week 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model. Before the lab session and during the lab session Complete items 1–4 before you arrive; items 5–8 are in-lab steps. Items 1–4 are not graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable — they are required inputs to your Phase 1 report, and students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab. Read the experiment-specific lab document for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document. Write a hypothesis — a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]." Sketch an AMVF diagram for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect — you will refine this after the lab. Identify your independent and dependent variables. Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor. Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis. Record your measurements using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound). Use MATLAB for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately — when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence. Phase 1 report — required sections 1. Experimental design Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least one confounding factor identified and addressed. 2. AMVF annotation Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one) to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan. 3. Theory — confirm the model and understand it The experiment-specific lab document provides a theoretical model for the physical phenomenon you are measuring. You are not expected to re-derive it from scratch, but you are expected to demonstrate that you understand it well enough to use it. This means: identify the physical principle(s) the model applies, confirm that the dimensions are consistent, name at least one simplifying assumption the model makes and describe the condition under which that assumption would break down. Then show the working equation with your specific experimental values substituted in — this is the prediction your model makes for your particular setup. If you cannot do this, you are not ready to run the analysis in the sections that follow. 4. Measurement record Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each. Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table. 5. Multi-sensor comparison Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold, and the conclusion numerically — not just visually. 6. Uncertainty propagation For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error sources. Show your work. 7. Model comparison Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance criterion. Interpret any discrepancy — is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model form limitations, or both? 8. Experimentally informed model Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be updated or refined. This is not about accepting or rejecting the model — it is about using the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what physical reasoning supports that change. 9. MATLAB figures All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the graph presentation framework from E1 W2. 10. Plain-language summary A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer. This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix. 11. Predict-from-specs (alternative sensor) Using only the spec sheet for an alternative sensor configuration — provided by the instructor in the Week 4 lab session — estimate what the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity would have been if that alternative sensor had been used instead. Propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet parameters, sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and approximate uncertainty bounds, and compare qualitatively to the uncertainty you achieved with your actual sensor suite. This workflow is the conceptual foundation for Phase 2, where you will apply the same predict-from-specs procedure to a complete experiment you have never run. Phase 1 report — format and submission Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections. Format: Markdown or PDF submitted to Canvas. Figures embedded in the document. Submission: See Canvas for due date. Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you have never run — predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit it before you see any real data. What "reverse" means In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it. In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not just of the physical phenomenon. You are essentially reverse-engineering the experiment. Note that in Phase 2 you are predicting the output of a single, specified sensor — the one described in your experiment packet. Phase 1 required you to use two sensors and compare them; Phase 2 is a prediction exercise, and your packet will identify which sensor and configuration to base your analysis on. Use only that sensor's specifications for your uncertainty propagation. This requires you to think about: What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup? What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs? How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity? What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs? Where are the likely failure modes — what could produce data that looks anomalous? A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate whether an experiment is worth running, whether the sensors are capable of resolving the quantity of interest, and whether you will be able to detect anomalies in real time during the test. What you receive for Phase 2 You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment. It contains: The theory primer for the experiment (same document the students who ran it received) The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent) The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments) A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab You do not receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet. Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections 1. Predicted system response What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key features — for example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s over 5 seconds, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions. 2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet. Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result as: [quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units]. 3. Predicted plot sketch Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs. time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included. Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction. 4. Dominant uncertainty sources List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution. Justify the ranking using your propagation results. 5. Acceptance criterion Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of your predicted value, you will consider the prediction confirmed. State this threshold before you see the data. 6. Risk assessment Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data — not because of a faulty experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you would investigate first. Submission: The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release date. Once submitted, your prediction is frozen. You will not be allowed to revise it after data is released. Grading philosophy for Phase 2 A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning and committed uncertainty analysis, scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This is not arbitrary — it is the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing. This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have questions about it, ask before you submit. Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions The pairing You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. This means: You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared. The presentation structure Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately 8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time). Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1) Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment — what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then: what the actual data showed. Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly) The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair. One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make that the other experiment’s model also makes — and where do those assumptions diverge? The most interesting synthesis often lives in that gap. Group of 4 (if applicable) If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly — each pair presents Segments 1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together. This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments. The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section before Week 4. Peer evaluation Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for each presentation. Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations. A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation. Vague evaluations ("good job, maybe add more data") receive no credit. See TIER1_rubric_student.md for the presentation rubric and peer evaluation criteria. Assignment Timeline Summary Phase Activity Format When Pre-lab Hypothesis, AMVF sketch, variable identification Bring to lab Before W2 lab session Phase 1 Run experiment, analyze data Lab sessions W2–W4 Phase 1 Individual report Written, Canvas Due W4 (see Canvas) Phase 2 Receive experiment packet — W4 release date Phase 2 Prediction memo (submitted before data release) Written, Canvas W4 deadline (before data release) Phase 3 Paired presentation (lightning talk) In-person, in class W5 Phase 3 Peer evaluations Written, submitted same day W5 Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 3 Lecture: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics E1 Week 3 Reading: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework E1 Week 4 Lab: Full Phase I Pipeline (this is Phase 1 of the Tier 1 assignment) E1 Week 5 Discussion: Lightning Talks & Peer Assessment E0 Supplemental: Technical Writing Basics and Presentation Best Practices E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice Experiment-specific lab document (provided separately for your assigned experiment) Coleman & Steele, 4th ed. — reference for uncertainty propagation ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment | E1 Weeks 2–5 Evergreen — structure, phases, analytical requirements Update-friendly — experiment names, Canvas due dates, group of 4 logistics, experiment packet contents

chunks.text: E1 Week 5 (Course Week 5) Assessment tier1 assignment # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** 2–5 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 **Structure... ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Weeks: 2–5 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 Structure: Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4) What This Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The

his Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical experiments differ — you will be assigned a specific experiment for each phase — but the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are identical for all students regardless of which experiment they are assigned. Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional, and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive. Why Three Phases? Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1. It is the natural order. It is also a good starting point — but it is only the beginning. A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it

od starting point — but it is only the beginning. A test engineer's job is much more than collecting data and placing it on top of a prediction. Before a test ever runs, a test engineer asks: what should the data look like? They build a model of the experiment itself — accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, the signal chain — and commit to a prediction. That prediction is not decoration. It is used to design the experiment: to determine whether the sensors are capable of resolving the quantity of interest, to anticipate failure modes, and to define in advance what a meaningful result looks like. The ability to watch data come in during a live test and immediately know whether it matches expectation — and to act on that in real time — is one of the most important skills a test

ately know whether it matches expectation — and to act on that in real time — is one of the most important skills a test engineer develops. This course will get there. The full experimental design workflow — where your prediction shapes the design of the test — is the focus of E3. For now, in E1 and E2, you are building the foundational mindset: the habit of pre-committing to a prediction before you see the data. This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model before you saw the data, you cannot dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is real, and you have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one

is real, and you have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 takes the prediction mindset one step further. You ran one experiment. Your partner ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data — an experiment you never ran, using only the setup description and sensor specs. This is deliberate. The goal is not to mimic the full test engineer workflow, but to develop the prediction-first mindset by working through it in the most challenging form: predicting an experiment you have no direct experience with. Think of Phase 3 as reverse-engineering the experiment. You are reconstructing what the data should look like from the physics and the instrument chain alone — without the shortcut of having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset

d the instrument chain alone — without the shortcut of having been in the room when the data was collected. This mindset — building a forward model before you have the answer — is exactly what you will need when you design your own experiment from scratch in E3. The three-phase structure is not the actual workflow of a test engineer. It is a scaffold for developing the mindset of one. Assignment Overview Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual) What you are assigned: One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool. What you do: Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and

improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates). Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind) What you are assigned: A different experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one you did not run in Phase 1. What you do: Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup description for this unfamiliar experiment — predict what the data should look like. You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware. What you produce: A Phase 2 Prediction Memo — submitted before data is released. When: Week 4 (prediction submitted; data released after the due date). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You will be paired with a

due date). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You will be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment — they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. What you do: Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments. What you produce: A combined oral presentation (lightning talk) — you and your partner present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4 presentation. When: Week 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Run your

chedule and sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model. Before the lab session and during the lab session Complete items 1–4 before you arrive; items 5–8 are in-lab steps. Items 1–4 are not graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable — they are required inputs to your Phase 1 report, and students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab. Read the experiment-specific lab document for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It

ment (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document. Write a hypothesis — a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]." Sketch an AMVF diagram for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect — you will refine this after the lab. Identify your independent and dependent variables. Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor. Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods to

control condition and at least one potential confounding factor. Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis. Record your measurements using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound). Use MATLAB for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately —

e whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately — when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence. Phase 1 report — required sections 1. Experimental design Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least one confounding factor identified and addressed. 2. AMVF annotation Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one) to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan. 3. Theory — confirm the model and understand it The experiment-specific lab document provides a theoretical model for the physical phenomenon you are measuring. You are not expected to re-derive it from scratch,

es a theoretical model for the physical phenomenon you are measuring. You are not expected to re-derive it from scratch, but you are expected to demonstrate that you understand it well enough to use it. This means: identify the physical principle(s) the model applies, confirm that the dimensions are consistent, name at least one simplifying assumption the model makes and describe the condition under which that assumption would break down. Then show the working equation with your specific experimental values substituted in — this is the prediction your model makes for your particular setup. If you cannot do this, you are not ready to run the analysis in the sections that follow. 4. Measurement record Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each.

at follow. 4. Measurement record Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each. Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table. 5. Multi-sensor comparison Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold, and the conclusion numerically — not just visually. 6. Uncertainty propagation For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error sources. Show your work. 7. Model comparison Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a named

rk. 7. Model comparison Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance criterion. Interpret any discrepancy — is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model form limitations, or both? 8. Experimentally informed model Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be updated or refined. This is not about accepting or rejecting the model — it is about using the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what physical reasoning supports that change. 9. MATLAB figures All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty bounds where

ATLAB figures All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the graph presentation framework from E1 W2. 10. Plain-language summary A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer. This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix. 11. Predict-from-specs (alternative sensor) Using only the spec sheet for an alternative sensor configuration — provided by the instructor in the Week 4 lab session — estimate what the uncertainty in your primary derived quantity would have been if that alternative sensor had been used instead. Propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet parameters, sketch

been if that alternative sensor had been used instead. Propagate the uncertainty from the spec sheet parameters, sketch the expected output range with labeled axes and approximate uncertainty bounds, and compare qualitatively to the uncertainty you achieved with your actual sensor suite. This workflow is the conceptual foundation for Phase 2, where you will apply the same predict-from-specs procedure to a complete experiment you have never run. Phase 1 report — format and submission Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections. Format: Markdown or PDF submitted to Canvas. Figures embedded in the document. Submission: See Canvas for due date. Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions The

gures embedded in the document. Submission: See Canvas for due date. Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you have never run — predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit it before you see any real data. What "reverse" means In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it. In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not just of the physical phenomenon. You are essentially reverse-engineering the experiment. Note that in Phase 2 you are predicting the output

l phenomenon. You are essentially reverse-engineering the experiment. Note that in Phase 2 you are predicting the output of a single, specified sensor — the one described in your experiment packet. Phase 1 required you to use two sensors and compare them; Phase 2 is a prediction exercise, and your packet will identify which sensor and configuration to base your analysis on. Use only that sensor's specifications for your uncertainty propagation. This requires you to think about: What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup? What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs? How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity? What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given

propagate to the derived quantity? What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs? Where are the likely failure modes — what could produce data that looks anomalous? A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate whether an experiment is worth running, whether the sensors are capable of resolving the quantity of interest, and whether you will be able to detect anomalies in real time during the test. What you receive for Phase 2 You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment. It contains: The theory primer for the experiment (same document the students who ran it received) The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent) The DAQ configuration (sample rate,

o ran it received) The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent) The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments) A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab You do not receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet. Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections 1. Predicted system response What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key features — for example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s over 5 seconds, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7

d angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s over 5 seconds, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions. 2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet. Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result as: [quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units]. 3. Predicted plot sketch Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs. time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included. Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction. 4.

to look like, with uncertainty bounds included. Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction. 4. Dominant uncertainty sources List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution. Justify the ranking using your propagation results. 5. Acceptance criterion Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of your predicted value, you will consider the prediction confirmed. State this threshold before you see the data. 6. Risk assessment Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data — not because of a faulty experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you would investigate first. Submission: The

case in the physics. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you would investigate first. Submission: The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release date. Once submitted, your prediction is frozen. You will not be allowed to revise it after data is released. Grading philosophy for Phase 2 A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning and committed uncertainty analysis, scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This is not arbitrary — it is the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy. An

al professional standard. An engineer who commits to a wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing. This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have questions about it, ask before you submit. Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions The pairing You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. This means: You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared.

ction for Experiment B Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared. The presentation structure Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately 8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time). Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1) Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment — what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then: what the

hase 2 prediction for the other's experiment — what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then: what the actual data showed. Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly) The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair. One useful starting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make that the other experiment’s model also makes

ting point for Segment 3: what assumption does your experiment’s model make that the other experiment’s model also makes — and where do those assumptions diverge? The most interesting synthesis often lives in that gap. Group of 4 (if applicable) If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly — each pair presents Segments 1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together. This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments. The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section before Week 4. Peer evaluation Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for

ed for your section before Week 4. Peer evaluation Every student in the audience completes a written peer evaluation for each presentation. Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations. A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation. Vague evaluations ("good job, maybe add more data") receive no credit. See TIER1_rubric_student.md for the presentation rubric and peer evaluation criteria. pages.title: L01
pages.text: E2 Week 1 (Course Week 6) Lecture L01 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement Talking Point: Welcome to Phase II. Everything we did in E1 — measuring, propagating uncertainty, comparing to a model — was preparation for the disc... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement Talking Point: Welcome to Phase II. Everything we did in E1 — measuring, propagating uncertainty, comparing to a model — was preparation for the discipline we are about to practice: prediction before exposure. In Phase I you were reactive analysts. In Phase II you become disciplined modelers, and the single most important habit of a disciplined modeler is this — you commit to what you expect before you see what happened. That is what this lecture is about, and it is the governing principle of everything we do in Weeks 6 through 10. slide 2: Two Scenarios, Same Data (Image: A split panel illustration — left side labeled "Scenario A: Postdiction" shows a scatter plot with a smooth curve clearly fitted through the data points, with an annotation reading "model adjusted after data collected." Right side labeled "Scenario B: Prediction" shows the same scatter plot with an identical-looking curve, but the curve was drawn before the data points were revealed, annotated "model committed before data released." Both panels look identical at a glance, but only Scenario B has an arrow pointing to a lock icon labeled "Pre-commitment document." Background tone is dark with gold text to match course theme.) Talking Point: I want you to look at these two panels and tell me which one represents valid engineering. At first glance, they look identical — the model matches the data in both cases. But there is a fundamental difference. In Scenario A, someone built a model after they already saw the data, and they adjusted the parameters until the curve fit well. In Scenario B, someone committed to the model before the data was released, and the match — or mismatch — tells us something true about how well the model captures reality. These two scenarios are structurally indistinguishable without documentation. That is why the pre-commitment document is not paperwork — it is the evidence that separates validation from curve fitting. slide 3: Opening Consolidation — What You Should Have Solid Before Week 7 (Image: A two-node diagram on a plain background. Node 1 is labeled "Transducer & Sensor Principles" with a small sensor-to-signal arrow icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W1 L2" tag. Node 2 is labeled "Statistical Properties & Distributions" with a small bell curve icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W2 L1" tag. Both nodes have a dashed orange border labeled "W7 will build on these." The visual emphasizes that these are foundation nodes — not new material, but material that will be load-bearing in W7.) Talking Point: Before we go any further into the new E2 content, I need to do a quick calibration check with you — not for a grade, but because two things from E1 are going to be load-bearing in Week 7, and I want to make sure they are solid. Here is the first: transducers and sensor principles. You were introduced to the idea in Week 1 Lecture 2 that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally. Week 7 builds the entire signal conditioning chain — noise, filtering, DAQ — directly on top of that foundation. Quick check for yourself: what does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity, and can you name an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error? If you are shaky on that, revisit Week 1 before next week. Here is the second: statistical distributions. You have been applying uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo since Week 2, but we are going to extend those tools to multi-variable systems in Week 9, and the interpretation demands go up significantly. Ask yourself: why does the uncertainty in a mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason? If you cannot articulate that, go back to Week 2 Lecture 1 and the Coleman and Steele reference on Canvas — it has a clear treatment of standard error and sampling distributions. I am not checking these now for a grade — I am giving you a signal about where to put your attention before Week 7. slide 4: The Cognitive Bias That Makes Postdiction Feel Like Validation (Image: A brain-shaped diagram divided into two halves. The left half is labeled "Before Data" and contains a simple idealized sine wave with the label "Mental model: clean, complete." The right half is labeled "After Data" and shows the same sine wave overlaid with noisy scatter data — but with a note bubble pointing to the brain saying "I knew it all along." Below the diagram is a short axis arrow labeled "Time →" with a lock icon at the "Before Data" end and an X-circle icon at the "After Data" end, illustrating that the window for honest prediction closes once data is seen. The tone is conceptual and slightly cautionary — this is a cognitive trap.) Talking Point: There is a well-documented cognitive bias that makes postdiction feel exactly like genuine prediction — it is called hindsight bias, and it affects trained engineers just as much as anyone else. Once you have seen the data, you cannot un-see it. Your brain retroactively adjusts your memory of what you expected, so the model you build after the fact feels like the model you would have built before. The insidious thing is that this happens without conscious intent — you are not trying to deceive anyone, including yourself. The result is a model that fits the data you have but may fail completely on data you have not collected yet. Postdiction produces models that describe observations rather than predict behavior, and in aerospace engineering, the difference between those two things has consequences. The pre-commitment document is the structural solution: it creates a record that cannot be retroactively adjusted. slide 5: The AMVF Runs Left to Right — And That Direction Is Not Arbitrary (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram reproduced in the course color scheme — gold nodes on dark background. The flowchart shows the mathematical modeling branch on the left and the physical modeling branch on the right, with the model node at top-left, the experimental data node at bottom-right, and validation as the comparison step in the center. A bold left-to-right arrow runs across the top of the diagram labeled "Model development logically precedes data collection." A second arrow in red, pointing right-to-left, is labeled with an X: "Postdiction: building the model after seeing data." The physical modeling branch nodes are highlighted to show they are the focus of E2.) Talking Point: You saw this diagram in Week 1 Lecture 1, and I told you then that the left-to-right flow is not arbitrary. Now I want to make that concrete. The AMVF runs from model development on the left to experimental data on the right because that is the logical sequence: you build a model, you make a prediction based on it, and then you measure to test whether the prediction holds. If you run the flowchart backwards — if you collect data first and build a model to match it — you are not using experimental data to validate a model. You are using experimental data to construct a model, and those are fundamentally different activities. In E2, we are going to hold ourselves to the forward direction, every time. Your prediction document is your evidence that you ran the flowchart in the right direction. slide 6: What You Felt in Tier 1 Phase 2 — Now Formalized as a Requirement (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two documents. On the left: a hand-written or typed one-page "Prediction Memo" with a timestamp in the upper right corner labeled "Submitted before data release." The memo has visible sections labeled "Expected Response," "Estimated Uncertainty," and "Acceptance Criterion." On the right: the same experiment's data plotted in MATLAB with the predicted curve overlaid — the prediction does not perfectly match the data, but is within the uncertainty band. A bracket connects the two documents with the label "Pre-commitment preserved: this is what makes the comparison meaningful." Warm gold accent color on the connecting bracket.) Talking Point: Many of you remember what it felt like in Week 5 when you had to commit to a prediction for an experiment you did not run, before seeing any data. Some of you found it uncomfortable — you wanted to see the data first and then decide what to say about it. That discomfort is exactly the right signal. What you felt is the gap between knowing something and predicting something, and in Phase II that gap is what we are asking you to cross every time, for every experiment. The Tier 1 Phase 2 prediction memo was practice. The Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document is the real thing, and it is a gate — not a suggestion. You do not get equipment access until your prediction is documented and reviewed. The quality of your reasoning in that document is what you are being evaluated on, not whether your prediction turned out to be correct. slide 7: The Test Engineer Identity — Why This Competency Matters Professionally (Image: A horizontal timeline illustration showing three career stages. Stage 1 (left) shows a new hire engineer reading a lab notebook and a legacy instrument schematic labeled "Understand what was done." Stage 2 (center) shows the same engineer with a laptop and a red-pencil annotation on a graph labeled "Identify limitations." Stage 3 (right) shows the engineer at a whiteboard with a design schematic and a sensor labeled "Propose improvements using current technology." An EAC logo badge is attached to Stage 2 and 3, labeled "EAC-identified priority competency." The background has a faint aerospace laboratory setting — wind tunnel, test stand — sketched in light gray.) Talking Point: The Engineering Advisory Council — the external board of working aerospace engineers who give us feedback on this curriculum — has told us explicitly that what E2 is teaching is exactly what new hires are expected to do when they join an organization. You walk into a legacy lab or an existing test program. Someone hands you documentation for an experiment that was designed ten years ago. Your job is to understand what was done and why, identify the limitations of that approach, and propose improvements using better sensors, better analysis methods, or better understanding. That is precisely what the Tier 2 canned experiments are structured to give you practice with. These are not academic exercises where you run a script and fill in blanks. They are recognizable test engineer work — and the skill of walking up to someone else's experimental infrastructure and knowing how to evaluate it is one that gets you hired and keeps you employed. slide 8: Closure This lecture opened E2 with the discipline that defines Phase II: prediction must precede measurement, and that sequence must be documented. Postdiction feels like validation because hindsight bias is invisible from the inside — but it is structurally indistinguishable from curve fitting and produces models that describe the past rather than predict the future. The AMVF flowchart runs left to right because model development is logically prior to data collection, and your prediction document is the evidence that you followed that sequence. In Lecture 2 this week, we will move from the epistemics of why prediction matters to the mechanics of how to build a defensible one: the five-element anatomy of the prediction document, the formal vocabulary for system response features and acceptance criteria, and the gate procedure before equipment access. Come ready to produce the real thing. Agent Notes Opening consolidation beat (slides 1–3) covers the two foundational E1 nodes (transducer principles, statistical distributions) that W7 will depend on. This is per the W6 L1 blueprint purpose statement, which specifies 10–15 min for this beat. EAC motivational framing and test engineer identity are incorporated in slides 6 and 7. AMVF continuity enforced in slides 1, 2, and 5 — the flowchart is explicitly revisited, not just mentioned. Slides 8–10 from the prior version (five-element checklist preview, experimentally informed model, gate logistics) have been moved to L2, per the V&V consolidation restructure approved 2026-05-28. L1 now covers four coherent moves: opening consolidation beat → postdiction as failure mode → AMVF direction → EAC professional identity. The experimentally informed model concept is introduced in L2 as part of the prediction document vocabulary, where it has stronger contextual support. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Why Prediction Must Precede Measurement Talking Point: Welcome to Phase II. Everything we did in E1 — measuring, propagating uncertainty, comparing to a model — was preparation for the discipline we are about to practice: prediction before exposure. In Phase I you were reactive analysts. In Phase II you become disciplined modelers, and the single most important habit of a disciplined modeler is this — you commit to what you expect before you see what happened. That is what this lecture is about, and it is the governing principle of everything we do in Weeks 6 through 10. slide 2: Two Scenarios, Same Data (Image: A split panel illustration — left side labeled "Scenario A: Postdiction" shows a scatter plot with a smooth curve clearly fitted through the data points, with an annotation reading "model adjusted after data collected." Right side labeled "Scenario B: Prediction" shows the same scatter plot with an identical-looking curve, but the curve was drawn before the data points were revealed, annotated "model committed before data released." Both panels look identical at a glance, but only Scenario B has an arrow pointing to a lock icon labeled "Pre-commitment document." Background tone is dark with gold text to match course theme.) Talking Point: I want you to look at these two panels and tell me which one represents valid engineering. At first glance, they look identical — the model matches the data in both cases. But there is a fundamental difference. In Scenario A, someone built a model after they already saw the data, and they adjusted the parameters until the curve fit well. In Scenario B, someone committed to the model before the data was released, and the match — or mismatch — tells us something true about how well the model captures reality. These two scenarios are structurally indistinguishable without documentation. That is why the pre-commitment document is not paperwork — it is the evidence that separates validation from curve fitting. slide 3: Opening Consolidation — What You Should Have Solid Before Week 7 (Image: A two-node diagram on a plain background. Node 1 is labeled "Transducer & Sensor Principles" with a small sensor-to-signal arrow icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W1 L2" tag. Node 2 is labeled "Statistical Properties & Distributions" with a small bell curve icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W2 L1" tag. Both nodes have a dashed orange border labeled "W7 will build on these." The visual emphasizes that these are foundation nodes — not new material, but material that will be load-bearing in W7.) Talking Point: Before we go any further into the new E2 content, I need to do a quick calibration check with you — not for a grade, but because two things from E1 are going to be load-bearing in Week 7, and I want to make sure they are solid. Here is the first: transducers and sensor principles. You were introduced to the idea in Week 1 Lecture 2 that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally. Week 7 builds the entire signal conditioning chain — noise, filtering, DAQ — directly on top of that foundation. Quick check for yourself: what does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity, and can you name an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error? If you are shaky on that, revisit Week 1 before next week. Here is the second: statistical distributions. You have been applying uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo since Week 2, but we are going to extend those tools to multi-variable systems in Week 9, and the interpretation demands go up significantly. Ask yourself: why does the uncertainty in a mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason? If you cannot articulate that, go back to Week 2 Lecture 1 and the Coleman and Steele reference on Canvas — it has a clear treatment of standard error and sampling distributions. I am not checking these now for a grade — I am giving you a signal about where to put your attention before Week 7. slide 4: The Cognitive Bias That Makes Postdiction Feel Like Validation (Image: A brain-shaped diagram divided into two halves. A bold left-to-right arrow runs across the top of the diagram labeled "Model development logically precedes data collection." A second arrow in red, pointing right-to-left, is labeled with an X: "Postdiction: building the model after seeing data." The physical modeling branch nodes are highlighted to show they are the focus of E2.)
