In Phase I you were reactive analysts. In Phase II you become disciplined modelers, and the single most important habit of a disciplined modeler is this — you commit to what you expect before you see what happened. That is what this lecture is about, and it is the governing principle of everything we do in Weeks 6 through 10. slide 2: Two Scenarios, Same Data (Image: A split panel illustration — left side labeled "Scenario A: Postdiction" shows a scatter plot with a smooth curve clearly fitted through the data points, with an annotation reading "model adjusted after data collected." Right side labeled "Scenario B:

rough the data points, with an annotation reading "model adjusted after data collected." Right side labeled "Scenario B: Prediction" shows the same scatter plot with an identical-looking curve, but the curve was drawn before the data points were revealed, annotated "model committed before data released." Both panels look identical at a glance, but only Scenario B has an arrow pointing to a lock icon labeled "Pre-commitment document." Background tone is dark with gold text to match course theme.) Talking Point: I want you to look at these two panels and tell me which one represents valid engineering. At first glance, they look identical — the model matches the data in both cases. But there is a fundamental difference. In Scenario A, someone built a model after they already saw the data,

both cases. But there is a fundamental difference. In Scenario A, someone built a model after they already saw the data, and they adjusted the parameters until the curve fit well. In Scenario B, someone committed to the model before the data was released, and the match — or mismatch — tells us something true about how well the model captures reality. These two scenarios are structurally indistinguishable without documentation. That is why the pre-commitment document is not paperwork — it is the evidence that separates validation from curve fitting. slide 3: Opening Consolidation — What You Should Have Solid Before Week 7 (Image: A two-node diagram on a plain background. Node 1 is labeled "Transducer & Sensor Principles" with a small sensor-to-signal arrow icon beneath it and a yellow

und. Node 1 is labeled "Transducer & Sensor Principles" with a small sensor-to-signal arrow icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W1 L2" tag. Node 2 is labeled "Statistical Properties & Distributions" with a small bell curve icon beneath it and a yellow "Last activated: W2 L1" tag. Both nodes have a dashed orange border labeled "W7 will build on these." The visual emphasizes that these are foundation nodes — not new material, but material that will be load-bearing in W7.) Talking Point: Before we go any further into the new E2 content, I need to do a quick calibration check with you — not for a grade, but because two things from E1 are going to be load-bearing in Week 7, and I want to make sure they are solid. Here is the first: transducers and sensor principles. You were

earing in Week 7, and I want to make sure they are solid. Here is the first: transducers and sensor principles. You were introduced to the idea in Week 1 Lecture 2 that sensors transduce physical quantities and that no instrument behaves ideally. Week 7 builds the entire signal conditioning chain — noise, filtering, DAQ — directly on top of that foundation. Quick check for yourself: what does it mean for a sensor to transduce a physical quantity, and can you name an example where the transduction mechanism itself introduces error? If you are shaky on that, revisit Week 1 before next week. Here is the second: statistical distributions. You have been applying uncertainty propagation and Monte Carlo since Week 2, but we are going to extend those tools to multi-variable systems in Week 9, and

ropagation and Monte Carlo since Week 2, but we are going to extend those tools to multi-variable systems in Week 9, and the interpretation demands go up significantly. Ask yourself: why does the uncertainty in a mean decrease with more trials? What is the underlying statistical reason? If you cannot articulate that, go back to Week 2 Lecture 1 and the Coleman and Steele reference on Canvas — it has a clear treatment of standard error and sampling distributions. I am not checking these now for a grade — I am giving you a signal about where to put your attention before Week 7. slide 4: The Cognitive Bias That Makes Postdiction Feel Like Validation (Image: A brain-shaped diagram divided into two halves. The left half is labeled "Before Data" and contains a simple idealized sine wave with

d diagram divided into two halves. The left half is labeled "Before Data" and contains a simple idealized sine wave with the label "Mental model: clean, complete." The right half is labeled "After Data" and shows the same sine wave overlaid with noisy scatter data — but with a note bubble pointing to the brain saying "I knew it all along." Below the diagram is a short axis arrow labeled "Time →" with a lock icon at the "Before Data" end and an X-circle icon at the "After Data" end, illustrating that the window for honest prediction closes once data is seen. The tone is conceptual and slightly cautionary — this is a cognitive trap.) Talking Point: There is a well-documented cognitive bias that makes postdiction feel exactly like genuine prediction — it is called hindsight bias, and it

umented cognitive bias that makes postdiction feel exactly like genuine prediction — it is called hindsight bias, and it affects trained engineers just as much as anyone else. Once you have seen the data, you cannot un-see it. Your brain retroactively adjusts your memory of what you expected, so the model you build after the fact feels like the model you would have built before. The insidious thing is that this happens without conscious intent — you are not trying to deceive anyone, including yourself. The result is a model that fits the data you have but may fail completely on data you have not collected yet. Postdiction produces models that describe observations rather than predict behavior, and in aerospace engineering, the difference between those two things has consequences. The

ather than predict behavior, and in aerospace engineering, the difference between those two things has consequences. The pre-commitment document is the structural solution: it creates a record that cannot be retroactively adjusted. slide 5: The AMVF Runs Left to Right — And That Direction Is Not Arbitrary (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram reproduced in the course color scheme — gold nodes on dark background. The flowchart shows the mathematical modeling branch on the left and the physical modeling branch on the right, with the model node at top-left, the experimental data node at bottom-right, and validation as the comparison step in the center. A bold left-to-right arrow runs across the top of the diagram labeled "Model development logically precedes data collection." A second arrow in

row runs across the top of the diagram labeled "Model development logically precedes data collection." A second arrow in red, pointing right-to-left, is labeled with an X: "Postdiction: building the model after seeing data." The physical modeling branch nodes are highlighted to show they are the focus of E2.) Talking Point: You saw this diagram in Week 1 Lecture 1, and I told you then that the left-to-right flow is not arbitrary. Now I want to make that concrete. The AMVF runs from model development on the left to experimental data on the right because that is the logical sequence: you build a model, you make a prediction based on it, and then you measure to test whether the prediction holds. If you run the flowchart backwards — if you collect data first and build a model to match it —

her the prediction holds. If you run the flowchart backwards — if you collect data first and build a model to match it — you are not using experimental data to validate a model. You are using experimental data to construct a model, and those are fundamentally different activities. In E2, we are going to hold ourselves to the forward direction, every time. Your prediction document is your evidence that you ran the flowchart in the right direction. slide 6: What You Felt in Tier 1 Phase 2 — Now Formalized as a Requirement (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two documents. On the left: a hand-written or typed one-page "Prediction Memo" with a timestamp in the upper right corner labeled "Submitted before data release." The memo has visible sections labeled "Expected Response," "Estimated

ht corner labeled "Submitted before data release." The memo has visible sections labeled "Expected Response," "Estimated Uncertainty," and "Acceptance Criterion." On the right: the same experiment's data plotted in MATLAB with the predicted curve overlaid — the prediction does not perfectly match the data, but is within the uncertainty band. A bracket connects the two documents with the label "Pre-commitment preserved: this is what makes the comparison meaningful." Warm gold accent color on the connecting bracket.) Talking Point: Many of you remember what it felt like in Week 5 when you had to commit to a prediction for an experiment you did not run, before seeing any data. Some of you found it uncomfortable — you wanted to see the data first and then decide what to say about it. That

y data. Some of you found it uncomfortable — you wanted to see the data first and then decide what to say about it. That discomfort is exactly the right signal. What you felt is the gap between knowing something and predicting something, and in Phase II that gap is what we are asking you to cross every time, for every experiment. The Tier 1 Phase 2 prediction memo was practice. The Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document is the real thing, and it is a gate — not a suggestion. You do not get equipment access until your prediction is documented and reviewed. The quality of your reasoning in that document is what you are being evaluated on, not whether your prediction turned out to be correct. slide 7: The Test Engineer Identity — Why This Competency Matters Professionally (Image: A horizontal

out to be correct. slide 7: The Test Engineer Identity — Why This Competency Matters Professionally (Image: A horizontal timeline illustration showing three career stages. Stage 1 (left) shows a new hire engineer reading a lab notebook and a legacy instrument schematic labeled "Understand what was done." Stage 2 (center) shows the same engineer with a laptop and a red-pencil annotation on a graph labeled "Identify limitations." Stage 3 (right) shows the engineer at a whiteboard with a design schematic and a sensor labeled "Propose improvements using current technology." An EAC logo badge is attached to Stage 2 and 3, labeled "EAC-identified priority competency." The background has a faint aerospace laboratory setting — wind tunnel, test stand — sketched in light gray.) Talking Point: The

kground has a faint aerospace laboratory setting — wind tunnel, test stand — sketched in light gray.) Talking Point: The Engineering Advisory Council — the external board of working aerospace engineers who give us feedback on this curriculum — has told us explicitly that what E2 is teaching is exactly what new hires are expected to do when they join an organization. You walk into a legacy lab or an existing test program. Someone hands you documentation for an experiment that was designed ten years ago. Your job is to understand what was done and why, identify the limitations of that approach, and propose improvements using better sensors, better analysis methods, or better understanding. That is precisely what the Tier 2 canned experiments are structured to give you practice with. These

tter understanding. That is precisely what the Tier 2 canned experiments are structured to give you practice with. These are not academic exercises where you run a script and fill in blanks. They are recognizable test engineer work — and the skill of walking up to someone else's experimental infrastructure and knowing how to evaluate it is one that gets you hired and keeps you employed. slide 8: Closure This lecture opened E2 with the discipline that defines Phase II: prediction must precede measurement, and that sequence must be documented. Postdiction feels like validation because hindsight bias is invisible from the inside — but it is structurally indistinguishable from curve fitting and produces models that describe the past rather than predict the future. The AMVF flowchart runs left

om curve fitting and produces models that describe the past rather than predict the future. The AMVF flowchart runs left to right because model development is logically prior to data collection, and your prediction document is the evidence that you followed that sequence. In Lecture 2 this week, we will move from the epistemics of why prediction matters to the mechanics of how to build a defensible one: the five-element anatomy of the prediction document, the formal vocabulary for system response features and acceptance criteria, and the gate procedure before equipment access. Come ready to produce the real thing. ## Agent Notes - Opening consolidation beat (slides 1–3) covers the two foundational E1 nodes (transducer principles, statistical distributions) that W7 will depend on. This is

covers the two foundational E1 nodes (transducer principles, statistical distributions) that W7 will depend on. This is per the W6 L1 blueprint purpose statement, which specifies 10–15 min for this beat. - EAC motivational framing and test engineer identity are incorporated in slides 6 and 7. - AMVF continuity enforced in slides 1, 2, and 5 — the flowchart is explicitly revisited, not just mentioned. - Slides 8–10 from the prior version (five-element checklist preview, experimentally informed model, gate logistics) have been moved to L2, per the V&V consolidation restructure approved 2026-05-28. L1 now covers four coherent moves: opening consolidation beat → postdiction as failure mode → AMVF direction → EAC professional identity. - The experimentally informed model concept is introduced

as failure mode → AMVF direction → EAC professional identity. - The experimentally informed model concept is introduced in L2 as part of the prediction document vocabulary, where it has stronger contextual support.

pages.title: L02
pages.text: E2 Week 1 (Course Week 6) Lecture L02 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Formal Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems Talking Point: In Week 1, you wrote a hypothesis for a simple canned experiment — one primary output, a clean physical model, and a straightfor... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Formal Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems Talking Point: In Week 1, you wrote a hypothesis for a simple canned experiment — one primary output, a clean physical model, and a straightforward comparison. This lecture extends that vocabulary to the systems you are going to work with in E2: more parameters, more potential failure modes, and less obvious predictions. The formal hypothesis framing tools are the same ones you already know, but the complexity of applying them has gone up, and the consequences of being vague — vague system response features, vague acceptance criteria — are more visible when the system is more complex. By the end of this lecture, you will have the full vocabulary for committing to a prediction that is worth something. slide 2: Revisiting the AMVF — The Experiment Is Not the End (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram with the mathematical modeling branch on the left side and the physical modeling branch on the right, rendered in the course color scheme. A bold annotation box with a gold border is placed at the top of the diagram, reading: "The experiment is not an end in itself — it is a test of a model." An arrow runs from the "Model" node at top-left down and across to the "Experimental Data" and "Validation" nodes, with the label "The question: does reality behave as the model predicts?" A second annotation at the bottom of the diagram reads: "What does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter?" The diagram is clean and uncluttered — two annotations maximum.) Talking Point: Before we get into the vocabulary of formal hypothesis framing, I want to anchor this lecture in the AMVF. The experiment you are running in E2 is not an end in itself — it is a test of a model. The question you should be asking about every E2 lab activity is: what does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter? This framing connects everything we do in this course to the broader curriculum and to your senior design projects. In ASEN 3501, experimental methods is the physical modeling branch — it is the part of the framework that bridges mathematical models and the real world. When you develop a predictive model, run an experiment, and compare the outcomes, you are contributing to the credibility of an engineering claim. Keeping that purpose in mind is what separates disciplined modeling from going through the motions. slide 3: What "Intended Use" Means and Why It Is the First Constraint (Image: A three-panel illustration showing the same physical system — a vibrating beam — at three different intended uses. Panel 1: "Intended use: predict resonant frequency within ±5% for mission planning." Panel 2: "Intended use: predict peak deflection under a specific load for structural margin." Panel 3: "Intended use: characterize damping ratio for control system design." Each panel shows a different set of highlighted model parameters and measurement requirements. Below the three panels, a single line reads: "The model that is adequate for one intended use may be completely inadequate for another." The panels are arranged horizontally, and each has a distinct colored border to help students visually separate the three cases.) Talking Point: Here is the first and most important framing constraint on any predictive model: intended use. A model is not validated in the abstract — it is validated for a purpose. The same physical system can require three entirely different models depending on what you are trying to predict and how accurately you need to predict it. If your intended use is predicting a resonant frequency to within five percent, a simple Euler-Bernoulli beam model with a mass correction might be perfectly adequate. If your intended use is predicting the damping ratio for a control system, you need a model that includes energy dissipation mechanisms that the simple beam model ignores entirely. The intended use statement drives every downstream modeling decision: what parameters need to be measured, how precisely, under what conditions, and what level of discrepancy counts as acceptable agreement. It is the first sentence of a defensible validation plan, and it must be written before any modeling decisions are made. slide 4: System Response Features — What You Are Actually Committing To (Image: A two-column layout. Left column header: "Vague system response feature (not acceptable)." Below it: three examples in red italic text — "The output will increase," "There will be oscillation," "The temperature will change over time." Right column header: "Committed system response feature (required)." Below it: three examples in green regular text — "Peak amplitude will reach 8.3 mm ± 0.4 mm by t = 0.5 s," "Oscillation frequency will be 47 Hz ± 2 Hz at the primary resonance mode," "Temperature will reach steady state within 90 seconds at a value of 52°C ± 3°C." At the bottom of the slide, a horizontal divider, then a caption: "A system response feature is falsifiable only if it specifies what would count as disagreement — meaning there exists a specific measurable outcome that would count as disagreement, not just one that would count as confirmation." The visual contrast between the red vague examples and the green specific examples is the central visual message.) Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific, falsifiable claim about what your model predicts the experiment will produce. Let me be direct about what "specific" means: it means a numerical value, a range, a time, a frequency — something that can be compared to a number. "The output will increase" is not a system response feature — it is an observation. "The output will reach a peak amplitude of 8.3 millimeters at approximately 0.5 seconds" is a system response feature — I can put data on the same axes and tell you whether the prediction held. The reason specificity matters is that a vague prediction can never be falsified, which means it can never teach you anything. If you said "the output will go up" and the output goes up, you have learned nothing. If you said "the output will reach 8.3 millimeters" and the output reaches 6.1, you have learned that your model is overestimating amplitude by 26%, and now you have a specific question: which model assumption is responsible for that gap? slide 5: Acceptable Agreement — What Does "Close Enough" Mean, and Who Decides? (Image: A single graph with a model prediction line shown as a solid gold curve and experimental data scatter plotted around it. The graph shows a clear acceptance band — a shaded region around the prediction line, labeled "Acceptance criterion: agreement within ±10% relative to predicted peak, or within uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger." Below the shaded region, two data points fall outside the band with arrows labeled "These points require explanation — not dismissal." The acceptance band is drawn with a dashed border to indicate it was defined before data collection. A note in the lower right corner reads: "The acceptance criterion must be stated before data is collected. It cannot be adjusted after." The graph style matches MATLAB-generated figures.) Talking Point: The acceptance criterion is the pre-stated answer to the question: how close does the data have to be to my prediction for me to say the model is adequate for the intended use? This is not a judgment you make after you see the results — it is a commitment you make before you collect any data. And it has to be numerical. "The model is good enough" is not an acceptance criterion. "Agreement within plus-or-minus ten percent relative to the predicted peak value, or within the propagated uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger" — that is an acceptance criterion. We use the larger criterion because claiming a meaningful disagreement when the difference is smaller than your measurement noise would be misleading — your uncertainty floor sets the minimum distinction you can honestly claim. Setting the acceptance criterion before data collection is what keeps you honest when the data comes back and you are tempted to judge whether it "looks good." slide 6: More Parameters, More Failure Modes — Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems (Image: A comparison between a simple system and a complex system. Left side: "E1 system — pendulum" with a single-equation model and two parameters (length, gravitational acceleration). Two labeled failure modes: "Amplitude too large (nonlinear)," "Air resistance ignored." Right side: "E2 system — wind tunnel / vibration rig" with a multi-parameter block diagram showing three input parameters, two environmental factors, and four potential model failure modes. The failure modes on the right are: "Boundary condition assumptions," "Unmodeled damping," "Nonlinear stiffness beyond small deflection," "Aerodynamic coupling effects." A bracket spans the right side labeled "Each failure mode corresponds to a specific anomalous data pattern." The comparison illustrates escalation of complexity without making the task seem impossible.) Talking Point: The core challenge of hypothesis framing in E2 is that the systems are more complex — more parameters, more coupling between effects, and more ways for the model to be wrong in a structured and physically meaningful way. In E1, you had a pendulum with one primary model parameter, and the dominant failure mode was clear — once the amplitude gets large, the small-angle assumption breaks. In E2, you might be working with a vibrating structure where boundary conditions, nonlinear stiffness, aerodynamic coupling, and damping mechanisms can all produce model-data discrepancy independently or together. The good news is that the vocabulary is exactly the same: you specify system response features, you commit to an acceptance criterion, and you name the anomalous patterns that would indicate each type of model failure. The discipline is identical — you are just applying it to a harder problem, and the payoff for doing it correctly is higher. slide 7: The Risk Assessment — Naming What Wrong Looks Like in Advance (Image: A three-row table illustration. Column headers: "Anomalous pattern," "Physical cause," "Implication for model." Row 1: "Residuals increase systematically with amplitude — systematic trend" | "Nonlinear stiffness — model assumes small deflection" | "Model-form error: linear model inadequate above this amplitude range." Row 2: "Measurements oscillate at a frequency not in the model" | "Unmodeled structural mode" | "Model-form error: additional degree of freedom required." Row 3: "Bias offset between prediction and data at all conditions" | "Parametric error — wrong stiffness value used" | "Parametric uncertainty: model form may be correct but input is wrong." Each row is color-coded: systematic trend in orange, unmodeled mode in red, parametric bias in yellow. The table is clear and readable — not crowded. Note: "model-form error" is a term we will formally define in W8. For now, think of it as: the model's structure is missing a physical effect — not just a wrong parameter value, but a wrong equation. It stems from flaws in the model's fundamental formulation.) Talking Point: The risk assessment section of your prediction document asks you to name, in advance, at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. This is not pessimism — it is predictive discipline. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict exactly what the data would look like if one of those assumptions breaks down. A linear spring model will produce residuals that increase systematically with amplitude if nonlinear stiffness is present — because the model cannot generate amplitude-dependent behavior. An unmodeled structural mode will produce oscillations at a frequency that is not in your prediction. A wrong parameter value will produce a bias offset that is consistent across all conditions. Each of these patterns is specific and distinguishable from random measurement noise, and the tool for distinguishing them is the uncertainty band you already computed. If a data pattern is inside the uncertainty band, it is consistent with noise. If it shows systematic structure that your model cannot produce, it is telling you something about the model. slide 8: What "Prediction Before Exposure" Actually Requires — The Five-Element Checklist (Image: A five-element checklist diagram — each element in its own horizontal band, numbered 1 through 5. Element 1: "System response features — specific values or ranges, shape of expected output." Element 2: "Model derivation — equations with substituted numbers, not just variable names." Element 3: "Estimated uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty." Element 4: "Acceptance criterion — numerical threshold committed before data collection." Element 5: "Risk assessment — at least one anomalous pattern named that would indicate model-form limitation rather than noise." Each band has a small checkmark icon at the left in gold. The overall layout is clean and structured — this is the checklist students will carry into the Tier 2 pre-lab session.) Talking Point: When I say prediction before exposure, I do not mean a vague directional statement — "I think the output will go up." A defensible prediction has five specific elements, and I want you to see them now because they are the required components of your Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document. First: system response features — what specific physical quantities do you expect, at what magnitudes, and in what shape over time or across the independent variable? Second: a model derivation — show the equations with numbers substituted, not just the symbolic form. Third: an estimated uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specs you have been given and express the result as estimate plus or minus uncertainty. Fourth: an acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold that defines what "agreement" means, stated before you collect any data. Fifth: a risk assessment — name at least one anomalous data pattern that would tell you the model form is wrong rather than the measurements being noisy. Each of these elements has a specific purpose, and we cover the reasoning behind each one in the slides that follow. slide 9: The Anatomy of a Tier 2 Prediction Document (Image: A visual breakdown of the prediction document structure, laid out as a one-page annotated mockup. The document is divided into five clearly labeled sections, each with a brief description: Section 1 "System Response Features — specific values, ranges, time-series shape committed in writing" | Section 2 "Model Derivation — equation with substituted numbers, intermediate steps shown" | Section 3 "Estimated Uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty" | Section 4 "Acceptance Criterion — numerical threshold stated before data collection" | Section 5 "Risk Assessment — at least one anomalous pattern with physical cause named." Each section has a word count indicator: "2–4 sentences each" or "one equation with substituted values." The document mockup has a visible header: "Group names, Date, Experiment: [Canned Experiment 1]" and a TA signature line at the bottom. The visual conveys that this is a real, formal document — not a worksheet.) Talking Point: Let me make this concrete. The Tier 2 prediction document has five required sections, and here is what each one looks like in practice. The first section is your system response features — you commit in writing to what specific physical quantities you expect, with magnitudes, units, and expected trend shape. Not "the output will increase" — "the peak deflection will reach this value at this point in the test, under this loading condition." The second section is your model derivation — you show the governing equations with numbers substituted in, not just symbolic expressions. The third section is your estimated uncertainty — you apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specifications you have been given and express the result in correct engineering form: estimate plus or minus uncertainty with units and method stated. The fourth section is your acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold. And the fifth section is your risk assessment. That document is shown to the TA or instructor before you are cleared for equipment access, and it is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. Write it as if your professional credibility depends on it, because in a real test program, it would. slide 10: What Makes a Hypothesis Framing "Complete" vs. "Vague" (Image: A two-column side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis framings for the same experiment. Left column labeled "Incomplete (not acceptable)" in red — shows a multi-line hypothesis that reads: "We expect the system to respond as predicted by our model. The output should match the theoretical value within a reasonable range, and if it doesn't, there may be some error sources we haven't accounted for." Right column labeled "Complete (required)" in green — shows a structured framing with explicit entries for each of the five elements: system response feature with numbers, model equation with substituted values, uncertainty estimate with partial derivative shown, acceptance criterion as a numerical inequality, and risk assessment identifying one specific anomalous pattern. The contrast in specificity between left and right columns is stark and intentional.) Talking Point: I want to show you the difference between a hypothesis framing that is incomplete and one that is complete, using the same experiment. The incomplete version on the left is what many groups produce when they are in a hurry or when they are unsure of the model — it says something like "we expect the output to match the theoretical value within a reasonable range." There is nothing falsifiable in that sentence. "Reasonable" is doing all the work, and it is doing it after the fact. The complete version on the right specifies exactly what values are expected, where those values came from, how uncertain those values are, what threshold defines agreement, and what specific data pattern would tell you the model has a structural problem. Notice that the complete version is not longer — it is just more specific. Specificity is not extra work — it is the minimum requirement for a prediction that can teach you something when it fails. slide 11: How the Experimentally Informed Model Changes the Task (Image: A two-stage diagram. Stage 1 is labeled "Traditional view: Validate or reject" and shows a simple binary branch — a model on the left, experimental data on the right, and two outcome arrows: one labeled "Model accepted (data agrees)" and one labeled "Model rejected (data disagrees)." Stage 2 is labeled "E2 view: Experiment informs model" and shows the same model and data with an additional feedback loop arrow pointing from "discrepancy analysis" back to the model, labeled "What does the gap reveal about what the model is missing?" The feedback loop arrow is drawn in bright gold to emphasize it is the new element. The overall layout is clean with enough contrast to read clearly on a projected slide.) Talking Point: I want to reframe what you think the canned experiments are for. You might assume the goal is to run the experiment, compare to your prediction, and conclude "the model is good" or "the model is not good." That is not the goal. The goal is to use the experimental results to understand what the model is missing — and to propose, specifically, how you would improve it based on what you learned. This is the experimentally informed model concept. In E2, it is a required deliverable. Section 3D of your Tier 2 report asks you to answer specifically: what model improvement does your data suggest? That question starts here, in the prediction document — when you name in advance what would reveal a model limitation, you are already setting up the question that Section 3D must answer. slide 12: Connecting Hypothesis Framing to the Tier 2 Analysis Sections (Image: A flow diagram showing how each prediction document element maps to a Tier 2 report section. The diagram has five boxes on the left labeled "Prediction Document Elements" with arrows pointing to corresponding boxes on the right labeled "Tier 2 Report Sections." Mapping: "System response features" → "Section 1: Pre-lab prediction (reproduced verbatim)." "Model derivation" → "Section 2: Experimental setup + Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome." "Uncertainty estimate" → "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis." "Acceptance criterion" → "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome comparison." "Risk assessment" → "Section 3A: Discrepancy classification." The connecting arrows are gold. Below the diagram, a small note: "The prediction document is not a separate deliverable — it is the foundation of the group report." The visual shows that the prediction document is structurally integrated into the analysis, not a separate hoop to jump through.) Talking Point: I want you to see that the prediction document and the group report are not two separate tasks — the prediction document is the first section of the report and the foundation of every analysis section that follows. The system response features you commit to become the benchmark against which you measure your residuals. The model derivation becomes the basis for your prediction-versus-outcome comparison. The uncertainty estimate feeds directly into the uncertainty analysis section and into whether the discrepancy you observe is larger than what measurement noise can explain. The acceptance criterion is what you apply in Section 3A to conclude whether the model is adequate for the stated intended use. And the risk assessment is what you compare against the anomalous patterns you actually observe. Every element of the prediction document does real analytical work later in the report. That is why we require it — not as a bureaucratic gate, but because a report without a committed prediction has no foundation. slide 13: The Pre-Commitment Gate — What to Expect in Week 6 Lab (Image: A simple process flow diagram showing four boxes connected by arrows. Box 1 (light gold): "Review canned experiment document — understand the system and sensor suite." Box 2 (medium gold): "Develop predictive model — derive equations, substitute values, estimate uncertainty." Box 3 (bright gold): "Produce prediction document — all five required elements, shown to TA/instructor." Box 4 (red-outlined): "Gate check — approved before equipment access is granted in Week 8." A stop sign icon appears on the arrow before Box 4 to indicate that this is a hard gate, not a suggested step. Below the flow diagram, a small note reads: "The prediction document is reproduced verbatim in Section 1 of the Tier 2 group report — it is not revised after the gate check.") Talking Point: Here is exactly what you are doing in the Week 6 lab session. Your group receives the canned experiment document for your assigned Tier 2 experiment. You read it carefully, understand the physical system, and then — before anyone touches any hardware — you develop a predictive model from physical principles. You derive the equations, substitute the system parameters you have been given, estimate uncertainty from the sensor specs, sketch the expected output, and write down an acceptance criterion and a risk assessment. You bring that document to the TA or instructor, and they review it. If the prediction is documented and the reasoning is sound, you are cleared for equipment access in Week 8. If it is not, you are not cleared. And here is the key thing about the document itself: you do not revise it after the gate check. Whatever you commit to in Week 6 is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. The honesty of that pre-commitment is what makes the comparison in Week 8 meaningful. slide 14: Closure Formal hypothesis framing for complex systems is the same discipline as the hypothesis work you did in E1, applied under higher stakes and with more parameters to account for. Intended use is the first constraint: it determines what model you need, how precisely you need it to be accurate, and what counts as adequate agreement. System response features must be specific enough to be falsifiable — a number, a range, a shape, something you can compare to data. The acceptance criterion defines agreement before the data exists. And the risk assessment names, in advance, what the data would look like if your model has a structural problem — which is the single best preparation for interpreting discrepancy correctly when it appears. In the Week 6 lab session, you will produce this prediction document for your Tier 2 canned experiment and present it for the pre-commitment gate check. The habits you build here — specificity, pre-commitment, honest acknowledgment of model limitations — are the habits of a test engineer, and they are what will make your validation report credible to someone who was not in the room when you ran the experiment. Connection to E1 hypothesis work is explicit in the title slide and in slide 6. Slide 11 (experimentally informed model, from L1 slide 9) is placed after the anatomy and vague/complete comparison, connecting the "how to predict" vocabulary to the "what prediction enables" purpose — specifically Section 3D. Slide 13 (gate procedure, from L1 slide 10) closes the lecture with the concrete lab-session procedure. Slide 7 (risk assessment) now includes an inline note defining "model-form error" as a forward-reference, per LOW item approved in the same consolidation. The Tier 2 report structure is previewed in slide 12, connecting hypothesis framing directly to the deliverable students are building across W6–W10. Tone throughout: spoken-word instructor voice, not lecture notes. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Formal Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems Talking Point: In Week 1, you wrote a hypothesis for a simple canned experiment — one primary output, a clean physical model, and a straightforward comparison. This lecture extends that vocabulary to the systems you are going to work with in E2: more parameters, more potential failure modes, and less obvious predictions. The formal hypothesis framing tools are the same ones you already know, but the complexity of applying them has gone up, and the consequences of being vague — vague system response features, vague acceptance criteria — are more visible when the system is more complex. By the end of this lecture, you will have the full vocabulary for committing to a prediction that is worth something. slide 2: Revisiting the AMVF — The Experiment Is Not the End (Image: The AMVF flowchart diagram with the mathematical modeling branch on the left side and the physical modeling branch on the right, rendered in the course color scheme. A bold annotation box with a gold border is placed at the top of the diagram, reading: "The experiment is not an end in itself — it is a test of a model." An arrow runs from the "Model" node at top-left down and across to the "Experimental Data" and "Validation" nodes, with the label "The question: does reality behave as the model predicts?" A second annotation at the bottom of the diagram reads: "What does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter?" The diagram is clean and uncluttered — two annotations maximum.) Talking Point: Before we get into the vocabulary of formal hypothesis framing, I want to anchor this lecture in the AMVF. The experiment you are running in E2 is not an end in itself — it is a test of a model. The question you should be asking about every E2 lab activity is: what does this experiment tell me about the system, and why does it matter? This framing connects everything we do in this course to the broader curriculum and to your senior design projects. In ASEN 3501, experimental methods is the physical modeling branch — it is the part of the framework that bridges mathematical models and the real world. When you develop a predictive model, run an experiment, and compare the outcomes, you are contributing to the credibility of an engineering claim. Keeping that purpose in mind is what separates disciplined modeling from going through the motions. slide 3: What "Intended Use" Means and Why It Is the First Constraint (Image: A three-panel illustration showing the same physical system — a vibrating beam — at three different intended uses. Panel 1: "Intended use: predict resonant frequency within ±5% for mission planning." Panel 2: "Intended use: predict peak deflection under a specific load for structural margin." Panel 3: "Intended use: characterize damping ratio for control system design." Each panel shows a different set of highlighted model parameters and measurement requirements. Below the three panels, a single line reads: "The model that is adequate for one intended use may be completely inadequate for another." The panels are arranged horizontally, and each has a distinct colored border to help students visually separate the three cases.) Talking Point: Here is the first and most important framing constraint on any predictive model: intended use. A model is not validated in the abstract — it is validated for a purpose. The same physical system can require three entirely different models depending on what you are trying to predict and how accurately you need to predict it. If your intended use is predicting a resonant frequency to within five percent, a simple Euler-Bernoulli beam model with a mass correction might be perfectly adequate. If your intended use is predicting the damping ratio for a control system, you need a model that includes energy dissipation mechanisms that the simple beam model ignores entirely. The intended use statement drives every downstream modeling decision: what parameters need to be measured, how precisely, under what conditions, and what level of discrepancy counts as acceptable agreement. It is the first sentence of a defensible validation plan, and it must be written before any modeling decisions are made. slide 4: System Response Features — What You Are Actually Committing To (Image: A two-column layout. Left column header: "Vague system response feature (not acceptable)." Below it: three examples in red italic text — "The output will increase," "There will be oscillation," "The temperature will change over time." Right column header: "Committed system response feature (required)." Below it: three examples in green regular text — "Peak amplitude will reach 8.3 mm ± 0.4 mm by t = 0.5 s," "Oscillation frequency will be 47 Hz ± 2 Hz at the primary resonance mode," "Temperature will reach steady state within 90 seconds at a value of 52°C ± 3°C." At the bottom of the slide, a horizontal divider, then a caption: "A system response feature is falsifiable only if it specifies what would count as disagreement — meaning there exists a specific measurable outcome that would count as disagreement, not just one that would count as confirmation." The visual contrast between the red vague examples and the green specific examples is the central visual message.) Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific, falsifiable claim about what your model predicts the experiment will produce. Let me be direct about what "specific" means: it means a numerical value, a range, a time, a frequency — something that can be compared to a number. "The output will increase" is not a system response feature — it is an observation. "The output will reach a peak amplitude of 8.3 millimeters at approximately 0.5 seconds" is a system response feature — I can put data on the same axes and tell you whether the prediction held. The reason specificity matters is that a vague prediction can never be falsified, which means it can never teach you anything. If you said "the output will go up" and the output goes up, you have learned nothing. If you said "the output will reach 8.3 millimeters" and the output reaches 6.1, you have learned that your model is overestimating amplitude by 26%, and now you have a specific question: which model assumption is responsible for that gap? slide 5: Acceptable Agreement — What Does "Close Enough" Mean, and Who Decides? (Image: A single graph with a model prediction line shown as a solid gold curve and experimental data scatter plotted around it. The graph shows a clear acceptance band — a shaded region around the prediction line, labeled "Acceptance criterion: agreement within ±10% relative to predicted peak, or within uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger." Below the shaded region, two data points fall outside the band with arrows labeled "These points require explanation — not dismissal." The acceptance band is drawn with a dashed border to indicate it was defined before data collection. A note in the lower right corner reads: "The acceptance criterion must be stated before data is collected. It cannot be adjusted after." The graph style matches MATLAB-generated figures.) Talking Point: The acceptance criterion is the pre-stated answer to the question: how close does the data have to be to my prediction for me to say the model is adequate for the intended use? This is not a judgment you make after you see the results — it is a commitment you make before you collect any data. And it has to be numerical. "The model is good enough" is not an acceptance criterion. "Agreement within plus-or-minus ten percent relative to the predicted peak value, or within the propagated uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger" — that is an acceptance criterion. We use the larger criterion because claiming a meaningful disagreement when the difference is smaller than your measurement noise would be misleading — your uncertainty floor sets the minimum distinction you can honestly claim. Setting the acceptance criterion before data collection is what keeps you honest when the data comes back and you are tempted to judge whether it "looks good." slide 6: More Parameters, More Failure Modes — Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems (Image: A comparison between a simple system and a complex system. Left side: "E1 system — pendulum" with a single-equation model and two parameters (length, gravitational acceleration). Two labeled failure modes: "Amplitude too large (nonlinear)," "Air resistance ignored." Right side: "E2 system — wind tunnel / vibration rig" with a multi-parameter block diagram showing three input parameters, two environmental factors, and four potential model failure modes. The failure modes on the right are: "Boundary condition assumptions," "Unmodeled damping," "Nonlinear stiffness beyond small deflection," "Aerodynamic coupling effects." A bracket spans the right side labeled "Each failure mode corresponds to a specific anomalous data pattern." The comparison illustrates escalation of complexity without making the task seem impossible.) Talking Point: The core challenge of hypothesis framing in E2 is that the systems are more complex — more parameters, more coupling between effects, and more ways for the model to be wrong in a structured and physically meaningful way. In E1, you had a pendulum with one primary model parameter, and the dominant failure mode was clear — once the amplitude gets large, the small-angle assumption breaks. In E2, you might be working with a vibrating structure where boundary conditions, nonlinear stiffness, aerodynamic coupling, and damping mechanisms can all produce model-data discrepancy independently or together. The good news is that the vocabulary is exactly the same: you specify system response features, you commit to an acceptance criterion, and you name the anomalous patterns that would indicate each type of model failure. The discipline is identical — you are just applying it to a harder problem, and the payoff for doing it correctly is higher. slide 7: The Risk Assessment — Naming What Wrong Looks Like in Advance (Image: A three-row table illustration. Column headers: "Anomalous pattern," "Physical cause," "Implication for model." Row 1: "Residuals increase systematically with amplitude — systematic trend" | "Nonlinear stiffness — model assumes small deflection" | "Model-form error: linear model inadequate above this amplitude range." Row 2: "Measurements oscillate at a frequency not in the model" | "Unmodeled structural mode" | "Model-form error: additional degree of freedom required." Row 3: "Bias offset between prediction and data at all conditions" | "Parametric error — wrong stiffness value used" | "Parametric uncertainty: model form may be correct but input is wrong." Each row is color-coded: systematic trend in orange, unmodeled mode in red, parametric bias in yellow. The table is clear and readable — not crowded. Note: "model-form error" is a term we will formally define in W8. For now, think of it as: the model's structure is missing a physical effect — not just a wrong parameter value, but a wrong equation. It stems from flaws in the model's fundamental formulation.) Talking Point: The risk assessment section of your prediction document asks you to name, in advance, at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. This is not pessimism — it is predictive discipline. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict exactly what the data would look like if one of those assumptions breaks down. A linear spring model will produce residuals that increase systematically with amplitude if nonlinear stiffness is present — because the model cannot generate amplitude-dependent behavior. An unmodeled structural mode will produce oscillations at a frequency that is not in your prediction. A wrong parameter value will produce a bias offset that is consistent across all conditions. Each of these patterns is specific and distinguishable from random measurement noise, and the tool for distinguishing them is the uncertainty band you already computed. If a data pattern is inside the uncertainty band, it is consistent with noise. If it shows systematic structure that your model cannot produce, it is telling you something about the model. slide 8: What "Prediction Before Exposure" Actually Requires — The Five-Element Checklist (Image: A five-element checklist diagram — each element in its own horizontal band, numbered 1 through 5. Element 1: "System response features — specific values or ranges, shape of expected output." Element 2: "Model derivation — equations with substituted numbers, not just variable names." Element 3: "Estimated uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty." Element 4: "Acceptance criterion — numerical threshold committed before data collection." Element 5: "Risk assessment — at least one anomalous pattern named that would indicate model-form limitation rather than noise." Each band has a small checkmark icon at the left in gold. The overall layout is clean and structured — this is the checklist students will carry into the Tier 2 pre-lab session.) Talking Point: When I say prediction before exposure, I do not mean a vague directional statement — "I think the output will go up." A defensible prediction has five specific elements, and I want you to see them now because they are the required components of your Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document. First: system response features — what specific physical quantities do you expect, at what magnitudes, and in what shape over time or across the independent variable? Second: a model derivation — show the equations with numbers substituted, not just the symbolic form. Third: an estimated uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specs you have been given and express the result as estimate plus or minus uncertainty. Fourth: an acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold that defines what "agreement" means, stated before you collect any data. Fifth: a risk assessment — name at least one anomalous data pattern that would tell you the model form is wrong rather than the measurements being noisy. Each of these elements has a specific purpose, and we cover the reasoning behind each one in the slides that follow. slide 9: The Anatomy of a Tier 2 Prediction Document (Image: A visual breakdown of the prediction document structure, laid out as a one-page annotated mockup. The document is divided into five clearly labeled sections, each with a brief description: Section 1 "System Response Features — specific values, ranges, time-series shape committed in writing" | Section 2 "Model Derivation — equation with substituted numbers, intermediate steps shown" | Section 3 "Estimated Uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty" | Section 4 "Acceptance Criterion — numerical threshold stated before data collection" | Section 5 "Risk Assessment — at least one anomalous pattern with physical cause named." Each section has a word count indicator: "2–4 sentences each" or "one equation with substituted values." The document mockup has a visible header: "Group names, Date, Experiment: [Canned Experiment 1]" and a TA signature line at the bottom. The visual conveys that this is a real, formal document — not a worksheet.) Talking Point: Let me make this concrete. The Tier 2 prediction document has five required sections, and here is what each one looks like in practice. The first section is your system response features — you commit in writing to what specific physical quantities you expect, with magnitudes, units, and expected trend shape. Not "the output will increase" — "the peak deflection will reach this value at this point in the test, under this loading condition." The second section is your model derivation — you show the governing equations with numbers substituted in, not just symbolic expressions. The third section is your estimated uncertainty — you apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specifications you have been given and express the result in correct engineering form: estimate plus or minus uncertainty with units and method stated. The fourth section is your acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold. And the fifth section is your risk assessment. That document is shown to the TA or instructor before you are cleared for equipment access, and it is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. Write it as if your professional credibility depends on it, because in a real test program, it would. slide 10: What Makes a Hypothesis Framing "Complete" vs. "Vague" (Image: A two-column side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis framings for the same experiment. Left column labeled "Incomplete (not acceptable)" in red — shows a multi-line hypothesis that reads: "We expect the system to respond as predicted by our model. The output should match the theoretical value within a reasonable range, and if it doesn't, there may be some error sources we haven't accounted for." Right column labeled "Complete (required)" in green — shows a structured framing with explicit entries for each of the five elements: system response feature with numbers, model equation with substituted values, uncertainty estimate with partial derivative shown, acceptance criterion as a numerical inequality, and risk assessment identifying one specific anomalous pattern. The contrast in specificity between left and right columns is stark and intentional.) Talking Point: I want to show you the difference between a hypothesis framing that is incomplete and one that is complete, using the same experiment. The incomplete version on the left is what many groups produce when they are in a hurry or when they are unsure of the model — it says something like "we expect the output to match the theoretical value within a reasonable range." There is nothing falsifiable in that sentence. "Reasonable" is doing all the work, and it is doing it after the fact. The complete version on the right specifies exactly what values are expected, where those values came from, how uncertain those values are, what threshold defines agreement, and what specific data pattern would tell you the model has a structural problem. Notice that the complete version is not longer — it is just more specific. Specificity is not extra work — it is the minimum requirement for a prediction that can teach you something when it fails. slide 11: How the Experimentally Informed Model Changes the Task (Image: A two-stage diagram. Stage 1 is labeled "Traditional view: Validate or reject" and shows a simple binary branch — a model on the left, experimental data on the right, and two outcome arrows: one labeled "Model accepted (data agrees)" and one labeled "Model rejected (data disagrees)." Stage 2 is labeled "E2 view: Experiment informs model" and shows the same model and data with an additional feedback loop arrow pointing from "discrepancy analysis" back to the model, labeled "What does the gap reveal about what the model is missing?" The feedback loop arrow is drawn in bright gold to emphasize it is the new element. The overall layout is clean with enough contrast to read clearly on a projected slide.) Talking Point: I want to reframe what you think the canned experiments are for. You might assume the goal is to run the experiment, compare to your prediction, and conclude "the model is good" or "the model is not good." That is not the goal. The goal is to use the experimental results to understand what the model is missing — and to propose, specifically, how you would improve it based on what you learned. This is the experimentally informed model concept. In E2, it is a required deliverable. Section 3D of your Tier 2 report asks you to answer specifically: what model improvement does your data suggest? That question starts here, in the prediction document — when you name in advance what would reveal a model limitation, you are already setting up the question that Section 3D must answer. slide 12: Connecting Hypothesis Framing to the Tier 2 Analysis Sections (Image: A flow diagram showing how each prediction document element maps to a Tier 2 report section. The diagram has five boxes on the left labeled "Prediction Document Elements" with arrows pointing to corresponding boxes on the right labeled "Tier 2 Report Sections." Mapping: "System response features" → "Section 1: Pre-lab prediction (reproduced verbatim)." "Model derivation" → "Section 2: Experimental setup + Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome." "Uncertainty estimate" → "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis." "Acceptance criterion" → "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome comparison." "Risk assessment" → "Section 3A: Discrepancy classification." The connecting arrows are gold. Below the diagram, a small note: "The prediction document is not a separate deliverable — it is the foundation of the group report." The visual shows that the prediction document is structurally integrated into the analysis, not a separate hoop to jump through.) Talking Point: I want you to see that the prediction document and the group report are not two separate tasks — the prediction document is the first section of the report and the foundation of every analysis section that follows. The system response features you commit to become the benchmark against which you measure your residuals. The model derivation becomes the basis for your prediction-versus-outcome comparison. The uncertainty estimate feeds directly into the uncertainty analysis section and into whether the discrepancy you observe is larger than what measurement noise can explain. The acceptance criterion is what you apply in Section 3A to conclude whether the model is adequate for the stated intended use. And the risk assessment is what you compare against the anomalous patterns you actually observe. Every element of the prediction document does real analytical work later in the report. That is why we require it — not as a bureaucratic gate, but because a report without a committed prediction has no foundation. slide 13: The Pre-Commitment Gate — What to Expect in Week 6 Lab (Image: A simple process flow diagram showing four boxes connected by arrows. Box 1 (light gold): "Review canned experiment document — understand the system and sensor suite." Box 2 (medium gold): "Develop predictive model — derive equations, substitute values, estimate uncertainty." Box 3 (bright gold): "Produce prediction document — all five required elements, shown to TA/instructor." Box 4 (red-outlined): "Gate check — approved before equipment access is granted in Week 8." A stop sign icon appears on the arrow before Box 4 to indicate that this is a hard gate, not a suggested step. Below the flow diagram, a small note reads: "The prediction document is reproduced verbatim in Section 1 of the Tier 2 group report — it is not revised after the gate check.") Talking Point: Here is exactly what you are doing in the Week 6 lab session. Your group receives the canned experiment document for your assigned Tier 2 experiment. You read it carefully, understand the physical system, and then — before anyone touches any hardware — you develop a predictive model from physical principles. You derive the equations, substitute the system parameters you have been given, estimate uncertainty from the sensor specs, sketch the expected output, and write down an acceptance criterion and a risk assessment. You bring that document to the TA or instructor, and they review it. If the prediction is documented and the reasoning is sound, you are cleared for equipment access in Week 8. If it is not, you are not cleared. And here is the key thing about the document itself: you do not revise it after the gate check. Whatever you commit to in Week 6 is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. The honesty of that pre-commitment is what makes the comparison in Week 8 meaningful. slide 14: Closure Formal hypothesis framing for complex systems is the same discipline as the hypothesis work you did in E1, applied under higher stakes and with more parameters to account for. Intended use is the first constraint: it determines what model you need, how precisely you need it to be accurate, and what counts as adequate agreement. System response features must be specific enough to be falsifiable — a number, a range, a shape, something you can compare to data. The acceptance criterion defines agreement before the data exists. And the risk assessment names, in advance, what the data would look like if your model has a structural problem — which is the single best preparation for interpreting discrepancy correctly when it appears. In the Week 6 lab session, you will produce this prediction document for your Tier 2 canned experiment and present it for the pre-commitment gate check. The habits you build here — specificity, pre-commitment, honest acknowledgment of model limitations — are the habits of a test engineer, and they are what will make your validation report credible to someone who was not in the room when you ran the experiment. ## Agent Notes - AMVF framing is incorporated in slide 2 as required by the W6 L2 blueprint purpose statement. - Intended use as framing constraint is the central thread of slides 3, 4, and 5. - Connection to E1 hypothesis work is explicit in the title slide and in slide 6. - Slides 8–10 from L1 (five-element checklist preview, experimentally informed model, gate logistics) have been integrated into L2 per the V&V consolidation restructure approved 2026-05-28. L2 now fully owns the prediction document mechanics content. - Slide 8 (five-element checklist overview) precedes slide 9 (full anatomy), providing a compact preview before the detailed walkthrough. - Slide 11 (experimentally informed model, from L1 slide 9) is placed after the anatomy and vague/complete comparison, connecting the "how to predict" vocabulary to the "what prediction enables" purpose — specifically Section 3D. - Slide 13 (gate procedure, from L1 slide 10) closes the lecture with the concrete lab-session procedure. - Slide 7 (risk assessment) now includes an inline note defining "model-form error" as a forward-reference, per LOW item approved in the same consolidation. - The Tier 2 report structure is previewed in slide 12, connecting hypothesis framing directly to the deliverable students are building across W6–W10. - Tone throughout: spoken-word instructor voice, not lecture notes.

Talking Point: The acceptance criterion is the pre-stated answer to the question: how close does the data have to be to my prediction for me to say the model is adequate for the intended use? This is not a judgment you make after you see the results — it is a commitment you make before you collect any data. And it has to be numerical. "The model is good enough" is not an acceptance criterion. "Agreement within plus-or-minus ten percent relative to the predicted peak value, or within the propagated uncertainty bounds, whichever is larger" — that is an acceptance criterion. We use the larger criterion because claiming a meaningful disagreement when the difference is smaller than your measurement noise would be misleading — your uncertainty floor sets the minimum distinction you can

is smaller than your measurement noise would be misleading — your uncertainty floor sets the minimum distinction you can honestly claim. Setting the acceptance criterion before data collection is what keeps you honest when the data comes back and you are tempted to judge whether it "looks good." slide 6: More Parameters, More Failure Modes — Hypothesis Framing for Complex Systems (Image: A comparison between a simple system and a complex system. between effects, and more ways for the model to be wrong in a structured and physically meaningful way. In E2, you might be working with a vibrating structure where boundary conditions, nonlinear stiffness, aerodynamic coupling, and damping mechanisms can all produce model-data discrepancy independently or together. The good news is that the vocabulary is exactly the same: you specify system response features, you commit to an acceptance criterion, and you name the anomalous patterns that would indicate each type of model failure. Talking Point: The risk assessment section of your prediction document asks you to name, in advance, at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form

r prediction document asks you to name, in advance, at least one anomalous data pattern that would indicate a model-form limitation rather than measurement noise. This is not pessimism — it is predictive discipline. If you understand your model's assumptions, you can predict exactly what the data would look like if one of those assumptions breaks down. A linear spring model will produce residuals that increase systematically with amplitude if nonlinear stiffness is present — because the model cannot generate amplitude-dependent behavior. An unmodeled structural mode will produce oscillations at a frequency that is not in your prediction. A wrong parameter value will produce a bias offset that is consistent across all conditions. Each of these patterns is specific and distinguishable from

duce a bias offset that is consistent across all conditions. Each of these patterns is specific and distinguishable from random measurement noise, and the tool for distinguishing them is the uncertainty band you already computed. If a data pattern is inside the uncertainty band, it is consistent with noise. If it shows systematic structure that your model cannot produce, it is telling you something about the model. slide 8: What "Prediction Before Exposure" Actually Requires — The Five-Element Checklist (Image: A five-element checklist diagram — each element in its own horizontal band, numbered 1 through 5. — you show the governing equations with numbers substituted in, not just symbolic expressions. a reasonable range." There is nothing falsifiable in that sentence. "Reasonable" is doing all the work, and it is doing it after the fact. slide 14: Closure Formal hypothesis framing for complex systems is the same discipline as the hypothesis work you did in E1, applied under higher stakes and with more parameters to account for. Intended use is the first constraint: it determines what model you need, how precisely you need it to be accurate, and what counts as adequate agreement. System response features must be specific enough to be falsifiable — a number, a range, a shape, something you can compare to data. The acceptance criterion defines agreement before the data exists. And the risk assessment names, in advance, what the data would look like if your model has a structural problem — which is the single best preparation for interpreting discrepancy correctly when it appears. In the Week 6 lab session, you will produce

gle best preparation for interpreting discrepancy correctly when it appears. In the Week 6 lab session, you will produce this prediction document for your Tier 2 canned experiment and present it for the pre-commitment gate check. The habits you build here — specificity, pre-commitment, honest acknowledgment of model limitations — are the habits of a test engineer, and they are what will make your validation report credible to someone who was not in the room when you ran the experiment. t, a clean physical model, and a straightforward comparison. predictive model, run an experiment, and compare the outcomes, you are contributing to the credibility of an engineering claim. Panel 1: "Intended use: predict resonant frequency within ±5% for mission planning." Panel 2: "Intended use: predict peak deflection under a specific load for structural margin." Each panel shows a different set of highlighted model parameters and measurement requirements. Talking Point: Here is the first and most important framing constraint on any predictive model: intended use. A model is not validated in the abstract — it is validated for a purpose. The same physical system can require three entirely different models depending on what you are trying to predict and how accurately you need to predict it. If your intended use is predicting a resonant frequency to within five percent, a simple

urately you need to predict it. If your intended use is predicting a resonant frequency to within five percent, a simple Euler-Bernoulli beam model with a mass correction might be perfectly adequate. If your intended use is predicting the damping ratio for a control system, you need a model that includes energy dissipation mechanisms that the simple beam model ignores entirely. The intended use statement drives every downstream modeling decision: what parameters need to be measured, how precisely, under what conditions, and what level of discrepancy counts as acceptable agreement. It is the first sentence of a defensible validation plan, and it must be written before any modeling decisions are made. slide 4: System Response Features — What You Are Actually Committing To (Image: A

Talking Point: A system response feature is a specific, falsifiable claim about what your model predicts the experiment will produce. Let me be direct about what "specific" means: it means a numerical value, a range, a time, a frequency — something that can be compared to a number. at can be compared to a number. "The output will increase" is not a system response feature — it is an observation. "The output will reach a peak amplitude of 8.3 millimeters at approximately 0.5 seconds" is a system response feature — I can put data on the same axes and tell you whether the prediction held. The reason specificity matters is that a vague prediction can never be falsified, which means it can never teach you anything. If you said "the output will go up" and the output goes up, you have learned nothing. If you said "the output will reach 8.3 millimeters" and the output reaches 6.1, you have learned that your model is overestimating amplitude by 26%, and now you have a specific question: which model assumption is responsible for that gap? slide 5: Acceptable Agreement — What
