Element 1: "System response features — specific values or ranges, shape of expected output." Element 2: "Model derivation — equations with substituted numbers, not just variable names." Element 3: "Estimated uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty." Element 4: "Acceptance criterion — numerical threshold committed before data collection." Element 5: "Risk assessment — at least one anomalous pattern named that would indicate model-form limitation rather than noise." Each band has a small checkmark icon at the left in

named that would indicate model-form limitation rather than noise." Each band has a small checkmark icon at the left in gold. The overall layout is clean and structured — this is the checklist students will carry into the Tier 2 pre-lab session.) Talking Point: When I say prediction before exposure, I do not mean a vague directional statement — "I think the output will go up." A defensible prediction has five specific elements, and I want you to see them now because they are the required components of your Tier 2 pre-lab prediction document. First: system response features — what specific physical quantities do you expect, at what magnitudes, and in what shape over time or across the independent variable? Second: a model derivation — show the equations with numbers substituted, not just

e or across the independent variable? Second: a model derivation — show the equations with numbers substituted, not just the symbolic form. Third: an estimated uncertainty in the primary derived quantity — apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specs you have been given and express the result as estimate plus or minus uncertainty. Fourth: an acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold that defines what "agreement" means, stated before you collect any data. Fifth: a risk assessment — name at least one anomalous data pattern that would tell you the model form is wrong rather than the measurements being noisy. Each of these elements has a specific purpose, and we cover the reasoning behind each one in the slides that follow. slide 9: The Anatomy of a Tier 2 Prediction Document

d we cover the reasoning behind each one in the slides that follow. slide 9: The Anatomy of a Tier 2 Prediction Document (Image: A visual breakdown of the prediction document structure, laid out as a one-page annotated mockup. The document is divided into five clearly labeled sections, each with a brief description: Section 1 "System Response Features — specific values, ranges, time-series shape committed in writing" | Section 2 "Model Derivation — equation with substituted numbers, intermediate steps shown" | Section 3 "Estimated Uncertainty — partial derivatives applied to sensor specs, result as estimate ± uncertainty" | Section 4 "Acceptance Criterion — numerical threshold stated before data collection" | Section 5 "Risk Assessment — at least one anomalous pattern with physical cause

reshold stated before data collection" | Section 5 "Risk Assessment — at least one anomalous pattern with physical cause named." Each section has a word count indicator: "2–4 sentences each" or "one equation with substituted values." The document mockup has a visible header: "Group names, Date, Experiment: [Canned Experiment 1]" and a TA signature line at the bottom. The visual conveys that this is a real, formal document — not a worksheet.) Talking Point: Let me make this concrete. The Tier 2 prediction document has five required sections, and here is what each one looks like in practice. The first section is your system response features — you commit in writing to what specific physical quantities you expect, with magnitudes, units, and expected trend shape. Not "the output will

to what specific physical quantities you expect, with magnitudes, units, and expected trend shape. Not "the output will increase" — "the peak deflection will reach this value at this point in the test, under this loading condition." The second section is your model derivation — you show the governing equations with numbers substituted in, not just symbolic expressions. The third section is your estimated uncertainty — you apply partial derivative propagation to the sensor specifications you have been given and express the result in correct engineering form: estimate plus or minus uncertainty with units and method stated. The fourth section is your acceptance criterion — a numerical threshold. And the fifth section is your risk assessment. That document is shown to the TA or instructor

n — a numerical threshold. And the fifth section is your risk assessment. That document is shown to the TA or instructor before you are cleared for equipment access, and it is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. Write it as if your professional credibility depends on it, because in a real test program, it would. slide 10: What Makes a Hypothesis Framing "Complete" vs. "Vague" (Image: A two-column side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis framings for the same experiment. Left column labeled "Incomplete (not acceptable)" in red — shows a multi-line hypothesis that reads: "We expect the system to respond as predicted by our model. The output should match the theoretical value within a reasonable range, and if it doesn't, there may be some error sources we haven't

uld match the theoretical value within a reasonable range, and if it doesn't, there may be some error sources we haven't accounted for." Right column labeled "Complete (required)" in green — shows a structured framing with explicit entries for each of the five elements: system response feature with numbers, model equation with substituted values, uncertainty estimate with partial derivative shown, acceptance criterion as a numerical inequality, and risk assessment identifying one specific anomalous pattern. The contrast in specificity between left and right columns is stark and intentional.) Talking Point: I want to show you the difference between a hypothesis framing that is incomplete and one that is complete, using the same experiment. The incomplete version on the left is what many

that is incomplete and one that is complete, using the same experiment. The incomplete version on the left is what many groups produce when they are in a hurry or when they are unsure of the model — it says something like "we expect the output to match the theoretical value within a reasonable range." There is nothing falsifiable in that sentence. "Reasonable" is doing all the work, and it is doing it after the fact. The complete version on the right specifies exactly what values are expected, where those values came from, how uncertain those values are, what threshold defines agreement, and what specific data pattern would tell you the model has a structural problem. Notice that the complete version is not longer — it is just more specific. Specificity is not extra work — it is the

em. Notice that the complete version is not longer — it is just more specific. Specificity is not extra work — it is the minimum requirement for a prediction that can teach you something when it fails. slide 11: How the Experimentally Informed Model Changes the Task (Image: A two-stage diagram. Stage 1 is labeled "Traditional view: Validate or reject" and shows a simple binary branch — a model on the left, experimental data on the right, and two outcome arrows: one labeled "Model accepted (data agrees)" and one labeled "Model rejected (data disagrees)." Stage 2 is labeled "E2 view: Experiment informs model" and shows the same model and data with an additional feedback loop arrow pointing from "discrepancy analysis" back to the model, labeled "What does the gap reveal about what the model

oop arrow pointing from "discrepancy analysis" back to the model, labeled "What does the gap reveal about what the model is missing?" The feedback loop arrow is drawn in bright gold to emphasize it is the new element. The overall layout is clean with enough contrast to read clearly on a projected slide.) Talking Point: I want to reframe what you think the canned experiments are for. You might assume the goal is to run the experiment, compare to your prediction, and conclude "the model is good" or "the model is not good." That is not the goal. The goal is to use the experimental results to understand what the model is missing — and to propose, specifically, how you would improve it based on what you learned. This is the experimentally informed model concept. In E2, it is a required

u would improve it based on what you learned. This is the experimentally informed model concept. In E2, it is a required deliverable. Section 3D of your Tier 2 report asks you to answer specifically: what model improvement does your data suggest? That question starts here, in the prediction document — when you name in advance what would reveal a model limitation, you are already setting up the question that Section 3D must answer. slide 12: Connecting Hypothesis Framing to the Tier 2 Analysis Sections (Image: A flow diagram showing how each prediction document element maps to a Tier 2 report section. The diagram has five boxes on the left labeled "Prediction Document Elements" with arrows pointing to corresponding boxes on the right labeled "Tier 2 Report Sections." Mapping: "System

nt Elements" with arrows pointing to corresponding boxes on the right labeled "Tier 2 Report Sections." Mapping: "System response features" → "Section 1: Pre-lab prediction (reproduced verbatim)." "Model derivation" → "Section 2: Experimental setup + Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome." "Uncertainty estimate" → "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis." "Acceptance criterion" → "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome comparison." "Risk assessment" → "Section 3A: Discrepancy classification." The connecting arrows are gold. Below the diagram, a small note: "The prediction document is not a separate deliverable — it is the foundation of the group report." The visual shows that the prediction document is structurally integrated into the analysis, not a separate hoop to jump through.) Talking Point: I

prediction document is structurally integrated into the analysis, not a separate hoop to jump through.) Talking Point: I want you to see that the prediction document and the group report are not two separate tasks — the prediction document is the first section of the report and the foundation of every analysis section that follows. The system response features you commit to become the benchmark against which you measure your residuals. The model derivation becomes the basis for your prediction-versus-outcome comparison. The uncertainty estimate feeds directly into the uncertainty analysis section and into whether the discrepancy you observe is larger than what measurement noise can explain. The acceptance criterion is what you apply in Section 3A to conclude whether the model is adequate

nt noise can explain. The acceptance criterion is what you apply in Section 3A to conclude whether the model is adequate for the stated intended use. And the risk assessment is what you compare against the anomalous patterns you actually observe. Every element of the prediction document does real analytical work later in the report. That is why we require it — not as a bureaucratic gate, but because a report without a committed prediction has no foundation. slide 13: The Pre-Commitment Gate — What to Expect in Week 6 Lab (Image: A simple process flow diagram showing four boxes connected by arrows. Box 1 (light gold): "Review canned experiment document — understand the system and sensor suite." Box 2 (medium gold): "Develop predictive model — derive equations, substitute values, estimate

system and sensor suite." Box 2 (medium gold): "Develop predictive model — derive equations, substitute values, estimate uncertainty." Box 3 (bright gold): "Produce prediction document — all five required elements, shown to TA/instructor." Box 4 (red-outlined): "Gate check — approved before equipment access is granted in Week 8." A stop sign icon appears on the arrow before Box 4 to indicate that this is a hard gate, not a suggested step. Below the flow diagram, a small note reads: "The prediction document is reproduced verbatim in Section 1 of the Tier 2 group report — it is not revised after the gate check.") Talking Point: Here is exactly what you are doing in the Week 6 lab session. Your group receives the canned experiment document for your assigned Tier 2 experiment. You read it

Week 6 lab session. Your group receives the canned experiment document for your assigned Tier 2 experiment. You read it carefully, understand the physical system, and then — before anyone touches any hardware — you develop a predictive model from physical principles. You derive the equations, substitute the system parameters you have been given, estimate uncertainty from the sensor specs, sketch the expected output, and write down an acceptance criterion and a risk assessment. You bring that document to the TA or instructor, and they review it. If the prediction is documented and the reasoning is sound, you are cleared for equipment access in Week 8. If it is not, you are not cleared. And here is the key thing about the document itself: you do not revise it after the gate check. Whatever

re not cleared. And here is the key thing about the document itself: you do not revise it after the gate check. Whatever you commit to in Week 6 is reproduced verbatim as Section 1 of your group report. The honesty of that pre-commitment is what makes the comparison in Week 8 meaningful. slide 14: Closure Formal hypothesis framing for complex systems is the same discipline as the hypothesis work you did in E1, applied under higher stakes and with more parameters to account for. Intended use is the first constraint: it determines what model you need, how precisely you need it to be accurate, and what counts as adequate agreement. System response features must be specific enough to be falsifiable — a number, a range, a shape, something you can compare to data. The acceptance criterion

cific enough to be falsifiable — a number, a range, a shape, something you can compare to data. The acceptance criterion defines agreement before the data exists. And the risk assessment names, in advance, what the data would look like if your model has a structural problem — which is the single best preparation for interpreting discrepancy correctly when it appears. In the Week 6 lab session, you will produce this prediction document for your Tier 2 canned experiment and present it for the pre-commitment gate check. The habits you build here — specificity, pre-commitment, honest acknowledgment of model limitations — are the habits of a test engineer, and they are what will make your validation report credible to someone who was not in the room when you ran the experiment. ## Agent Notes

will make your validation report credible to someone who was not in the room when you ran the experiment. ## Agent Notes - AMVF framing is incorporated in slide 2 as required by the W6 L2 blueprint purpose statement. - Intended use as framing constraint is the central thread of slides 3, 4, and 5. - Connection to E1 hypothesis work is explicit in the title slide and in slide 6. - Slides 8–10 from L1 (five-element checklist preview, experimentally informed model, gate logistics) have been integrated into L2 per the V&V consolidation restructure approved 2026-05-28. L2 now fully owns the prediction document mechanics content. - Slide 8 (five-element checklist overview) precedes slide 9 (full anatomy), providing a compact preview before the detailed walkthrough. - Slide 11 (experimentally

precedes slide 9 (full anatomy), providing a compact preview before the detailed walkthrough. - Slide 11 (experimentally informed model, from L1 slide 9) is placed after the anatomy and vague/complete comparison, connecting the "how to predict" vocabulary to the "what prediction enables" purpose — specifically Section 3D. - Slide 13 (gate procedure, from L1 slide 10) closes the lecture with the concrete lab-session procedure. - Slide 7 (risk assessment) now includes an inline note defining "model-form error" as a forward-reference, per LOW item approved in the same consolidation. - The Tier 2 report structure is previewed in slide 12, connecting hypothesis framing directly to the deliverable students are building across W6–W10. - Tone throughout: spoken-word instructor voice, not lecture
tly to the deliverable students are building across W6–W10. - Tone throughout: spoken-word instructor voice, not lecture notes.

pages.title: R01
pages.text: E2 Week 1 (Course Week 6) Reading R01 # The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First ## How hindsight bias turns curve-fitting into fake validation The most dangerous validation failure in engineering looks exactly like success. A team ru... The Problem with Postdiction — Why Prediction Must Come First How hindsight bias turns curve-fitting into fake validation The most dangerous validation failure in engineering looks exactly like success. A team runs an experiment, collects data, and builds a model that fits the data beautifully. R² (a measure of how well the model's curve fits the data it was computed from) is high. The curves overlap. Everyone is satisfied. But here is what actually happened: the team saw the data first, then built the model to match it. What they produced was not a validated model — it was a very sophisticated description of the data they already had. The moment they call it "validated," they have committed one of the oldest epistemic errors in science: postdiction . Postdiction is the practice of building a model after seeing the data and then treating the quality of the fit as evidence that the model is predictively valid. It feels like validation. It produces outputs that look like validation. But it is structurally indistinguishable from curve fitting — and curve fitting has no predictive content whatsoever. A polynomial of sufficient degree will fit any dataset perfectly. That does not mean the polynomial predicts anything. The cognitive mechanism behind postdiction has been studied extensively in psychology. Hindsight bias , the tendency to believe after learning an outcome that you would have predicted it all along, is so deeply embedded in human reasoning that it operates even when people are explicitly warned against it. When you see data and then build a model, your brain cannot un-see the data. Every modeling decision you make is shaped by what you know the answer should look like. The model converges toward the data not because the physics demands it, but because your judgment is contaminated. Example: a team builds a model after seeing that their experiment produced a nonlinear curve. They are convinced they would have predicted the nonlinearity. They almost certainly would not have. Confirmation bias compounds the problem as the second mechanism reinforcing the first. Once you have a model that fits the data, you notice the agreements and rationalize the discrepancies. Small systematic trends in the residuals become "experimental scatter." Anomalous points become "outlier runs." The model is right, and anything that says otherwise must be explained away. The validation report is filled with language like "agreement is within 10%, which is acceptable given the measurement uncertainty." The 10% tolerance was chosen after looking at the data, not before. These failures are not hypothetical. The history of engineering is littered with models that were extensively "validated" against the same data used to build them, then broke down at the first encounter with conditions outside that dataset. Structural load models validated against data from the test specimens they were built on. Aerodynamic performance models tuned to match existing flight data and then used to predict configurations that behaved completely differently. In each case, the validation was sincere — the engineers believed it — and the failure was still catastrophic. The insidiousness of postdiction is that it is invisible from the inside. If you have already seen the data, you cannot know how much your modeling choices were shaped by it. You cannot subtract the contamination. The only protection is structural: the model must be committed before any data is collected, and that commitment must be documented. This is why prediction-before-exposure is not a stylistic preference or an academic exercise. It is the mechanism that makes validation meaningful. A model that accurately predicts data it has never seen is evidence of something real. A model that fits data it was built on is evidence of nothing except the modeler's ability to adjust parameters. This is the same discipline you practiced in Tier 1 Phase 2 — your prediction memo was due before data was released for exactly this reason. The discipline has a formal structure, and you will practice it throughout E2. Before any Tier 2 experiment is run, your group commits to a specific prediction: the expected value of the primary output quantity, the expected shape of the system response, the estimated uncertainty in your prediction, and a numerical criterion for what counts as adequate agreement. That commitment is documented, shown to the instructor or TA, and then frozen. It is not revised when the data comes in. It is not adjusted when the fit is off. When the data is released in Week 8, you will compare your committed prediction to what was actually measured. If the agreement is poor — if your model missed — that is not a failed experiment. A poor prediction from a well-reasoned model is rich diagnostic information. It tells you something true about the physics that your model did not capture. That kind of information is useful. A "successful validation" produced by postdiction tells you only what you already knew. In professional test engineering, prediction-before-exposure is not optional. A validation report that does not document when the model was committed relative to when the data was collected is not a credible validation report. It is an anecdote. The sequence — model first, data second — is the line between engineering analysis and storytelling. The Takeaway: Postdiction is not a mistake you make by accident — it is a failure mode that feels exactly like success, and the only structural protection against it is committing your prediction, in writing, before the data exists. In E2, that commitment is a required gate, not a formality; understanding why it exists is what separates a test engineer from a curve-fitter. The Week 6 lectures walk through the mechanics of the prediction document itself — the five required elements and the gate procedure — if you want the structural detail behind this principle.

pages.title: L01
pages.text: E2 Week 2 (Course Week 7) Lecture L01 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Nyquist Sampling, Aliasing & Signal Bandwidth Talking Point: Before you can run a credible measurement on a complex aerospace system, you need to understand what you are asking the digital... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Nyquist Sampling, Aliasing & Signal Bandwidth Talking Point: Before you can run a credible measurement on a complex aerospace system, you need to understand what you are asking the digital measurement system to do. A sensor converts a physical quantity into an analog electrical signal — a continuous stream of information about the world. A data acquisition system takes discrete snapshots of that signal at a fixed rate. Everything about whether those snapshots faithfully represent the original signal depends on one thing: how fast you sample relative to how fast the signal changes. That relationship is governed by the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, and when it is violated, the result is not noise — it is a plausible-looking signal that is completely wrong. That is the subject of this lecture. slide 2: What Signal Bandwidth Actually Is Signal bandwidth — the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information in the signal — is a property of the physical phenomenon, not the sensor or DAQ. (Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: a time-domain signal showing a complex waveform — a mix of a slow sinusoidal component and a faster oscillation superimposed on it. The signal is continuous, plotted as a smooth analog curve, with a label "Measured physical phenomenon — continuous in time." Right panel: the same signal's frequency-domain representation (FFT magnitude spectrum) plotted as a bar chart, with two visible peaks labeled "Low-frequency component: 10 Hz" and "High-frequency component: 340 Hz." A bracket spans from 0 to 340 Hz and is labeled "Signal bandwidth: 340 Hz — this is a property of the phenomenon, not the sensor." The visual contrast between the time domain (messy, complex) and the frequency domain (clean, peaks visible) is the main teaching point of this slide.) Talking Point: Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring — it is not a property of your sensor or your DAQ system. It describes the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information about the signal. A structural vibration signal from an aerospace component might have a dominant resonance at 10 hertz and a higher-frequency mode at 340 hertz. The signal bandwidth of that measurement extends to 340 hertz — regardless of what sample rate you choose. Why does this matter? Because if you choose a sample rate without knowing the signal bandwidth, you are making a claim about the signal's frequency content without evidence. The claim you are making is: "I believe this signal has no content above half my sample rate." If that claim is wrong, your data is not merely imprecise — it is silently corrupted in a way that looks real. slide 3: The Sampling Theorem — The Fundamental Constraint (Image: A diagram showing a continuous analog signal (smooth sinusoidal curve) with two sets of sample points overlaid. The top set of samples is spaced closely — labeled "Sample rate = 10× signal frequency." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a dashed curve through the sample points, closely tracks the original. The bottom set of samples is spaced too widely — labeled "Sample rate = 0.8× signal frequency (undersampled)." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a red dashed curve, produces a much lower-frequency sinusoid that does not match the original. A boxed statement in the center of the slide reads: "Nyquist Criterion: sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal." The original signal and the alias are clearly color-coded — original in gold, alias in red.) Talking Point: The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem tells us the minimum sample rate required to faithfully represent a signal. The requirement is simple: your sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal you are trying to measure. That frequency — half the sample rate — is called the Nyquist frequency, and it is the boundary above which information is lost. Note that I said "greater than twice" — not "equal to twice." In practice, engineers apply a margin above the theoretical minimum because real signals do not have perfectly sharp cutoffs. The practical rule is that your sample rate should be at least five to ten times the highest frequency of interest, depending on how well you can characterize the signal's frequency content. Choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment: you are claiming that the signal has no content above the Nyquist frequency, and if that claim is wrong, you will not know it from the data alone. slide 4: What Aliasing Actually Looks Like — The Stroboscopic Wheel (Image: A three-frame stroboscopic effect illustration. Frame 1: A wheel with a single marker dot, spinning at 10 revolutions per second, is illuminated by a strobe light flashing at 12 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly forward — dotted arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow forward." Frame 2: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 9 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly backward — arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow backward." Frame 3: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 10 Hz. The wheel appears to stop — labeled "Apparent motion: stationary." Below the three frames: "None of these stroboscopic observations are noise. They are coherent, plausible, and completely wrong." A caption reads: "This is aliasing. The observed frequency is the difference between the signal and the strobe — it is not in the original signal at all.") Talking Point: The most important thing I want you to understand about aliasing is that it does not produce obvious noise — it produces a coherent, plausible-looking signal at a different frequency. The stroboscopic wheel is the perfect illustration. When a wheel spinning at ten revolutions per second is illuminated by a strobe flashing at twelve hertz, it appears to spin slowly forward at two revolutions per second. Your DAQ is the strobe — it observes the voltage signal at fixed intervals. If those intervals are too far apart relative to how fast the signal changes, the reconstructed waveform is as wrong as the backward-spinning wheel. The wheel is not actually slowing down — your measurement system is creating a false low-frequency signal out of the difference between the true signal and your sample rate. This is aliasing. The alias frequency is the difference between the true signal frequency and the sampling frequency, and it falls inside your measurement bandwidth — which is exactly why it is dangerous. You cannot look at an aliased signal and know it is wrong by visual inspection. It looks like a real, physical signal, because digitally, it is one. slide 5: Why Aliasing Is Invisible — The Silent Corruption Problem (Image: A side-by-side comparison. Left: a real physical vibration signal — a clean sinusoid at 380 Hz, shown in the frequency domain as a single spectral peak. Right: the same signal as captured by a DAQ system sampling at 500 Hz — the 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 - 380 = 120 Hz, appearing as a spectral peak at 120 Hz. Both frequency-domain plots are labeled with axes (frequency in Hz, magnitude in arbitrary units). The caption at the bottom reads: "The 120 Hz alias peak looks real. There is no visual marker in the data that indicates corruption." An annotation on the right plot reads: "You would report this as a 120 Hz structural mode — and you would be wrong.") Talking Point: Here is the scenario I want you to sit with. You are running a structural vibration test on an aerospace component. You expect significant frequency content up to about 300 hertz. You set your sample rate at 500 hertz, which gives you a Nyquist frequency of 250 hertz. You are reasonably satisfied. What you do not know is that there is a structural mode at 380 hertz — well above your Nyquist frequency. That 380 hertz signal aliases into your measurement at 500 minus 380, which is 120 hertz. You now have a perfectly clean, well-resolved spectral peak at 120 hertz that you did not expect. You write it up as a structural resonance you found, present it to your team, and potentially design a control system to avoid exciting it. None of that is real. There is no 120 hertz mode. The mode at 380 hertz is real, and it is invisible to your measurement. This is why aliasing is a silent corruption problem — it does not announce itself. The only protection is choosing a sufficient sample rate before you collect data. slide 6: Anti-Aliasing Filters — The Structural Solution (Image: A block diagram of the signal processing chain between a physical sensor and a digital data record. The chain has five stages from left to right: "Physical sensor" → "Amplifier/Conditioning" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." The anti-aliasing filter stage is highlighted with a gold border. An annotation shows the filter's cutoff frequency labeled "fc < fs/2" (cutoff frequency must be below the Nyquist frequency). A second annotation on the ADC stage reads: "Once digitized, aliased content cannot be removed — information is permanently lost." A dashed vertical line between the anti-aliasing filter and the ADC is labeled "This line is the point of no return — aliasing must be prevented before digitization.") Talking Point: The structural solution to aliasing is the anti-aliasing filter: a low-pass filter placed in the signal conditioning chain before the analog-to-digital converter, designed to attenuate frequency content above the Nyquist frequency before it can be aliased into the digital record. The critical principle here is timing. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain — before the ADC — because once the signal has been digitized, the alias is already mixed into the data. A digital filter applied after digitization cannot un-alias a signal, because the aliased content and the real content at the same frequency are indistinguishable in the digital record. The information that was lost when the signal was undersampled is permanently gone. This is why the design of the anti-aliasing filter must be decided before you set up your DAQ configuration — it is not a post-processing step, it is a pre-measurement design decision. slide 7: Choosing the Sample Rate — The Engineering Decision (Image: A horizontal decision diagram with three components. Component 1: "Characterize signal bandwidth" — shown as a frequency spectrum with the highest expected frequency labeled fmax. Component 2: "Apply Nyquist margin" — shown as the formula "fs ≥ 5 × fmax (practical engineering margin)" with a note "Theoretical minimum: fs > 2 × fmax." A footnote beneath Component 2 reads: "The 5–10× rule accounts for the anti-aliasing filter's transition band — it is not a universal constant. Lower margins may be acceptable with a steeper filter; wider margins may be needed when signal bandwidth is uncertain." Component 3: "Verify against DAQ hardware limits" — shown as a hardware specification block with maximum sample rate and resolution. The three components are connected by arrows. Below the diagram: "Sample rate selection is a modeling commitment — you are claiming no energy above fs/2. Document your reasoning." The diagram uses a clean flowchart style with the course color scheme.) Talking Point: In practice, how do you choose a sample rate? Start by characterizing the signal bandwidth — identify the highest frequency that contains information relevant to your measurement. For structural modes, this might come from a preliminary analysis of the natural frequencies using the geometry and material properties. For aerodynamic measurements, it might come from the expected turbulent fluctuation frequencies at your operating conditions. Once you have an estimate of the maximum frequency of interest, apply a practical margin: use a sample rate at least five to ten times that frequency, not just two times. The factor-of-two Nyquist minimum leaves almost no margin for filter roll-off, hardware imperfections, or unexpected high-frequency content. Then verify that your chosen sample rate is achievable with your DAQ hardware given the number of channels and the resolution you need. Document this decision in your report — not just what sample rate you used, but why you chose it based on the expected signal bandwidth. slide 8: Identifying Signal Bandwidth from Real Data — MATLAB in the Loop (Image: Two MATLAB figure panels. Left panel: a time-domain signal from a structural vibration measurement — a decaying oscillation with visible high-frequency content superimposed. The x-axis is labeled "Time (s)," y-axis "Acceleration (m/s²)." Right panel: the frequency-domain FFT of the same signal, with the x-axis labeled "Frequency (Hz)" and y-axis "Magnitude (m/s²)." Two spectral peaks are labeled: "Primary mode: 47 Hz" and "Secondary mode: 312 Hz." A vertical dashed red line is labeled "Nyquist frequency for this measurement: 500 Hz (sample rate = 1000 Hz)." The bandwidth bracket extends from 0 to 312 Hz, with the note: "312 Hz << 500 Hz — adequate margin." The figure style matches MATLAB default with course-consistent axis labels.) Talking Point: In the Week 7 lab session, you are going to do exactly this — use MATLAB to visualize the signal from your Tier 2 canned experiment in both the time domain and the frequency domain. The time-domain plot tells you a lot about the character of the signal: is it a steady oscillation, a decaying transient, a step response? But the frequency-domain FFT is what tells you about bandwidth. You are looking for the highest frequency at which there is meaningful spectral energy — not noise floor, but actual signal content. That frequency is the lower bound on your Nyquist frequency, and half of it is the minimum cutoff frequency for your anti-aliasing filter. In this example, the primary structural mode is at 47 hertz and the secondary mode is at 312 hertz. If the sample rate were 600 hertz instead of 1000, the secondary mode at 312 hertz would be right against the Nyquist limit with almost no margin — any filter imperfection would potentially alias secondary mode content into the measurement. The 1000 hertz sample rate gives you comfortable margin. Seeing this in your own data, for your own experiment, is what makes the Nyquist criterion real rather than abstract. slide 9: What Aliasing Looks Like in Aerospace Data — A Worked Example (Image: A simulated MATLAB FFT plot showing two scenarios side by side. Left: "Adequately sampled (fs = 2000 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz, 420 Hz, and 780 Hz, all clearly resolved within the 0–1000 Hz bandwidth. Right: "Undersampled (fs = 800 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz and 420 Hz, but the 780 Hz component has aliased to 800 - 780 = 20 Hz, appearing as a prominent low-frequency peak. The 20 Hz alias peak is labeled in red: "780 Hz signal aliased to 20 Hz — reported as a structural mode if not recognized." Vertical dashed lines show the Nyquist frequency for each case: 1000 Hz on the left and 400 Hz on the right. A banner across the top reads: "Same physical signal — two entirely different frequency-domain representations based solely on sample rate choice.") Talking Point: Let me make aliasing numerically concrete. Here is a physical signal with three real components at 80, 420, and 780 hertz. On the left, sampled at 2000 hertz — all three components are cleanly resolved in the FFT. On the right, the same physical signal sampled at 800 hertz. The Nyquist frequency is now 400 hertz. The 80-hertz and 420-hertz components are there — but the 420-hertz component is already very close to the Nyquist limit and may be attenuated by the anti-aliasing filter if it is too aggressive. And the 780-hertz component aliases to 800 minus 780, which is 20 hertz. That 20-hertz peak is prominent in the undersampled FFT. If you did not know the truth, you would report it as a 20-hertz structural mode. You might even try to correlate it to some physical mechanism at that frequency. This is not a rare failure mode — it is exactly the kind of error that gets made when sample rates are chosen without first characterizing the signal bandwidth. slide 10: Connecting to the Tier 2 Lab — What You Are Evaluating (Image: A structured checklist layout with four evaluation criteria. Each criterion is in its own row with an evaluation question on the left and a corresponding action on the right. Row 1: "Does the sample rate satisfy Nyquist with adequate margin?" → "Check: fs ≥ 5 × fmax based on FFT analysis." Row 2: "Is the anti-aliasing filter cutoff appropriate?" → "Check: fc < fs/2 and fc does not attenuate signal content of interest." Row 3: "Are there unexpected peaks in the FFT?" → "Investigate: could any be aliased content from above the Nyquist frequency?" Row 4: "Would a different sample rate or filter choice change the results?" → "Propose: document in the DAQ configuration section and in the improvement proposal." The checklist is designed to look like a structured analysis protocol — students can use it directly in the W7 lab session and in the Tier 2 group report Section 6.) Talking Point: This is what you will do with the tools from this lecture in the Week 7 lab session. You are going to examine the DAQ configuration for your Tier 2 canned experiment — the actual sample rate and filtering choices that were made — and evaluate whether those choices are defensible given the signal content you measure. First: compute the Nyquist frequency for the chosen sample rate and verify that it provides adequate margin above the signal bandwidth you measure in the FFT. Second: locate the anti-aliasing filter specification in the DAQ documentation and verify that the cutoff is below the Nyquist frequency without attenuating signal content of interest. Third: look for unexpected peaks in the FFT — anything that might be aliased content from above the Nyquist frequency. And fourth: identify at least one DAQ configuration improvement that would increase measurement quality, and document it. This analysis feeds directly into the DAQ configuration justification section of your group report and into your improvement proposal — and it is grounded in the theory we covered today. slide 11: Closure The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem is not an abstract mathematical curiosity — it is the fundamental constraint on every digital measurement you will make in an aerospace career. Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon, not the sensor, and choosing a sample rate without characterizing the bandwidth is making an untested claim about what the signal contains. Aliasing does not look like noise — it looks like a real signal at a plausible frequency, and the only protection against it is adequate sample rate and proper anti-aliasing filtering, both chosen before data collection begins. In the Week 7 lab, you will see this in real data from your Tier 2 experiment — the FFT analysis is not just a visualization exercise, it is the diagnostic tool for evaluating whether the sampling choices in your experiment are defensible. In Lecture 2 this week, we go one level deeper: the signal conditioning chain between the sensor and the DAQ, and how filtering, gain, and noise source management complete the picture of what it means to capture a signal faithfully. Agent Notes Content is grounded in the W7 L1 blueprint purpose statement: Nyquist theorem, aliasing mechanism, signal bandwidth as a property of the phenomenon, practical consequences. The stroboscopic wheel analogy (slide 4) is drawn from the W7 R1 reading description — using it in the lecture creates conceptual continuity with the companion reading. Anti-aliasing filter is introduced at the end of this lecture (slide 6) at awareness level; full signal conditioning chain is the subject of W7 L2. Slide 10 connects directly to the W7 lab activity and to the Tier 2 report DAQ configuration section. MATLAB FFT workflow (slide 8) previews the lab activity without duplicating it — students see the tool, then apply it. No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 4 primary treatment per blueprint. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Nyquist Sampling, Aliasing & Signal Bandwidth Talking Point: Before you can run a credible measurement on a complex aerospace system, you need to understand what you are asking the digital measurement system to do. A sensor converts a physical quantity into an analog electrical signal — a continuous stream of information about the world. A data acquisition system takes discrete snapshots of that signal at a fixed rate. Everything about whether those snapshots faithfully represent the original signal depends on one thing: how fast you sample relative to how fast the signal changes. That relationship is governed by the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, and when it is violated, the result is not noise — it is a plausible-looking signal that is completely wrong. That is the subject of this lecture. slide 2: What Signal Bandwidth Actually Is **Signal bandwidth** — the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information in the signal — is a property of the physical phenomenon, not the sensor or DAQ. (Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: a time-domain signal showing a complex waveform — a mix of a slow sinusoidal component and a faster oscillation superimposed on it. The signal is continuous, plotted as a smooth analog curve, with a label "Measured physical phenomenon — continuous in time." Right panel: the same signal's frequency-domain representation (FFT magnitude spectrum) plotted as a bar chart, with two visible peaks labeled "Low-frequency component: 10 Hz" and "High-frequency component: 340 Hz." A bracket spans from 0 to 340 Hz and is labeled "Signal bandwidth: 340 Hz — this is a property of the phenomenon, not the sensor." The visual contrast between the time domain (messy, complex) and the frequency domain (clean, peaks visible) is the main teaching point of this slide.) Talking Point: Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring — it is not a property of your sensor or your DAQ system. It describes the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information about the signal. A structural vibration signal from an aerospace component might have a dominant resonance at 10 hertz and a higher-frequency mode at 340 hertz. The signal bandwidth of that measurement extends to 340 hertz — regardless of what sample rate you choose. Why does this matter? Because if you choose a sample rate without knowing the signal bandwidth, you are making a claim about the signal's frequency content without evidence. The claim you are making is: "I believe this signal has no content above half my sample rate." If that claim is wrong, your data is not merely imprecise — it is silently corrupted in a way that looks real. slide 3: The Sampling Theorem — The Fundamental Constraint (Image: A diagram showing a continuous analog signal (smooth sinusoidal curve) with two sets of sample points overlaid. The top set of samples is spaced closely — labeled "Sample rate = 10× signal frequency." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a dashed curve through the sample points, closely tracks the original. The bottom set of samples is spaced too widely — labeled "Sample rate = 0.8× signal frequency (undersampled)." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a red dashed curve, produces a much lower-frequency sinusoid that does not match the original. A boxed statement in the center of the slide reads: "Nyquist Criterion: sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal." The original signal and the alias are clearly color-coded — original in gold, alias in red.) Talking Point: The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem tells us the minimum sample rate required to faithfully represent a signal. The requirement is simple: your sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal you are trying to measure. That frequency — half the sample rate — is called the Nyquist frequency, and it is the boundary above which information is lost. Note that I said "greater than twice" — not "equal to twice." In practice, engineers apply a margin above the theoretical minimum because real signals do not have perfectly sharp cutoffs. The practical rule is that your sample rate should be at least five to ten times the highest frequency of interest, depending on how well you can characterize the signal's frequency content. Choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment: you are claiming that the signal has no content above the Nyquist frequency, and if that claim is wrong, you will not know it from the data alone. slide 4: What Aliasing Actually Looks Like — The Stroboscopic Wheel (Image: A three-frame stroboscopic effect illustration. Frame 1: A wheel with a single marker dot, spinning at 10 revolutions per second, is illuminated by a strobe light flashing at 12 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly forward — dotted arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow forward." Frame 2: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 9 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly backward — arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow backward." Frame 3: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 10 Hz. The wheel appears to stop — labeled "Apparent motion: stationary." Below the three frames: "None of these stroboscopic observations are noise. They are coherent, plausible, and completely wrong." A caption reads: "This is aliasing. The observed frequency is the difference between the signal and the strobe — it is not in the original signal at all.") You cannot look at an aliased signal and know it is wrong by visual inspection. n (clean, peaks visible) is the main teaching point of this slide.) Talking Point: Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring — it is not a property of your sensor or your DAQ system. It describes the range of frequencies that contain meaningful information about the signal. A structural vibration signal from an aerospace component might have a dominant resonance at 10 hertz and a higher-frequency mode at 340 hertz. The signal bandwidth of that measurement extends to 340 hertz — regardless of what sample rate you choose. Why does this matter? Because if you choose a sample rate without knowing the signal bandwidth, you are making a claim about the signal's frequency content without evidence. The claim you are making is: "I believe this signal has no

claim about the signal's frequency content without evidence. The claim you are making is: "I believe this signal has no content above half my sample rate." If that claim is wrong, your data is not merely imprecise — it is silently corrupted in a way that looks real. slide 3: The Sampling Theorem — The Fundamental Constraint (Image: A diagram showing a continuous analog signal (smooth sinusoidal curve) with two sets of sample points overlaid. The top set of samples is spaced closely — labeled "Sample rate = 10× signal frequency." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a dashed curve through the sample points, closely tracks the original. The bottom set of samples is spaced too widely — labeled "Sample rate = 0.8× signal frequency (undersampled)." The resulting reconstructed signal,

is spaced too widely — labeled "Sample rate = 0.8× signal frequency (undersampled)." The resulting reconstructed signal, shown as a red dashed curve, produces a much lower-frequency sinusoid that does not match the original. A boxed statement in the center of the slide reads: "Nyquist Criterion: sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal." The original signal and the alias are clearly color-coded — original in gold, alias in red.) Talking Point: The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem tells us the minimum sample rate required to faithfully represent a signal. The requirement is simple: your sample rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency present in the signal you are trying to measure. That frequency — half the sample rate — is called the

highest frequency present in the signal you are trying to measure. That frequency — half the sample rate — is called the Nyquist frequency, and it is the boundary above which information is lost. Note that I said "greater than twice" — not "equal to twice." In practice, engineers apply a margin above the theoretical minimum because real signals do not have perfectly sharp cutoffs. The practical rule is that your sample rate should be at least five to ten times the highest frequency of interest, depending on how well you can characterize the signal's frequency content. Choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment: you are claiming that the signal has no content above the Nyquist frequency, and if that claim is wrong, you will not know it from the data alone. slide 4: What Aliasing

bove the Nyquist frequency, and if that claim is wrong, you will not know it from the data alone. slide 4: What Aliasing Actually Looks Like — The Stroboscopic Wheel (Image: A three-frame stroboscopic effect illustration. Frame 1: A wheel with a single marker dot, spinning at 10 revolutions per second, is illuminated by a strobe light flashing at 12 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly forward — dotted arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow forward." Frame 2: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 9 Hz. The wheel appears to spin slowly backward — arrow labeled "Apparent motion: slow backward." Frame 3: The same wheel spinning at 10 rev/s, strobe at 10 Hz. The wheel appears to stop — labeled "Apparent motion: stationary." Below the three frames: "None of these stroboscopic

. The wheel appears to stop — labeled "Apparent motion: stationary." Below the three frames: "None of these stroboscopic observations are noise. They are coherent, plausible, and completely wrong." A caption reads: "This is aliasing. The observed frequency is the difference between the signal and the strobe — it is not in the original signal at all.") Talking Point: The most important thing I want you to understand about aliasing is that it does not produce obvious noise — it produces a coherent, plausible-looking signal at a different frequency. The stroboscopic wheel is the perfect illustration. When a wheel spinning at ten revolutions per second is illuminated by a strobe flashing at twelve hertz, it appears to spin slowly forward at two revolutions per second. Your DAQ is the strobe —

robe flashing at twelve hertz, it appears to spin slowly forward at two revolutions per second. Your DAQ is the strobe — it observes the voltage signal at fixed intervals. If those intervals are too far apart relative to how fast the signal changes, the reconstructed waveform is as wrong as the backward-spinning wheel. The wheel is not actually slowing down — your measurement system is creating a false low-frequency signal out of the difference between the true signal and your sample rate. This is aliasing. The alias frequency is the difference between the true signal frequency and the sampling frequency, and it falls inside your measurement bandwidth — which is exactly why it is dangerous. You cannot look at an aliased signal and know it is wrong by visual inspection. It looks like a

tly why it is dangerous. You cannot look at an aliased signal and know it is wrong by visual inspection. It looks like a real, physical signal, because digitally, it is one. slide 5: Why Aliasing Is Invisible — The Silent Corruption Problem (Image: A side-by-side comparison. Left: a real physical vibration signal — a clean sinusoid at 380 Hz, shown in the frequency domain as a single spectral peak. Right: the same signal as captured by a DAQ system sampling at 500 Hz — the 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 - 380 = 120 Hz, appearing as a spectral peak at 120 Hz. Both frequency-domain plots are labeled with axes (frequency in Hz, magnitude in arbitrary units). The caption at the bottom reads: "The 120 Hz alias peak looks real. There is no visual marker in the data that indicates corruption." An

he bottom reads: "The 120 Hz alias peak looks real. There is no visual marker in the data that indicates corruption." An annotation on the right plot reads: "You would report this as a 120 Hz structural mode — and you would be wrong.") Talking Point: Here is the scenario I want you to sit with. You are running a structural vibration test on an aerospace component. You expect significant frequency content up to about 300 hertz. You set your sample rate at 500 hertz, which gives you a Nyquist frequency of 250 hertz. You are reasonably satisfied. What you do not know is that there is a structural mode at 380 hertz — well above your Nyquist frequency. That 380 hertz signal aliases into your measurement at 500 minus 380, which is 120 hertz. You now have a perfectly clean, well-resolved

signal aliases into your measurement at 500 minus 380, which is 120 hertz. You now have a perfectly clean, well-resolved spectral peak at 120 hertz that you did not expect. You write it up as a structural resonance you found, present it to your team, and potentially design a control system to avoid exciting it. None of that is real. There is no 120 hertz mode. The mode at 380 hertz is real, and it is invisible to your measurement. This is why aliasing is a silent corruption problem — it does not announce itself. The only protection is choosing a sufficient sample rate before you collect data. slide 6: Anti-Aliasing Filters — The Structural Solution (Image: A block diagram of the signal processing chain between a physical sensor and a digital data record. The chain has five stages from

agram of the signal processing chain between a physical sensor and a digital data record. The chain has five stages from left to right: "Physical sensor" → "Amplifier/Conditioning" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." The anti-aliasing filter stage is highlighted with a gold border. An annotation shows the filter's cutoff frequency labeled "fc < fs/2" (cutoff frequency must be below the Nyquist frequency). A second annotation on the ADC stage reads: "Once digitized, aliased content cannot be removed — information is permanently lost." A dashed vertical line between the anti-aliasing filter and the ADC is labeled "This line is the point of no return — aliasing must be prevented before digitization.") Talking Point: The

C is labeled "This line is the point of no return — aliasing must be prevented before digitization.") Talking Point: The structural solution to aliasing is the anti-aliasing filter: a low-pass filter placed in the signal conditioning chain before the analog-to-digital converter, designed to attenuate frequency content above the Nyquist frequency before it can be aliased into the digital record. The critical principle here is timing. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain — before the ADC — because once the signal has been digitized, the alias is already mixed into the data. A digital filter applied after digitization cannot un-alias a signal, because the aliased content and the real content at the same frequency are indistinguishable in the digital record. The

because the aliased content and the real content at the same frequency are indistinguishable in the digital record. The information that was lost when the signal was undersampled is permanently gone. This is why the design of the anti-aliasing filter must be decided before you set up your DAQ configuration — it is not a post-processing step, it is a pre-measurement design decision. slide 7: Choosing the Sample Rate — The Engineering Decision (Image: A horizontal decision diagram with three components. Component 1: "Characterize signal bandwidth" — shown as a frequency spectrum with the highest expected frequency labeled fmax. Component 2: "Apply Nyquist margin" — shown as the formula "fs ≥ 5 × fmax (practical engineering margin)" with a note "Theoretical minimum: fs > 2 × fmax." A

shown as the formula "fs ≥ 5 × fmax (practical engineering margin)" with a note "Theoretical minimum: fs > 2 × fmax." A footnote beneath Component 2 reads: "The 5–10× rule accounts for the anti-aliasing filter's transition band — it is not a universal constant. Lower margins may be acceptable with a steeper filter; wider margins may be needed when signal bandwidth is uncertain." Below the diagram: "Sample rate selection is a modeling commitment — you are claiming no energy above fs/2. Document your reasoning." The diagram uses a clean flowchart style with the course color scheme.) Talking Point: In practice, how

your reasoning." The diagram uses a clean flowchart style with the course color scheme.) Talking Point: In practice, how do you choose a sample rate? Start by characterizing the signal bandwidth — identify the highest frequency that contains information relevant to your measurement. For structural modes, this might come from a preliminary analysis of the natural frequencies using the geometry and material properties. For aerodynamic measurements, it might come from the expected turbulent fluctuation frequencies at your operating conditions. Once you have an estimate of the maximum frequency of interest, apply a practical margin: use a sample rate at least five to ten times that frequency, not just two times. The factor-of-two Nyquist minimum leaves almost no margin for filter roll-off,

times that frequency, not just two times. The factor-of-two Nyquist minimum leaves almost no margin for filter roll-off, hardware imperfections, or unexpected high-frequency content. Then verify that your chosen sample rate is achievable with your DAQ hardware given the number of channels and the resolution you need. Document this decision in your report — not just what sample rate you used, but why you chose it based on the expected signal bandwidth. slide 8: Identifying Signal Bandwidth from Real Data — MATLAB in the Loop (Image: Two MATLAB figure panels. Left panel: a time-domain signal from a structural vibration measurement — a decaying oscillation with visible high-frequency content superimposed. le high-frequency content superimposed. The x-axis is labeled "Time (s)," y-axis "Acceleration (m/s²)." Right panel: the frequency-domain FFT of the same signal, with the x-axis labeled "Frequency (Hz)" and y-axis "Magnitude (m/s²)." Two spectral peaks are labeled: "Primary mode: 47 Hz" and "Secondary mode: 312 Hz." A vertical dashed red line is labeled "Nyquist frequency for this measurement: 500 Hz (sample rate = 1000 Hz)." The bandwidth bracket extends from 0 to 312 Hz, with the note: "312 Hz << 500 Hz — adequate margin." The figure style matches MATLAB default with course-consistent axis labels.) Talking Point: In the Week 7 lab session, you are going to do exactly this — use MATLAB to visualize the signal from your Tier 2 canned experiment in both the time domain and the frequency

y this — use MATLAB to visualize the signal from your Tier 2 canned experiment in both the time domain and the frequency domain. The time-domain plot tells you a lot about the character of the signal: is it a steady oscillation, a decaying transient, a step response? But the frequency-domain FFT is what tells you about bandwidth. You are looking for the highest frequency at which there is meaningful spectral energy — not noise floor, but actual signal content. That frequency is the lower bound on your Nyquist frequency, and half of it is the minimum cutoff frequency for your anti-aliasing filter. In this example, the primary structural mode is at 47 hertz and the secondary mode is at 312 hertz. If the sample rate were 600 hertz instead of 1000, the secondary mode at 312 hertz would be

ondary mode is at 312 hertz. If the sample rate were 600 hertz instead of 1000, the secondary mode at 312 hertz would be right against the Nyquist limit with almost no margin — any filter imperfection would potentially alias secondary mode content into the measurement. The 1000 hertz sample rate gives you comfortable margin. Seeing this in your own data, for your own experiment, is what makes the Nyquist criterion real rather than abstract. slide 9: What Aliasing Looks Like in Aerospace Data — A Worked Example (Image: A simulated MATLAB FFT plot showing two scenarios side by side. Left: "Adequately sampled (fs = 2000 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz, 420 Hz, and 780 Hz, all clearly resolved within the 0–1000 Hz bandwidth. Right: "Undersampled (fs = 800 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz and

all clearly resolved within the 0–1000 Hz bandwidth. Right: "Undersampled (fs = 800 Hz)" — FFT shows peaks at 80 Hz and 420 Hz, but the 780 Hz component has aliased to 800 - 780 = 20 Hz, appearing as a prominent low-frequency peak. The 20 Hz alias peak is labeled in red: "780 Hz signal aliased to 20 Hz — reported as a structural mode if not recognized." Vertical dashed lines show the Nyquist frequency for each case: 1000 Hz on the left and 400 Hz on the right. A banner across the top reads: "Same physical signal — two entirely different frequency-domain representations based solely on sample rate choice.") Talking Point: Let me make aliasing numerically concrete. Here is a physical signal with three real components at 80, 420, and 780 hertz. On the left, sampled at 2000 hertz — all three

s a physical signal with three real components at 80, 420, and 780 hertz. On the left, sampled at 2000 hertz — all three components are cleanly resolved in the FFT. On the right, the same physical signal sampled at 800 hertz. The Nyquist frequency is now 400 hertz. The 80-hertz and 420-hertz components are there — but the 420-hertz component is already very close to the Nyquist limit and may be attenuated by the anti-aliasing filter if it is too aggressive. And the 780-hertz component aliases to 800 minus 780, which is 20 hertz. That 20-hertz peak is prominent in the undersampled FFT. If you did not know the truth, you would report it as a 20-hertz structural mode. You might even try to correlate it to some physical mechanism at that frequency. This is not a rare failure mode — it is

You might even try to correlate it to some physical mechanism at that frequency. This is not a rare failure mode — it is exactly the kind of error that gets made when sample rates are chosen without first characterizing the signal bandwidth. slide 10: Connecting to the Tier 2 Lab — What You Are Evaluating (Image: A structured checklist layout with four evaluation criteria. heck: fc < fs/2 and fc does not attenuate signal content of interest." Row 3: "Are there unexpected peaks in the FFT?" → "Investigate: could any be aliased content from above the Nyquist frequency?" Talking Point: This is what you will do with the tools from this lecture in the Week 7 lab session. You are going to examine the DAQ configuration for your Tier 2 canned experiment — the actual sample rate and filtering choices that were made — and evaluate whether

n for your Tier 2 canned experiment — the actual sample rate and filtering choices that were made — and evaluate whether those choices are defensible given the signal content you measure. First: compute the Nyquist frequency for the chosen sample rate and verify that it provides adequate margin above the signal bandwidth you measure in the FFT. Second: locate the anti-aliasing filter specification in the DAQ documentation and verify that the cutoff is below the Nyquist frequency without attenuating signal content of interest. Third: look for unexpected peaks in the FFT — anything that might be aliased content from above the Nyquist frequency. And fourth: identify at least one DAQ configuration improvement that would increase measurement quality, and document it. This analysis feeds

at least one DAQ configuration improvement that would increase measurement quality, and document it. This analysis feeds directly into the DAQ configuration justification section of your group report and into your improvement proposal — and it is grounded in the theory we covered today. slide 11: Closure The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem is not an abstract mathematical curiosity — it is the fundamental constraint on every digital measurement you will make in an aerospace career. Signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon, not the sensor, and choosing a sample rate without characterizing the bandwidth is making an untested claim about what the signal contains. Aliasing does not look like noise — it looks like a real signal at a plausible frequency, and the only protection

tains. Aliasing does not look like noise — it looks like a real signal at a plausible frequency, and the only protection against it is adequate sample rate and proper anti-aliasing filtering, both chosen before data collection begins. In the Week 7 lab, you will see this in real data from your Tier 2 experiment — the FFT analysis is not just a visualization exercise, it is the diagnostic tool for evaluating whether the sampling choices in your experiment are defensible. A sensor converts a physical quantity into an analog electrical signal — a continuous stream of information about the world. A data acquisition system takes discrete snapshots of that signal at a fixed rate. Everything about whether those snapshots faithfully represent the original signal depends on one thing: how fast you sample relative to how fast the signal changes. That relationship is governed

signal depends on one thing: how fast you sample relative to how fast the signal changes.
