anti-aliasing filter is not actually providing aliasing protection at this sample rate. Any content between 500 and 1000 hertz would alias into the measurement, and the filter is not attenuating it. The fix is straightforward: either increase the sample rate to at least 2 kilohertz so the Nyquist frequency is above the filter cutoff, or add a supplementary analog filter with a lower cutoff. This is exactly the kind of configuration evaluation you will do in the Week 7 lab — reading the instrument documentation, checking whether the configuration is self-consistent, and identifying where it is not. slide 9: Connecting to the Improvement Proposal — What You Are Looking For (Image: A two-column illustration. Left column header: "What to look for in the existing configuration." Three items

For (Image: A two-column illustration. Left column header: "What to look for in the existing configuration." Three items listed: "Is the sample rate high enough given the signal bandwidth?" | "Is the anti-aliasing filter cutoff appropriate?" | "Are there identifiable noise sources that could be reduced?" Right column header: "How to frame the improvement proposal." Three items listed: "Propose a specific sample rate with Nyquist margin calculation." | "Propose a specific filter cutoff and order with justification." | "Identify specific noise mitigation strategies (shielded cable, differential measurement, single-point ground)." A connecting arrow between the two columns is labeled "The improvement proposal is grounded in this analysis — not a generic 'use better equipment' suggestion." At

is labeled "The improvement proposal is grounded in this analysis — not a generic 'use better equipment' suggestion." At the bottom: "Improvement proposal category (b): DAQ/signal conditioning — this is the CLO 4 graded deliverable in the Tier 2 report.") Talking Point: Section 6 of your Tier 2 group report asks you to propose improvements to the experiment, and category (b) is specifically DAQ and signal conditioning. This is the CLO 4 graded deliverable of E2, and the standard is higher than "use a better sensor." A credible improvement proposal is grounded in your data and your analysis. If you found from the FFT that the signal has significant energy at frequencies above the current Nyquist frequency, you propose a specific increased sample rate with the Nyquist margin calculation

es above the current Nyquist frequency, you propose a specific increased sample rate with the Nyquist margin calculation shown. If you found that the anti-aliasing filter cutoff is poorly positioned relative to the sample rate, you propose a specific alternative cutoff with the filter order and the rationale. If you identified a ground loop in the noise floor, you propose differential measurement or cable shielding with an explanation of why the current configuration produces the noise. The improvement proposal is where the theory you learned this week gets applied to the specific experiment you ran. Here is what a fully quantified improvement argument looks like: the FFT reveals a structural mode at 380 Hz. Current sample rate is 500 Hz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 250 Hz — below the

FFT reveals a structural mode at 380 Hz. Current sample rate is 500 Hz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 250 Hz — below the 380 Hz mode. The 380 Hz signal aliases to 500 − 380 = 120 Hz and appears as a false spectral peak. Proposed improvement: increase sample rate to 2 kHz, giving a Nyquist frequency of 1 kHz — adequate margin above a potential 780 Hz secondary mode. That is the level of quantification the rubric awards for improvement proposal excellence. slide 10: The Signal Conditioning Choices Are Modeling Decisions (Image: A diagram showing the concept of signal conditioning as a modeling commitment. The center of the diagram has a bold statement: "Every signal conditioning choice encodes an assumption about the signal." Three examples branch from the center: "Sample rate choice" →

nditioning choice encodes an assumption about the signal." Three examples branch from the center: "Sample rate choice" → "Assumes: no meaningful signal energy above fs/2." "Filter cutoff choice" → "Assumes: no signal content between fc and fs/2 that matters for the intended use." "Gain choice" → "Assumes: maximum signal amplitude will not exceed ADC input range." Each assumption has a consequence box attached: "Violated sample rate assumption → aliasing." "Violated filter cutoff assumption → attenuated signal content OR aliased content not removed." "Violated gain assumption → clipping — signal is permanently distorted." The visual style uses a spider diagram or concept map layout, with the central statement in a large gold circle and the branches radiating outward.) Talking Point: I want

map layout, with the central statement in a large gold circle and the branches radiating outward.) Talking Point: I want to close this lecture with the unifying frame. Every signal conditioning choice you make is a modeling decision — it encodes an assumption about the signal you are trying to capture. Choosing a sample rate of 2 kilohertz means you are claiming the signal has no meaningful energy above 1 kilohertz. Choosing an anti-aliasing filter cutoff of 800 hertz means you are claiming that any signal content between 800 hertz and 1 kilohertz can be discarded without affecting your measurement quality for the intended use. Choosing a gain of 100 means you are claiming the maximum signal amplitude will not exceed 5 volts after amplification. Each of these assumptions can be wrong, and

ng the maximum signal amplitude will not exceed 5 volts after amplification. Each of these assumptions can be wrong, and when they are, the consequences range from reduced precision to completely corrupted data — and the corrupted data will not always be obviously wrong. The only protection is making these decisions based on analysis of the expected signal, documenting the reasoning, and being prepared to defend each choice when your measurement is scrutinized. That is what makes a measurement credible, and that is what the DAQ configuration section of your report is for. slide 11: Closure Signal conditioning is not a black box that happens before your MATLAB workspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the physical signal you are trying to

kspace — it is a sequence of design decisions that each encode an assumption about the physical signal you are trying to capture. Amplification matches sensor output to ADC dynamic range and controls quantization error contribution. The anti-aliasing filter must be placed in the analog domain before the ADC, with a cutoff below the Nyquist frequency, because aliased content cannot be removed after digitization. Noise enters the chain at every stage — from the sensor, from the cable, from the amplifier, from the ground configuration — and identifying and documenting known noise sources is a required part of your experimental report. Analog and digital filtering serve different purposes and must not be confused: digital filters cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Every one of these

e different purposes and must not be confused: digital filters cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Every one of these choices belongs in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report, argued from the signal bandwidth and the intended use of the measurement. The Week 7 lab will give you the data and the documentation to perform that analysis for your own experiment. Agent Notes Content is grounded in the W7 L2 blueprint purpose statement: signal conditioning chain, amplification, anti-aliasing filter design rationale, noise sources, analog vs. digital filtering contrast. The analog vs. digital filtering contrast (slide 6) is specifically required by the blueprint: "an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles." Slide 10 frames all signal

uired by the blueprint: "an explicit segment contrasting analog and digital filtering roles." Slide 10 frames all signal conditioning choices as modeling commitments — connecting this lecture to the AMVF spine and the E2 prediction discipline. The worked example in slide 8 uses the sensor spec evaluation format that students will apply in the W7 lab. Slide 9 connects directly to the Tier 2 improvement proposal category (b), per blueprint note about the lab feeding into the group report. No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 4 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Analog Signal Conditioning — Filtering, Noise & Gain Talking Point: In the last lecture, we established that choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment — you are claiming that the physical signal has no meaningful

ished that choosing a sample rate is a modeling commitment — you are claiming that the physical signal has no meaningful content above the Nyquist frequency. But the sample rate is only part of the story. Between the physical sensor and the DAQ digitizer, the signal passes through a conditioning chain: it gets amplified, filtered, and modified in ways that can improve the measurement — or corrupt it — depending on the choices made. This lecture covers that chain: what each stage does, why it is there, and the most important design decisions you need to make and document before any data is collected. slide 2: The Signal Conditioning Chain — An Overview (Image: A horizontal block diagram of the complete signal conditioning chain from physical phenomenon to digital data record. Five blocks

tal block diagram of the complete signal conditioning chain from physical phenomenon to digital data record. Five blocks connected by arrows, left to right: "Physical Sensor / Transducer" → "Signal Amplifier (Gain + Offset)" → "Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-pass)" → "Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)" → "Digital Data Record." Each block has a small icon below it: the sensor has a waveform, the amplifier has a gain triangle symbol, the filter has a frequency roll-off curve, the ADC has a staircase digitization symbol, and the data record has a MATLAB plot icon. A bracket below the first three blocks is labeled "Analog domain — these stages happen before digitization." A bracket below the last two blocks is labeled "Digital domain — once here, analog information is locked in." The color of the

ket below the last two blocks is labeled "Digital domain — once here, analog information is locked in." The color of the analog domain bracket is gold; the digital domain bracket is a lighter gray.) Talking Point: Let me give you the full picture of what happens between a physical event and a number in a MATLAB variable. The sensor transduces the physical quantity into an electrical signal — typically a voltage or current proportional to what is being measured. That signal then passes through an amplifier, which increases the signal level to a range that the ADC can resolve effectively. It then passes through the anti-aliasing filter, which removes frequency content above the Nyquist frequency. Then the ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at the chosen rate and converts each sample

ve the Nyquist frequency. Then the ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at the chosen rate and converts each sample to a digital number. Every design decision in the analog domain — how much gain, what filter cutoff, what filter order — affects the quality of the digital record. And critically: none of these effects can be undone after digitization. If you amplified the wrong band, you cannot de-amplify it. If you filtered with the wrong cutoff, you cannot unfilter. The measurement is fixed the moment it crosses into the digital domain. slide 3: Gain and Offset — Why Amplification Is a Design Decision (Image: A two-panel illustration. Left panel: "Under-amplified signal" — a small-amplitude sensor output waveform (about 10% of the ADC input range) plotted on a voltage axis labeled 0

ignal" — a small-amplitude sensor output waveform (about 10% of the ADC input range) plotted on a voltage axis labeled 0 to 10 V. The waveform only occupies 0 to 1 V of the range. A label reads: "Signal uses only 10% of ADC range — poor resolution, high quantization error contribution." Right panel: "Properly amplified signal" — the same waveform amplified to occupy 60–80% of the ADC input range. A label reads: "Signal occupies 70% of range — resolution optimized." Below both panels, a caption: "Gain selection directly controls how much of the ADC's resolution is used to represent the signal. An under-amplified signal wastes bits." A small inset shows the ADC quantization staircase to reinforce the concept.) Talking Point: The purpose of the amplifier stage is to match the sensor's output

n staircase to reinforce the concept.) Talking Point: The purpose of the amplifier stage is to match the sensor's output range to the ADC's input range — to use as much of the ADC's dynamic range as possible without clipping. An ADC converts an analog voltage to a digital integer within its input range. If your sensor output spans 0.1 volts but your ADC input range is 0 to 10 volts, you are using only one percent of the available digital counts to represent the entire signal. Every bit that you are not using is measurement precision that you are throwing away. Proper gain selection puts the signal in the operating region where the ADC provides meaningful resolution. This is directly related to quantization error — the uncertainty introduced by the discrete nature of the ADC output.

n. This is directly related to quantization error — the uncertainty introduced by the discrete nature of the ADC output. Quantization error is typically estimated as plus-or-minus half of one least-significant-bit, and its contribution to the total measurement uncertainty is smaller when the signal occupies more of the ADC range. slide 4: Noise Sources in the Signal Chain — What You Are Fighting (Image: A layered diagram of the signal conditioning chain with noise sources identified at each stage. The sensor block has an arrow labeled "Sensor noise: thermal noise (Johnson noise), sensitivity drift." The signal cable between sensor and amplifier has a lightning bolt icon labeled "Electromagnetic interference (EMI): coupling from nearby power lines, motors, RF sources." The amplifier block

labeled "Electromagnetic interference (EMI): coupling from nearby power lines, motors, RF sources." The amplifier block has an arrow labeled "Amplifier noise: thermal noise, bias current." A ground symbol between components is labeled "Ground loops: current flowing through shared ground path produces measurable voltage offset." Each noise source has an estimated contribution in parentheses: "sensor thermal noise: typically μV level," "EMI: can be mV level," "amplifier noise: typically nV to μV," "ground loops: can be mV to hundreds of mV." The visual is a chain diagram with noise injected at each stage — cumulative noise accumulates through the chain.) Talking Point: Noise enters the signal chain at every stage, and understanding where it comes from is the first step toward controlling

Noise enters the signal chain at every stage, and understanding where it comes from is the first step toward controlling it. The sensor itself introduces thermal noise — a fundamental quantum-mechanical effect that is proportional to temperature and resistance. The cable between the sensor and the amplifier can pick up electromagnetic interference from nearby power lines, motors, or radio-frequency sources. The amplifier adds its own noise, and if the circuit does not have a clean, single-point ground reference, current flowing through the shared ground path can add a DC offset or a 60-hertz hum to the signal. Ground loops are one of the most common causes of unexplained noise in experimental measurements, and they are almost entirely preventable with careful grounding practice. A ground

d noise in experimental measurements, and they are almost entirely preventable with careful grounding practice. A ground loop typically shows up as a 60 Hz peak in the FFT — a regular spike at the power line frequency that doesn't move regardless of how the physical system is loaded or excited. The takeaway is that noise is not a fixed property of your measurement system — it depends on how the system is assembled and set up, and improving signal quality sometimes requires changing the setup, not the hardware. slide 5: The Anti-Aliasing Filter — Design Rationale and Cutoff Selection (Image: A frequency-domain plot showing the amplitude response of a low-pass filter on the y-axis (in dB, from 0 to −60 dB) and frequency on the x-axis (from 0 to twice the sampling frequency). The filter has

he y-axis (in dB, from 0 to −60 dB) and frequency on the x-axis (from 0 to twice the sampling frequency). The filter has a flat passband extending from 0 to the cutoff frequency fc, a transition band from fc to the Nyquist frequency fs/2, and a stopband attenuating above fs/2. Three vertical dashed lines mark: fc (filter cutoff), fmax (highest frequency of signal interest), and fs/2 (Nyquist frequency). The relationships between these three frequencies are labeled: "fc must be above fmax — do not attenuate signal content," "fc must be below fs/2 — all content above Nyquist must be attenuated before ADC," "Transition band determines filter order — steeper rolloff requires higher-order filter." A note reads: "There is always a tradeoff: narrower transition band = higher filter order = more

s higher-order filter." A note reads: "There is always a tradeoff: narrower transition band = higher filter order = more phase distortion.") Talking Point: The anti-aliasing filter's job is precise: it must attenuate all frequency content above the Nyquist frequency sufficiently that no alias can contaminate the measurement, while passing all signal content of interest without attenuation. The cutoff frequency sits in the transition zone between "signal content I want" and "content that would alias." This means the cutoff must be above the highest frequency of interest — otherwise you are filtering out signal — and it must be below the Nyquist frequency — otherwise you are not completing the anti-aliasing job. The width of the transition band is controlled by the filter order: a

wise you are not completing the anti-aliasing job. The width of the transition band is controlled by the filter order: a higher-order filter has a steeper rolloff, which gives you more separation between the passband and the stopband for a given sampling rate. But there is a tradeoff: higher-order filters introduce more phase distortion in the passband, which can affect the timing relationships in the signal. This is not a deep filter design course — but you need to be able to read a filter specification and understand whether the cutoff is in the right place relative to your signal bandwidth and sample rate. slide 6: Analog vs. Digital Filtering — The Critical Distinction (Image: A two-column comparison table. Column header left: "Analog filtering (pre-ADC)." Column header right:

istinction (Image: A two-column comparison table. Column header left: "Analog filtering (pre-ADC)." Column header right: "Digital filtering (post-ADC)." Row 1: "When it happens" | "Before digitization — in the signal conditioning chain" | "After digitization — in MATLAB or DSP hardware." Row 2 [BOLD/HIGHLIGHTED]: "Can prevent aliasing?" | "YES — that is its primary purpose" | "NO — aliased content is already mixed into the digital data; it cannot be separated." Row 3: "Introduces phase delay?" | "Yes, order-dependent" | "Yes, filter-type-dependent; can be zero-phase with filtfilt in MATLAB." Row 4: "Can introduce artifacts?" | "Rarely, if properly designed" | "Yes — ringing near sharp transitions, edge effects, passband ripple." Row 5: "Primary use" | "Anti-aliasing before ADC; noise

ringing near sharp transitions, edge effects, passband ripple." Row 5: "Primary use" | "Anti-aliasing before ADC; noise rejection" | "Post-processing: smoothing, band selection, noise removal from already-captured signal." A bold annotation at the bottom reads: "Anti-aliasing must happen in the analog domain. This is not a preference — it is a physical requirement." The 'Can prevent aliasing?' row is visually emphasized — bold text or highlighted background — to distinguish it from the other rows.) Talking Point: You do not need to design filters — you need to know which domain handles which problem. The single most important row in this table is the one about aliasing prevention. I want to be direct about the most important practical implication of this table. The anti-aliasing filter

g prevention. I want to be direct about the most important practical implication of this table. The anti-aliasing filter must operate in the analog domain, before the analog-to-digital converter, because once a signal has been aliased into the digital record, no digital filter can separate it from the legitimate signal content at the same frequency. Digital filters — including MATLAB's filtfilt and butter functions — are powerful post-processing tools, and they have real uses: smoothing noisy data, extracting a specific frequency band from a captured record, or removing a known noise frequency. But they are applied to data that has already been digitized, which means they cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Additionally, digital filters introduce their own artifacts: zero-phase filters

y cannot retroactively correct aliasing. Additionally, digital filters introduce their own artifacts: zero-phase filters like filtfilt perform the filtering twice in opposite directions, which removes phase delay but doubles the transient effects at the edges of the data record. Finite impulse response filters can introduce ringing near sharp transitions. You do not need to design filters from scratch in this course. You do need to know that the choice of whether to filter in the analog or digital domain is a consequential design decision, and it must be documented in any credible experimental report. slide 7: Documentation Requirements — Why Signal Conditioning Is a Required Report Section (Image: A structured documentation template showing required entries for a DAQ configuration

s a Required Report Section (Image: A structured documentation template showing required entries for a DAQ configuration justification section. Six rows: Row 1 "Sample rate selected" — value and Nyquist frequency derived. Row 2 "Signal bandwidth estimate" — how estimated (from model, from literature, from preliminary measurement). Row 3 "Anti-aliasing filter" — type, cutoff frequency, order, vendor and model if applicable. Row 4 "Gain setting" — value and basis (ADC input range vs. expected sensor output range). Row 5 "Noise sources identified" — at least two named, estimated magnitude, mitigation applied. Row 6 "Known limitations" — any configuration choice that could affect data quality, with rationale for why it was accepted. The template is formatted as a simple table with a header

d affect data quality, with rationale for why it was accepted. The template is formatted as a simple table with a header "DAQ Configuration Justification — [Experiment Name]" and a date field. The visual communicates that this is a formal required document, not a checkbox.) Talking Point: Every one of the choices we discussed today — sample rate, gain, filter cutoff, filter order, grounding configuration — is a required documentation item in the DAQ configuration justification section of your Tier 2 group report. Not because documentation is bureaucratic, but because these choices affect whether your data is trustworthy, and a reader who wants to evaluate your validation claim needs to know that you made these choices deliberately and can explain them. The DAQ configuration section is not

claim needs to know that you made these choices deliberately and can explain them. The DAQ configuration section is not a settings list — it is an argument. You are arguing that the choices you made are appropriate for the signal you are trying to capture, given the bandwidth of the physical phenomenon, the Nyquist criterion, the range of the sensor output, and the noise environment of the measurement. If you cannot justify each choice, you cannot defend your data, and you cannot make a credible validation claim. This documentation habit is exactly what distinguishes a well-run experimental campaign from a black-box measurement. slide 8: Worked Example — Evaluating a Sensor's Signal Conditioning Setup (Image: A one-page instrument specification excerpt mockup showing four key parameters:

nsor's Signal Conditioning Setup (Image: A one-page instrument specification excerpt mockup showing four key parameters: "Sensor output range: 0 to 50 mV (full scale)," "Noise floor: 5 μV RMS," "Recommended gain: 100 (amplified output: 0 to 5 V)," "Anti-aliasing filter: 2nd-order Butterworth, −3 dB at 1 kHz (−3 dB means the filter has reduced the signal amplitude to approximately 71% of its original value at this frequency)." Below the spec excerpt, a MATLAB figure shows the frequency-domain FFT of the amplified sensor output, with a spectral peak at 240 Hz labeled "Primary signal component" and a flat noise floor. A vertical dashed line at 1000 Hz is labeled "Filter cutoff," and a vertical dashed line at 500 Hz (half the cutoff, assuming a 1000 Hz sample rate) is labeled "Nyquist

lter cutoff," and a vertical dashed line at 500 Hz (half the cutoff, assuming a 1000 Hz sample rate) is labeled "Nyquist frequency." An annotation reads: "Signal bandwidth (240 Hz) << Nyquist (500 Hz) — adequate margin. But: filter cutoff (1000 Hz) is above Nyquist (500 Hz) — the filter is NOT providing anti-aliasing protection at this sample rate." The problem is highlighted in red.) Talking Point: Let me walk through a real evaluation using actual instrument specifications. The sensor has a full-scale output range of 0 to 50 millivolts, a noise floor of 5 microvolts RMS, a recommended gain of 100 — which brings the output to 0 to 5 volts, matching a standard ADC input range — and a built-in second-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter with a negative-3-dB cutoff at 1 kilohertz. range — and a built-in second-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter with a negative-3-dB cutoff at 1 kilohertz. If you are sampling at 1000 hertz, the Nyquist frequency is 500 hertz. The filter cutoff is at 1 kilohertz — which is above the Nyquist frequency. That means the anti-aliasing filter is not actually providing aliasing protection at this sample rate. Any content between 500 and 1000 hertz would alias into the measurement, and the filter is not attenuating it. The fix is straightforward: either increase the sample rate to at least 2 kilohertz so the Nyquist frequency is above the filter cutoff, or add a supplementary analog filter with a lower cutoff. pages.title: R01
pages.text: E2 Week 2 (Course Week 7) Reading R01 # Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement ## Why the fastest thing you can't see can still destroy your data Watch a helicopter's rotor blades in a film and you will sometimes see them appear to rotate... Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement Why the fastest thing you can't see can still destroy your data Watch a helicopter's rotor blades in a film and you will sometimes see them appear to rotate backward — or hover perfectly still at full throttle. Nothing is wrong with the blades. Something is wrong with the camera. A film camera captures still frames at a fixed rate — typically 24 per second. If the blades complete almost exactly one full rotation between each frame, they appear stationary in the footage. If they complete slightly more than a full rotation, they appear to spin slowly backward. The blades are moving at full speed. The camera, sampling the scene too slowly relative to the rotation rate, produces an artifact that looks like real motion. This optical illusion is not just a curiosity. It is a precise physical analogy for one of the most consequential pitfalls in digital data acquisition: aliasing . Every time you ran data collection in E1, someone had already chosen the sample rate for you. In E2, that decision is yours to justify. Every digital measurement system — every DAQ board, every microcontroller analog input, every data logger — works the same way the film camera does. It takes samples of a continuous physical signal at discrete moments in time. Between samples, it knows nothing. The physical signal could be doing anything, and the measurement system will never know. The theorem formalizes the helicopter illusion precisely. The same artifact appears in accelerometer data from a vibration test when sample rate is insufficient: the 380 Hz structural mode appears in your data file as a 120 Hz signal, and there is nothing in the data that tells you it is wrong. Here is the formal statement: the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem establishes the fundamental limit: to faithfully capture a signal that contains frequency content up to some maximum frequency f_max , you must sample at a rate of at least 2f_max . This threshold — twice the highest frequency in the signal — is called the Nyquist rate . Sample faster than this and you can reconstruct the original signal. Sample slower, and you cannot. What happens when you sample slower than the Nyquist rate? The same thing that happened to the helicopter blades. High-frequency content in the signal does not disappear — it folds back into the measured spectrum and appears as a false low-frequency signal. A vibration at 500 Hz, sampled at 600 samples per second, will appear in your data as a 100 Hz oscillation. That 100 Hz component is not in the physical system. It is an artifact of the sampling process. And critically: it looks exactly like a real signal. There is no asterisk next to it, no error flag, no warning in the data file. It is a ghost that wears the costume of truth. Here is the part that trips up experienced engineers: signal bandwidth is a property of the physical phenomenon you are measuring, not a property of your sensor or your DAQ system. If you are measuring acoustic vibrations in a rocket nozzle, the signal bandwidth is determined by the dynamics of the combustion process — not by your microphone, not by your DAQ board, and not by whatever sampling rate you happened to inherit from a previous project. Before you choose a sampling rate, you must ask: what is the highest frequency my signal of interest actually contains? This sounds simple. In practice it requires genuine knowledge of the physics. A structural vibration test on a beam might have fundamental modes below 100 Hz and essentially no relevant content above 1 kHz — a 2,500 samples-per-second rate would be conservative and appropriate. A combustion pressure transient in a rocket engine test might contain critical content up to 50 kHz — that same 2,500 samples-per-second rate would alias catastrophically, producing clean-looking data that is entirely fictitious. When you commit to a sampling rate, you are making a claim. You are stating, before you collect any data, that your signal contains no frequency content above half your chosen sampling rate. If that claim is wrong — if the signal has energy at higher frequencies that you didn't anticipate — the data is silently corrupted. No error message. No obvious artifact. Just a dataset that reflects a physical reality that doesn't exist. Anti-aliasing filters exist to enforce this claim in hardware. By attenuating signal content above the Nyquist frequency before the signal reaches the analog-to-digital converter, they prevent high-frequency content from folding into the measured spectrum. They are not optional safeguards — they are the mechanism that makes the Nyquist guarantee meaningful. Their design (specifically, where to set the cutoff frequency relative to the signal bandwidth and the sampling rate) is one of the topics the W7 lectures will address directly. This reading stops here, before filter design, before DAQ configuration choices, before MATLAB analysis. Those topics are covered in the companion reading (R2) and in the Week 7 lectures. The job of this reading is narrower: ensure that before you walk into class, you understand why these choices matter. Sampling rate is not a technical setting you accept as a lab default. It is a modeling commitment. It encodes assumptions about the physical world that, if wrong, produce data that looks real and is wrong. When your Tier 2 canned experiment uses a specific sampling rate, you should be able to defend that choice on physical grounds — and identify what you would be missing if the rate were too low. The Takeaway: Aliasing is not an obscure artifact of bad equipment — it is a fundamental consequence of digital sampling that is invisible in the data itself, and the only defense is understanding the physics of your signal well enough to make the Nyquist commitment confidently before you collect the first data point.

Sampling, Aliasing & the Limits of Digital Measurement Why the fastest thing you can't see can still destroy your data Watch a helicopter's rotor blades in a film and you will sometimes see them appear to rotate backward — or hover perfectly still at full throttle. Nothing is wrong with the blades. Something is wrong with the camera. A film camera captures still frames at a fixed rate — typically 24 per second. If the blades complete almost exactly one full rotation between each frame, they appear stationary in the footage. If they

the blades complete almost exactly one full rotation between each frame, they appear stationary in the footage. If they complete slightly more than a full rotation, they appear to spin slowly backward. The blades are moving at full speed. The camera, sampling the scene too slowly relative to the rotation rate, produces an artifact that looks like real motion. This optical illusion is not just a curiosity. It is a precise physical analogy for one of the most consequential pitfalls in digital data acquisition: aliasing . Every time you ran data collection in E1, someone had already chosen the sample rate for you. In E2, that decision is yours to justify. Every digital measurement system — every DAQ board, every microcontroller analog input, every data logger — works the same way the film

easurement system — every DAQ board, every microcontroller analog input, every data logger — works the same way the film camera does. It takes samples of a continuous physical signal at discrete moments in time. Between samples, it knows nothing. The physical signal could be doing anything, and the measurement system will never know. The theorem formalizes the helicopter illusion precisely. The same artifact appears in accelerometer data from a vibration test when sample rate is insufficient: the 380 Hz structural mode appears in your data file as a 120 Hz signal, and there is nothing in the data that tells you it is wrong. Here is the formal statement: the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem establishes the fundamental limit: to faithfully capture a signal that contains frequency content up

on sampling theorem establishes the fundamental limit: to faithfully capture a signal that contains frequency content up to some maximum frequency f_max , you must sample at a rate of at least 2f_max . pages.title: R02
pages.text: E2 Week 2 (Course Week 7) Reading R02 # DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference ## Every configuration choice is a claim about your signal. Treat it that way. A data acquisition system handed to you with factory defaults is not... DAQ Configuration Choices & Practical Setup — A Decision Reference Every configuration choice is a claim about your signal. Treat it that way. A data acquisition system handed to you with factory defaults is not a neutral tool. It is a collection of someone else's assumptions about what you are measuring. Quick-Reference: DAQ Configuration Decisions Choice Key Question Decision Rule Channel configuration Is the signal source floating or subject to ground loop noise? Default differential; use single-ended only for large signals with shared ground and short cables Input range What is the expected signal amplitude? Set to the smallest range that comfortably brackets the signal; do not accept ±10 V defaults for millivolt signals Sample rate What is the signal bandwidth, and what anti-aliasing margin is required? Sample at 5× to 10× the signal bandwidth; document the Nyquist argument explicitly Anti-aliasing filter cutoff What is the transition band of the filter relative to the chosen sample rate? Set cutoff at ≤ 0.4 × sample rate; document relationship to signal bandwidth Triggering Does the event onset need to be captured, or is the signal slowly varying? Hardware trigger for transient events; software trigger acceptable for quasi-static signals Understanding those assumptions (channel configuration, resolution, sample rate, triggering) is not optional bookkeeping. Each choice encodes a constraint on what the system can faithfully represent, and each constraint contributes its own error source to your measurement. This reading is a reference document. Use it when setting up your Tier 2 experiment and again when drafting the DAQ configuration justification section of the Tier 2 group report. Unlike the companion reading (R1), which builds physical intuition for aliasing and sampling, this reading is deliberately dense: it is meant to be consulted, not just read once. Channel Configuration: Single-Ended vs. Differential Most DAQ systems offer two channel configuration modes, and the difference matters whenever noise or ground potential is a concern. Single-ended configuration measures voltage between the signal line and a shared system ground. It uses twice as many channels for a given number of signals compared to differential, which makes it attractive when channel count is a constraint. The vulnerability: any noise voltage that appears between the sensor's ground reference and the DAQ system's ground reference is measured as signal. In environments with multiple power supplies, motors, or long cable runs, this ground-referencing error, called a ground loop , can swamp a low-level sensor signal. Differential configuration measures voltage between two dedicated signal lines, rejecting any voltage that appears equally on both (called common-mode rejection ). Differential inputs are the right choice whenever your signal source is floating, or whenever the signal amplitude is small enough that ground loop noise is a significant fraction of the signal magnitude. A floating signal source has no connection to the local measurement ground: battery-powered sensors and isolated transducers are common examples, and their reference point may drift relative to the DAQ ground. Most precision measurement applications — strain gauges, thermocouples, load cells — use differential configuration for this reason. The practical decision: single-ended is acceptable when the signal is large (volts, not millivolts), cable runs are short, and all equipment shares a common ground. In any other case, default to differential. Resolution: Quantization Error and Its Contribution to Uncertainty Every analog-to-digital converter ( ADC ) maps a continuous voltage range onto a finite number of discrete digital levels. A 12-bit ADC divides its input range into 2¹² = 4,096 discrete levels. A 16-bit ADC divides the same range into 65,536 levels. The voltage difference between adjacent levels is the quantization step size — also called the least significant bit (LSB) voltage. For a ±10 V input range on a 12-bit ADC, the quantization step size is 20 V / 4,096 ≈ 4.9 mV. That is the smallest voltage change the system can detect. Any signal change smaller than one LSB is invisible — it either rounds up to the next level or rounds down. The resulting error is quantization error , and it contributes to the total measurement uncertainty. For most well-designed experiments, quantization error is dominated by other error sources (sensor noise, thermal drift, amplifier noise). But there are important exceptions. If you are measuring a slowly varying, low-amplitude signal (structural creep, thermal expansion, very low-level acoustic vibration) and your input range is set far wider than the signal amplitude, the effective resolution shrinks dramatically. A signal that occupies only 1% of the ADC input range is being measured with roughly 100 times worse effective resolution than the ADC spec implies. The practical decision: set the input range to the smallest value that comfortably brackets your expected signal. Do not accept ±10 V defaults when your signal is ±100 mV. Sample Rate: Nyquist Argument Plus Engineering Margin The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem (covered in R1) establishes a lower bound: sample at least twice the highest frequency in your signal. In practice, that lower bound is a starting point, not a design target. A signal sampled at exactly twice its highest frequency requires a perfect, infinitely sharp anti-aliasing filter — which does not exist in hardware. Real anti-aliasing filters have a transition band: a frequency range over which the attenuation rolls off gradually from passband to stopband. To accommodate this transition band, the practical rule is to sample at a rate 5× to 10× the signal bandwidth , not 2×. This margin relaxes the filter requirements and provides buffer against uncertainty in the exact upper frequency of the signal. When documenting your sample rate choice in the Tier 2 report, the required argument is: (1) the estimated signal bandwidth (from physical reasoning, not from the data), (2) the anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency, (3) the chosen sampling rate and the margin it provides over the Nyquist minimum, and (4) any known limitations if the margin is tight. Triggering and Synchronization Many aerospace experiments involve transient events — a structure loaded to failure, a motor spun up to operating speed, a thermal cycle initiated. In these cases, data collection must be synchronized to the event. Software triggering starts acquisition when a command is issued. The delay between the command and the first sample is on the order of milliseconds — acceptable for slow-varying signals, problematic for transient events. Hardware triggering starts acquisition when an analog voltage crosses a threshold or a digital line goes high, with sub-microsecond response. For any experiment where the timing of data collection relative to the physical event matters, hardware triggering is required. Pre-trigger buffers allow the DAQ system to capture data that occurred before the trigger event — the system records continuously, and the trigger selects the retention window. This is essential for capturing the onset of a transient before the threshold condition is detected. For example: if you want to capture the onset of a structural impact, hardware triggering on a load cell threshold ensures data collection starts the moment the load exceeds a set value, not a few milliseconds later when the computer catches up. For slowly varying signals (thermal measurements, quasi-static loading), software triggering is usually sufficient. A Worked Example: The Bit-Rollover Artifact This example requires knowing that some sensors count events as unsigned integers (always positive, values 0 to 65,535) while many DAQ systems record data as signed integers (positive and negative). When a sensor wraps from its maximum count back to zero, a signed integer recording sees a large negative jump. Consider a counter-based displacement sensor that outputs a count value from 0 to 65,535 (a 16-bit unsigned integer) representing a full rotation. The sensor counts up as the shaft rotates in one direction. If the shaft reverses just past the 65,535 count, the next reading is 0 — a rollover. When that value is recorded by a DAQ system that stores the output as a signed 16-bit integer, the jump from 65,535 to 0 appears in the data as a sudden, large negative-going step. In a time-series plot, this step looks like a real physical event — a rapid reversal, a discontinuity, an impulse. It is not. It is a sensor counter rollover recorded by a DAQ system that was not configured to handle unsigned output. Only knowledge of the sensor's output format and awareness of the DAQ configuration reveals the artifact for what it is. Uncertainty analysis cannot identify this type of artifact — it looks like a physical reading that is within sensor range. The defense is documentation: reading the DAQ configuration against the sensor datasheet before collecting data, not after. Documentation Requirement Every DAQ configuration choice must be documented in your Tier 2 report with justification, not just a list of settings. The required entries for each choice: Choice What to document Channel configuration Single-ended or differential; reason Input range Value; why this range was chosen for the signal amplitude Sample rate Value; Nyquist argument; margin above minimum Anti-aliasing filter cutoff Value; relationship to signal bandwidth and sample rate Triggering Type; reason; pre-trigger buffer if applicable Resolution ADC bit depth; quantization step size; assessment of whether it limits the measurement A DAQ configuration section that lists settings without justification does not demonstrate that the choices were made defensibly. The goal is to show that you understood what each setting controls and chose it on physical grounds. The Takeaway: DAQ configuration is not a setup task to complete before the real work begins. It is part of the measurement design, and every undocumented default is an undocumented assumption about your signal that may or may not be valid for your experiment.

pages.title: L01
pages.text: E2 Week 3 (Course Week 8) Lecture L01 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Model-Form Error & Discrepancy as Diagnostic Information Talking Point: This lecture addresses what I consider the hardest conceptual shift of Phase II, and I want to state it plainly at th... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Model-Form Error & Discrepancy as Diagnostic Information Talking Point: This lecture addresses what I consider the hardest conceptual shift of Phase II, and I want to state it plainly at the start. When the data comes back from your Tier 2 experiment and it does not match your prediction perfectly — and it will not — your first instinct is going to be to explain the discrepancy away. To say the sensors drifted, or something went wrong with the setup, or there was noise. That instinct is wrong. Discrepancy is not failure. Discrepancy is data about the model, and the structure of the discrepancy tells you something specific and true about what the model is missing. That is the subject of this lecture: learning to read the gap between prediction and measurement as information rather than embarrassment. slide 2: Three Types of Discrepancy — A Taxonomy That Matters Residual = predicted value − measured value at a single data point. A collection of residuals across all data points is the diagnostic fingerprint of your model's limitations. (Image: A three-row table with clear visual differentiation. Column headers: "Type," "What it looks like in residuals," "Physical interpretation." Row 1 — "Parametric uncertainty": residuals are randomly scattered around zero with no trend, all within the uncertainty band. Accompanying mini-plot shows scatter plot of residuals vs. independent variable — points distributed randomly, bounded by ±uncertainty band. Label: "Model form is adequate — wrong parameter values." Row 2 — "Model-form error": residuals show a systematic trend — increasing with the independent variable, or consistently positive in one range and negative in another. Mini-plot shows a clear slope or curve in the residuals. Label: "Model is structurally missing a physical effect." Row 3 — "Measurement error": residuals are randomly scattered but exceed the uncertainty band consistently in one direction. Mini-plot shows scatter above the band. Label: "Systematic bias in measurement, not in the model." The three rows are color-coded: Row 1 in gold, Row 2 in orange-red, Row 3 in gray. The visual distinction between random scatter and systematic trend is the central teaching point.) Talking Point: Before you can interpret a discrepancy correctly, you need a vocabulary for what kind of discrepancy it is. The first type is parametric uncertainty: the residuals — the point-by-point differences between your prediction and the measured data — are randomly scattered around zero, no consistent trend, and the magnitude of the scatter is consistent with your propagated uncertainty estimate. Random scatter within the uncertainty band means the model is not obviously wrong. If the scatter magnitude exceeds what your measurement noise predicts, better parameter values would reduce it — this is parametric. If scatter is consistent with the noise floor, the model may be adequate — but random scatter alone cannot prove this; it can only fail to show the model is structurally wrong. This pattern means the structure of the physics is probably right, but the parameter values you used may be imprecise. The fix is better characterization of the input parameters. The second type is model-form error: the residuals show a systematic trend. They increase with the independent variable, or they are consistently negative in one operating region and positive in another, or they curve in a way your model cannot produce. This pattern means the model is structurally missing something — a nonlinearity, an unmodeled coupling, a physical effect that your governing equation ignores. The third type is measurement error: the residuals are randomly scattered but are consistently offset from zero beyond what the uncertainty band predicts. This points to a systematic bias in the measurement — a miscalibrated sensor, a ground loop, or a systematic setup error — rather than a model problem. Each type has a different remedy, and confusing them leads to the wrong fix. slide 3: Residuals as the Diagnostic Signal (Image: Four residual plots in a two-by-two grid, each showing residuals (y-axis: prediction minus measurement) vs. an independent variable (x-axis). Plot 1: "Random scatter — within uncertainty band." Residuals distributed randomly, all bounded by dashed ±σ lines. Caption: "Consistent with measurement noise — model may be adequate." Plot 2: "Systematic linear trend." Residuals increase linearly with the independent variable — clear slope. Caption: "Model missing a first-order physical effect." Plot 3: "Nonlinear systematic pattern." Residuals curve upward at both ends and are near zero at the center. Caption: "Model misses nonlinearity or boundary effect." Plot 4: "Constant bias." Residuals are randomly scattered but consistently displaced above zero beyond the uncertainty band. Caption: "Systematic measurement error — not a model problem." All four plots use the same axis scales for direct comparison. The dashed uncertainty band is present in all four plots as a reference.) Talking Point: These four residual plots are the interpretive toolkit for your Tier 2 data analysis. When you plot your residuals — prediction minus measurement, for every data point — the shape of that plot is the primary diagnostic signal. Random scatter within the uncertainty band is good news for the model: it means the structure of the physics is probably right, and the scatter you see is consistent with the measurement precision you quantified in the prediction document. A systematic linear trend means the model is missing a physical effect that scales with the independent variable — if you are testing a structural beam and the residuals increase with applied load, the model may be missing compliance or geometric nonlinearity. A curved pattern — near zero at intermediate conditions, large at the extremes — is a classic signature of a small-deflection or small-angle assumption breaking down at the limits of the operating range. And a constant bias means you have a systematic measurement error: a sensor that needs recalibration, or a zero-offset that was never corrected. Every one of these patterns has a specific physical interpretation, and your job in the Tier 2 analysis is to name which one you see and explain what it implies. slide 4: Parametric Uncertainty — When the Form Is Right but the Numbers Are Wrong (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two model predictions vs. the same experimental data. Left panel: "Original prediction" — a dashed prediction curve that consistently overestimates the measured data by a roughly constant multiplicative factor. The residuals at the bottom are flat (no trend) but consistently negative — indicating the model overpredicts everywhere. Right panel: "Revised prediction after parameter correction" — the prediction curve after the stiffness parameter is corrected using the data as a reference; the revised curve passes through the data scatter within uncertainty bounds. A caption reads: "Parametric uncertainty diagnosis: systematic bias with no trend → correct the parameter, not the model structure." The two panels have the same axis scales, with measured data shown as scatter points in both.) Talking Point: When the residuals are flat — no trend, consistently negative or positive by a roughly constant amount — you have a parametric error. The model's structure is correct: it can produce the right shape of response, but it is using the wrong numerical value for one of the input parameters. In E2, the most common source of this pattern is an input parameter that you estimated from a textbook or datasheet that turns out to be different from the actual hardware — a material stiffness that has manufacturing variation, a mass that was estimated from a nominal drawing rather than measured directly, an aerodynamic coefficient that is sensitive to surface finish. The diagnostic step is to ask: which parameter, if adjusted by a physically plausible amount, would close the gap between the model and the data? If a small adjustment to one parameter brings the curves into agreement, that is strong evidence that the model form is correct and the parameter is the issue. This distinction matters because the fix for a parametric error — better parameter characterization — is completely different from the fix for a model-form error. slide 5: Model-Form Error — When the Structure Is Wrong (Image: A single graph showing a physical model prediction curve (linear, monotonically increasing) and experimental data scatter (which follows the prediction at low values of the independent variable but deviates nonlinearly at high values — the data bends upward, suggesting stiffening behavior not in the model). A residual plot below the main graph shows the characteristic systematic trend: residuals near zero at low independent variable values, growing positive at high values. An annotation points to the residual trend: "Systematic positive trend above x = 3 N — model missing geometric stiffening." A second annotation at the top: "This gap is not noise. The uncertainty band is shown. The trend is outside it." The uncertainty band (dashed lines) is present in both the main plot and the residual plot for reference.) Talking Point: Model-form error is the most informative type of discrepancy because it is telling you something true about the physics that your model does not capture. When the residuals show a systematic trend — they increase with the independent variable, or they are consistently of one sign in one operating regime and the opposite sign in another — the model cannot produce that pattern through any choice of parameters. The model is structurally incomplete. In the example here, the model predicts a linear response and the data agrees at small values, but at larger values the data stiffens in a way the linear model cannot follow. The residuals grow systematically. This is a textbook signature of geometric nonlinearity: the linear beam model assumes small displacements, and at large displacements the geometry changes enough to increase effective stiffness. The model is not wrong in the absolute sense — it is inadequate beyond the small-displacement regime, for the intended use of predicting behavior at large loads. This is exactly the kind of diagnostic information that makes a model more useful: you now know the regime in which the linear model is adequate and the regime in which a geometric nonlinearity term is required. The same pattern appears in aerospace contexts: measured drag coefficient shows a positive trend with Reynolds number that the model does not predict — this is a model-form error indicating that the laminar-to-turbulent transition is not captured in the model's drag expression. Different domain, same diagnostic logic. slide 6: Boundary Condition Realism — The Assumption You Did Not Know You Were Making (Image: A two-panel illustration comparing two structural configurations. Left panel: "Idealized model: fully fixed boundary condition." Shows a cantilever beam with the left end drawn as a rigid, built-in wall, with the label "Model assumption: zero rotation and zero displacement at support." Right panel: "Real experimental setup: imperfect fixity." Shows the same beam but the support has a small angular deviation — the label reads "Actual boundary: slight rotation compliance — the support is not perfectly rigid." A comparison of residuals from each assumption is shown below as two short residual plots: the rigid assumption produces residuals with a systematic trend, while a flexible-support model reduces the trend substantially. Caption: "Boundary condition assumptions are often the dominant source of model-form error in structural experiments.") Talking Point: One of the most common sources of model-form error in structural and mechanical experiments is not the governing equation itself — it is the boundary conditions. When you build a model, you make assumptions about how the system is supported, loaded, and constrained. In a textbook, boundaries are perfect: a fixed support is perfectly rigid, a pinned joint has zero friction, a load is applied at a mathematical point. In a real experiment, none of these idealizations are exactly true. A support that is intended to be fully fixed may have slight rotational compliance. A joint that is supposed to be pinned may have friction that introduces a restoring moment. These deviations from the ideal boundary condition appear in the residuals as systematic trends — typically largest near the boundary, decreasing with distance from it. When you see this pattern, the diagnostic question is: which of my boundary condition assumptions is least realistic? If a small adjustment to the boundary stiffness or the load application point closes the residual trend, that is the culprit. Naming this in your Tier 2 analysis — identifying which assumption was violated and what it implies for the model — is the highest-quality type of discrepancy analysis. slide 7: Measurement Error vs. Model Error — A Case for Uncertainty Bounds (Image: A comparison of two scenarios, presented as two MATLAB scatter plots side by side. Left: "Scenario A — discrepancy within uncertainty bounds." Measured data scatter plotted around a model prediction line. Horizontal dashed uncertainty lines at ±σ. All data points within the band. Caption: "No grounds to claim model failure — discrepancy is consistent with measurement precision." Right: "Scenario B — systematic bias beyond uncertainty bounds." Same model prediction line, but measured data is consistently below it, outside the uncertainty band at nearly every point. Caption: "Systematic bias: not random scatter. Source investigation required." Below the two panels, a question prompt: "Is the cause of the bias in the model, the measurement, or both? How would you distinguish?" A dotted arrow between the panels labeled "The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis — it defines what random scatter looks like.") Talking Point: The uncertainty band you computed in your prediction document is not just a formality — it is your null hypothesis. It answers the question: what would the residuals look like if the only differences between prediction and data were due to measurement noise at the level you estimated? If the residuals are within the band, you cannot claim a meaningful disagreement — the data is consistent with your model within the precision of your measurement. If the residuals are consistently outside the band, something is wrong in a non-random way. The question then is: is the systematic bias coming from the model or from the measurement? To distinguish them, you need to ask whether the bias is constant across different operating conditions, different sensors, or different setups. If you repeat the measurement with a different sensor type or a different calibration reference and the bias shifts, it is a measurement error. If the bias is consistent regardless of how you measure, it is more likely a model error. This is exactly why the calibration status review — which we will cover in Lecture 2 today — is a required section of the Tier 2 group report: it is the systematic investigation of whether your measurement has a known bias source. slide 8: Using Discrepancy to Improve the Model — The Experimentally Informed Model (Image: A four-stage circular process diagram. Stage 1 (top): "Predictive model — physical equations, committed prediction." Arrow to Stage 2 (right): "Experiment — data collected." Arrow to Stage 3 (bottom): "Discrepancy analysis — classify residual pattern as parametric, model-form, or measurement." Arrow to Stage 4 (left): "Model revision proposal — specific update to the model based on what the discrepancy revealed." Arrow back to Stage 1 labeled "Updated model — inform future predictions." The diagram is rendered in the course color scheme with a clean circular layout. A gold star icon appears on the Stage 4 box with the label: "This is the required deliverable in the Tier 2 report — not just 'the model was close enough'.") Talking Point: Returning to the experimentally informed model concept that was introduced at the start of E2: the goal of running a canned experiment in Phase II is not to validate the model in a binary sense and move on. The goal is to use the discrepancy analysis to propose a specific improvement to the model. Stage 1 is your committed prediction — you built a model, derived a prediction, and committed to it. Stage 2 is the experiment — you collected data. Stage 3 is the discrepancy analysis — you classified the residual pattern as parametric uncertainty, model-form error, or measurement error, and you named the physical cause. Stage 4 is the model revision proposal: based on what the residual pattern revealed, what specific change to the model — a different boundary condition assumption, an added nonlinear term, a corrected parameter value — would reduce the discrepancy? This is what the Tier 2 report asks you to provide. Not "the model was close enough" and not "there was some error in the experiment." A specific, physically justified proposal for how the model could be improved based on what the data told you. slide 9: Applying the Taxonomy to Your Tier 2 Data (Image: A structured worksheet-style layout. Three decision steps arranged vertically. Step 1: "Plot residuals — prediction minus measurement — versus the independent variable." Sub-question: "Are the residuals random or systematic?" Two branches: "Random → Step 2." "Systematic → Model-form error or boundary condition issue → name the physical effect." Step 2 (from "Random"): "Compare residual magnitude to uncertainty band." Sub-question: "Are residuals within the band?" Two branches: "Yes → Parametric uncertainty or adequate agreement." "No, consistently biased → Systematic measurement or parameter error." Step 3: "If constant bias beyond band — perform calibration status check." "Was the sensor within calibration interval? Was a zero offset correction applied?" A footer note reads: "This three-step process is the required analysis structure for Section 3A of the Tier 2 group report.") Talking Point: Let me give you a concrete decision pathway for your Tier 2 analysis. Start by plotting the residuals — prediction minus measurement — as a function of your independent variable. Ask whether the pattern is random or systematic. If you see a clear trend, stop there: you have model-form error, and your job is to name the physical effect that could produce that trend. If the pattern looks random, the next question is whether the magnitude is within the uncertainty band you estimated in your prediction document. If yes, the model may be adequate for the intended use within the bounds of your measurement precision. If no — if the scatter is consistently outside the band, or if there is a consistent offset — you have a systematic error to investigate. The calibration status of your instruments is the first place to look, which is exactly why the calibration status review is Section 3C of your group report and not an optional appendix. These three steps are the core of Section 3A of the Tier 2 report, and we will work through them together in the lab session today. slide 10: A Model That Disagrees Informatively Is More Valuable Than One That Agrees Approximately (Image: A two-row comparison. Row 1 header: "Case A — approximate agreement, no diagnostic information." Shows a prediction curve overlaid on data scatter that roughly matches everywhere, residuals small and without trend. A note reads: "Conclusion: 'Model is close enough.' What was learned: nothing specific." Row 2 header: "Case B — structured disagreement, specific diagnostic." Shows a prediction curve that clearly overestimates at high values, with residuals showing a systematic trend. A note reads: "Conclusion: 'Model overestimates stiffening at large deflections — geometric nonlinearity not captured.' What was learned: the regime of model adequacy, a specific model improvement target, and the physical mechanism responsible." A banner at the bottom: "Case B is more scientifically valuable even though the model is 'less accurate.' The structure of the disagreement is the information.") Talking Point: I want to leave you with a reframing that I think is genuinely important for how you approach engineering work. A model that agrees within uncertainty bounds has demonstrated adequacy — that is a success. A structured disagreement is a different kind of success: it reveals specifically where the model breaks down and why. Both outcomes have value; only one points toward model improvement. Approximate agreement everywhere means you learned that the model is not obviously wrong — you have limited information about where it breaks down, under what conditions, or why. Structured disagreement means you have learned something specific: the model is missing geometric stiffening above a certain deflection, or the aerodynamic drag coefficient is approximately 30% higher than the textbook value at these Reynolds numbers, or the support compliance reduces the apparent natural frequency by 15% compared to the fixed-end assumption. These are specific, actionable findings that make the model more useful for its intended purpose. The test engineer who delivers a report saying "the model is close enough" has done less valuable work than the one who delivers a report saying "the model is adequate within this operating regime, and here is the specific modification required to extend its validity." That is what Phase II is teaching you to do. slide 11: Closure Discrepancy between a model and experimental data is not embarrassment — it is information, and the structure of the discrepancy is the diagnostic signal. Parametric uncertainty produces random scatter within the uncertainty band and calls for better parameter characterization. Model-form error produces systematic trends in the residuals and calls for identifying the missing physical effect. Systematic measurement error produces consistent bias beyond the uncertainty band and calls for a calibration status review. The uncertainty band you computed in your prediction document is not a formality — it is your null hypothesis, defining what random scatter looks like. A model that disagrees in a structured way is telling you something true about the physics, and a test engineer who can read that structure and translate it into a specific model improvement proposal is delivering value that cannot be replaced by running more trials or using a better sensor. In Lecture 2 this week, we will go deeper on calibration — not just as a procedure, but as a source of uncertainty that propagates into your measurement outcome and must be documented to support a credible validation claim. CLO 3 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Model-Form Error & Discrepancy as Diagnostic Information Talking Point: This lecture addresses what I consider the hardest conceptual shift of Phase II, and I want to state it plainly at the start. When the data comes back from your Tier 2 experiment and it does not match your prediction perfectly — and it will not — your first instinct is going to be to explain the discrepancy away. To say the sensors drifted, or something went wrong with the setup, or there was noise. That instinct is wrong. Discrepancy is not failure. Discrepancy is data about the model, and the structure of the discrepancy tells you something specific and true about what the model is missing. That is the subject of this lecture: learning to read the gap between prediction and measurement as information rather than embarrassment. slide 2: Three Types of Discrepancy — A Taxonomy That Matters **Residual** = predicted value − measured value at a single data point. A collection of residuals across all data points is the diagnostic fingerprint of your model's limitations. (Image: A three-row table with clear visual differentiation. Column headers: "Type," "What it looks like in residuals," "Physical interpretation." Row 1 — "Parametric uncertainty": residuals are randomly scattered around zero with no trend, all within the uncertainty band. Accompanying mini-plot shows scatter plot of residuals vs. independent variable — points distributed randomly, bounded by ±uncertainty band. Label: "Model form is adequate — wrong parameter values." Row 2 — "Model-form error": residuals show a systematic trend — increasing with the independent variable, or consistently positive in one range and negative in another. Mini-plot shows a clear slope or curve in the residuals. Label: "Model is structurally missing a physical effect." Row 3 — "Measurement error": residuals are randomly scattered but exceed the uncertainty band consistently in one direction. Mini-plot shows scatter above the band. Label: "Systematic bias in measurement, not in the model." The three rows are color-coded: Row 1 in gold, Row 2 in orange-red, Row 3 in gray. The visual distinction between random scatter and systematic trend is the central teaching point.) Talking Point: Before you can interpret a discrepancy correctly, you need a vocabulary for what kind of discrepancy it is. The first type is parametric uncertainty: the residuals — the point-by-point differences between your prediction and the measured data — are randomly scattered around zero, no consistent trend, and the magnitude of the scatter is consistent with your propagated uncertainty estimate. Random scatter within the uncertainty band means the model is not obviously wrong. If the scatter magnitude exceeds what your measurement noise predicts, better parameter values would reduce it — this is parametric. If scatter is consistent with the noise floor, the model may be adequate — but random scatter alone cannot prove this; it can only fail to show the model is structurally wrong. This pattern means the structure of the physics is probably right, but the parameter values you used may be imprecise. The fix is better characterization of the input parameters. The second type is model-form error: the residuals show a systematic trend. They increase with the independent variable, or they are consistently negative in one operating region and positive in another, or they curve in a way your model cannot produce. This pattern means the model is structurally missing something — a nonlinearity, an unmodeled coupling, a physical effect that your governing equation ignores. The third type is measurement error: the residuals are randomly scattered but are consistently offset from zero beyond what the uncertainty band predicts. This points to a systematic bias in the measurement — a miscalibrated sensor, a ground loop, or a systematic setup error — rather than a model problem. Each type has a different remedy, and confusing them leads to the wrong fix. slide 3: Residuals as the Diagnostic Signal (Image: Four residual plots in a two-by-two grid, each showing residuals (y-axis: prediction minus measurement) vs. an independent variable (x-axis).
