When you plot your residuals — prediction minus measurement, for every data point — the shape of that plot is the primary diagnostic signal. Random scatter within the uncertainty band is good news for the model: it means the structure of the physics is probably right, and the scatter you see is consistent with the measurement precision you quantified in the prediction document. A systematic linear trend means the model is missing a physical effect that scales with the independent variable — if you are testing a structural beam and the residuals increase with applied load, the model may be missing compliance or geometric nonlinearity. A curved pattern — near zero at intermediate conditions, large at the extremes

ing compliance or geometric nonlinearity. A curved pattern — near zero at intermediate conditions, large at the extremes — is a classic signature of a small-deflection or small-angle assumption breaking down at the limits of the operating range. And a constant bias means you have a systematic measurement error: a sensor that needs recalibration, or a zero-offset that was never corrected. Every one of these patterns has a specific physical interpretation, and your job in the Tier 2 analysis is to name which one you see and explain what it implies. slide 4: Parametric Uncertainty — When the Form Is Right but the Numbers Are Wrong (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two model predictions vs. the same experimental data. Left panel: "Original prediction" — a dashed prediction curve that

two model predictions vs. the same experimental data. Left panel: "Original prediction" — a dashed prediction curve that consistently overestimates the measured data by a roughly constant multiplicative factor. The residuals at the bottom are flat (no trend) but consistently negative — indicating the model overpredicts everywhere. Right panel: "Revised prediction after parameter correction" — the prediction curve after the stiffness parameter is corrected using the data as a reference; the revised curve passes through the data scatter within uncertainty bounds. A caption reads: "Parametric uncertainty diagnosis: systematic bias with no trend → correct the parameter, not the model structure." The two panels have the same axis scales, with measured data shown as scatter points in both.)

not the model structure." The two panels have the same axis scales, with measured data shown as scatter points in both.) Talking Point: When the residuals are flat — no trend, consistently negative or positive by a roughly constant amount — you have a parametric error. The model's structure is correct: it can produce the right shape of response, but it is using the wrong numerical value for one of the input parameters. In E2, the most common source of this pattern is an input parameter that you estimated from a textbook or datasheet that turns out to be different from the actual hardware — a material stiffness that has manufacturing variation, a mass that was estimated from a nominal drawing rather than measured directly, an aerodynamic coefficient that is sensitive to surface finish. The

rom a nominal drawing rather than measured directly, an aerodynamic coefficient that is sensitive to surface finish. The diagnostic step is to ask: which parameter, if adjusted by a physically plausible amount, would close the gap between the model and the data? If a small adjustment to one parameter brings the curves into agreement, that is strong evidence that the model form is correct and the parameter is the issue. This distinction matters because the fix for a parametric error — better parameter characterization — is completely different from the fix for a model-form error. slide 5: Model-Form Error — When the Structure Is Wrong (Image: A single graph showing a physical model prediction curve (linear, monotonically increasing) and experimental data scatter (which follows the

ng a physical model prediction curve (linear, monotonically increasing) and experimental data scatter (which follows the prediction at low values of the independent variable but deviates nonlinearly at high values — the data bends upward, suggesting stiffening behavior not in the model). A residual plot below the main graph shows the characteristic systematic trend: residuals near zero at low independent variable values, growing positive at high values. An annotation points to the residual trend: "Systematic positive trend above x = 3 N — model missing geometric stiffening." A second annotation at the top: "This gap is not noise. The uncertainty band is shown. The trend is outside it." The uncertainty band (dashed lines) is present in both the main plot and the residual plot for

The trend is outside it." The uncertainty band (dashed lines) is present in both the main plot and the residual plot for reference.) Talking Point: Model-form error is the most informative type of discrepancy because it is telling you something true about the physics that your model does not capture. When the residuals show a systematic trend — they increase with the independent variable, or they are consistently of one sign in one operating regime and the opposite sign in another — the model cannot produce that pattern through any choice of parameters. The model is structurally incomplete. In the example here, the model predicts a linear response and the data agrees at small values, but at larger values the data stiffens in a way the linear model cannot follow. The residuals grow

rees at small values, but at larger values the data stiffens in a way the linear model cannot follow. The residuals grow systematically. This is a textbook signature of geometric nonlinearity: the linear beam model assumes small displacements, and at large displacements the geometry changes enough to increase effective stiffness. The model is not wrong in the absolute sense — it is inadequate beyond the small-displacement regime, for the intended use of predicting behavior at large loads. This is exactly the kind of diagnostic information that makes a model more useful: you now know the regime in which the linear model is adequate and the regime in which a geometric nonlinearity term is required. The same pattern appears in aerospace contexts: measured drag coefficient shows a positive

onlinearity term is required. The same pattern appears in aerospace contexts: measured drag coefficient shows a positive trend with Reynolds number that the model does not predict — this is a model-form error indicating that the laminar-to-turbulent transition is not captured in the model's drag expression. Different domain, same diagnostic logic. slide 6: Boundary Condition Realism — The Assumption You Did Not Know You Were Making (Image: A two-panel illustration comparing two structural configurations. Left panel: "Idealized model: fully fixed boundary condition." Shows a cantilever beam with the left end drawn as a rigid, built-in wall, with the label "Model assumption: zero rotation and zero displacement at support." Right panel: "Real experimental setup: imperfect fixity." Shows the

on: zero rotation and zero displacement at support." Right panel: "Real experimental setup: imperfect fixity." Shows the same beam but the support has a small angular deviation — the label reads "Actual boundary: slight rotation compliance — the support is not perfectly rigid." A comparison of residuals from each assumption is shown below as two short residual plots: the rigid assumption produces residuals with a systematic trend, while a flexible-support model reduces the trend substantially. Caption: "Boundary condition assumptions are often the dominant source of model-form error in structural experiments.") Talking Point: One of the most common sources of model-form error in structural and mechanical experiments is not the governing equation itself — it is the boundary conditions.

orm error in structural and mechanical experiments is not the governing equation itself — it is the boundary conditions. When you build a model, you make assumptions about how the system is supported, loaded, and constrained. In a textbook, boundaries are perfect: a fixed support is perfectly rigid, a pinned joint has zero friction, a load is applied at a mathematical point. In a real experiment, none of these idealizations are exactly true. A support that is intended to be fully fixed may have slight rotational compliance. A joint that is supposed to be pinned may have friction that introduces a restoring moment. These deviations from the ideal boundary condition appear in the residuals as systematic trends — typically largest near the boundary, decreasing with distance from it. When you

in the residuals as systematic trends — typically largest near the boundary, decreasing with distance from it. When you see this pattern, the diagnostic question is: which of my boundary condition assumptions is least realistic? If a small adjustment to the boundary stiffness or the load application point closes the residual trend, that is the culprit. Naming this in your Tier 2 analysis — identifying which assumption was violated and what it implies for the model — is the highest-quality type of discrepancy analysis. slide 7: Measurement Error vs. Model Error — A Case for Uncertainty Bounds (Image: A comparison of two scenarios, presented as two MATLAB scatter plots side by side. Left: "Scenario A — discrepancy within uncertainty bounds." Measured data scatter plotted around a model

s side by side. Left: "Scenario A — discrepancy within uncertainty bounds." Measured data scatter plotted around a model prediction line. Horizontal dashed uncertainty lines at ±σ. All data points within the band. Caption: "No grounds to claim model failure — discrepancy is consistent with measurement precision." Right: "Scenario B — systematic bias beyond uncertainty bounds." Same model prediction line, but measured data is consistently below it, outside the uncertainty band at nearly every point. Caption: "Systematic bias: not random scatter. Source investigation required." Below the two panels, a question prompt: "Is the cause of the bias in the model, the measurement, or both? How would you distinguish?" A dotted arrow between the panels labeled "The uncertainty band is your null

ement, or both? How would you distinguish?" A dotted arrow between the panels labeled "The uncertainty band is your null hypothesis — it defines what random scatter looks like.") Talking Point: The uncertainty band you computed in your prediction document is not just a formality — it is your null hypothesis. It answers the question: what would the residuals look like if the only differences between prediction and data were due to measurement noise at the level you estimated? If the residuals are within the band, you cannot claim a meaningful disagreement — the data is consistent with your model within the precision of your measurement. If the residuals are consistently outside the band, something is wrong in a non-random way. The question then is: is the systematic bias coming from the

y outside the band, something is wrong in a non-random way. The question then is: is the systematic bias coming from the model or from the measurement? To distinguish them, you need to ask whether the bias is constant across different operating conditions, different sensors, or different setups. If you repeat the measurement with a different sensor type or a different calibration reference and the bias shifts, it is a measurement error. If the bias is consistent regardless of how you measure, it is more likely a model error. This is exactly why the calibration status review — which we will cover in Lecture 2 today — is a required section of the Tier 2 group report: it is the systematic investigation of whether your measurement has a known bias source. slide 8: Using Discrepancy to Improve

the systematic investigation of whether your measurement has a known bias source. slide 8: Using Discrepancy to Improve the Model — The Experimentally Informed Model (Image: A four-stage circular process diagram. Stage 1 (top): "Predictive model — physical equations, committed prediction." Arrow to Stage 2 (right): "Experiment — data collected." Arrow to Stage 3 (bottom): "Discrepancy analysis — classify residual pattern as parametric, model-form, or measurement." Arrow to Stage 4 (left): "Model revision proposal — specific update to the model based on what the discrepancy revealed." Arrow back to Stage 1 labeled "Updated model — inform future predictions." The diagram is rendered in the course color scheme with a clean circular layout. A gold star icon appears on the Stage 4 box with

am is rendered in the course color scheme with a clean circular layout. A gold star icon appears on the Stage 4 box with the label: "This is the required deliverable in the Tier 2 report — not just 'the model was close enough'.") Talking Point: Returning to the experimentally informed model concept that was introduced at the start of E2: the goal of running a canned experiment in Phase II is not to validate the model in a binary sense and move on. The goal is to use the discrepancy analysis to propose a specific improvement to the model. Stage 1 is your committed prediction — you built a model, derived a prediction, and committed to it. Stage 2 is the experiment — you collected data. Stage 3 is the discrepancy analysis — you classified the residual pattern as parametric uncertainty,

you collected data. Stage 3 is the discrepancy analysis — you classified the residual pattern as parametric uncertainty, model-form error, or measurement error, and you named the physical cause. Stage 4 is the model revision proposal: based on what the residual pattern revealed, what specific change to the model — a different boundary condition assumption, an added nonlinear term, a corrected parameter value — would reduce the discrepancy? This is what the Tier 2 report asks you to provide. Not "the model was close enough" and not "there was some error in the experiment." A specific, physically justified proposal for how the model could be improved based on what the data told you. slide 9: Applying the Taxonomy to Your Tier 2 Data (Image: A structured worksheet-style layout. Three

the data told you. slide 9: Applying the Taxonomy to Your Tier 2 Data (Image: A structured worksheet-style layout. Three decision steps arranged vertically. Step 1: "Plot residuals — prediction minus measurement — versus the independent variable." Sub-question: "Are the residuals random or systematic?" Two branches: "Random → Step 2." "Systematic → Model-form error or boundary condition issue → name the physical effect." Step 2 (from "Random"): "Compare residual magnitude to uncertainty band." Sub-question: "Are residuals within the band?" Two branches: "Yes → Parametric uncertainty or adequate agreement." "No, consistently biased → Systematic measurement or parameter error." Step 3: "If constant bias beyond band — perform calibration status check." "Was the sensor within calibration

er error." Step 3: "If constant bias beyond band — perform calibration status check." "Was the sensor within calibration interval? Was a zero offset correction applied?" A footer note reads: "This three-step process is the required analysis structure for Section 3A of the Tier 2 group report.") Talking Point: Let me give you a concrete decision pathway for your Tier 2 analysis. Start by plotting the residuals — prediction minus measurement — as a function of your independent variable. Ask whether the pattern is random or systematic. If you see a clear trend, stop there: you have model-form error, and your job is to name the physical effect that could produce that trend. If the pattern looks random, the next question is whether the magnitude is within the uncertainty band you estimated in

If the pattern looks random, the next question is whether the magnitude is within the uncertainty band you estimated in your prediction document. If yes, the model may be adequate for the intended use within the bounds of your measurement precision. If no — if the scatter is consistently outside the band, or if there is a consistent offset — you have a systematic error to investigate. The calibration status of your instruments is the first place to look, which is exactly why the calibration status review is Section 3C of your group report and not an optional appendix. These three steps are the core of Section 3A of the Tier 2 report, and we will work through them together in the lab session today. slide 10: A Model That Disagrees Informatively Is More Valuable Than One That Agrees

together in the lab session today. slide 10: A Model That Disagrees Informatively Is More Valuable Than One That Agrees Approximately (Image: A two-row comparison. Row 1 header: "Case A — approximate agreement, no diagnostic information." Shows a prediction curve overlaid on data scatter that roughly matches everywhere, residuals small and without trend. A note reads: "Conclusion: 'Model is close enough.' What was learned: nothing specific." Row 2 header: "Case B — structured disagreement, specific diagnostic." Shows a prediction curve that clearly overestimates at high values, with residuals showing a systematic trend. A note reads: "Conclusion: 'Model overestimates stiffening at large deflections — geometric nonlinearity not captured.' What was learned: the regime of model adequacy, a

tiffening at large deflections — geometric nonlinearity not captured.' What was learned: the regime of model adequacy, a specific model improvement target, and the physical mechanism responsible." A banner at the bottom: "Case B is more scientifically valuable even though the model is 'less accurate.' The structure of the disagreement is the information.") Talking Point: I want to leave you with a reframing that I think is genuinely important for how you approach engineering work. A model that agrees within uncertainty bounds has demonstrated adequacy — that is a success. A structured disagreement is a different kind of success: it reveals specifically where the model breaks down and why. Both outcomes have value; only one points toward model improvement. Approximate agreement everywhere

reaks down and why. Both outcomes have value; only one points toward model improvement. Approximate agreement everywhere means you learned that the model is not obviously wrong — you have limited information about where it breaks down, under what conditions, or why. Structured disagreement means you have learned something specific: the model is missing geometric stiffening above a certain deflection, or the aerodynamic drag coefficient is approximately 30% higher than the textbook value at these Reynolds numbers, or the support compliance reduces the apparent natural frequency by 15% compared to the fixed-end assumption. These are specific, actionable findings that make the model more useful for its intended purpose. The test engineer who delivers a report saying "the model is close

make the model more useful for its intended purpose. The test engineer who delivers a report saying "the model is close enough" has done less valuable work than the one who delivers a report saying "the model is adequate within this operating regime, and here is the specific modification required to extend its validity." That is what Phase II is teaching you to do. slide 11: Closure Discrepancy between a model and experimental data is not embarrassment — it is information, and the structure of the discrepancy is the diagnostic signal. Parametric uncertainty produces random scatter within the uncertainty band and calls for better parameter characterization. Model-form error produces systematic trends in the residuals and calls for identifying the missing physical effect. Systematic

form error produces systematic trends in the residuals and calls for identifying the missing physical effect. Systematic measurement error produces consistent bias beyond the uncertainty band and calls for a calibration status review. The uncertainty band you computed in your prediction document is not a formality — it is your null hypothesis, defining what random scatter looks like. A model that disagrees in a structured way is telling you something true about the physics, and a test engineer who can read that structure and translate it into a specific model improvement proposal is delivering value that cannot be replaced by running more trials or using a better sensor. In Lecture 2 this week, we will go deeper on calibration — not just as a procedure, but as a source of uncertainty that

In Lecture 2 this week, we will go deeper on calibration — not just as a procedure, but as a source of uncertainty that propagates into your measurement outcome and must be documented to support a credible validation claim. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded entirely in the W8 L1 blueprint purpose statement: model-form error, parametric uncertainty, boundary condition realism, systematic residual interpretation. - The three-type taxonomy (parametric / model-form / measurement) is introduced in slide 2 and applied throughout — this is the required analytical vocabulary for Section 3A of the Tier 2 report. - The experimentally informed model concept (slide 8) is a required E2 deliverable orientation per instructor_clarification_notes.md item 5. - Slide 10 explicitly frames structured

equired E2 deliverable orientation per instructor_clarification_notes.md item 5. - Slide 10 explicitly frames structured disagreement as more valuable than approximate agreement — echoing the lecture narrative spine from Conceptual_Arc.md. - Connects forward to W8 L2 (calibration status review) in closure slide. - No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 3 primary treatment.

pages.title: L02
pages.text: E2 Week 3 (Course Week 8) Lecture L02 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Talking Point: We introduced the calibration-versus-validation distinction in Week 3 of E1, and you have been applying t... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Talking Point: We introduced the calibration-versus-validation distinction in Week 3 of E1, and you have been applying that distinction conceptually ever since. This lecture deepens it. Calibration is not just a concept — it is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget, and the uncertainty it introduces propagates directly into your final measurement outcome. In Phase II, every group is required to document the calibration status of their instruments and assess whether that status is adequate for the intended use of the experiment. This lecture gives you the formal tools to do that correctly. slide 2: Calibration vs. Validation — The Distinction Deepened (Image: A side-by-side two-column comparison diagram. Left column header: "Calibration." Right column header: "Validation." Row 1 — "What it is": "Calibration: a measurement activity — comparing a sensor's output to a known reference and adjusting the relationship between input and output to reduce systematic bias." | "Validation: a modeling activity — testing whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's behavior." Row 2 — "What it operates on": "Calibration: the instrument — changes the sensor's calibration coefficients or correction curve." | "Validation: the model — provides evidence about whether the model is adequate for the intended use." Row 3 — "What 'success' looks like": "Calibration: the sensor output agrees with the reference standard within the stated calibration uncertainty." | "Validation: the model prediction agrees with the experimental outcome within the acceptance criterion." Row 4 — "What a 'fail' means": "Calibration: the instrument needs adjustment or replacement." | "Validation: the model is inadequate for the intended use — revision required." The table is clean and readable with alternating row shading. A caption at the bottom: "Conflating these two activities is one of the most common sources of credibility loss in experimental reports.") Talking Point: In Week 3 of E1, I told you that calibration and validation are distinct activities and that conflating them undermines experimental credibility. Now let me explain precisely why. Calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference — it is an operation on the instrument. When you recalibrate a load cell, you are changing the relationship between force applied and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical model predicts a physical system's behavior accurately — it is an operation on the model. When you compare a structural model's predicted deflection to the measured deflection, you are testing the model. The two activities operate on different objects and serve different purposes. The common error is running an experiment, comparing the data to a model, finding agreement, and concluding the model is validated — when what actually happened is that the sensor was calibrated against the physical system, which is a measurement activity, not a modeling validation. If the same data used to calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an independent validation test. You have demonstrated that your sensor is internally consistent, and nothing more. slide 3: The Calibration Uncertainty Budget — What Calibration Adds to the Error Stack (Image: A stacked bar chart illustration showing the total measurement uncertainty budget as a stack of three components. Component 1 (bottom, gold): "Random measurement noise — estimated from repeated trials." Component 2 (middle, lighter gold): "Sensor systematic bias — corrected by calibration." Component 3 (top, orange): "Residual calibration uncertainty — what remains after calibration." Below the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the systematic bias component but introduces its own uncertainty: the calibration uncertainty propagates into the corrected measurement." To the right, a simple equation: "u_total² = u_noise² + u_calibration²" where each term is labeled. A note below: "Calibration uncertainty comes from: (1) reference standard uncertainty, (2) curve-fit residuals, (3) environmental drift since last calibration." The visual communicates that calibration is not free — it trades one source of uncertainty for another, smaller one.) Talking Point: Every calibration has its own uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three components. First, the reference standard uncertainty — the known reference used to calibrate your sensor is itself not perfectly accurate; it has a stated uncertainty that transfers directly to your calibration. Second, the curve-fit residuals — when you fit a calibration curve to the reference-versus-sensor-output data, the fit is not perfect; the residual scatter around the calibration curve represents uncertainty in the corrected sensor output at any given input. Third, environmental drift since the last calibration — sensors change over time and with environmental conditions, so a sensor that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the magnitude of that drift is uncertain. The total calibration uncertainty is the root-sum-square (RSS) of these three components — the same quadrature combination you first used in E1 Week 2 — and it adds in quadrature to the random measurement noise in your total uncertainty budget. Calibration makes measurements more accurate by reducing systematic bias, but it does not make measurements perfect — it trades a large systematic error for a smaller residual one, and that residual must be documented and propagated. slide 4: Calibration Interval and Calibration Status — What to Look For (Image: An instrument calibration certificate mockup showing five key fields: "Instrument: Load Cell LCxxx, Serial XXXXXXXX," "Last calibration date: 2025-11-10," "Calibration interval: 12 months," "Calibration due date: 2026-11-10," "Calibration uncertainty: ±0.3% full scale (k=2, 95% confidence)." Below the certificate, a status assessment panel with three conditions color-coded: Green check — "In calibration: current date is before due date." Yellow caution — "Near expiration: within 30 days of due date — use with awareness." Red X — "Out of calibration: current date is past due date — result credibility is uncertain, document prominently." A small note: "The calibration uncertainty stated on the certificate (±0.3%) propagates into your final measurement outcome whether the instrument is in calibration or not. Document both the status and the uncertainty.") Talking Point: The calibration status review in your Tier 2 group report requires you to find and document three things for each instrument in the experiment: the last calibration date, the calibration interval, and whether the instrument is currently within its calibration interval. This information is on the instrument's calibration certificate — a document that is typically attached to the instrument physically, or in the lab documentation file. The calibration certificate also states the calibration uncertainty in the form of a numerical value and a coverage factor. The coverage factor k=2 means the stated uncertainty represents a 95% confidence interval (k=2 is the coverage factor for a 95% CI introduced in E1 Week 2 — a reminder that the CI half-width is k times the standard uncertainty) — which is the standard we use throughout this course. If the instrument is out of calibration, you must document that prominently and assess what it means for the credibility of your result. An out-of-calibration instrument does not necessarily mean the data is worthless, but it means the systematic bias component of the uncertainty is unknown and cannot be bounded by the certificate. You need to acknowledge that limitation explicitly and assess whether it changes your conclusion about whether the model is adequate for the intended use. slide 5: Verifying an Off-the-Shelf Sensor Against First Principles — A Worked Example (Image: A workbench setup diagram for the P=IV power sensor verification example. The setup shows a commercial power sensor connected to a resistive load, alongside a standard digital multimeter measuring voltage and a second multimeter measuring current. Below the setup: a table with three rows — "Power sensor reading: 12.4 W," "First-principles: P = V × I = 12.2 W," "Discrepancy: 0.2 W (1.6%)." A propagated uncertainty column shows "Power sensor stated accuracy: ±2% full scale (±0.3 W at 12.4 W)" and "First-principles uncertainty (from Lecture E1 W2): ±0.15 W." The two results are compared: "12.4 ± 0.3 W vs. 12.2 ± 0.15 W — overlap within combined uncertainty bounds." Caption: "Verification: checking sensor output against first principles. Distinct from calibration: you are not adjusting the sensor — you are checking whether it is consistent with an independent standard.") Talking Point: Let me show you a concrete example of sensor verification using first principles — this is related to the P=IV lab you did in Week 2 of E1, and I want you to see the connection explicitly. A commercial power sensor is attached to a resistive load. You want to verify that the sensor's output is physically consistent — not to recalibrate it, but to check whether it agrees with first principles using standard instruments. You use two multimeters: one measuring voltage, one measuring current. You compute power from P equals V times I, using the same partial-derivative uncertainty propagation you applied in Lab 1. The power sensor reads 12.4 watts. The first-principles computation gives 12.2 watts. The discrepancy is 0.2 watts. The propagated uncertainties overlap — 12.4 plus-or-minus 0.3 watts and 12.2 plus-or-minus 0.15 watts. This is a verification result: you are not adjusting the sensor's calibration, you are confirming that its output is consistent with an independent physical measurement within the combined uncertainty bounds. If the results did not overlap, you would have grounds to suspect a calibration issue and seek a formal recalibration before using the sensor for a validation claim. This is the calibration-versus-validation distinction applied to a commercial sensor in a real scenario. slide 6: What the Calibration Status Means for Your Validation Claim (Image: A two-by-two decision matrix. X-axis: "Calibration status" — labeled "In calibration" (left) and "Out of calibration" (right). Y-axis: "Model-data agreement" — labeled "Agreement within bounds" (top) and "Discrepancy beyond bounds" (bottom). Four cells with interpretations: Top-left (in calibration, agreement): "Strong validation case — measurement uncertainty is characterized and agreement is established." Top-right (out of calibration, agreement): "Weak validation case — agreement may be coincidental; calibration-related bias is unknown." Bottom-left (in calibration, discrepancy): "Credible diagnostic — discrepancy is real, calibration is not the cause; investigate model." Bottom-right (out of calibration, discrepancy): "Inconclusive — cannot determine whether discrepancy is from model or from out-of-calibration bias; instrument must be recalibrated before conclusions can be drawn." Each cell has a color code: Top-left green, Top-right yellow, Bottom-left orange, Bottom-right red.) Talking Point: This two-by-two matrix captures the practical relationship between calibration status and validation credibility. The strongest validation case is the top-left cell: the instruments are in calibration, the calibration uncertainty is documented, and the model agrees with the data within the uncertainty bounds. You can make a credible claim about model adequacy because the uncertainty is bounded and characterized. The bottom-left cell is the credible diagnostic case: the instruments are in calibration, but the model disagrees with the data. Because calibration is not the issue, the discrepancy is real and you can investigate the model confidently. The weakest cases are on the right side of the matrix. If the instrument is out of calibration and the model appears to agree, you cannot determine whether the agreement is genuine or coincidental — the uncalibrated sensor may be biased in a way that makes the model look correct by accident. And if the instrument is out of calibration and there is a discrepancy, you cannot determine whether the discrepancy is from the model or from the unmeasured calibration drift. The practical implication is clear: calibration status must be verified before you run the experiment, not discovered during the analysis. slide 7: Propagating Calibration Uncertainty into the Final Result (Image: A step-by-step calculation layout. Step 1: "Sensor calibration uncertainty from certificate: u_cal = ±0.3% full scale = ±0.12 N for a 40 N full-scale load cell." Step 2: "Random measurement noise (from repeated trials): u_noise = ±0.08 N (standard uncertainty, k=1)." Step 3: "Root-sum-square combination: u_total = sqrt(u_cal² + u_noise²) = sqrt((0.12)² + (0.08)²) = sqrt(0.0144 + 0.0064) = sqrt(0.0208) = 0.144 N." Step 4: "Final result: F = 23.4 ± 0.29 N (k=2, 95% confidence)." A note at the bottom: "The calibration uncertainty (0.12 N) was larger than the noise (0.08 N) in this example — it is the dominant error source. Improving the calibration reference would reduce the total uncertainty more than improving the measurement setup.") Talking Point: Let me walk through the arithmetic of propagating calibration uncertainty into a final measurement result — this is exactly what you will include in Section 3C of your Tier 2 group report. The load cell in this example has a calibration uncertainty of plus-or-minus 0.3 percent of full scale, which for a 40-newton full-scale sensor is plus-or-minus 0.12 newtons. The repeated-trial random noise, estimated from the scatter in repeated measurements, is plus-or-minus 0.08 newtons as a one-sigma standard uncertainty. We combine these in quadrature — root-sum-square — to get the total measurement uncertainty before the coverage factor. Multiplying by two for 95% confidence gives a final result of 23.4 plus-or-minus 0.29 newtons. Notice that in this example the calibration uncertainty is larger than the random noise — it is the dominant contributor to the total uncertainty budget. If the experiment is sensitive enough that 0.29 newtons is insufficient precision for the intended use, the right action is to seek a better calibration reference, not to take more data and average down the noise. This is exactly the kind of dominant error source analysis that belongs in the Tier 2 group report. slide 8: The Section 3C Deliverable — What the Calibration Review Requires (Image: A structured documentation checklist for the Section 3C calibration status review. Six items, each with a checkbox: "1. Instrument name and serial number — for each sensor in the experiment." "2. Last calibration date — from certificate or instrument tag." "3. Calibration interval — stated on certificate (e.g., 12 months, 6 months)." "4. Current compliance status — in calibration / out of calibration / near expiration." "5. Calibration uncertainty — stated value from certificate, with coverage factor, propagated into final measurement uncertainty." "6. Conclusion — one sentence: given the calibration status and propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the intended use of this experiment?" A note at the bottom: "If any instrument is out of calibration, state this prominently and assess the implication for the validation conclusion. Do not ignore it.") Talking Point: The calibration status review is one of six required sections of your Tier 2 group report, and this checklist is exactly what it needs to contain. For every sensor in your Tier 2 experiment, you find the calibration certificate, document the last calibration date and the calibration interval, determine whether the instrument is currently in compliance, and record the calibration uncertainty with the coverage factor. You then propagate that uncertainty into the total measurement uncertainty budget using the root-sum-square method we just walked through. And finally, you write a one-sentence conclusion: given the calibration status and the propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the experiment's intended use? That sentence is the conclusion of Section 3C, and it feeds directly into the overall validation conclusion in the group report. If an instrument is out of calibration, you do not hide that — you document it, assess what it means for the uncertainty budget, and state explicitly whether the validation claim is weakened or invalidated by the calibration status. slide 9: A Complete Section 3C — What a Passing-Grade Entry Looks Like (Image: A formatted document mockup resembling a short report section. Header: "Section 3C: Calibration Status Review." Body is divided into five labeled fields, each on its own line with a value: "Instrument: Omega LCL-010 load cell, Serial #4471-A." "Last calibration date: 2025-09-15." "Calibration interval: 12 months (due: 2026-09-15 — currently IN calibration)." "Propagated calibration uncertainty (two arithmetic lines): u_cal = ±0.3% full scale (k=2) = 0.003 × 40 N = ±0.12 N; combined with noise: u_total = 2 × √((0.12)² + (0.08)²) = 2 × 0.144 N = ±0.29 N (95% confidence)." "Conclusion: The load cell is within its calibration interval. The calibration uncertainty (±0.12 N) is the dominant contributor to the total measurement uncertainty (±0.29 N). This precision is adequate for detecting model-data discrepancies above ±0.5 N as required by the stated intended use." A gold border frames the document mockup with a caption: "This is the format and level of specificity that meets the Tier 2 rubric pass standard for Section 3C." All values are fictional.) Talking Point: Let me show you exactly what a complete, passing-grade Section 3C entry looks like — not the checklist, but the actual prose that goes in the report. Instrument name and serial number — in this case, the load cell used in the canned experiment. Last calibration date: September 2025. Required interval: twelve months. Compliance status: in calibration, due September 2026. Then two lines of arithmetic: the certificate states a calibration uncertainty of plus-or-minus 0.3 percent of full scale, which for a 40-newton sensor is plus-or-minus 0.12 newtons at 95% confidence. Root-sum-square that with the random noise — plus-or-minus 0.08 newtons at one sigma — and you get a total combined uncertainty of plus-or-minus 0.29 newtons at 95% confidence. Then the one-sentence conclusion: the load cell is within calibration, the calibration uncertainty is the dominant contributor at 0.12 newtons, and this precision is adequate for detecting discrepancies above the 0.5-newton adequacy threshold set in the acceptance criterion. That is a complete Section 3C. It has a number, it has a conclusion, and it connects the calibration evidence back to the stated intended use. Every group that writes a Section 3C at this level will meet the rubric standard. slide 11: When Residuals Prompt Recalibration — Crossing from Calibration into Model Refinement (Image: A flow diagram with a branch point. Starting node: "Run experiment, compute residuals." First branch: "Residuals random and within uncertainty band" → "Model form adequate — document and proceed." Second branch: "Residuals systematic — trend present" → "Diagnose model-form error." Third branch: "Residuals show constant bias beyond uncertainty band" → Branch point: "Is the instrument in calibration?" → Yes: "Bias is real — investigate model parameters or systematic setup error." → No: "Recalibrate — then re-examine residuals with updated uncertainty." A special arrow exits the "Recalibrate" step and points back to "Run experiment" labeled: "If recalibration substantially changes the predicted value, you may have just performed model refinement disguised as measurement correction — document this explicitly." A warning icon appears on the recalibration loop with the label: "Check: are you correcting the sensor or fitting the model?") Talking Point: There is a subtle but important boundary between calibrating an instrument and refining a model, and it is possible to cross it without realizing it. When you observe a constant bias in the residuals and the instrument is out of calibration, the right first action is to recalibrate. But here is the warning: if the recalibration substantially changes the sensor's calibration coefficients in a way that happens to reduce the bias in your validation comparison — and if you discovered this by looking at the residuals rather than by comparing the sensor to an independent standard — you may have just used the experimental data to adjust the model rather than to test it. That is postdiction at the calibration level. Here is what the failure mode looks like in practice: a group finds their load cell consistently over-reads relative to the model. They "recalibrate" by adjusting the sensitivity coefficient until the readings match the model prediction. This is not calibration — it is model fitting. The correct approach: recalibrate the load cell against an independent reference force standard, then re-examine whether the residual remains. The protection is to ensure that recalibration is done against an independent reference standard, not in response to the model comparison. If you recalibrate against a reference and the residuals still show a bias, the bias is real and the model needs attention. If the recalibration resolves the bias, you have corrected a measurement error — and you need to document that the recalibration was performed against an independent standard, not in response to the data comparison. slide 12: Connecting Calibration Status to the Quad Chart Presentation (Image: A quad chart cell mockup — specifically the bottom-left cell labeled "Calibration Status." The cell contains four elements: "1. Instruments reviewed: [load cell, pressure transducer, accelerometer]." "2. Compliance: [load cell — in calibration (±0.3% full scale), pressure transducer — out of calibration since 2026-03-01, accelerometer — in calibration (±1% full scale)]." "3. Propagated calibration uncertainty: 0.29 N combined." "4. Conclusion: Load cell and accelerometer are adequate. Pressure transducer out of calibration — result uncertainty in that channel is unbounded; validation conclusion for pressure-dependent quantities is weakened." The cell is bounded by a gold frame to indicate it is one of four quad chart cells. A note below the mockup: "This cell is self-contained — it must communicate the key conclusion (adequate or not adequate) without reference to the other cells.") Talking Point: In the Week 10 quad chart presentation, one of the four cells is dedicated to the calibration status review. This is not a summary of what you found — it is a complete, self-contained argument in a small space. You list the instruments, their compliance status, the propagated calibration uncertainty, and the one-sentence conclusion about adequacy for the intended use. Notice in this example that one instrument is out of calibration — the pressure transducer, which expired in March. The conclusion does not hide this — it states explicitly that validation conclusions for pressure-dependent quantities are weakened because the calibration uncertainty is unbounded for that channel. That is the honest engineering statement, and it is the kind of statement that earns credibility from the audience and from the Q&A. Trying to hide an out-of-calibration instrument in the quad chart, or glossing over it in the report, is not just intellectually dishonest — it is the kind of error that gets caught under direct questioning, and it damages credibility much more than acknowledging the limitation directly. slide 13: Closure Calibration is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget — reference standard uncertainty, curve-fit residuals, and environmental drift since the last calibration — and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome as a root-sum-square contribution alongside random noise. Calibration status review is not a bureaucratic checkbox: it determines whether your measurement system is adequate for the intended use of the experiment, and it shapes the credibility of every validation claim you make. An out-of-calibration instrument does not invalidate your work, but it must be documented honestly and its implications for the uncertainty budget must be assessed. The distinction between calibrating an instrument — correcting its output against an independent standard — and using experimental data to fit a model remains critical here: if you discover a calibration issue by examining the residuals, the recalibration must be against an independent reference, not against the data itself. In the lab session today, you will locate the calibration documentation for the instruments in your Tier 2 experiment, complete the calibration status review, and begin the Section 3C analysis. That documentation is your evidence that you ran the measurement system correctly — and evidence is what makes validation credible. Agent Notes Content is grounded entirely in the W8 L2 blueprint purpose statement: calibration as a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget, calibration status review, off-the-shelf sensor verification example, calibration vs. validation distinction deepened. The P=IV off-the-shelf sensor verification example (slide 5) is specifically required by the blueprint, and the connection to Lab 1 is made explicit. Slide 9 addresses the boundary between recalibration and model refinement — the "crossing from calibration into model refinement" scenario described in the blueprint. The quad chart cell mockup in slide 10 connects the lecture material directly to the W10 deliverable. Calibration uncertainty budget arithmetic (slide 7) is worked through numerically, consistent with the course's expectation that every uncertainty claim is quantified. No Phase III concepts introduced. CLO 1, CLO 3 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Calibration Revisited — Formal Procedure & Calibration Status Review Talking Point: We introduced the calibration-versus-validation distinction in Week 3 of E1, and you have been applying that distinction conceptually ever since. This lecture deepens it. Calibration is not just a concept — it is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget, and the uncertainty it introduces propagates directly into your final measurement outcome. In Phase II, every group is required to document the calibration status of their instruments and assess whether that status is adequate for the intended use of the experiment. This lecture gives you the formal tools to do that correctly. slide 2: Calibration vs. Validation — The Distinction Deepened (Image: A side-by-side two-column comparison diagram. Left column header: "Calibration." Right column header: "Validation." Row 1 — "What it is": "Calibration: a measurement activity — comparing a sensor's output to a known reference and adjusting the relationship between input and output to reduce systematic bias." | "Validation: a modeling activity — testing whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's behavior." Row 2 — "What it operates on": "Calibration: the instrument — changes the sensor's calibration coefficients or correction curve." | "Validation: the model — provides evidence about whether the model is adequate for the intended use." Row 3 — "What 'success' looks like": "Calibration: the sensor output agrees with the reference standard within the stated calibration uncertainty." | "Validation: the model prediction agrees with the experimental outcome within the acceptance criterion." Row 4 — "What a 'fail' means": "Calibration: the instrument needs adjustment or replacement." | "Validation: the model is inadequate for the intended use — revision required." The table is clean and readable with alternating row shading. A caption at the bottom: "Conflating these two activities is one of the most common sources of credibility loss in experimental reports.") Talking Point: In Week 3 of E1, I told you that calibration and validation are distinct activities and that conflating them undermines experimental credibility. Now let me explain precisely why. Calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference — it is an operation on the instrument. When you recalibrate a load cell, you are changing the relationship between force applied and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical model predicts a physical system's behavior accurately — it is an operation on the model. When you compare a structural model's predicted deflection to the measured deflection, you are testing the model. The two activities operate on different objects and serve different purposes. The common error is running an experiment, comparing the data to a model, finding agreement, and concluding the model is validated — when what actually happened is that the sensor was calibrated against the physical system, which is a measurement activity, not a modeling validation. If the same data used to calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an independent validation test. You have demonstrated that your sensor is internally consistent, and nothing more. slide 3: The Calibration Uncertainty Budget — What Calibration Adds to the Error Stack (Image: A stacked bar chart illustration showing the total measurement uncertainty budget as a stack of three components. Component 1 (bottom, gold): "Random measurement noise — estimated from repeated trials." Component 2 (middle, lighter gold): "Sensor systematic bias — corrected by calibration." Component 3 (top, orange): "Residual calibration uncertainty — what remains after calibration." Below the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the systematic bias component but introduces its own uncertainty: the calibration uncertainty propagates into the corrected measurement." To the right, a simple equation: "u_total² = u_noise² + u_calibration²" where each term is labeled. A note below: "Calibration uncertainty comes from: (1) reference standard uncertainty, (2) curve-fit residuals, (3) environmental drift since last calibration." The visual communicates that calibration is not free — it trades one source of uncertainty for another, smaller one.) Talking Point: Every calibration has its own uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three components. First, the reference standard uncertainty — the known reference used to calibrate your sensor is itself not perfectly accurate; it has a stated uncertainty that transfers directly to your calibration. Second, the curve-fit residuals — when you fit a calibration curve to the reference-versus-sensor-output data, the fit is not perfect; the residual scatter around the calibration curve represents uncertainty in the corrected sensor output at any given input. Third, environmental drift since the last calibration — sensors change over time and with environmental conditions, so a sensor that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the magnitude of that drift is uncertain. The total calibration uncertainty is the root-sum-square (RSS) of these three components — the same quadrature combination you first used in E1 Week 2 — and it adds in quadrature to the random measurement noise in your total uncertainty budget. Calibration makes measurements more accurate by reducing systematic bias, but it does not make measurements perfect — it trades a large systematic error for a smaller residual one, and that residual must be documented and propagated. slide 4: Calibration Interval and Calibration Status — What to Look For (Image: An instrument calibration certificate mockup showing five key fields: "Instrument: Load Cell LCxxx, Serial XXXXXXXX," "Last calibration date: 2025-11-10," "Calibration interval: 12 months," "Calibration due date: 2026-11-10," "Calibration uncertainty: ±0.3% full scale (k=2, 95% confidence)." Below the certificate, a status assessment panel with three conditions color-coded: Green check — "In calibration: current date is before due date." Yellow caution — "Near expiration: within 30 days of due date — use with awareness." Red X — "Out of calibration: current date is past due date — result credibility is uncertain, document prominently." A small note: "The calibration uncertainty stated on the certificate (±0.3%) propagates into your final measurement outcome whether the instrument is in calibration or not. Document both the status and the uncertainty.") Talking Point: The calibration status review in your Tier 2 group report requires you to find and document three things for each instrument in the experiment: the last calibration date, the calibration interval, and whether the instrument is currently within its calibration interval. This information is on the instrument's calibration certificate — a document that is typically attached to the instrument physically, or in the lab documentation file. The calibration certificate also states the calibration uncertainty in the form of a numerical value and a coverage factor. The coverage factor k=2 means the stated uncertainty represents a 95% confidence interval (k=2 is the coverage factor for a 95% CI introduced in E1 Week 2 — a reminder that the CI half-width is k times the standard uncertainty) — which is the standard we use throughout this course. If the instrument is out of calibration, you must document that prominently and assess what it means for the credibility of your result. An out-of-calibration instrument does not necessarily mean the data is worthless, but it means the systematic bias component of the uncertainty is unknown and cannot be bounded by the certificate. You need to acknowledge that limitation explicitly and assess whether it changes your conclusion about whether the model is adequate for the intended use. slide 5: Verifying an Off-the-Shelf Sensor Against First Principles — A Worked Example (Image: A workbench setup diagram for the P=IV power sensor verification example. The setup shows a commercial power sensor connected to a resistive load, alongside a standard digital multimeter measuring voltage and a second multimeter measuring current. Below the setup: a table with three rows — "Power sensor reading: 12.4 W," "First-principles: P = V × I = 12.2 W," "Discrepancy: 0.2 W (1.6%)." A propagated uncertainty column shows "Power sensor stated accuracy: ±2% full scale (±0.3 W at 12.4 W)" and "First-principles uncertainty (from Lecture E1 W2): ±0.15 W." The two results are compared: "12.4 ± 0.3 W vs. 12.2 ± 0.15 W — overlap within combined uncertainty bounds." Caption: "Verification: checking sensor output against first principles. Distinct from calibration: you are not adjusting the sensor — you are checking whether it is consistent with an independent standard.") Talking Point: Let me show you a concrete example of sensor verification using first principles — this is related to the P=IV lab you did in Week 2 of E1, and I want you to see the connection explicitly. A commercial power sensor is attached to a resistive load. You want to verify that the sensor's output is physically consistent — not to recalibrate it, but to check whether it agrees with first principles using standard instruments. You use two multimeters: one measuring voltage, one measuring current. You compute power from P equals V times I, using the same partial-derivative uncertainty propagation you applied in Lab 1. The power sensor reads 12.4 watts. The first-principles computation gives 12.2 watts. The discrepancy is 0.2 watts. The propagated uncertainties overlap — 12.4 plus-or-minus 0.3 watts and 12.2 plus-or-minus 0.15 watts. This is a verification result: you are not adjusting the sensor's calibration, you are confirming that its output is consistent with an independent physical measurement within the combined uncertainty bounds. If the results did not overlap, you would have grounds to suspect a calibration issue and seek a formal recalibration before using the sensor for a validation claim. This is the calibration-versus-validation distinction applied to a commercial sensor in a real scenario. slide 6: What the Calibration Status Means for Your Validation Claim (Image: A two-by-two decision matrix. X-axis: "Calibration status" — labeled "In calibration" (left) and "Out of calibration" (right). Y-axis: "Model-data agreement" — labeled "Agreement within bounds" (top) and "Discrepancy beyond bounds" (bottom). Four cells with interpretations: Top-left (in calibration, agreement): "Strong validation case — measurement uncertainty is characterized and agreement is established." Top-right (out of calibration, agreement): "Weak validation case — agreement may be coincidental; calibration-related bias is unknown." Bottom-left (in calibration, discrepancy): "Credible diagnostic — discrepancy is real, calibration is not the cause; investigate model." Bottom-right (out of calibration, discrepancy): "Inconclusive — cannot determine whether discrepancy is from model or from out-of-calibration bias; instrument must be recalibrated before conclusions can be drawn." Each cell has a color code: Top-left green, Top-right yellow, Bottom-left orange, Bottom-right red.) Talking Point: This two-by-two matrix captures the practical relationship between calibration status and validation credibility. The strongest validation case is the top-left cell: the instruments are in calibration, the calibration uncertainty is documented, and the model agrees with the data within the uncertainty bounds. You can make a credible claim about model adequacy because the uncertainty is bounded and characterized. The bottom-left cell is the credible diagnostic case: the instruments are in calibration, but the model disagrees with the data. Because calibration is not the issue, the discrepancy is real and you can investigate the model confidently. The weakest cases are on the right side of the matrix. If the instrument is out of calibration and the model appears to agree, you cannot determine whether the agreement is genuine or coincidental — the uncalibrated sensor may be biased in a way that makes the model look correct by accident. And if the instrument is out of calibration and there is a discrepancy, you cannot determine whether the discrepancy is from the model or from the unmeasured calibration drift. The practical implication is clear: calibration status must be verified before you run the experiment, not discovered during the analysis. slide 7: Propagating Calibration Uncertainty into the Final Result (Image: A step-by-step calculation layout. Step 1: "Sensor calibration uncertainty from certificate: u_cal = ±0.3% full scale = ±0.12 N for a 40 N full-scale load cell." Step 2: "Random measurement noise (from repeated trials): u_noise = ±0.08 N (standard uncertainty, k=1)." Step 3: "Root-sum-square combination: u_total = sqrt(u_cal² + u_noise²) = sqrt((0.12)² + (0.08)²) = sqrt(0.0144 + 0.0064) = sqrt(0.0208) = 0.144 N." Step 4: "Final result: F = 23.4 ± 0.29 N (k=2, 95% confidence)." A note at the bottom: "The calibration uncertainty (0.12 N) was larger than the noise (0.08 N) in this example — it is the dominant error source. Improving the calibration reference would reduce the total uncertainty more than improving the measurement setup.") Talking Point: Let me walk through the arithmetic of propagating calibration uncertainty into a final measurement result — this is exactly what you will include in Section 3C of your Tier 2 group report. The load cell in this example has a calibration uncertainty of plus-or-minus 0.3 percent of full scale, which for a 40-newton full-scale sensor is plus-or-minus 0.12 newtons. The repeated-trial random noise, estimated from the scatter in repeated measurements, is plus-or-minus 0.08 newtons as a one-sigma standard uncertainty. We combine these in quadrature — root-sum-square — to get the total measurement uncertainty before the coverage factor. Multiplying by two for 95% confidence gives a final result of 23.4 plus-or-minus 0.29 newtons. Notice that in this example the calibration uncertainty is larger than the random noise — it is the dominant contributor to the total uncertainty budget. If the experiment is sensitive enough that 0.29 newtons is insufficient precision for the intended use, the right action is to seek a better calibration reference, not to take more data and average down the noise. This is exactly the kind of dominant error source analysis that belongs in the Tier 2 group report. slide 8: The Section 3C Deliverable — What the Calibration Review Requires (Image: A structured documentation checklist for the Section 3C calibration status review. Six items, each with a checkbox: "1. Instrument name and serial number — for each sensor in the experiment." "2. Last calibration date — from certificate or instrument tag." "3. Calibration interval — stated on certificate (e.g., 12 months, 6 months)." "4. Current compliance status — in calibration / out of calibration / near expiration." "5. Calibration uncertainty — stated value from certificate, with coverage factor, propagated into final measurement uncertainty." "6. Conclusion — one sentence: given the calibration status and propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the intended use of this experiment?" A note at the bottom: "If any instrument is out of calibration, state this prominently and assess the implication for the validation conclusion. Do not ignore it.") Talking Point: The calibration status review is one of six required sections of your Tier 2 group report, and this checklist is exactly what it needs to contain. For every sensor in your Tier 2 experiment, you find the calibration certificate, document the last calibration date and the calibration interval, determine whether the instrument is currently in compliance, and record the calibration uncertainty with the coverage factor. You then propagate that uncertainty into the total measurement uncertainty budget using the root-sum-square method we just walked through. And finally, you write a one-sentence conclusion: given the calibration status and the propagated uncertainty, is the measurement system adequate for the experiment's intended use? That sentence is the conclusion of Section 3C, and it feeds directly into the overall validation conclusion in the group report. If an instrument is out of calibration, you do not hide that — you document it, assess what it means for the uncertainty budget, and state explicitly whether the validation claim is weakened or invalidated by the calibration status. slide 9: A Complete Section 3C — What a Passing-Grade Entry Looks Like (Image: A formatted document mockup resembling a short report section. Header: "Section 3C: Calibration Status Review." Body is divided into five labeled fields, each on its own line with a value: "Instrument: Omega LCL-010 load cell, Serial #4471-A." "Last calibration date: 2025-09-15." "Calibration interval: 12 months (due: 2026-09-15 — currently IN calibration)." "Propagated calibration uncertainty (two arithmetic lines): u_cal = ±0.3% full scale (k=2) = 0.003 × 40 N = ±0.12 N; combined with noise: u_total = 2 × √((0.12)² + (0.08)²) = 2 × 0.144 N = ±0.29 N (95% confidence)." "Conclusion: The load cell is within its calibration interval. The calibration uncertainty (±0.12 N) is the dominant contributor to the total measurement uncertainty (±0.29 N). This precision is adequate for detecting model-data discrepancies above ±0.5 N as required by the stated intended use." A gold border frames the document mockup with a caption: "This is the format and level of specificity that meets the Tier 2 rubric pass standard for Section 3C." All values are fictional.) Talking Point: Let me show you exactly what a complete, passing-grade Section 3C entry looks like — not the checklist, but the actual prose that goes in the report. Instrument name and serial number — in this case, the load cell used in the canned experiment. Last calibration date: September 2025. Required interval: twelve months. Compliance status: in calibration, due September 2026. Then two lines of arithmetic: the certificate states a calibration uncertainty of plus-or-minus 0.3 percent of full scale, which for a 40-newton sensor is plus-or-minus 0.12 newtons at 95% confidence. Root-sum-square that with the random noise — plus-or-minus 0.08 newtons at one sigma — and you get a total combined uncertainty of plus-or-minus 0.29 newtons at 95% confidence. Then the one-sentence conclusion: the load cell is within calibration, the calibration uncertainty is the dominant contributor at 0.12 newtons, and this precision is adequate for detecting discrepancies above the 0.5-newton adequacy threshold set in the acceptance criterion. That is a complete Section 3C. It has a number, it has a conclusion, and it connects the calibration evidence back to the stated intended use. Every group that writes a Section 3C at this level will meet the rubric standard. slide 11: When Residuals Prompt Recalibration — Crossing from Calibration into Model Refinement (Image: A flow diagram with a branch point. Starting node: "Run experiment, compute residuals." First branch: "Residuals random and within uncertainty band" → "Model form adequate — document and proceed." Second branch: "Residuals systematic — trend present" → "Diagnose model-form error." Third branch: "Residuals show constant bias beyond uncertainty band" → Branch point: "Is the instrument in calibration?" → Yes: "Bias is real — investigate model parameters or systematic setup error." → No: "Recalibrate — then re-examine residuals with updated uncertainty." A special arrow exits the "Recalibrate" step and points back to "Run experiment" labeled: "If recalibration substantially changes the predicted value, you may have just performed model refinement disguised as measurement correction — document this explicitly." A warning icon appears on the recalibration loop with the label: "Check: are you correcting the sensor or fitting the model?") Talking Point: There is a subtle but important boundary between calibrating an instrument and refining a model, and it is possible to cross it without realizing it. When you observe a constant bias in the residuals and the instrument is out of calibration, the right first action is to recalibrate. But here is the warning: if the recalibration substantially changes the sensor's calibration coefficients in a way that happens to reduce the bias in your validation comparison — and if you discovered this by looking at the residuals rather than by comparing the sensor to an independent standard — you may have just used the experimental data to adjust the model rather than to test it. That is postdiction at the calibration level. Here is what the failure mode looks like in practice: a group finds their load cell consistently over-reads relative to the model. They "recalibrate" by adjusting the sensitivity coefficient until the readings match the model prediction. This is not calibration — it is model fitting. The correct approach: recalibrate the load cell against an independent reference force standard, then re-examine whether the residual remains. The protection is to ensure that recalibration is done against an independent reference standard, not in response to the model comparison. If you recalibrate against a reference and the residuals still show a bias, the bias is real and the model needs attention. If the recalibration resolves the bias, you have corrected a measurement error — and you need to document that the recalibration was performed against an independent standard, not in response to the data comparison. slide 12: Connecting Calibration Status to the Quad Chart Presentation (Image: A quad chart cell mockup — specifically the bottom-left cell labeled "Calibration Status." The cell contains four elements: "1. Instruments reviewed: [load cell, pressure transducer, accelerometer]." "2. Compliance: [load cell — in calibration (±0.3% full scale), pressure transducer — out of calibration since 2026-03-01, accelerometer — in calibration (±1% full scale)]." "3. Propagated calibration uncertainty: 0.29 N combined." "4. Conclusion: Load cell and accelerometer are adequate. Pressure transducer out of calibration — result uncertainty in that channel is unbounded; validation conclusion for pressure-dependent quantities is weakened." The cell is bounded by a gold frame to indicate it is one of four quad chart cells. A note below the mockup: "This cell is self-contained — it must communicate the key conclusion (adequate or not adequate) without reference to the other cells.") Talking Point: In the Week 10 quad chart presentation, one of the four cells is dedicated to the calibration status review. This is not a summary of what you found — it is a complete, self-contained argument in a small space. You list the instruments, their compliance status, the propagated calibration uncertainty, and the one-sentence conclusion about adequacy for the intended use. Notice in this example that one instrument is out of calibration — the pressure transducer, which expired in March. The conclusion does not hide this — it states explicitly that validation conclusions for pressure-dependent quantities are weakened because the calibration uncertainty is unbounded for that channel. That is the honest engineering statement, and it is the kind of statement that earns credibility from the audience and from the Q&A. Trying to hide an out-of-calibration instrument in the quad chart, or glossing over it in the report, is not just intellectually dishonest — it is the kind of error that gets caught under direct questioning, and it damages credibility much more than acknowledging the limitation directly. slide 13: Closure Calibration is a formal procedure with its own uncertainty budget — reference standard uncertainty, curve-fit residuals, and environmental drift since the last calibration — and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome as a root-sum-square contribution alongside random noise. es directly into your final measurement outcome. This lecture gives you the formal tools to do that correctly. slide 2: Calibration vs. Validation — The Distinction Deepened (Image: A side-by-side two-column comparison diagram. Left column header: "Calibration." Right column header: "Validation." Row 1 — "What it is": "Calibration: a measurement activity — comparing a sensor's output to a known reference and adjusting the relationship between input and output to reduce systematic bias." | "Validation: a modeling activity — testing whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's

Validation: a modeling activity — testing whether a mathematical model makes accurate predictions of a physical system's behavior." Row 2 — "What it operates on": "Calibration: the instrument — changes the sensor's calibration coefficients or correction curve." | "Validation: the model — provides evidence about whether the model is adequate for the intended use." Row 3 — "What 'success' looks like": "Calibration: the sensor output agrees with the reference standard within the stated calibration uncertainty." | "Validation: the model prediction agrees with the experimental outcome within the acceptance criterion." ment needs adjustment or replacement." | "Validation: the model is inadequate for the intended use — revision required." The table is clean and readable with alternating row shading. A caption at the bottom: "Conflating these two activities is one of the most common sources of credibility loss in experimental reports.") Talking Point: In Week 3 of E1, I told you that calibration and validation are distinct activities and that conflating them undermines experimental credibility. Now let me explain precisely why. Calibration adjusts a sensor's output to match a known reference — it is an operation on the instrument. When you recalibrate a load cell, you are changing the relationship between force applied and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical

relationship between force applied and voltage output to reduce systematic bias. Validation tests whether a mathematical model predicts a physical system's behavior accurately — it is an operation on the model. When you compare a structural model's predicted deflection to the measured deflection, you are testing the model. The two activities operate on different objects and serve different purposes. The common error is running an experiment, comparing the data to a model, finding agreement, and concluding the model is validated — when what actually happened is that the sensor was calibrated against the physical system, which is a measurement activity, not a modeling validation. If the same data used to calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an

validation. If the same data used to calibrate the sensor is then used to validate the model, you have not performed an independent validation test. You have demonstrated that your sensor is internally consistent, and nothing more. slide 3: The Calibration Uncertainty Budget — What Calibration Adds to the Error Stack (Image: A stacked bar chart illustration showing the total measurement uncertainty budget as a stack of three components. Component 1 (bottom, gold): "Random measurement noise — estimated from repeated trials." Component 2 (middle, lighter gold): "Sensor systematic bias — corrected by calibration." Component 3 (top, orange): "Residual calibration uncertainty — what remains after calibration." Below the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the

ertainty — what remains after calibration." Below the corrected measurement bar, a label reads: "Calibration reduces the systematic bias component but introduces its own uncertainty: the calibration uncertainty propagates into the corrected measurement." To the right, a simple equation: "u_total² = u_noise² + u_calibration²" where each term is labeled. A note below: "Calibration uncertainty comes from: (1) reference standard uncertainty, (2) curve-fit residuals, (3) environmental drift since last calibration." The visual communicates that calibration is not free — it trades one source of uncertainty for another, smaller one.) Talking Point: Every calibration has its own uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three

uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into your final measurement outcome. The calibration uncertainty has three components. First, the reference standard uncertainty — the known reference used to calibrate your sensor is itself not perfectly accurate; it has a stated uncertainty that transfers directly to your calibration. Second, the curve-fit residuals — when you fit a calibration curve to the reference-versus-sensor-output data, the fit is not perfect; the residual scatter around the calibration curve represents uncertainty in the corrected sensor output at any given input. Third, environmental drift since the last calibration — sensors change over time and with environmental conditions, so a sensor that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the

e and with environmental conditions, so a sensor that was calibrated six months ago may have drifted since then, and the magnitude of that drift is uncertain. The total calibration uncertainty is the root-sum-square (RSS) of these three components — the same quadrature combination you first used in E1 Week 2 — and it adds in quadrature to the random measurement noise in your total uncertainty budget. Calibration makes measurements more accurate by reducing systematic bias, but it does not make measurements perfect — it trades a large systematic error for a smaller residual one, and that residual must be documented and propagated. slide 4: Calibration Interval and Calibration Status — What to Look For (Image: An instrument calibration certificate mockup showing five key fields:

ntation file. The calibration certificate also states the calibration uncertainty in the form of a numerical value and a coverage factor. nd 12.2 plus-or-minus 0.15 watts.
