pages.title: L01
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Lecture L01 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Comparing Datasets — Statistical Significance & Confidence Interval Overlap Talking Point: Every comparison we have made in this course so far has been between a model prediction and a sing... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Comparing Datasets — Statistical Significance & Confidence Interval Overlap Talking Point: Every comparison we have made in this course so far has been between a model prediction and a single experimental measurement, or between a single measurement and an accepted reference value. Week 9 extends the toolkit to a harder and more common case: comparing two or more datasets — multiple test conditions, multiple groups, multiple configurations — and making a quantitative, defensible statement about whether they are meaningfully different. This is the question that drives most real engineering decisions: is Configuration A statistically distinguishable from Configuration B? Is the treatment effect real, or is it within the noise? The statistical tools we cover today give you the vocabulary to answer those questions without relying on visual inspection. slide 2: From One Point to Many — The Comparison Problem (Image: A three-panel illustration showing an escalating comparison challenge. Panel 1: "Single measurement vs. model." A single data point with an error bar plotted against a model prediction line. Caption: "E1: does this measurement agree with this prediction?" Panel 2: "Single dataset vs. model prediction curve." A scatter of data points across a range of conditions plotted against a prediction curve, with a residual band. Caption: "E2 so far: does this dataset agree with this model across conditions?" Panel 3: "Two datasets compared to each other." Two sets of scatter points — one in gold, one in gray — at the same set of conditions, overlapping but with visible offsets at some points. Error bars on each point. Caption: "E2 Week 9: are these two datasets statistically distinguishable? Does the difference matter for the intended use?" Each panel is clean with labeled axes and consistent visual style. The escalation from left to right illustrates the growing analytical challenge.) Talking Point: Let me frame the challenge before introducing the tools. In E1, the comparison question was simple: does this measurement agree with a prediction? One number, one comparison, one uncertainty estimate. In E2 Weeks 6 through 8, we extended that to comparing a full dataset to a model prediction curve — the residual analysis we covered in Lecture 1 this week. But the full comparison problem is often harder than either of those cases. In a real experimental campaign, you might run the same experiment at two different operating conditions, or with two different configurations, or with two different test setups. The question is whether those two datasets are meaningfully different from each other — whether the difference you observe is real, or whether it is within the noise of your measurement system. That question requires comparing means, comparing distributions, and making a statement about whether the observed difference is statistically distinguishable. That is what today's lecture provides. slide 3: Standard Error of the Mean — Why More Trials Reduce Uncertainty Use standard error (σ/√n) when comparing means. Use standard deviation (σ) when characterizing individual measurement scatter. (Image: A conceptual illustration showing three sample distributions, each representing the distribution of a sample mean from an experiment with n = 5, n = 20, and n = 100 trials respectively. All three distributions are centered at the same true value. The n=5 distribution is wide and low. The n=20 distribution is narrower and taller. The n=100 distribution is very narrow and tall. Each distribution is labeled with its standard error: "σ/√5," "σ/√20," "σ/√100" respectively, where σ is the population standard deviation. An annotation reads: "The uncertainty in the mean decreases as 1/√n — this is why averaging reduces the mean's uncertainty even if each trial has the same noise." A box below the three distributions reads: "Key distinction: σ is the standard deviation of individual measurements; σ/√n is the standard error — the uncertainty in the mean of n measurements." The visual clearly shows the distributions narrowing as n increases.) Talking Point: Before we compare datasets, I need to make sure the statistical foundation from Week 2 is solid — specifically the distinction between the standard deviation of individual measurements and the standard error of the mean. The standard deviation characterizes how much scatter there is in any single measurement trial — it describes the width of the measurement distribution. The standard error of the mean is the uncertainty in the average value computed from n trials, and it is equal to the standard deviation divided by the square root of n. This is the answer to the question I posed at the start of E2 Week 6: why does the uncertainty in the mean decrease with more trials? Because the mean is a statistic computed from n measurements, and each additional measurement provides additional information that narrows the estimate of the true value. The standard error is what we use when comparing means across datasets — not the standard deviation. If you compare means using standard deviations, you are overestimating the uncertainty in the comparison by the factor of square root of n. slide 4: Confidence Interval Overlap — The Practical Comparison Test (Image: Four scenarios of confidence interval comparison, arranged in a two-by-two grid. Each scenario shows two means (represented as vertical bars with horizontal caps at their 95% confidence interval bounds). Scenario 1: "Clear non-overlap." The two confidence intervals are completely separated. Caption: "Statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence — the difference is real at this precision level." Scenario 2: "Partial overlap." The confidence intervals overlap by about 30% of their width. Caption: "Likely distinguishable — a formal significance test is needed to confirm." Scenario 3: "Full overlap, one inside the other." One interval is entirely contained within the other. Caption: "Not distinguishable at this sample size — results are consistent with same population mean." Scenario 4: "Confidence intervals overlap at one edge." Just touching. Caption: "Borderline case — significance depends on the specific test and its assumptions." Each scenario has the same visual style: gold bars, labeled mean values, labeled confidence bounds.) Talking Point: The most practical comparison tool for engineering data is confidence interval overlap. If you have computed a 95% confidence interval for the mean of each dataset, you can use the overlap — or lack of overlap — between those intervals as a quick test of whether the datasets are statistically distinguishable. If the two confidence intervals do not overlap at all, the datasets are statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence. If the intervals have moderate overlap, a formal significance test may be needed to confirm. If one interval is entirely contained within the other, the datasets are not distinguishable at this sample size — the precision of your measurement is insufficient to tell them apart. One important caveat on borderline cases: CI overlap is a conservative test. If 95% CIs don't overlap, the means are certainly distinguishable at 95% confidence. If they partially overlap, the means may still be statistically different at the 5% level — use a t-test for borderline cases rather than declaring "not significant" based on visual overlap alone. The confidence interval overlap test is intuitive and visually interpretable, which is why it is preferred in experimental engineering over reporting raw p-values, which require careful interpretation to avoid the common misreading of "statistically significant means practically important." I will come back to that distinction in a moment, because it is critical for the Tier 2 analysis. slide 5: What Statistical Significance Actually Means — And What It Does Not (Image: A conceptual diagram with two independent axes. X-axis: "Statistical significance" — a bar ranging from "Not statistically significant" to "Statistically significant" with a vertical dashed line labeled "Significance threshold (α = 0.05)." Y-axis: "Engineering significance" — a bar ranging from "Below engineering threshold" to "Above engineering threshold" with a horizontal dashed line labeled "Engineering acceptance criterion." Four quadrants: Top-right: "Statistically significant AND engineering significant — the difference matters." Top-left: "Statistically significant but NOT engineering significant — real difference, but too small to matter for the intended use." Bottom-right: "Engineering significant but NOT statistically significant — the difference would matter, but sample size is too small to detect it." Bottom-left: "Neither — no meaningful difference." Each quadrant has a brief decision note. The diagram communicates that statistical significance and engineering significance are independent dimensions.) Talking Point: Statistical significance and engineering significance are not the same thing, and confusing them leads to wrong conclusions. A result is statistically significant if the probability that the observed difference arose by chance — under the null hypothesis that the populations are identical — is below a threshold, typically five percent. A result is engineering significant if the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. These two things are independent. Here is a concrete case where they diverge: Configuration A reads 100.0 ± 0.2 N; Configuration B reads 100.3 ± 0.2 N. The 95% CIs don't overlap — the 0.3 N difference is statistically significant. But 0.3 N is far below the 2% engineering threshold of 2.0 N. Real difference, irrelevant consequence — top-left quadrant. With a large enough sample, you can achieve statistical significance for a difference that is utterly irrelevant to the engineering decision — a load cell reads 0.05% higher under two test configurations, and with 10,000 measurements, that difference is statistically significant. But if your validation criterion is agreement within 2%, a 0.05% difference has no engineering significance. Conversely, a difference that is large enough to matter for the intended use may not achieve statistical significance if your sample size is too small to distinguish it from noise. For the Tier 2 analysis, the question is always: does this difference matter for the stated intended use? Statistical significance is the test of whether the difference is real. Engineering significance is the test of whether it matters. slide 6: Comparing Two Means — A Worked Example in MATLAB (Image: A MATLAB figure showing a comparison of two experimental datasets. Two bar charts with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals: "Configuration A: mean = 47.3 N, 95% CI = [45.8, 48.8] N" and "Configuration B: mean = 52.1 N, 95% CI = [50.9, 53.3] N." The two confidence intervals are completely non-overlapping — there is a visible gap between [45.8, 48.8] and [50.9, 53.3]. A horizontal dashed line labeled "Acceptance criterion: agreement within ±5% of nominal (47.3 N baseline)" is shown, and Configuration B's mean falls outside the acceptance criterion band. MATLAB-style figure with labeled axes "Configuration" and "Force (N)," a legend, and a caption reading "Configuration B mean exceeds acceptance criterion band — difference is statistically significant and engineering significant at this intended use.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a concrete comparison using MATLAB-style output. Two configurations of the same experimental rig are tested: Configuration A is the baseline, Configuration B has a modification. The sample means and 95% confidence intervals are shown. The two intervals do not overlap — Configuration B is statistically distinguishable from Configuration A at 95% confidence. The engineering acceptance criterion, which was pre-stated as agreement within 5% of the nominal value from Configuration A, is shown as the dashed band. Configuration B's mean falls outside that band. Therefore, the difference is both statistically significant — the intervals do not overlap — and engineering significant — the difference exceeds the pre-stated acceptance criterion. Both conclusions together constitute a defensible validation finding. Notice that I stated the acceptance criterion was pre-stated — this is the prediction-before-exposure principle applied to the comparison test. If you define "close enough" after you see both datasets, the comparison loses its independence. slide 7: When Datasets Disagree — Three Interpretive Paths (Image: A flowchart with three branches from the root question "Two datasets show a meaningful difference. What does it tell you?" Branch 1: "Both datasets from different test conditions → The system responds differently under the two conditions — the model should account for the sensitivity." Branch 2: "Both datasets from nominally identical conditions → Measurement system issue OR uncontrolled variable — investigate repeatability and setup." Branch 3: "One dataset is a model prediction, one is experimental → Model-data discrepancy — apply residual analysis from W8." Each branch has a MATLAB analysis type labeled below it: Branch 1 → "Confidence interval comparison + regression across conditions." Branch 2 → "Repeatability analysis — standard deviation of repeated trials at same condition." Branch 3 → "Residual plot + discrepancy classification (W8 vocabulary)." The flowchart is clean with the same visual style as previous flowcharts in the course.) Talking Point: When two datasets differ, the interpretation depends on what the two datasets represent. If the datasets are from two different test conditions — different configurations, different operating points — then the difference is a characterization of the system: it responds differently under the two conditions, and that information is useful for understanding the model. If the datasets are from nominally identical conditions and should be repeatable, then a meaningful difference indicates a measurement system problem or an uncontrolled variable. And if one of the datasets is a model prediction and the other is experimental data, you are back in the residual analysis territory from Lecture 1. The statistical comparison tools are the same across all three cases — confidence intervals, standard errors, overlap tests — but the engineering interpretation depends entirely on what the datasets represent. Before you compute, ask: what are these two datasets, and what would a difference between them mean? slide 8: Hypothesis Testing — Conceptual Level (Image: A conceptual diagram of hypothesis testing without any formulas. Two states are shown: "Null hypothesis H₀: the two population means are the same — any observed difference is due to sampling variability." "Alternative hypothesis H₁: the two population means are genuinely different — the observed difference is real." A sample difference distribution is shown in the center, labeled "Distribution of possible differences if H₀ is true." A vertical dashed line on the right tail of the distribution is labeled "Observed difference." A shaded region in the right tail beyond the observed difference is labeled "p-value: the probability of observing a difference this large if H₀ is true." A decision box below reads: "If p-value < α (typically 0.05), we reject H₀ — the difference is statistically significant." A caution note: "p < 0.05 does NOT mean: there is a 95% probability that H₁ is true. It means: the data is unlikely under H₀. These are different statements.") Talking Point: I want to give you a conceptual understanding of formal hypothesis testing without requiring you to implement the full statistical machinery from scratch. The null hypothesis is the assumption that any difference you observe between two datasets is due to sampling variability alone — the two populations are actually identical. The alternative hypothesis is that the populations are genuinely different. The p-value is the probability of observing a difference as large as the one you measured, assuming the null hypothesis is true. If the p-value is below the threshold — conventionally five percent — we say the result is statistically significant: the data is unlikely to have arisen by chance under the null hypothesis. Here is the caution I want you to internalize: a p-value below five percent does not mean there is a 95% probability that the difference is real. It means the data is inconsistent with the null hypothesis at the five-percent level. This distinction matters because p-values are routinely misread, and in engineering contexts, a misread p-value can lead to over-confident conclusions. The confidence interval overlap test I showed you earlier is often more interpretable in engineering contexts precisely because it is more directly connected to the physical question: are these two values close enough to be considered the same for the intended use? slide 9: MATLAB Implementation — Comparing Means and CI Overlap (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results shown alongside. Code lines (styled as MATLAB code): "% Sample means and standard deviations from two test conditions"; "n_A = 25; mean_A = 47.3; std_A = 3.2;"; "n_B = 25; mean_B = 52.1; std_B = 2.9;"; "SE_A = std_A / sqrt(n_A); % Standard error of mean A"; "SE_B = std_B / sqrt(n_B); % Standard error of mean B"; "CI_A = mean_A + [-1 1] 2 SE_A; % 95% CI (approx k=2)"; "CI_B = mean_B + [-1 1] 2 SE_B;"; "% Check overlap"; "overlap = CI_A(2) >= CI_B(1); % Logical: 1=overlap, 0=no overlap." Results panel below the code: "CI_A = [45.9, 48.6] N" | "CI_B = [50.9, 53.2] N" | "overlap = 0 (no overlap — statistically distinguishable at 95% CI)." A note below the code: "k=2 is an approximation for 95% CI. For small samples (n < 30), use the t-distribution critical value instead of 2." The code is readable and styled as a MATLAB script with comment lines.) Talking Point: Here is the MATLAB implementation you will use in the lab session today. The standard error of the mean is the sample standard deviation divided by the square root of n — that is the line that converts from the standard deviation of individual measurements to the uncertainty in the mean. The 95% confidence interval uses a coverage factor of 2 as an approximation for large samples. For small samples — fewer than about 30 trials — the t-distribution gives a larger critical value than 2, and you would use MATLAB's tinv function to get the exact value for the degrees of freedom. The overlap check is a simple logical comparison: does the upper bound of the first confidence interval exceed the lower bound of the second? In this example it does not — CI_A ends at 48.6 and CI_B begins at 50.9 — so the intervals do not overlap and the datasets are statistically distinguishable. This is the starting point for your Section 4 uncertainty analysis in the Tier 2 report: you need to compute the 95% confidence interval for your measurement means and compare them to the model prediction or to a second test condition. slide 10: Connecting Statistical Comparison to the Tier 2 Report and Improvement Proposal (Image: A flow diagram connecting the statistical comparison analysis to three report sections. Starting node: "Statistical comparison analysis — CI overlap, significance test, difference characterization." Three arrows branch to: Arrow 1: "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome" — labeled "Use CI of experimental mean to evaluate whether model prediction falls within bounds." Arrow 2: "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis" — labeled "Report standard error, CI, comparison of Monte Carlo vs. partial derivative method." Arrow 3: "Section 6: Improvement proposal — category (c): experimental design" — labeled "If statistical power was insufficient to distinguish configurations, propose increased n or improved measurement precision." A note at the bottom: "Statistical comparison is both an analysis tool (Sections 3A and 3B) and a diagnostic for improvement (Section 6). The improvement proposal must be grounded in the statistical analysis, not generic.") Talking Point: The statistical comparison tools you learn today connect to the Tier 2 report in two directions. First, they are analysis tools: you use confidence interval comparison to evaluate whether the model prediction falls within the uncertainty bounds of your experimental mean, and you report the standard error and CI in the uncertainty analysis section. Second, and less obviously, they are diagnostic tools for the improvement proposal. If your confidence intervals were too wide to distinguish the model prediction from the data — not because the model is good, but because your measurement was imprecise — then the improvement proposal should address that: more trials at each condition, or a more precise sensor, or reduced noise through better signal conditioning. The improvement must be grounded in the statistical result. "Our CI was too wide to make a conclusion about the model at the required precision" is a specific finding, and the proposed fix — a specific increase in sample size or measurement precision — is the corresponding improvement. That is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit. slide 10b: Advance Organizer — Comparative Analysis Discussion (Discussion preview) Talking Point: Before the discussion activity: in the comparative analysis today, apply the W8 taxonomy. Look for R² reported without a residual plot — that is adequacy claimed without the diagnostic. Look for statistical significance treated as validation — a p-value does not tell you the model is correct, it tells you the difference is unlikely to be random. Look for confidence intervals described as probability statements about the true value, rather than statements about the estimation procedure. See W9 Reading 3 for the full evaluator's checklist we will use. Use those as your anchor, and approach the sample report as a test engineer evaluating credibility — not as a reader looking for what is correct. slide 11: Closure Comparing datasets requires more than visual inspection — it requires computing confidence intervals for each mean, evaluating overlap to test for statistical distinguishability, and connecting the statistical result to an engineering conclusion about whether the difference matters for the intended use. Statistical significance and engineering significance are independent: a statistically significant difference may be too small to matter, and an engineering-significant difference may not be detectable at your current sample size. The null hypothesis framework tells you whether the data is inconsistent with the assumption of identical populations — it does not tell you whether the difference is important. That judgment belongs to you and depends on the pre-stated acceptance criterion, which is why the acceptance criterion must be written before the data is collected. In Lecture 2 today, we return to Monte Carlo simulation and introduce regression — tools that extend the comparison from means at single operating points to models evaluated across full datasets. Together, these two lectures complete the E2 statistical comparison toolkit that you will apply in the lab session and in Sections 3 and 4 of your Tier 2 group report. Agent Notes Content is grounded in the W9 L1 blueprint purpose statement: comparing means, standard errors, confidence interval overlap, hypothesis testing at conceptual level, statistical vs. engineering significance. The statistical vs. engineering significance distinction (slide 5) is explicitly required by the blueprint: "the difference between statistical significance (unlikely by chance) and engineering significance (meaningful for the intended use)." Connects forward to W9 L2 on Monte Carlo and regression. MATLAB implementation (slide 9) previews the lab activity and is consistent with the course's MATLAB-based analysis approach. The n=25 sample size in the worked example is intentionally large to simplify the k=2 approximation; the note about t-distribution for small samples is included for accuracy. No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Comparing Datasets — Statistical Significance & Confidence Interval Overlap Talking Point: Every comparison we have made in this course so far has been between a model prediction and a single experimental measurement, or between a single measurement and an accepted reference value. Week 9 extends the toolkit to a harder and more common case: comparing two or more datasets — multiple test conditions, multiple groups, multiple configurations — and making a quantitative, defensible statement about whether they are meaningfully different. This is the question that drives most real engineering decisions: is Configuration A statistically distinguishable from Configuration B? Is the treatment effect real, or is it within the noise? The statistical tools we cover today give you the vocabulary to answer those questions without relying on visual inspection. slide 2: From One Point to Many — The Comparison Problem (Image: A three-panel illustration showing an escalating comparison challenge. Panel 1: "Single measurement vs. model." A single data point with an error bar plotted against a model prediction line. Caption: "E1: does this measurement agree with this prediction?" Panel 2: "Single dataset vs. model prediction curve." A scatter of data points across a range of conditions plotted against a prediction curve, with a residual band. Caption: "E2 so far: does this dataset agree with this model across conditions?" Panel 3: "Two datasets compared to each other." Two sets of scatter points — one in gold, one in gray — at the same set of conditions, overlapping but with visible offsets at some points. Error bars on each point. Caption: "E2 Week 9: are these two datasets statistically distinguishable? Does the difference matter for the intended use?" Each panel is clean with labeled axes and consistent visual style. The escalation from left to right illustrates the growing analytical challenge.) Talking Point: Let me frame the challenge before introducing the tools. In E1, the comparison question was simple: does this measurement agree with a prediction? One number, one comparison, one uncertainty estimate. In E2 Weeks 6 through 8, we extended that to comparing a full dataset to a model prediction curve — the residual analysis we covered in Lecture 1 this week. But the full comparison problem is often harder than either of those cases. In a real experimental campaign, you might run the same experiment at two different operating conditions, or with two different configurations, or with two different test setups. The question is whether those two datasets are meaningfully different from each other — whether the difference you observe is real, or whether it is within the noise of your measurement system. That question requires comparing means, comparing distributions, and making a statement about whether the observed difference is statistically distinguishable. That is what today's lecture provides. slide 3: Standard Error of the Mean — Why More Trials Reduce Uncertainty **Use standard error (σ/√n) when comparing means. Use standard deviation (σ) when characterizing individual measurement scatter.** (Image: A conceptual illustration showing three sample distributions, each representing the distribution of a sample mean from an experiment with n = 5, n = 20, and n = 100 trials respectively. All three distributions are centered at the same true value. The n=5 distribution is wide and low. The n=20 distribution is narrower and taller. The n=100 distribution is very narrow and tall. Each distribution is labeled with its standard error: "σ/√5," "σ/√20," "σ/√100" respectively, where σ is the population standard deviation. An annotation reads: "The uncertainty in the mean decreases as 1/√n — this is why averaging reduces the mean's uncertainty even if each trial has the same noise." A box below the three distributions reads: "Key distinction: σ is the standard deviation of individual measurements; σ/√n is the standard error — the uncertainty in the mean of n measurements." The visual clearly shows the distributions narrowing as n increases.) Talking Point: Before we compare datasets, I need to make sure the statistical foundation from Week 2 is solid — specifically the distinction between the standard deviation of individual measurements and the standard error of the mean. The standard deviation characterizes how much scatter there is in any single measurement trial — it describes the width of the measurement distribution. The standard error of the mean is the uncertainty in the average value computed from n trials, and it is equal to the standard deviation divided by the square root of n. This is the answer to the question I posed at the start of E2 Week 6: why does the uncertainty in the mean decrease with more trials? Because the mean is a statistic computed from n measurements, and each additional measurement provides additional information that narrows the estimate of the true value. The standard error is what we use when comparing means across datasets — not the standard deviation. If you compare means using standard deviations, you are overestimating the uncertainty in the comparison by the factor of square root of n. slide 4: Confidence Interval Overlap — The Practical Comparison Test (Image: Four scenarios of confidence interval comparison, arranged in a two-by-two grid. Each scenario shows two means (represented as vertical bars with horizontal caps at their 95% confidence interval bounds). Scenario 1: "Clear non-overlap." The two confidence intervals are completely separated. Caption: "Statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence — the difference is real at this precision level." Scenario 2: "Partial overlap." The confidence intervals overlap by about 30% of their width. Caption: "Likely distinguishable — a formal significance test is needed to confirm." Scenario 3: "Full overlap, one inside the other." One interval is entirely contained within the other. Caption: "Not distinguishable at this sample size — results are consistent with same population mean." Scenario 4: "Confidence intervals overlap at one edge." Just touching. Caption: "Borderline case — significance depends on the specific test and its assumptions." Each scenario has the same visual style: gold bars, labeled mean values, labeled confidence bounds.) Talking Point: The most practical comparison tool for engineering data is confidence interval overlap. If you have computed a 95% confidence interval for the mean of each dataset, you can use the overlap — or lack of overlap — between those intervals as a quick test of whether the datasets are statistically distinguishable. If the two confidence intervals do not overlap at all, the datasets are statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence. If the intervals have moderate overlap, a formal significance test may be needed to confirm. If one interval is entirely contained within the other, the datasets are not distinguishable at this sample size — the precision of your measurement is insufficient to tell them apart. One important caveat on borderline cases: CI overlap is a conservative test. If 95% CIs don't overlap, the means are certainly distinguishable at 95% confidence. If they partially overlap, the means may still be statistically different at the 5% level — use a t-test for borderline cases rather than declaring "not significant" based on visual overlap alone. The confidence interval overlap test is intuitive and visually interpretable, which is why it is preferred in experimental engineering over reporting raw p-values, which require careful interpretation to avoid the common misreading of "statistically significant means practically important." I will come back to that distinction in a moment, because it is critical for the Tier 2 analysis. slide 5: What Statistical Significance Actually Means — And What It Does Not (Image: A conceptual diagram with two independent axes. X-axis: "Statistical significance" — a bar ranging from "Not statistically significant" to "Statistically significant" with a vertical dashed line labeled "Significance threshold (α = 0.05)." Y-axis: "Engineering significance" — a bar ranging from "Below engineering threshold" to "Above engineering threshold" with a horizontal dashed line labeled "Engineering acceptance criterion." Four quadrants: Top-right: "Statistically significant AND engineering significant — the difference matters." Top-left: "Statistically significant but NOT engineering significant — real difference, but too small to matter for the intended use." Bottom-right: "Engineering significant but NOT statistically significant — the difference would matter, but sample size is too small to detect it." Bottom-left: "Neither — no meaningful difference." Each quadrant has a brief decision note. The diagram communicates that statistical significance and engineering significance are independent dimensions.) Talking Point: Statistical significance and engineering significance are not the same thing, and confusing them leads to wrong conclusions. A result is statistically significant if the probability that the observed difference arose by chance — under the null hypothesis that the populations are identical — is below a threshold, typically five percent. A result is engineering significant if the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. These two things are independent. Here is a concrete case where they diverge: Configuration A reads 100.0 ± 0.2 N; Configuration B reads 100.3 ± 0.2 N. The 95% CIs don't overlap — the 0.3 N difference is statistically significant. But 0.3 N is far below the 2% engineering threshold of 2.0 N. Real difference, irrelevant consequence — top-left quadrant. With a large enough sample, you can achieve statistical significance for a difference that is utterly irrelevant to the engineering decision — a load cell reads 0.05% higher under two test configurations, and with 10,000 measurements, that difference is statistically significant. But if your validation criterion is agreement within 2%, a 0.05% difference has no engineering significance. Conversely, a difference that is large enough to matter for the intended use may not achieve statistical significance if your sample size is too small to distinguish it from noise. For the Tier 2 analysis, the question is always: does this difference matter for the stated intended use? Statistical significance is the test of whether the difference is real. Engineering significance is the test of whether it matters. slide 6: Comparing Two Means — A Worked Example in MATLAB (Image: A MATLAB figure showing a comparison of two experimental datasets. Two bar charts with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals: "Configuration A: mean = 47.3 N, 95% CI = [45.8, 48.8] N" and "Configuration B: mean = 52.1 N, 95% CI = [50.9, 53.3] N." The two confidence intervals are completely non-overlapping — there is a visible gap between [45.8, 48.8] and [50.9, 53.3]. A horizontal dashed line labeled "Acceptance criterion: agreement within ±5% of nominal (47.3 N baseline)" is shown, and Configuration B's mean falls outside the acceptance criterion band. MATLAB-style figure with labeled axes "Configuration" and "Force (N)," a legend, and a caption reading "Configuration B mean exceeds acceptance criterion band — difference is statistically significant and engineering significant at this intended use.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a concrete comparison using MATLAB-style output. Two configurations of the same experimental rig are tested: Configuration A is the baseline, Configuration B has a modification. The sample means and 95% confidence intervals are shown. The two intervals do not overlap — Configuration B is statistically distinguishable from Configuration A at 95% confidence. The engineering acceptance criterion, which was pre-stated as agreement within 5% of the nominal value from Configuration A, is shown as the dashed band. Configuration B's mean falls outside that band. Therefore, the difference is both statistically significant — the intervals do not overlap — and engineering significant — the difference exceeds the pre-stated acceptance criterion. Both conclusions together constitute a defensible validation finding. Notice that I stated the acceptance criterion was pre-stated — this is the prediction-before-exposure principle applied to the comparison test. If you define "close enough" after you see both datasets, the comparison loses its independence. slide 7: When Datasets Disagree — Three Interpretive Paths (Image: A flowchart with three branches from the root question "Two datasets show a meaningful difference. What does it tell you?" Branch 1: "Both datasets from different test conditions → The system responds differently under the two conditions — the model should account for the sensitivity." Branch 2: "Both datasets from nominally identical conditions → Measurement system issue OR uncontrolled variable — investigate repeatability and setup." Branch 3: "One dataset is a model prediction, one is experimental → Model-data discrepancy — apply residual analysis from W8." Each branch has a MATLAB analysis type labeled below it: Branch 1 → "Confidence interval comparison + regression across conditions." Branch 2 → "Repeatability analysis — standard deviation of repeated trials at same condition." Branch 3 → "Residual plot + discrepancy classification (W8 vocabulary)." The flowchart is clean with the same visual style as previous flowcharts in the course.) Talking Point: When two datasets differ, the interpretation depends on what the two datasets represent. If the datasets are from two different test conditions — different configurations, different operating points — then the difference is a characterization of the system: it responds differently under the two conditions, and that information is useful for understanding the model. If the datasets are from nominally identical conditions and should be repeatable, then a meaningful difference indicates a measurement system problem or an uncontrolled variable. And if one of the datasets is a model prediction and the other is experimental data, you are back in the residual analysis territory from Lecture 1. The statistical comparison tools are the same across all three cases — confidence intervals, standard errors, overlap tests — but the engineering interpretation depends entirely on what the datasets represent. Before you compute, ask: what are these two datasets, and what would a difference between them mean? slide 8: Hypothesis Testing — Conceptual Level (Image: A conceptual diagram of hypothesis testing without any formulas. Two states are shown: "Null hypothesis H₀: the two population means are the same — any observed difference is due to sampling variability." "Alternative hypothesis H₁: the two population means are genuinely different — the observed difference is real." A sample difference distribution is shown in the center, labeled "Distribution of possible differences if H₀ is true." A vertical dashed line on the right tail of the distribution is labeled "Observed difference." A shaded region in the right tail beyond the observed difference is labeled "p-value: the probability of observing a difference this large if H₀ is true." A decision box below reads: "If p-value < α (typically 0.05), we reject H₀ — the difference is statistically significant." A caution note: "p < 0.05 does NOT mean: there is a 95% probability that H₁ is true. It means: the data is unlikely under H₀. These are different statements.") Talking Point: I want to give you a conceptual understanding of formal hypothesis testing without requiring you to implement the full statistical machinery from scratch. The null hypothesis is the assumption that any difference you observe between two datasets is due to sampling variability alone — the two populations are actually identical. The alternative hypothesis is that the populations are genuinely different. The p-value is the probability of observing a difference as large as the one you measured, assuming the null hypothesis is true. If the p-value is below the threshold — conventionally five percent — we say the result is statistically significant: the data is unlikely to have arisen by chance under the null hypothesis. Here is the caution I want you to internalize: a p-value below five percent does not mean there is a 95% probability that the difference is real. It means the data is inconsistent with the null hypothesis at the five-percent level. This distinction matters because p-values are routinely misread, and in engineering contexts, a misread p-value can lead to over-confident conclusions. The confidence interval overlap test I showed you earlier is often more interpretable in engineering contexts precisely because it is more directly connected to the physical question: are these two values close enough to be considered the same for the intended use? slide 9: MATLAB Implementation — Comparing Means and CI Overlap (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results shown alongside. Code lines (styled as MATLAB code): "% Sample means and standard deviations from two test conditions"; "n_A = 25; mean_A = 47.3; std_A = 3.2;"; "n_B = 25; mean_B = 52.1; std_B = 2.9;"; "SE_A = std_A / sqrt(n_A); % Standard error of mean A"; "SE_B = std_B / sqrt(n_B); % Standard error of mean B"; "CI_A = mean_A + [-1 1] * 2 * SE_A; % 95% CI (approx k=2)"; "CI_B = mean_B + [-1 1] * 2 * SE_B;"; "% Check overlap"; "overlap = CI_A(2) >= CI_B(1); % Logical: 1=overlap, 0=no overlap." Results panel below the code: "CI_A = [45.9, 48.6] N" | "CI_B = [50.9, 53.2] N" | "overlap = 0 (no overlap — statistically distinguishable at 95% CI)." A note below the code: "k=2 is an approximation for 95% CI. For small samples (n < 30), use the t-distribution critical value instead of 2." The code is readable and styled as a MATLAB script with comment lines.) Talking Point: Here is the MATLAB implementation you will use in the lab session today. The standard error of the mean is the sample standard deviation divided by the square root of n — that is the line that converts from the standard deviation of individual measurements to the uncertainty in the mean. The 95% confidence interval uses a coverage factor of 2 as an approximation for large samples. For small samples — fewer than about 30 trials — the t-distribution gives a larger critical value than 2, and you would use MATLAB's tinv function to get the exact value for the degrees of freedom. The overlap check is a simple logical comparison: does the upper bound of the first confidence interval exceed the lower bound of the second? In this example it does not — CI_A ends at 48.6 and CI_B begins at 50.9 — so the intervals do not overlap and the datasets are statistically distinguishable. This is the starting point for your Section 4 uncertainty analysis in the Tier 2 report: you need to compute the 95% confidence interval for your measurement means and compare them to the model prediction or to a second test condition. slide 10: Connecting Statistical Comparison to the Tier 2 Report and Improvement Proposal (Image: A flow diagram connecting the statistical comparison analysis to three report sections. Starting node: "Statistical comparison analysis — CI overlap, significance test, difference characterization." Three arrows branch to: Arrow 1: "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome" — labeled "Use CI of experimental mean to evaluate whether model prediction falls within bounds." Arrow 2: "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis" — labeled "Report standard error, CI, comparison of Monte Carlo vs. partial derivative method." Arrow 3: "Section 6: Improvement proposal — category (c): experimental design" — labeled "If statistical power was insufficient to distinguish configurations, propose increased n or improved measurement precision." A note at the bottom: "Statistical comparison is both an analysis tool (Sections 3A and 3B) and a diagnostic for improvement (Section 6). The improvement proposal must be grounded in the statistical analysis, not generic.") Talking Point: The statistical comparison tools you learn today connect to the Tier 2 report in two directions. First, they are analysis tools: you use confidence interval comparison to evaluate whether the model prediction falls within the uncertainty bounds of your experimental mean, and you report the standard error and CI in the uncertainty analysis section. Second, and less obviously, they are diagnostic tools for the improvement proposal. If your confidence intervals were too wide to distinguish the model prediction from the data — not because the model is good, but because your measurement was imprecise — then the improvement proposal should address that: more trials at each condition, or a more precise sensor, or reduced noise through better signal conditioning. The improvement must be grounded in the statistical result. "Our CI was too wide to make a conclusion about the model at the required precision" is a specific finding, and the proposed fix — a specific increase in sample size or measurement precision — is the corresponding improvement. That is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit. slide 10b: Advance Organizer — Comparative Analysis Discussion (Discussion preview) Talking Point: Before the discussion activity: in the comparative analysis today, apply the W8 taxonomy. Look for R² reported without a residual plot — that is adequacy claimed without the diagnostic. Look for statistical significance treated as validation — a p-value does not tell you the model is correct, it tells you the difference is unlikely to be random. Look for confidence intervals described as probability statements about the true value, rather than statements about the estimation procedure. See W9 Reading 3 for the full evaluator's checklist we will use. Use those as your anchor, and approach the sample report as a test engineer evaluating credibility — not as a reader looking for what is correct. slide 11: Closure Comparing datasets requires more than visual inspection — it requires computing confidence intervals for each mean, evaluating overlap to test for statistical distinguishability, and connecting the statistical result to an engineering conclusion about whether the difference matters for the intended use. Statistical significance and engineering significance are independent: a statistically significant difference may be too small to matter, and an engineering-significant difference may not be detectable at your current sample size. The null hypothesis framework tells you whether the data is inconsistent with the assumption of identical populations — it does not tell you whether the difference is important. That judgment belongs to you and depends on the pre-stated acceptance criterion, which is why the acceptance criterion must be written before the data is collected. In Lecture 2 today, we return to Monte Carlo simulation and introduce regression — tools that extend the comparison from means at single operating points to models evaluated across full datasets. Together, these two lectures complete the E2 statistical comparison toolkit that you will apply in the lab session and in Sections 3 and 4 of your Tier 2 group report. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded in the W9 L1 blueprint purpose statement: comparing means, standard errors, confidence interval overlap, hypothesis testing at conceptual level, statistical vs. engineering significance. - The statistical vs. engineering significance distinction (slide 5) is explicitly required by the blueprint: "the difference between statistical significance (unlikely by chance) and engineering significance (meaningful for the intended use)." - Connects forward to W9 L2 on Monte Carlo and regression. - MATLAB implementation (slide 9) previews the lab activity and is consistent with the course's MATLAB-based analysis approach. - The n=25 sample size in the worked example is intentionally large to simplify the k=2 approximation; the note about t-distribution for small samples is included for accuracy. - No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2 primary treatment.

chunks.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Lecture L01 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Comparing Datasets — Statistical Significance & Confidence Interval Overlap Talking Point: Every comparison we have made in this course so far has been between a model prediction and a sing... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Comparing Datasets — Statistical Significance & Confidence Interval Overlap Talking Point: Every comparison we have made in this course so far has been between a model prediction and a single experimental measurement, or between a single measurement and an accepted reference value. Week 9 extends the toolkit to a harder and more common case: comparing two or more datasets — multiple test conditions, multiple groups, multiple configurations — and making a quantitative, defensible statement about whether they

onditions, multiple groups, multiple configurations — and making a quantitative, defensible statement about whether they are meaningfully different. This is the question that drives most real engineering decisions: is Configuration A statistically distinguishable from Configuration B? Is the treatment effect real, or is it within the noise? The statistical tools we cover today give you the vocabulary to answer those questions without relying on visual inspection. slide 2: From One Point to Many — The Comparison Problem (Image: A three-panel illustration showing an escalating comparison challenge. Panel 1: "Single measurement vs. model." A single data point with an error bar plotted against a model prediction line. Caption: "E1: does this measurement agree with this prediction?" Panel 2:

r bar plotted against a model prediction line. Caption: "E1: does this measurement agree with this prediction?" Panel 2: "Single dataset vs. model prediction curve." A scatter of data points across a range of conditions plotted against a prediction curve, with a residual band. Caption: "E2 so far: does this dataset agree with this model across conditions?" Panel 3: "Two datasets compared to each other." Two sets of scatter points — one in gold, one in gray — at the same set of conditions, overlapping but with visible offsets at some points. Error bars on each point. Caption: "E2 Week 9: are these two datasets statistically distinguishable? Does the difference matter for the intended use?" Each panel is clean with labeled axes and consistent visual style. The escalation from left to right

the intended use?" Each panel is clean with labeled axes and consistent visual style. The escalation from left to right illustrates the growing analytical challenge.) Talking Point: Let me frame the challenge before introducing the tools. In E1, the comparison question was simple: does this measurement agree with a prediction? One number, one comparison, one uncertainty estimate. In E2 Weeks 6 through 8, we extended that to comparing a full dataset to a model prediction curve — the residual analysis we covered in Lecture 1 this week. But the full comparison problem is often harder than either of those cases. In a real experimental campaign, you might run the same experiment at two different operating conditions, or with two different configurations, or with two different test setups. The

ment at two different operating conditions, or with two different configurations, or with two different test setups. The question is whether those two datasets are meaningfully different from each other — whether the difference you observe is real, or whether it is within the noise of your measurement system. That question requires comparing means, comparing distributions, and making a statement about whether the observed difference is statistically distinguishable. That is what today's lecture provides. slide 3: Standard Error of the Mean — Why More Trials Reduce Uncertainty Use standard error (σ/√n) when comparing means. Use standard deviation (σ) when characterizing individual measurement scatter. (Image: A conceptual illustration showing three sample distributions, each representing

individual measurement scatter. (Image: A conceptual illustration showing three sample distributions, each representing the distribution of a sample mean from an experiment with n = 5, n = 20, and n = 100 trials respectively. All three distributions are centered at the same true value. The n=5 distribution is wide and low. The n=20 distribution is narrower and taller. The n=100 distribution is very narrow and tall. Each distribution is labeled with its standard error: "σ/√5," "σ/√20," "σ/√100" respectively, where σ is the population standard deviation. An annotation reads: "The uncertainty in the mean decreases as 1/√n — this is why averaging reduces the mean's uncertainty even if each trial has the same noise." A box below the three distributions reads: "Key distinction: σ is the

ncertainty even if each trial has the same noise." A box below the three distributions reads: "Key distinction: σ is the standard deviation of individual measurements; σ/√n is the standard error — the uncertainty in the mean of n measurements." The visual clearly shows the distributions narrowing as n increases.) Talking Point: Before we compare datasets, I need to make sure the statistical foundation from Week 2 is solid — specifically the distinction between the standard deviation of individual measurements and the standard error of the mean. The standard deviation characterizes how much scatter there is in any single measurement trial — it describes the width of the measurement distribution. The standard error of the mean is the uncertainty in the average value computed from n trials,

measurement distribution. The standard error of the mean is the uncertainty in the average value computed from n trials, and it is equal to the standard deviation divided by the square root of n. This is the answer to the question I posed at the start of E2 Week 6: why does the uncertainty in the mean decrease with more trials? Because the mean is a statistic computed from n measurements, and each additional measurement provides additional information that narrows the estimate of the true value. The standard error is what we use when comparing means across datasets — not the standard deviation. If you compare means using standard deviations, you are overestimating the uncertainty in the comparison by the factor of square root of n. slide 4: Confidence Interval Overlap — The Practical

he uncertainty in the comparison by the factor of square root of n. slide 4: Confidence Interval Overlap — The Practical Comparison Test (Image: Four scenarios of confidence interval comparison, arranged in a two-by-two grid. Each scenario shows two means (represented as vertical bars with horizontal caps at their 95% confidence interval bounds). Scenario 1: "Clear non-overlap." The two confidence intervals are completely separated. Caption: "Statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence — the difference is real at this precision level." Scenario 2: "Partial overlap." The confidence intervals overlap by about 30% of their width. Caption: "Likely distinguishable — a formal significance test is needed to confirm." Scenario 3: "Full overlap, one inside the other." One interval is entirely

rmal significance test is needed to confirm." Scenario 3: "Full overlap, one inside the other." One interval is entirely contained within the other. Caption: "Not distinguishable at this sample size — results are consistent with same population mean." Scenario 4: "Confidence intervals overlap at one edge." Just touching. Caption: "Borderline case — significance depends on the specific test and its assumptions." Each scenario has the same visual style: gold bars, labeled mean values, labeled confidence bounds.) Talking Point: The most practical comparison tool for engineering data is confidence interval overlap. If you have computed a 95% confidence interval for the mean of each dataset, you can use the overlap — or lack of overlap — between those intervals as a quick test of whether the

n of each dataset, you can use the overlap — or lack of overlap — between those intervals as a quick test of whether the datasets are statistically distinguishable. If the two confidence intervals do not overlap at all, the datasets are statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence. If the intervals have moderate overlap, a formal significance test may be needed to confirm. If one interval is entirely contained within the other, the datasets are not distinguishable at this sample size — the precision of your measurement is insufficient to tell them apart. One important caveat on borderline cases: CI overlap is a conservative test. If 95% CIs don't overlap, the means are certainly distinguishable at 95% confidence. If they partially overlap, the means may still be statistically different

e certainly distinguishable at 95% confidence. If they partially overlap, the means may still be statistically different at the 5% level — use a t-test for borderline cases rather than declaring "not significant" based on visual overlap alone. The confidence interval overlap test is intuitive and visually interpretable, which is why it is preferred in experimental engineering over reporting raw p-values, which require careful interpretation to avoid the common misreading of "statistically significant means practically important." I will come back to that distinction in a moment, because it is critical for the Tier 2 analysis. slide 5: What Statistical Significance Actually Means — And What It Does Not (Image: A conceptual diagram with two independent axes. X-axis: "Statistical

cance Actually Means — And What It Does Not (Image: A conceptual diagram with two independent axes. X-axis: "Statistical significance" — a bar ranging from "Not statistically significant" to "Statistically significant" with a vertical dashed line labeled "Significance threshold (α = 0.05)." Y-axis: "Engineering significance" — a bar ranging from "Below engineering threshold" to "Above engineering threshold" with a horizontal dashed line labeled "Engineering acceptance criterion." Four quadrants: Top-right: "Statistically significant AND engineering significant — the difference matters." Top-left: "Statistically significant but NOT engineering significant — real difference, but too small to matter for the intended use." Bottom-right: "Engineering significant but NOT statistically

difference, but too small to matter for the intended use." Bottom-right: "Engineering significant but NOT statistically significant — the difference would matter, but sample size is too small to detect it." Bottom-left: "Neither — no meaningful difference." Each quadrant has a brief decision note. The diagram communicates that statistical significance and engineering significance are independent dimensions.) Talking Point: Statistical significance and engineering significance are not the same thing, and confusing them leads to wrong conclusions. A result is statistically significant if the probability that the observed difference arose by chance — under the null hypothesis that the populations are identical — is below a threshold, typically five percent. A result is engineering

ll hypothesis that the populations are identical — is below a threshold, typically five percent. A result is engineering significant if the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. These two things are independent. Here is a concrete case where they diverge: Configuration A reads 100.0 ± 0.2 N; Configuration B reads 100.3 ± 0.2 N. The 95% CIs don't overlap — the 0.3 N difference is statistically significant. But 0.3 N is far below the 2% engineering threshold of 2.0 N. Real difference, irrelevant consequence — top-left quadrant. With a large enough sample, you can achieve statistical significance for a difference that is utterly irrelevant to the engineering decision — a load cell reads 0.05% higher under two test configurations, and with 10,000 measurements, that

engineering decision — a load cell reads 0.05% higher under two test configurations, and with 10,000 measurements, that difference is statistically significant. But if your validation criterion is agreement within 2%, a 0.05% difference has no engineering significance. Conversely, a difference that is large enough to matter for the intended use may not achieve statistical significance if your sample size is too small to distinguish it from noise. For the Tier 2 analysis, the question is always: does this difference matter for the stated intended use? Statistical significance is the test of whether the difference is real. Engineering significance is the test of whether it matters. slide 6: Comparing Two Means — A Worked Example in MATLAB (Image: A MATLAB figure showing a comparison of two

t matters. slide 6: Comparing Two Means — A Worked Example in MATLAB (Image: A MATLAB figure showing a comparison of two experimental datasets.
