and a diagnostic for improvement (Section 6). The improvement proposal must be grounded in the statistical analysis, not generic.") Talking Point: The statistical comparison tools you learn today connect to the Tier 2 report in two directions. First, they are analysis tools: you use confidence interval comparison to evaluate whether the model prediction falls within the uncertainty bounds of your experimental mean, and you report the standard error and CI in the uncertainty analysis section. Second, and less obviously, they are diagnostic tools for the improvement proposal. If your confidence intervals were too wide to distinguish the model prediction from the data — not because the model is good, but because your measurement was imprecise — then the improvement proposal should address

not because the model is good, but because your measurement was imprecise — then the improvement proposal should address that: more trials at each condition, or a more precise sensor, or reduced noise through better signal conditioning. The improvement must be grounded in the statistical result. "Our CI was too wide to make a conclusion about the model at the required precision" is a specific finding, and the proposed fix — a specific increase in sample size or measurement precision — is the corresponding improvement. That is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit. slide 10b: Advance Organizer — Comparative Analysis Discussion (Discussion preview) Talking Point: Before the discussion activity: in the comparative analysis today, apply the W8 taxonomy. Look for R²

ew) Talking Point: Before the discussion activity: in the comparative analysis today, apply the W8 taxonomy. Look for R² reported without a residual plot — that is adequacy claimed without the diagnostic. Look for statistical significance treated as validation — a p-value does not tell you the model is correct, it tells you the difference is unlikely to be random. Look for confidence intervals described as probability statements about the true value, rather than statements about the estimation procedure. See W9 Reading 3 for the full evaluator's checklist we will use. Use those as your anchor, and approach the sample report as a test engineer evaluating credibility — not as a reader looking for what is correct. slide 11: Closure Comparing datasets requires more than visual inspection — it

as a reader looking for what is correct. slide 11: Closure Comparing datasets requires more than visual inspection — it requires computing confidence intervals for each mean, evaluating overlap to test for statistical distinguishability, and connecting the statistical result to an engineering conclusion about whether the difference matters for the intended use. Statistical significance and engineering significance are independent: a statistically significant difference may be too small to matter, and an engineering-significant difference may not be detectable at your current sample size. The null hypothesis framework tells you whether the data is inconsistent with the assumption of identical populations — it does not tell you whether the difference is important. That judgment belongs to

assumption of identical populations — it does not tell you whether the difference is important. That judgment belongs to you and depends on the pre-stated acceptance criterion, which is why the acceptance criterion must be written before the data is collected. In Lecture 2 today, we return to Monte Carlo simulation and introduce regression — tools that extend the comparison from means at single operating points to models evaluated across full datasets. Together, these two lectures complete the E2 statistical comparison toolkit that you will apply in the lab session and in Sections 3 and 4 of your Tier 2 group report. Agent Notes Content is grounded in the W9 L1 blueprint purpose statement: comparing means, standard errors, confidence interval overlap, hypothesis testing at conceptual

print purpose statement: comparing means, standard errors, confidence interval overlap, hypothesis testing at conceptual level, statistical vs. engineering significance. The statistical vs. engineering significance distinction (slide 5) is explicitly required by the blueprint: "the difference between statistical significance (unlikely by chance) and engineering significance (meaningful for the intended use)." Connects forward to W9 L2 on Monte Carlo and regression. MATLAB implementation (slide 9) previews the lab activity and is consistent with the course's MATLAB-based analysis approach. The n=25 sample size in the worked example is intentionally large to simplify the k=2 approximation; the note about t-distribution for small samples is included for accuracy. No CLO 4 or Phase III

y the k=2 approximation; the note about t-distribution for small samples is included for accuracy. No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Comparing Datasets — Statistical Significance & Confidence Interval Overlap Talking Point: Every comparison we have made in this course so far has been between a model prediction and a single experimental measurement, or between a single measurement and an accepted reference value. Week 9 extends the toolkit to a harder and more common case: comparing two or more datasets — multiple test conditions, multiple groups, multiple configurations — and making a quantitative, defensible statement about whether they are meaningfully different. This is the question that drives most real engineering

fensible statement about whether they are meaningfully different. This is the question that drives most real engineering decisions: is Configuration A statistically distinguishable from Configuration B? Is the treatment effect real, or is it within the noise? The statistical tools we cover today give you the vocabulary to answer those questions without relying on visual inspection. slide 2: From One Point to Many — The Comparison Problem (Image: A three-panel illustration showing an escalating comparison challenge. Panel 1: "Single measurement vs. model." A single data point with an error bar plotted against a model prediction line. Caption: "E1: does this measurement agree with this prediction?" Panel 2: "Single dataset vs. model prediction curve." A scatter of data points across a range

ree with this prediction?" Panel 2: "Single dataset vs. model prediction curve." A scatter of data points across a range of conditions plotted against a prediction curve, with a residual band. Caption: "E2 so far: does this dataset agree with this model across conditions?" Panel 3: "Two datasets compared to each other." Two sets of scatter points — one in gold, one in gray — at the same set of conditions, overlapping but with visible offsets at some points. Error bars on each point. Caption: "E2 Week 9: are these two datasets statistically distinguishable? Does the difference matter for the intended use?" Each panel is clean with labeled axes and consistent visual style. The escalation from left to right illustrates the growing analytical challenge.) Talking Point: Let me frame the

style. The escalation from left to right illustrates the growing analytical challenge.) Talking Point: Let me frame the challenge before introducing the tools. In E1, the comparison question was simple: does this measurement agree with a prediction? One number, one comparison, one uncertainty estimate. In E2 Weeks 6 through 8, we extended that to comparing a full dataset to a model prediction curve — the residual analysis we covered in Lecture 1 this week. But the full comparison problem is often harder than either of those cases. In a real experimental campaign, you might run the same experiment at two different operating conditions, or with two different configurations, or with two different test setups. The question is whether those two datasets are meaningfully different from each

ions, or with two different test setups. The question is whether those two datasets are meaningfully different from each other — whether the difference you observe is real, or whether it is within the noise of your measurement system. That question requires comparing means, comparing distributions, and making a statement about whether the observed difference is statistically distinguishable. That is what today's lecture provides. slide 3: Standard Error of the Mean — Why More Trials Reduce Uncertainty **Use standard error (σ/√n) when comparing means. Use standard deviation (σ) when characterizing individual measurement scatter.** (Image: A conceptual illustration showing three sample distributions, each representing the distribution of a sample mean from an experiment with n = 5, n = 20,

g three sample distributions, each representing the distribution of a sample mean from an experiment with n = 5, n = 20, and n = 100 trials respectively. All three distributions are centered at the same true value. The n=5 distribution is wide and low. The n=20 distribution is narrower and taller. The n=100 distribution is very narrow and tall. Each distribution is labeled with its standard error: "σ/√5," "σ/√20," "σ/√100" respectively, where σ is the population standard deviation. An annotation reads: "The uncertainty in the mean decreases as 1/√n — this is why averaging reduces the mean's uncertainty even if each trial has the same noise." A box below the three distributions reads: "Key distinction: σ is the standard deviation of individual measurements; σ/√n is the standard error — the

utions reads: "Key distinction: σ is the standard deviation of individual measurements; σ/√n is the standard error — the uncertainty in the mean of n measurements." The visual clearly shows the distributions narrowing as n increases.) Talking Point: Before we compare datasets, I need to make sure the statistical foundation from Week 2 is solid — specifically the distinction between the standard deviation of individual measurements and the standard error of the mean. The standard deviation characterizes how much scatter there is in any single measurement trial — it describes the width of the measurement distribution. The standard error of the mean is the uncertainty in the average value computed from n trials, and it is equal to the standard deviation divided by the square root of n. This

he average value computed from n trials, and it is equal to the standard deviation divided by the square root of n. This is the answer to the question I posed at the start of E2 Week 6: why does the uncertainty in the mean decrease with more trials? Because the mean is a statistic computed from n measurements, and each additional measurement provides additional information that narrows the estimate of the true value. The standard error is what we use when comparing means across datasets — not the standard deviation. If you compare means using standard deviations, you are overestimating the uncertainty in the comparison by the factor of square root of n. slide 4: Confidence Interval Overlap — The Practical Comparison Test (Image: Four scenarios of confidence interval comparison, arranged

ence Interval Overlap — The Practical Comparison Test (Image: Four scenarios of confidence interval comparison, arranged in a two-by-two grid. Each scenario shows two means (represented as vertical bars with horizontal caps at their 95% confidence interval bounds). Scenario 1: "Clear non-overlap." The two confidence intervals are completely separated. Caption: "Statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence — the difference is real at this precision level." Scenario 2: "Partial overlap." The confidence intervals overlap by about 30% of their width. Caption: "Likely distinguishable — a formal significance test is needed to confirm." Scenario 3: "Full overlap, one inside the other." One interval is entirely contained within the other. Caption: "Not distinguishable at this sample size —

ide the other." One interval is entirely contained within the other. Caption: "Not distinguishable at this sample size — results are consistent with same population mean." Scenario 4: "Confidence intervals overlap at one edge." Just touching. Caption: "Borderline case — significance depends on the specific test and its assumptions." Each scenario has the same visual style: gold bars, labeled mean values, labeled confidence bounds.) Talking Point: The most practical comparison tool for engineering data is confidence interval overlap. If you have computed a 95% confidence interval for the mean of each dataset, you can use the overlap — or lack of overlap — between those intervals as a quick test of whether the datasets are statistically distinguishable. If the two confidence intervals do

intervals as a quick test of whether the datasets are statistically distinguishable. If the two confidence intervals do not overlap at all, the datasets are statistically distinguishable at 95% confidence. If the intervals have moderate overlap, a formal significance test may be needed to confirm. If one interval is entirely contained within the other, the datasets are not distinguishable at this sample size — the precision of your measurement is insufficient to tell them apart. One important caveat on borderline cases: CI overlap is a conservative test. If 95% CIs don't overlap, the means are certainly distinguishable at 95% confidence. If they partially overlap, the means may still be statistically different at the 5% level — use a t-test for borderline cases rather than declaring "not

eans may still be statistically different at the 5% level — use a t-test for borderline cases rather than declaring "not significant" based on visual overlap alone. The confidence interval overlap test is intuitive and visually interpretable, which is why it is preferred in experimental engineering over reporting raw p-values, which require careful interpretation to avoid the common misreading of "statistically significant means practically important." I will come back to that distinction in a moment, because it is critical for the Tier 2 analysis. slide 5: What Statistical Significance Actually Means — And What It Does Not (Image: A conceptual diagram with two independent axes. X-axis: "Statistical significance" — a bar ranging from "Not statistically significant" to "Statistically

ependent axes. X-axis: "Statistical significance" — a bar ranging from "Not statistically significant" to "Statistically significant" with a vertical dashed line labeled "Significance threshold (α = 0.05)." Y-axis: "Engineering significance" — a bar ranging from "Below engineering threshold" to "Above engineering threshold" with a horizontal dashed line labeled "Engineering acceptance criterion." Four quadrants: Top-right: "Statistically significant AND engineering significant — the difference matters." Top-left: "Statistically significant but NOT engineering significant — real difference, but too small to matter for the intended use." Bottom-right: "Engineering significant but NOT statistically significant — the difference would matter, but sample size is too small to detect it."

significant but NOT statistically significant — the difference would matter, but sample size is too small to detect it." Bottom-left: "Neither — no meaningful difference." Each quadrant has a brief decision note. The diagram communicates that statistical significance and engineering significance are independent dimensions.) Talking Point: Statistical significance and engineering significance are not the same thing, and confusing them leads to wrong conclusions. A result is statistically significant if the probability that the observed difference arose by chance — under the null hypothesis that the populations are identical — is below a threshold, typically five percent. A result is engineering significant if the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. These two things

. A result is engineering significant if the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. These two things are independent. Here is a concrete case where they diverge: Configuration A reads 100.0 ± 0.2 N; Configuration B reads 100.3 ± 0.2 N. The 95% CIs don't overlap — the 0.3 N difference is statistically significant. But 0.3 N is far below the 2% engineering threshold of 2.0 N. Real difference, irrelevant consequence — top-left quadrant. With a large enough sample, you can achieve statistical significance for a difference that is utterly irrelevant to the engineering decision — a load cell reads 0.05% higher under two test configurations, and with 10,000 measurements, that difference is statistically significant. But if your validation criterion is agreement within 2%, a

0 measurements, that difference is statistically significant. But if your validation criterion is agreement within 2%, a 0.05% difference has no engineering significance. Conversely, a difference that is large enough to matter for the intended use may not achieve statistical significance if your sample size is too small to distinguish it from noise. For the Tier 2 analysis, the question is always: does this difference matter for the stated intended use? Statistical significance is the test of whether the difference is real. Engineering significance is the test of whether it matters. slide 6: Comparing Two Means — A Worked Example in MATLAB (Image: A MATLAB figure showing a comparison of two experimental datasets. Two bar charts with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals:

showing a comparison of two experimental datasets. Two bar charts with error bars representing 95% confidence intervals: "Configuration A: mean = 47.3 N, 95% CI = [45.8, 48.8] N" and "Configuration B: mean = 52.1 N, 95% CI = [50.9, 53.3] N." The two confidence intervals are completely non-overlapping — there is a visible gap between [45.8, 48.8] and [50.9, 53.3]. A horizontal dashed line labeled "Acceptance criterion: agreement within ±5% of nominal (47.3 N baseline)" is shown, and Configuration B's mean falls outside the acceptance criterion band. MATLAB-style figure with labeled axes "Configuration" and "Force (N)," a legend, and a caption reading "Configuration B mean exceeds acceptance criterion band — difference is statistically significant and engineering significant at this

n B mean exceeds acceptance criterion band — difference is statistically significant and engineering significant at this intended use.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a concrete comparison using MATLAB-style output. Two configurations of the same experimental rig are tested: Configuration A is the baseline, Configuration B has a modification. The sample means and 95% confidence intervals are shown. The two intervals do not overlap — Configuration B is statistically distinguishable from Configuration A at 95% confidence. The engineering acceptance criterion, which was pre-stated as agreement within 5% of the nominal value from Configuration A, is shown as the dashed band. Configuration B's mean falls outside that band. Therefore, the difference is both statistically significant — the

band. Configuration B's mean falls outside that band. Therefore, the difference is both statistically significant — the intervals do not overlap — and engineering significant — the difference exceeds the pre-stated acceptance criterion. Both conclusions together constitute a defensible validation finding. Notice that I stated the acceptance criterion was pre-stated — this is the prediction-before-exposure principle applied to the comparison test. If you define "close enough" after you see both datasets, the comparison loses its independence. slide 7: When Datasets Disagree — Three Interpretive Paths (Image: A flowchart with three branches from the root question "Two datasets show a meaningful difference. What does it tell you?" Branch 1: "Both datasets from different test conditions →

datasets show a meaningful difference. What does it tell you?" Branch 1: "Both datasets from different test conditions → The system responds differently under the two conditions — the model should account for the sensitivity." Branch 2: "Both datasets from nominally identical conditions → Measurement system issue OR uncontrolled variable — investigate repeatability and setup." Branch 3: "One dataset is a model prediction, one is experimental → Model-data discrepancy — apply residual analysis from W8." Each branch has a MATLAB analysis type labeled below it: Branch 1 → "Confidence interval comparison + regression across conditions." Branch 2 → "Repeatability analysis — standard deviation of repeated trials at same condition." Branch 3 → "Residual plot + discrepancy classification (W8

s — standard deviation of repeated trials at same condition." Branch 3 → "Residual plot + discrepancy classification (W8 vocabulary)." The flowchart is clean with the same visual style as previous flowcharts in the course.) Talking Point: When two datasets differ, the interpretation depends on what the two datasets represent. If the datasets are from two different test conditions — different configurations, different operating points — then the difference is a characterization of the system: it responds differently under the two conditions, and that information is useful for understanding the model. If the datasets are from nominally identical conditions and should be repeatable, then a meaningful difference indicates a measurement system problem or an uncontrolled variable. And if one of

peatable, then a meaningful difference indicates a measurement system problem or an uncontrolled variable. And if one of the datasets is a model prediction and the other is experimental data, you are back in the residual analysis territory from Lecture 1. The statistical comparison tools are the same across all three cases — confidence intervals, standard errors, overlap tests — but the engineering interpretation depends entirely on what the datasets represent. Before you compute, ask: what are these two datasets, and what would a difference between them mean? slide 8: Hypothesis Testing — Conceptual Level (Image: A conceptual diagram of hypothesis testing without any formulas. Two states are shown: "Null hypothesis H₀: the two population means are the same — any observed difference is

formulas. Two states are shown: "Null hypothesis H₀: the two population means are the same — any observed difference is due to sampling variability." "Alternative hypothesis H₁: the two population means are genuinely different — the observed difference is real." A sample difference distribution is shown in the center, labeled "Distribution of possible differences if H₀ is true." A vertical dashed line on the right tail of the distribution is labeled "Observed difference." A shaded region in the right tail beyond the observed difference is labeled "p-value: the probability of observing a difference this large if H₀ is true." A decision box below reads: "If p-value < α (typically 0.05), we reject H₀ — the difference is statistically significant." A caution note: "p < 0.05 does NOT mean:

(typically 0.05), we reject H₀ — the difference is statistically significant." A caution note: "p < 0.05 does NOT mean: there is a 95% probability that H₁ is true. It means: the data is unlikely under H₀. These are different statements.") Talking Point: I want to give you a conceptual understanding of formal hypothesis testing without requiring you to implement the full statistical machinery from scratch. The null hypothesis is the assumption that any difference you observe between two datasets is due to sampling variability alone — the two populations are actually identical. The alternative hypothesis is that the populations are genuinely different. The p-value is the probability of observing a difference as large as the one you measured, assuming the null hypothesis is true. If the

the probability of observing a difference as large as the one you measured, assuming the null hypothesis is true. If the p-value is below the threshold — conventionally five percent — we say the result is statistically significant: the data is unlikely to have arisen by chance under the null hypothesis. Here is the caution I want you to internalize: a p-value below five percent does not mean there is a 95% probability that the difference is real. It means the data is inconsistent with the null hypothesis at the five-percent level. This distinction matters because p-values are routinely misread, and in engineering contexts, a misread p-value can lead to over-confident conclusions. The confidence interval overlap test I showed you earlier is often more interpretable in engineering contexts

nclusions. The confidence interval overlap test I showed you earlier is often more interpretable in engineering contexts precisely because it is more directly connected to the physical question: are these two values close enough to be considered the same for the intended use? slide 9: MATLAB Implementation — Comparing Means and CI Overlap (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results shown alongside. Code lines (styled as MATLAB code): "% Sample means and standard deviations from two test conditions"; "n_A = 25; mean_A = 47.3; std_A = 3.2;"; "n_B = 25; mean_B = 52.1; std_B = 2.9;"; "SE_A = std_A / sqrt(n_A); % Standard error of mean A"; "SE_B = std_B / sqrt(n_B); % Standard error of mean B"; "CI_A = mean_A + [-1 1] * 2 * SE_A; % 95% CI (approx k=2)"; "CI_B = mean_B + [-1 1] * 2 * SE_B;"; "%

d error of mean B"; "CI_A = mean_A + [-1 1] * 2 * SE_A; % 95% CI (approx k=2)"; "CI_B = mean_B + [-1 1] * 2 * SE_B;"; "% Check overlap"; "overlap = CI_A(2) >= CI_B(1); % Logical: 1=overlap, 0=no overlap." Results panel below the code: "CI_A = [45.9, 48.6] N" | "CI_B = [50.9, 53.2] N" | "overlap = 0 (no overlap — statistically distinguishable at 95% CI)." A note below the code: "k=2 is an approximation for 95% CI. For small samples (n < 30), use the t-distribution critical value instead of 2." The code is readable and styled as a MATLAB script with comment lines.) Talking Point: Here is the MATLAB implementation you will use in the lab session today. The standard error of the mean is the sample standard deviation divided by the square root of n — that is the line that converts from the

r of the mean is the sample standard deviation divided by the square root of n — that is the line that converts from the standard deviation of individual measurements to the uncertainty in the mean. The 95% confidence interval uses a coverage factor of 2 as an approximation for large samples. For small samples — fewer than about 30 trials — the t-distribution gives a larger critical value than 2, and you would use MATLAB's tinv function to get the exact value for the degrees of freedom. The overlap check is a simple logical comparison: does the upper bound of the first confidence interval exceed the lower bound of the second? In this example it does not — CI_A ends at 48.6 and CI_B begins at 50.9 — so the intervals do not overlap and the datasets are statistically distinguishable. This is

.6 and CI_B begins at 50.9 — so the intervals do not overlap and the datasets are statistically distinguishable. This is the starting point for your Section 4 uncertainty analysis in the Tier 2 report: you need to compute the 95% confidence interval for your measurement means and compare them to the model prediction or to a second test condition. slide 10: Connecting Statistical Comparison to the Tier 2 Report and Improvement Proposal (Image: A flow diagram connecting the statistical comparison analysis to three report sections. Starting node: "Statistical comparison analysis — CI overlap, significance test, difference characterization." Three arrows branch to: Arrow 1: "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome" — labeled "Use CI of experimental mean to evaluate whether model prediction falls

: "Section 3A: Prediction vs. outcome" — labeled "Use CI of experimental mean to evaluate whether model prediction falls within bounds." Arrow 2: "Section 3B: Uncertainty analysis" — labeled "Report standard error, CI, comparison of Monte Carlo vs. partial derivative method." Arrow 3: "Section 6: Improvement proposal — category (c): experimental design" — labeled "If statistical power was insufficient to distinguish configurations, propose increased n or improved measurement precision." A note at the bottom: "Statistical comparison is both an analysis tool (Sections 3A and 3B) and a diagnostic for improvement (Section 6). The improvement proposal must be grounded in the statistical analysis, not generic.") Talking Point: The statistical comparison tools you learn today connect to the Tier

statistical analysis, not generic.") Talking Point: The statistical comparison tools you learn today connect to the Tier 2 report in two directions. First, they are analysis tools: you use confidence interval comparison to evaluate whether the model prediction falls within the uncertainty bounds of your experimental mean, and you report the standard error and CI in the uncertainty analysis section. Second, and less obviously, they are diagnostic tools for the improvement proposal. If your confidence intervals were too wide to distinguish the model prediction from the data — not because the model is good, but because your measurement was imprecise — then the improvement proposal should address that: more trials at each condition, or a more precise sensor, or reduced noise through better

t proposal should address that: more trials at each condition, or a more precise sensor, or reduced noise through better signal conditioning. The improvement must be grounded in the statistical result. "Our CI was too wide to make a conclusion about the model at the required precision" is a specific finding, and the proposed fix — a specific increase in sample size or measurement precision — is the corresponding improvement. That is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit. slide 10b: Advance Organizer — Comparative Analysis Discussion (Discussion preview) Talking Point: Before the discussion activity: in the comparative analysis today, apply the W8 taxonomy. Look for R² reported without a residual plot — that is adequacy claimed without the diagnostic. Look for

he W8 taxonomy. Look for R² reported without a residual plot — that is adequacy claimed without the diagnostic. Look for statistical significance treated as validation — a p-value does not tell you the model is correct, it tells you the difference is unlikely to be random. Look for confidence intervals described as probability statements about the true value, rather than statements about the estimation procedure. See W9 Reading 3 for the full evaluator's checklist we will use. Use those as your anchor, and approach the sample report as a test engineer evaluating credibility — not as a reader looking for what is correct. slide 11: Closure Comparing datasets requires more than visual inspection — it requires computing confidence intervals for each mean, evaluating overlap to test for

s more than visual inspection — it requires computing confidence intervals for each mean, evaluating overlap to test for statistical distinguishability, and connecting the statistical result to an engineering conclusion about whether the difference matters for the intended use. Statistical significance and engineering significance are independent: a statistically significant difference may be too small to matter, and an engineering-significant difference may not be detectable at your current sample size. The null hypothesis framework tells you whether the data is inconsistent with the assumption of identical populations — it does not tell you whether the difference is important. That judgment belongs to you and depends on the pre-stated acceptance criterion, which is why the acceptance

important. That judgment belongs to you and depends on the pre-stated acceptance criterion, which is why the acceptance criterion must be written before the data is collected. In Lecture 2 today, we return to Monte Carlo simulation and introduce regression — tools that extend the comparison from means at single operating points to models evaluated across full datasets. Together, these two lectures complete the E2 statistical comparison toolkit that you will apply in the lab session and in Sections 3 and 4 of your Tier 2 group report. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded in the W9 L1 blueprint purpose statement: comparing means, standard errors, confidence interval overlap, hypothesis testing at conceptual level, statistical vs. engineering significance. - The statistical vs. engineering

lap, hypothesis testing at conceptual level, statistical vs. engineering significance. - The statistical vs. engineering significance distinction (slide 5) is explicitly required by the blueprint: "the difference between statistical significance (unlikely by chance) and engineering significance (meaningful for the intended use)." - Connects forward to W9 L2 on Monte Carlo and regression. - MATLAB implementation (slide 9) previews the lab activity and is consistent with the course's MATLAB-based analysis approach. - The n=25 sample size in the worked example is intentionally large to simplify the k=2 approximation; the note about t-distribution for small samples is included for accuracy. - No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2 primary treatment.

pages.title: L02
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Lecture L02 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Regression, Goodness-of-Fit & Monte Carlo for Multi-Variable Systems Talking Point: In Week 2, you ran Monte Carlo simulation for a scalar derived quantity — electrical power computed from... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Regression, Goodness-of-Fit & Monte Carlo for Multi-Variable Systems Talking Point: In Week 2, you ran Monte Carlo simulation for a scalar derived quantity — electrical power computed from current and voltage — and you compared the result to the partial derivative propagation method. That was an introduction to the conceptual tool. In Week 9, we return to Monte Carlo with a harder problem: a multi-variable aerospace system where several inputs have correlated uncertainty, and where the model relationship between inputs and outputs is nonlinear. We also introduce regression as a tool for assessing model adequacy across a full dataset — not at a single operating point, but across all the conditions you tested. These two tools complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit, and they map directly to sections of your Tier 2 group report. slide 2: Regression — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A scatter plot with two regression fits overlaid. The x-axis is labeled "Applied load (N)" and the y-axis is labeled "Measured deflection (mm)." Scatter data points are plotted in gray. Two model fit lines are overlaid: a linear regression line labeled "Linear fit: R² = 0.97" in gold, and a physical model prediction curve labeled "Theory (Euler-Bernoulli): no free parameters" in a dashed white line. The linear regression line passes through the scatter well, but diverges slightly from the theory curve at the upper end of the range. An annotation on the linear fit: "High R² — but this is not the same as validating the physical model." An annotation on the theory curve: "Physical model prediction — tested against data independently." Caption: "Regression fits a curve to data. Validation tests a physical model against data. These are different activities.") Talking Point: Regression and model validation are not the same activity, and the distinction matters. Regression fits a mathematical curve — linear, polynomial, exponential — to a dataset to describe how the output varies with the inputs. A high R-squared value tells you that the curve explains most of the variance in the data. What it does not tell you is whether the curve is the physically correct model. In this example, a linear regression achieves R-squared of 0.97 — the fit is good. But the physical model for this system predicts a specific nonlinear relationship based on the governing equations. The regression line does not know about the physics — it only knows about the data in this range. If you tested outside this range, the linear regression might extrapolate poorly even though it fits well here, while the physics-based model extrapolates correctly because it captures the governing behavior. This is why regression is a diagnostic tool for model adequacy — you compare the residuals from the physical model to the residuals from a purely data-driven fit — not a validation verdict in itself. slide 3: Goodness-of-Fit Measures — What R² Does and Does Not Tell You (Image: Three scatter plots in a row, each showing a model fit and residual plot below it. Plot 1: "R² = 0.98, random residuals." The fit is good and residuals are scattered randomly around zero. Caption: "Both R² and residuals support model adequacy." Plot 2: "R² = 0.98, systematic trend in residuals." The fit looks good visually, but the residual plot shows a clear nonlinear trend — the model overestimates at low and high values and underestimates in the middle. Caption: "High R² but systematic residuals — model is structurally wrong. R² alone is misleading." Plot 3: "R² = 0.82, random residuals." The fit is imperfect visually but residuals are randomly scattered. Caption: "Lower R² but random residuals — model form may be correct; precision is the limitation." An overall caption at the bottom: "Always inspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual structure tells you about model form adequacy." The three plots are compact but readable, with consistent axis scales.) Talking Point: R-squared is one of the most overused and misinterpreted statistics in engineering data analysis, and I want to spend a moment on what it actually measures. R-squared tells you what fraction of the variance in the data is explained by the model — equivalently, how much of the total scatter around the mean is captured by the fitted curve. A high R-squared does not mean the model is physically correct. R²=0.98 can coexist with systematic residuals because R² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is positive in one region and negative in another can average out, masking the structural failure. Look at the middle panel: R-squared is 0.98, which looks excellent, but the residuals — the pattern of errors — show a clear nonlinear trend. The model is overestimating at the extremes and underestimating in the middle. That pattern tells you the model is missing a physical effect — the form is wrong even though the overall fit looks good by the R-squared metric. The right panel shows the opposite: R-squared is 0.82, which might make you nervous, but the residuals are random scatter. The model form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the lower R-squared reflects measurement precision, not model error. The lesson is to always look at the residual plot alongside R-squared. R-squared is a summary statistic; the residual structure is the diagnostic. slide 4: Returning to Monte Carlo — From E1 to E2 Complexity (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column: "E1 Week 2 — Scalar Monte Carlo." Shows the P=IV example: single output (power), two input variables (V, I), simple product model, independent Gaussian inputs. A histogram of the simulated power distribution is shown. Caption: "10,000 trials, two independent inputs, one nonlinear function." Right column: "E2 Week 9 — Multi-variable Monte Carlo." Shows a more complex aerospace system: three input variables (structural stiffness k, damping c, applied force F), correlated inputs (k and c are correlated in a material sense), and a nonlinear governing equation. A histogram of the simulated peak deflection distribution is shown — slightly skewed, not perfectly Gaussian. Caption: "10,000 trials, three correlated inputs, nonlinear governing equation — output distribution is non-Gaussian." An annotation between the columns: "Same conceptual tool — different level of complexity. The core MATLAB structure is identical." The visual continuity between E1 and E2 is the central message of this slide.) Talking Point: We first used Monte Carlo in Week 2 to propagate uncertainty through the power equation: P equals IV. We had two independent inputs — voltage and current — and we sampled them from Gaussian distributions based on the instrument specifications. The output distribution was nearly Gaussian because the function was nearly linear. In Week 9, we apply the same conceptual tool to a more complex aerospace system, and three things change. First, there are more input variables — perhaps stiffness, damping, and applied force. Second, some inputs may be correlated — for example, if both stiffness and damping come from the same material property measurement, their uncertainties are not independent. Third, the governing equation is nonlinear — the output distribution will not be Gaussian even if all the inputs are Gaussian. The MATLAB structure is almost identical to what you did in Week 2: sample each input, evaluate the model for each sample set, collect the output distribution, compute the standard deviation and percentiles. What changes is the interpretation — the non-Gaussian output requires you to report percentiles rather than assuming a symmetric uncertainty interval, and the correlated inputs require a joint sampling strategy rather than independent draws. slide 5: Correlated Inputs — Advanced Case (Note) (Image: A simple note card layout on the slide. Gold border around a card-shaped area with the header: "Advanced case — beyond the scope of this course." Body text: "Most groups in E2 have independent inputs and should use standard randn(N,1) sampling for each input. If two or more of your inputs come from the same source (e.g., same calibration procedure or same material batch), their uncertainties are correlated — independent sampling will underestimate worst-case outcomes. See W9 Reading 2 for the Cholesky approach and MATLAB implementation." At the bottom of the card: a small arrow icon pointing to "R2: Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case." The rest of the slide is dark background with minimal text, emphasizing this is a brief informational note rather than lecture content.) Talking Point: I want to flag one case that is beyond the scope of the standard E2 workflow — I am mentioning it here for completeness, not because most groups will need it. If two or more of your input uncertainties come from the same source — the same calibration procedure, the same material property measurement, the same batch test — those inputs are correlated, and sampling them independently in Monte Carlo will underestimate the worst-case output uncertainty. The standard fix uses a Cholesky decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. This is an advanced topic. The full explanation, geometric intuition, and MATLAB code are in Reading 2 this week. If you have genuinely independent inputs — which most of you do — use the standard randn approach from the previous slide and do not worry about this. If you have reason to believe two of your inputs are correlated, read R2 before implementing your Monte Carlo. slide 6: MATLAB Implementation — Multi-Variable Monte Carlo (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results, styled consistently with the previous lecture's code slides. Code: "% Multi-variable Monte Carlo for peak deflection"; "% NOTE: This example assumes independent inputs (standard case)."; "% For correlated inputs, see slide 5 and W9 Reading 2."; "N = 10000; % Number of trials"; "% Input distributions (mean, std)"; "k = 1200 + 50 randn(N,1); % Stiffness [N/m]"; "c = 0.15 + 0.01 randn(N,1); % Damping ratio [-]"; "F = 25.0 + 0.5 randn(N,1); % Applied force [N]"; "% Evaluate model"; "x_peak = F ./ k . (1 ./ sqrt(1 - c.^2)); % Approx. peak response"; "% Statistics"; "mean_x = mean(x_peak);"; "std_x = std(x_peak);"; "CI_95 = prctile(x_peak, [2.5 97.5]); % 95% CI from percentiles" Results panel: "mean_x = 0.02087 m" | "std_x = 0.00094 m" | "CI_95 = [0.01903, 0.02271] m." A histogram of x_peak is shown to the right, slightly right-skewed. A MATLAB-style caption: "Peak deflection: 0.0209 m, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m. Non-Gaussian distribution — reported from percentiles.") Talking Point: Here is the MATLAB implementation for a three-variable Monte Carlo analysis of a structural system. The inputs are stiffness, damping ratio, and applied force, each sampled from Gaussian distributions based on the uncertainty estimates from the sensor specifications and material characterization. The model is a simple approximate expression for peak dynamic response. After 10,000 evaluations, we extract the mean and standard deviation of the output distribution, but notice that we report the 95% confidence interval using the percentile function rather than by computing mean plus-or-minus two standard deviations. This is important: for a non-Gaussian output distribution, the symmetric interval computed from the standard deviation may not correspond to the correct 95% coverage. The percentile-based interval is distribution-free and always gives the correct coverage. In this example the distribution is slightly right-skewed — the upper tail is longer than the lower tail — which is physically meaningful: there is more room for the system to exceed the nominal response than to fall below it. Report both the mean and the percentile-based interval in the Tier 2 uncertainty analysis section. slide 7: Comparing Monte Carlo to Partial Derivative Propagation — When They Agree and When They Do Not (Image: A comparison table with three rows. Column headers: "Method," "E1 Power example result," "E2 structural example result." Row 1 — "Partial derivative propagation": "P = 12.3 ± 0.21 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209 ± 0.00096 m (symmetric, Gaussian assumed)." Row 2 — "Monte Carlo (10,000 trials)": "P = 12.3 ± 0.19 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m (non-symmetric, from percentiles)." Row 3 — "Agreement assessment": "Agree closely — power equation nearly linear, Gaussian inputs, symmetric output" | "Agree closely in mean; CI slightly asymmetric in Monte Carlo — nonlinearity causes minor departure from Gaussian." A caption: "When the model is linear and inputs are Gaussian, both methods agree. When the model is nonlinear, Monte Carlo captures asymmetric distributions that partial derivatives cannot." A note: "When the two methods give substantially different results, the Monte Carlo result should be used — it makes fewer assumptions about the model's linearity.") Talking Point: This comparison table connects back to the Week 2 activity explicitly, as the blueprint asks us to do. In the power equation example, partial derivative propagation and Monte Carlo give almost identical results, because the power equation is nearly linear and the Gaussian input assumption is good. In the structural dynamics example, they agree on the mean but the Monte Carlo confidence interval is slightly asymmetric. The partial derivative method assumes a linear model and symmetric Gaussian output — it cannot capture the right-skewed distribution we saw in the previous slide. The Monte Carlo result is more accurate when the model is nonlinear because it makes fewer assumptions about the output distribution shape. When the two methods agree closely, either can be used, but for your Tier 2 report, we ask you to report both and compare them — that comparison is evidence that you understand when each method is appropriate and where they diverge. If they give substantially different results, use the Monte Carlo result and explain why the partial derivative method underestimates the uncertainty. slide 8: Residual Analysis Across a Full Dataset — Applying Regression to Validate Model Adequacy (Image: A two-panel MATLAB figure. Top panel: Scatter data plotted against the independent variable (applied load, x-axis) with three overlaid lines: (1) the physical model prediction (dashed gold), (2) a linear regression fit to the data (solid white), and (3) the ±σ uncertainty band around the physical model (shaded region). Data points are gray circles. The physical model is slightly below the data in the middle range. Bottom panel: Residual plot (model minus data) vs. the same x-axis. Residuals are shown as scatter points with the ±σ uncertainty band shown as dashed lines. The residuals in the middle range are consistently positive, indicating systematic over-prediction. A diagonal trend line through the residuals is labeled "Systematic trend — possible model-form issue at mid-range conditions." Caption: "Regression is not the model. The physical model prediction and the regression line diverge at mid-range — the residual analysis reveals the discrepancy.") Talking Point: Here is how regression connects to the model adequacy question. In the top panel, you see the physical model prediction, a linear regression fit to the data, and the experimental scatter. They look similar at a glance. But the bottom panel — the residual plot of the physical model versus the data — shows a systematic positive trend in the middle range: the physical model consistently over-predicts the measured deflection at intermediate loads. This is a model-form error signal: the linear regression tracks the data well because it has a free slope parameter that adjusts to fit, but the physical model is constrained by its governing equation and cannot adjust. The systematic residual tells you the physical model is missing something in the mid-range behavior — possibly a compliance effect at intermediate loads that the boundary condition assumption does not capture. This is exactly the analysis you did in Week 8 using residuals at individual points, now applied systematically across the full dataset using regression as the reference. The regression is a diagnostic reference, not the answer. slide 9: Applying These Tools to the Tier 2 Lab — What the Section 4 Deliverable Requires (Image: A structured checklist for the Section 4 (uncertainty analysis) deliverable. Five rows: "1. Partial derivative derivation — propagate uncertainty for the primary derived quantity using the governing equation." "2. Dominant error source ranking — which input contributes most to the total uncertainty, and how does it change with operating conditions?" "3. Monte Carlo simulation — ≥10,000 samples, Gaussian inputs based on sensor specs, output distribution histogram." "4. Compare methods — partial derivative and Monte Carlo results stated together, differences noted and explained." "5. Interpret adequacy — given the total uncertainty, is the experiment sensitive enough to distinguish the model prediction from the data within the acceptance criterion?" Each row has a MATLAB icon on the left and a report section tag on the right. The checklist is clean and formatted as a structured deliverable specification.) Talking Point: Section 4 of the Tier 2 group report is the uncertainty analysis section, and it requires all five of these elements. You already completed the partial derivative derivation in the prediction document — reproduce it and update it with the actual measured values. The dominant error source ranking tells you which input drives the total uncertainty, and you should check whether that ranking changes across the operating conditions you tested — sometimes a different input dominates at high versus low values of the independent variable. The Monte Carlo simulation uses 10,000 samples at minimum, draws inputs from Gaussian distributions based on the actual sensor specifications, and outputs a distribution histogram with the mean and 95% CI reported from percentiles. Then you compare the partial derivative result to the Monte Carlo result and note the agreement or disagreement. And finally — the hardest part — you interpret whether the experiment is sensitive enough. If the total uncertainty is comparable to the model-data discrepancy you found, you cannot distinguish whether the discrepancy is real or within measurement noise. That is an important finding: it means a more precise measurement is needed to resolve the model adequacy question at the required confidence level. The ultimate question for Section 3B is whether the total uncertainty bound you compute here allows you to evaluate your committed Week 6 acceptance criterion. If the propagated uncertainty is comparable to the model-data discrepancy, you cannot resolve whether your acceptance criterion is met or not — and that finding belongs in your conclusion. slide 10: The Improvement Proposal — Grounding in Statistical Analysis (Image: A worked example of an improvement proposal entry, formatted as a short structured paragraph. Header: "Section 6 — Improvement Proposal, Category (c): Experimental Design." Body: "The current test matrix includes 8 repeated trials at each of 3 operating conditions. The 95% confidence interval for the mean at each condition is ±1.8 N (from standard error analysis). The model-data discrepancy at the critical operating condition (Condition 2) is 2.1 N — just outside the confidence interval at the current sample size. To resolve the model adequacy question at this condition with 95% confidence, we estimate a sample size of approximately 14 trials per condition would be required (based on the observed standard deviation of 4.2 N and the target half-CI of 1.1 N). An additional 6 trials per condition would close the gap. Alternatively, reducing the random measurement noise through improved signal conditioning (DAQ improvement category b) would achieve the same sensitivity improvement without additional trials." A gold border frames this example and a label reads: "This is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit — specific, grounded in data, actionable.") Talking Point: This is what a full-quality improvement proposal looks like when it is grounded in the statistical analysis. The group identified that their confidence interval width of 1.8 newtons is just barely larger than the model-data discrepancy of 2.1 newtons — close enough that they cannot definitively classify the discrepancy as either real or within noise. They then computed the sample size that would be needed to narrow the confidence interval sufficiently to resolve the question. They arrived at a specific number of additional trials — six more per condition. And they noted an alternative path: improving measurement precision through signal conditioning would achieve the same sensitivity without requiring additional trials. Both paths are specific, quantified, and grounded in the analysis results. That is the standard. The improvement proposal is not a reflection on what went well — it is a forward-looking engineering recommendation, derived from your data, that would make the experiment more informative. That is the skill E3 is going to ask you to demonstrate independently for an experiment you design yourself. slide 11: Closure Regression is a diagnostic tool for evaluating model adequacy across a full dataset — not a substitute for a physical model, and not a validation verdict. R-squared tells you about variance explained; the residual structure tells you about model form adequacy, and you must look at both. Monte Carlo simulation, which we first applied to electrical power in Week 2, extends to multi-variable aerospace systems with correlated inputs and nonlinear models, where partial derivative propagation underestimates uncertainty due to its linearity and Gaussian output assumptions. When both methods agree, either may be reported; when they disagree, use the Monte Carlo result and explain why. These tools together — confidence interval comparison, regression, and Monte Carlo — complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit. Section 4 of your Tier 2 group report requires all three, and the improvement proposal in Section 6 must be grounded in what the analysis revealed about measurement sensitivity. In the lab session today, you apply all of this to your own Tier 2 dataset. The tools are not abstract — they are the specific instruments for finishing your group report, and the quality of how you apply and interpret them is what distinguishes a good experimental engineer from one who can only follow a procedure. CLO 2, CLO 3 primary treatment. slide 1: Title Slide Title: Regression, Goodness-of-Fit & Monte Carlo for Multi-Variable Systems Talking Point: In Week 2, you ran Monte Carlo simulation for a scalar derived quantity — electrical power computed from current and voltage — and you compared the result to the partial derivative propagation method. That was an introduction to the conceptual tool. In Week 9, we return to Monte Carlo with a harder problem: a multi-variable aerospace system where several inputs have correlated uncertainty, and where the model relationship between inputs and outputs is nonlinear. We also introduce regression as a tool for assessing model adequacy across a full dataset — not at a single operating point, but across all the conditions you tested. These two tools complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit, and they map directly to sections of your Tier 2 group report. slide 2: Regression — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A scatter plot with two regression fits overlaid. The x-axis is labeled "Applied load (N)" and the y-axis is labeled "Measured deflection (mm)." Scatter data points are plotted in gray. Two model fit lines are overlaid: a linear regression line labeled "Linear fit: R² = 0.97" in gold, and a physical model prediction curve labeled "Theory (Euler-Bernoulli): no free parameters" in a dashed white line. The linear regression line passes through the scatter well, but diverges slightly from the theory curve at the upper end of the range. An annotation on the linear fit: "High R² — but this is not the same as validating the physical model." An annotation on the theory curve: "Physical model prediction — tested against data independently." Caption: "Regression fits a curve to data. Validation tests a physical model against data. These are different activities.") Talking Point: Regression and model validation are not the same activity, and the distinction matters. Regression fits a mathematical curve — linear, polynomial, exponential — to a dataset to describe how the output varies with the inputs. A high R-squared value tells you that the curve explains most of the variance in the data. What it does not tell you is whether the curve is the physically correct model. In this example, a linear regression achieves R-squared of 0.97 — the fit is good. But the physical model for this system predicts a specific nonlinear relationship based on the governing equations. The regression line does not know about the physics — it only knows about the data in this range. If you tested outside this range, the linear regression might extrapolate poorly even though it fits well here, while the physics-based model extrapolates correctly because it captures the governing behavior. This is why regression is a diagnostic tool for model adequacy — you compare the residuals from the physical model to the residuals from a purely data-driven fit — not a validation verdict in itself. slide 3: Goodness-of-Fit Measures — What R² Does and Does Not Tell You (Image: Three scatter plots in a row, each showing a model fit and residual plot below it. Plot 1: "R² = 0.98, random residuals." The fit is good and residuals are scattered randomly around zero. Caption: "Both R² and residuals support model adequacy." Plot 2: "R² = 0.98, systematic trend in residuals." The fit looks good visually, but the residual plot shows a clear nonlinear trend — the model overestimates at low and high values and underestimates in the middle. Caption: "High R² but systematic residuals — model is structurally wrong. R² alone is misleading." Plot 3: "R² = 0.82, random residuals." The fit is imperfect visually but residuals are randomly scattered. Caption: "Lower R² but random residuals — model form may be correct; precision is the limitation." An overall caption at the bottom: "Always inspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual structure tells you about model form adequacy." The three plots are compact but readable, with consistent axis scales.) Talking Point: R-squared is one of the most overused and misinterpreted statistics in engineering data analysis, and I want to spend a moment on what it actually measures. R-squared tells you what fraction of the variance in the data is explained by the model — equivalently, how much of the total scatter around the mean is captured by the fitted curve. A high R-squared does not mean the model is physically correct. R²=0.98 can coexist with systematic residuals because R² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is positive in one region and negative in another can average out, masking the structural failure. Look at the middle panel: R-squared is 0.98, which looks excellent, but the residuals — the pattern of errors — show a clear nonlinear trend. The model is overestimating at the extremes and underestimating in the middle. That pattern tells you the model is missing a physical effect — the form is wrong even though the overall fit looks good by the R-squared metric. The right panel shows the opposite: R-squared is 0.82, which might make you nervous, but the residuals are random scatter. The model form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the lower R-squared reflects measurement precision, not model error. The lesson is to always look at the residual plot alongside R-squared. R-squared is a summary statistic; the residual structure is the diagnostic. slide 4: Returning to Monte Carlo — From E1 to E2 Complexity (Image: A two-column comparison.
