That is an important finding: it means a more precise measurement is needed to resolve the model adequacy question at the required confidence level. The ultimate question for Section 3B is whether the total uncertainty bound you compute here allows you to evaluate your committed Week 6 acceptance criterion. If the propagated uncertainty is comparable to the model-data discrepancy, you cannot resolve whether your acceptance criterion is met or not — and that finding belongs in your conclusion. slide 10: The Improvement Proposal — Grounding in Statistical Analysis (Image: A worked example of an improvement proposal entry, formatted as a short structured paragraph. Header: "Section 6 — Improvement Proposal, Category (c): Experimental Design." Body: "The current test matrix includes 8 repeated trials at each of 3 operating conditions. The 95% confidence interval for the mean at each condition is ±1.8 N (from standard error analysis). The model-data discrepancy at the critical operating condition (Condition 2) is 2.1 N — just outside the confidence interval at the current sample size. To resolve the model adequacy question at this condition with 95% confidence, we estimate a sample size of approximately 14 trials per condition would be required (based on the observed standard deviation of 4.2 N and the target half-CI of 1.1 N). An additional 6 trials per condition would close the gap. Alternatively, reducing the random measurement noise through improved signal conditioning (DAQ improvement category b) would achieve the same sensitivity improvement without additional trials." A gold border frames this example and a label reads: "This is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit — specific, grounded in data, actionable.") Talking Point: This is what a full-quality improvement proposal looks like when it is grounded in the statistical analysis. The group identified that their confidence interval width of 1.8 newtons is just barely larger than the model-data discrepancy of 2.1 newtons — close enough that they cannot definitively classify the discrepancy as either real or within noise. They then computed the sample size that would be needed to narrow the confidence interval sufficiently to resolve the question. They arrived at a specific number of additional trials — six more per condition. And they noted an alternative path: improving measurement precision through signal conditioning would achieve the same sensitivity without requiring additional trials. Both paths are specific, quantified, and grounded in the analysis results. That is the standard. The improvement proposal is not a reflection on what went well — it is a forward-looking engineering recommendation, derived from your data, that would make the experiment more informative. That is the skill E3 is going to ask you to demonstrate independently for an experiment you design yourself. slide 11: Closure Regression is a diagnostic tool for evaluating model adequacy across a full dataset — not a substitute for a physical model, and not a validation verdict. R-squared tells you about variance explained; the residual structure tells you about model form adequacy, and you must look at both. Monte Carlo simulation, which we first applied to electrical power in Week 2, extends to multi-variable aerospace systems with correlated inputs and nonlinear models, where partial derivative propagation underestimates uncertainty due to its linearity and Gaussian output assumptions. When both methods agree, either may be reported; when they disagree, use the Monte Carlo result and explain why. These tools together — confidence interval comparison, regression, and Monte Carlo — complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit. Section 4 of your Tier 2 group report requires all three, and the improvement proposal in Section 6 must be grounded in what the analysis revealed about measurement sensitivity. In the lab session today, you apply all of this to your own Tier 2 dataset. The tools are not abstract — they are the specific instruments for finishing your group report, and the quality of how you apply and interpret them is what distinguishes a good experimental engineer from one who can only follow a procedure. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded in the W9 L2 blueprint purpose statement: regression, goodness-of-fit, Monte Carlo for multi-variable systems, explicit connection to E1 W2 Monte Carlo activity. - The connection to E1 W2 Monte Carlo is made explicit in slides 1 and 4, per blueprint requirement: "The connection to E1 Week 2 is made explicit — the conceptual tool is the same, the domain is now more complex." - Correlated inputs (slide 5) are included because the blueprint specifies "several inputs have correlated uncertainty." - Slide 7 provides the method comparison (partial derivative vs. Monte Carlo) required by the Tier 2 Section 4 deliverable description. - The improvement proposal example (slide 10) demonstrates the quality of Section 6 grounding in statistical analysis, per instructor_clarification_notes.md items on critique and improvement. - No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2, CLO 3 primary treatment.

chunks.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Lecture L02 slide 1: Title Slide Title: Regression, Goodness-of-Fit & Monte Carlo for Multi-Variable Systems Talking Point: In Week 2, you ran Monte Carlo simulation for a scalar derived quantity — electrical power computed from... slide 1: Title Slide Title: Regression, Goodness-of-Fit & Monte Carlo for Multi-Variable Systems Talking Point: In Week 2, you ran Monte Carlo simulation for a scalar derived quantity — electrical power computed from current and voltage — and you compared the result to the partial derivative propagation method. That was an introduction to the conceptual tool. In Week 9, we return to Monte Carlo with a harder problem: a multi-variable aerospace system where several inputs have correlated uncertainty, and where the model relationship

em: a multi-variable aerospace system where several inputs have correlated uncertainty, and where the model relationship between inputs and outputs is nonlinear. We also introduce regression as a tool for assessing model adequacy across a full dataset — not at a single operating point, but across all the conditions you tested. These two tools complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit, and they map directly to sections of your Tier 2 group report. slide 2: Regression — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A scatter plot with two regression fits overlaid. The x-axis is labeled "Applied load (N)" and the y-axis is labeled "Measured deflection (mm)." Scatter data points are plotted in gray. Two model fit lines are overlaid: a linear regression line labeled "Linear fit: R² = 0.97" in gold,

are plotted in gray. Two model fit lines are overlaid: a linear regression line labeled "Linear fit: R² = 0.97" in gold, and a physical model prediction curve labeled "Theory (Euler-Bernoulli): no free parameters" in a dashed white line. The linear regression line passes through the scatter well, but diverges slightly from the theory curve at the upper end of the range. An annotation on the linear fit: "High R² — but this is not the same as validating the physical model." An annotation on the theory curve: "Physical model prediction — tested against data independently." Caption: "Regression fits a curve to data. Validation tests a physical model against data. These are different activities.") Talking Point: Regression and model validation are not the same activity, and the distinction

re different activities.") Talking Point: Regression and model validation are not the same activity, and the distinction matters. Regression fits a mathematical curve — linear, polynomial, exponential — to a dataset to describe how the output varies with the inputs. A high R-squared value tells you that the curve explains most of the variance in the data. What it does not tell you is whether the curve is the physically correct model. In this example, a linear regression achieves R-squared of 0.97 — the fit is good. But the physical model for this system predicts a specific nonlinear relationship based on the governing equations. The regression line does not know about the physics — it only knows about the data in this range. If you tested outside this range, the linear regression might

the physics — it only knows about the data in this range. If you tested outside this range, the linear regression might extrapolate poorly even though it fits well here, while the physics-based model extrapolates correctly because it captures the governing behavior. This is why regression is a diagnostic tool for model adequacy — you compare the residuals from the physical model to the residuals from a purely data-driven fit — not a validation verdict in itself. slide 3: Goodness-of-Fit Measures — What R² Does and Does Not Tell You (Image: Three scatter plots in a row, each showing a model fit and residual plot below it. Plot 1: "R² = 0.98, random residuals." The fit is good and residuals are scattered randomly around zero. Caption: "Both R² and residuals support model adequacy." Plot 2:

good and residuals are scattered randomly around zero. Caption: "Both R² and residuals support model adequacy." Plot 2: "R² = 0.98, systematic trend in residuals." The fit looks good visually, but the residual plot shows a clear nonlinear trend — the model overestimates at low and high values and underestimates in the middle. Caption: "High R² but systematic residuals — model is structurally wrong. R² alone is misleading." Plot 3: "R² = 0.82, random residuals." The fit is imperfect visually but residuals are randomly scattered. Caption: "Lower R² but random residuals — model form may be correct; precision is the limitation." An overall caption at the bottom: "Always inspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual structure tells you about model form

nspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual structure tells you about model form adequacy." The three plots are compact but readable, with consistent axis scales.) Talking Point: R-squared is one of the most overused and misinterpreted statistics in engineering data analysis, and I want to spend a moment on what it actually measures. R-squared tells you what fraction of the variance in the data is explained by the model — equivalently, how much of the total scatter around the mean is captured by the fitted curve. A high R-squared does not mean the model is physically correct. R²=0.98 can coexist with systematic residuals because R² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is positive in one region and negative in

² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is positive in one region and negative in another can average out, masking the structural failure. Look at the middle panel: R-squared is 0.98, which looks excellent, but the residuals — the pattern of errors — show a clear nonlinear trend. The model is overestimating at the extremes and underestimating in the middle. That pattern tells you the model is missing a physical effect — the form is wrong even though the overall fit looks good by the R-squared metric. The right panel shows the opposite: R-squared is 0.82, which might make you nervous, but the residuals are random scatter. The model form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the lower R-squared reflects measurement

el form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the lower R-squared reflects measurement precision, not model error. The lesson is to always look at the residual plot alongside R-squared. R-squared is a summary statistic; the residual structure is the diagnostic. slide 4: Returning to Monte Carlo — From E1 to E2 Complexity (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column: "E1 Week 2 — Scalar Monte Carlo." Shows the P=IV example: single output (power), two input variables (V, I), simple product model, independent Gaussian inputs. A histogram of the simulated power distribution is shown. Caption: "10,000 trials, two independent inputs, one nonlinear function." Right column: "E2 Week 9 — Multi-variable Monte Carlo." Shows a more complex aerospace system: three

onlinear function." Right column: "E2 Week 9 — Multi-variable Monte Carlo." Shows a more complex aerospace system: three input variables (structural stiffness k, damping c, applied force F), correlated inputs (k and c are correlated in a material sense), and a nonlinear governing equation. A histogram of the simulated peak deflection distribution is shown — slightly skewed, not perfectly Gaussian. Caption: "10,000 trials, three correlated inputs, nonlinear governing equation — output distribution is non-Gaussian." An annotation between the columns: "Same conceptual tool — different level of complexity. The core MATLAB structure is identical." The visual continuity between E1 and E2 is the central message of this slide.) Talking Point: We first used Monte Carlo in Week 2 to propagate

between E1 and E2 is the central message of this slide.) Talking Point: We first used Monte Carlo in Week 2 to propagate uncertainty through the power equation: P equals IV. We had two independent inputs — voltage and current — and we sampled them from Gaussian distributions based on the instrument specifications. The output distribution was nearly Gaussian because the function was nearly linear. In Week 9, we apply the same conceptual tool to a more complex aerospace system, and three things change. First, there are more input variables — perhaps stiffness, damping, and applied force. Second, some inputs may be correlated — for example, if both stiffness and damping come from the same material property measurement, their uncertainties are not independent. Third, the governing equation is

e from the same material property measurement, their uncertainties are not independent. Third, the governing equation is nonlinear — the output distribution will not be Gaussian even if all the inputs are Gaussian. The MATLAB structure is almost identical to what you did in Week 2: sample each input, evaluate the model for each sample set, collect the output distribution, compute the standard deviation and percentiles. What changes is the interpretation — the non-Gaussian output requires you to report percentiles rather than assuming a symmetric uncertainty interval, and the correlated inputs require a joint sampling strategy rather than independent draws. slide 5: Correlated Inputs — Advanced Case (Note) (Image: A simple note card layout on the slide. Gold border around a card-shaped

orrelated Inputs — Advanced Case (Note) (Image: A simple note card layout on the slide. Gold border around a card-shaped area with the header: "Advanced case — beyond the scope of this course." Body text: "Most groups in E2 have independent inputs and should use standard randn(N,1) sampling for each input. If two or more of your inputs come from the same source (e.g., same calibration procedure or same material batch), their uncertainties are correlated — independent sampling will underestimate worst-case outcomes. See W9 Reading 2 for the Cholesky approach and MATLAB implementation." At the bottom of the card: a small arrow icon pointing to "R2: Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case." The rest of the slide is dark background with minimal text, emphasizing this is a brief

Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case." The rest of the slide is dark background with minimal text, emphasizing this is a brief informational note rather than lecture content.) Talking Point: I want to flag one case that is beyond the scope of the standard E2 workflow — I am mentioning it here for completeness, not because most groups will need it. If two or more of your input uncertainties come from the same source — the same calibration procedure, the same material property measurement, the same batch test — those inputs are correlated, and sampling them independently in Monte Carlo will underestimate the worst-case output uncertainty. The standard fix uses a Cholesky decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. This is an advanced topic. The full explanation,

decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. This is an advanced topic. The full explanation, geometric intuition, and MATLAB code are in Reading 2 this week. If you have genuinely independent inputs — which most of you do — use the standard randn approach from the previous slide and do not worry about this. If you have reason to believe two of your inputs are correlated, read R2 before implementing your Monte Carlo. slide 6: MATLAB Implementation — Multi-Variable Monte Carlo (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results, styled consistently with the previous lecture's code slides. Code: "% Multi-variable Monte Carlo for peak deflection"; "% NOTE: This example assumes independent inputs (standard case)."; "% For correlated inputs, see slide 5 and W9 Reading 2."; "N

This example assumes independent inputs (standard case)."; "% For correlated inputs, see slide 5 and W9 Reading 2."; "N = 10000; % Number of trials"; "% Input distributions (mean, std)"; "k = 1200 + 50 randn(N,1); % Stiffness [N/m]"; "c = 0.15 + 0.01 randn(N,1); % Damping ratio [-]"; "F = 25.0 + 0.5 randn(N,1); % Applied force [N]"; "% Evaluate model"; "x_peak = F ./ k . (1 ./ sqrt(1 - c.^2)); % Approx. peak response"; "% Statistics"; "mean_x = mean(x_peak);"; "std_x = std(x_peak);"; "CI_95 = prctile(x_peak, [2.5 97.5]); % 95% CI from percentiles" Results panel: "mean_x = 0.02087 m" | "std_x = 0.00094 m" | "CI_95 = [0.01903, 0.02271] m." A histogram of x_peak is shown to the right, slightly right-skewed. A MATLAB-style caption: "Peak deflection: 0.0209 m, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m.

hown to the right, slightly right-skewed. A MATLAB-style caption: "Peak deflection: 0.0209 m, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m. Non-Gaussian distribution — reported from percentiles.") Talking Point: Here is the MATLAB implementation for a three-variable Monte Carlo analysis of a structural system. The inputs are stiffness, damping ratio, and applied force, each sampled from Gaussian distributions based on the uncertainty estimates from the sensor specifications and material characterization. The model is a simple approximate expression for peak dynamic response. After 10,000 evaluations, we extract the mean and standard deviation of the output distribution, but notice that we report the 95% confidence interval using the percentile function rather than by computing mean plus-or-minus two

that we report the 95% confidence interval using the percentile function rather than by computing mean plus-or-minus two standard deviations. This is important: for a non-Gaussian output distribution, the symmetric interval computed from the standard deviation may not correspond to the correct 95% coverage. The percentile-based interval is distribution-free and always gives the correct coverage. In this example the distribution is slightly right-skewed — the upper tail is longer than the lower tail — which is physically meaningful: there is more room for the system to exceed the nominal response than to fall below it. Report both the mean and the percentile-based interval in the Tier 2 uncertainty analysis section. slide 7: Comparing Monte Carlo to Partial Derivative Propagation — When

rval in the Tier 2 uncertainty analysis section. slide 7: Comparing Monte Carlo to Partial Derivative Propagation — When They Agree and When They Do Not (Image: A comparison table with three rows. Column headers: "Method," "E1 Power example result," "E2 structural example result." Row 1 — "Partial derivative propagation": "P = 12.3 ± 0.21 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209 ± 0.00096 m (symmetric, Gaussian assumed)." Row 2 — "Monte Carlo (10,000 trials)": "P = 12.3 ± 0.19 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m (non-symmetric, from percentiles)." Row 3 — "Agreement assessment": "Agree closely — power equation nearly linear, Gaussian inputs, symmetric output" | "Agree closely in mean; CI slightly asymmetric in Monte Carlo — nonlinearity causes minor departure from Gaussian." A caption: "When

ly in mean; CI slightly asymmetric in Monte Carlo — nonlinearity causes minor departure from Gaussian." A caption: "When the model is linear and inputs are Gaussian, both methods agree. When the model is nonlinear, Monte Carlo captures asymmetric distributions that partial derivatives cannot." A note: "When the two methods give substantially different results, the Monte Carlo result should be used — it makes fewer assumptions about the model's linearity.") Talking Point: This comparison table connects back to the Week 2 activity explicitly, as the blueprint asks us to do. In the power equation example, partial derivative propagation and Monte Carlo give almost identical results, because the power equation is nearly linear and the Gaussian input assumption is good. In the structural

ntical results, because the power equation is nearly linear and the Gaussian input assumption is good. In the structural dynamics example, they agree on the mean but the Monte Carlo confidence interval is slightly asymmetric. The partial derivative method assumes a linear model and symmetric Gaussian output — it cannot capture the right-skewed distribution we saw in the previous slide. The Monte Carlo result is more accurate when the model is nonlinear because it makes fewer assumptions about the output distribution shape. When the two methods agree closely, either can be used, but for your Tier 2 report, we ask you to report both and compare them — that comparison is evidence that you understand when each method is appropriate and where they diverge. If they give substantially different

vidence that you understand when each method is appropriate and where they diverge. If they give substantially different results, use the Monte Carlo result and explain why the partial derivative method underestimates the uncertainty. slide 8: Residual Analysis Across a Full Dataset — Applying Regression to Validate Model Adequacy (Image: A two-panel MATLAB figure. Top panel: Scatter data plotted against the independent variable (applied load, x-axis) with three overlaid lines: (1) the physical model prediction (dashed gold), (2) a linear regression fit to the data (solid white), and (3) the ±σ uncertainty band around the physical model (shaded region). Data points are gray circles. The physical model is slightly below the data in the middle range. Bottom panel: Residual plot (model minus

ray circles. The physical model is slightly below the data in the middle range. Bottom panel: Residual plot (model minus data) vs. the same x-axis. Residuals are shown as scatter points with the ±σ uncertainty band shown as dashed lines. The residuals in the middle range are consistently positive, indicating systematic over-prediction. A diagonal trend line through the residuals is labeled "Systematic trend — possible model-form issue at mid-range conditions." Caption: "Regression is not the model. The physical model prediction and the regression line diverge at mid-range — the residual analysis reveals the discrepancy.") Talking Point: Here is how regression connects to the model adequacy question. In the top panel, you see the physical model prediction, a linear regression fit to the

to the model adequacy question. In the top panel, you see the physical model prediction, a linear regression fit to the data, and the experimental scatter. They look similar at a glance. But the bottom panel — the residual plot of the physical model versus the data — shows a systematic positive trend in the middle range: the physical model consistently over-predicts the measured deflection at intermediate loads. This is a model-form error signal: the linear regression tracks the data well because it has a free slope parameter that adjusts to fit, but the physical model is constrained by its governing equation and cannot adjust. The systematic residual tells you the physical model is missing something in the mid-range behavior — possibly a compliance effect at intermediate loads that the

sical model is missing something in the mid-range behavior — possibly a compliance effect at intermediate loads that the boundary condition assumption does not capture. This is exactly the analysis you did in Week 8 using residuals at individual points, now applied systematically across the full dataset using regression as the reference. The regression is a diagnostic reference, not the answer. slide 9: Applying These Tools to the Tier 2 Lab — What the Section 4 Deliverable Requires (Image: A structured checklist for the Section 4 (uncertainty analysis) deliverable. Dominant error source ranking — which input contributes most to the total uncertainty,

Compare methods — partial derivative and Monte Carlo results stated together, differences noted and explained." "5. Interpret adequacy — given the total uncertainty, is the experiment sensitive enough to distinguish the model prediction from the data within the acceptance criterion?" Each row has a MATLAB icon on the left and a report section tag on the right. The checklist is clean and formatted as a structured deliverable specification.) An overall caption at the bottom: "Always inspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual

rall caption at the bottom: "Always inspect residual plots alongside R². R² tells you about variance explained. Residual structure tells you about model form adequacy." The three plots are compact but readable, with consistent axis scales.) Talking Point: R-squared is one of the most overused and misinterpreted statistics in engineering data analysis, and I want to spend a moment on what it actually measures. R-squared tells you what fraction of the variance in the data is explained by the model — equivalently, how much of the total scatter around the mean is captured by the fitted curve. A high R-squared does not mean the model is physically correct. R²=0.98 can coexist with systematic residuals because R² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is

ist with systematic residuals because R² averages model performance across the full dataset — a systematic trend that is positive in one region and negative in another can average out, masking the structural failure. Look at the middle panel: R-squared is 0.98, which looks excellent, but the residuals — the pattern of errors — show a clear nonlinear trend. The model is overestimating at the extremes and underestimating in the middle. That pattern tells you the model is missing a physical effect — the form is wrong even though the overall fit looks good by the R-squared metric. The right panel shows the opposite: R-squared is 0.82, which might make you nervous, but the residuals are random scatter. The model form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the

residuals are random scatter. The model form may be correct — the scatter is consistent with measurement noise — and the lower R-squared reflects measurement precision, not model error. The lesson is to always look at the residual plot alongside R-squared. R-squared is a summary statistic; the residual structure is the diagnostic. slide 4: Returning to Monte Carlo — From E1 to E2 Complexity (Image: A two-column comparison. Left column: "E1 Week 2 — Scalar Monte Carlo." Shows the P=IV example: single output (power), two input variables (V, I), simple product model, independent Gaussian inputs. A histogram of the simulated power distribution is shown. Caption: "10,000 trials, two independent inputs, one nonlinear function." Right column: "E2 Week 9 — Multi-variable Monte Carlo." Shows a

trials, two independent inputs, one nonlinear function." Right column: "E2 Week 9 — Multi-variable Monte Carlo." Shows a more complex aerospace system: three input variables (structural stiffness k, damping c, applied force F), correlated inputs (k and c are correlated in a material sense), and a nonlinear governing equation. A histogram of the simulated peak deflection distribution is shown — slightly skewed, not perfectly Gaussian. Caption: "10,000 trials, three correlated inputs, nonlinear governing equation — output distribution is non-Gaussian." An annotation between the columns: "Same conceptual tool — different level of complexity. The core MATLAB structure is identical." The visual continuity between E1 and E2 is the central message of this slide.) Talking Point: We first used

identical." The visual continuity between E1 and E2 is the central message of this slide.) Talking Point: We first used Monte Carlo in Week 2 to propagate uncertainty through the power equation: P equals IV. We had two independent inputs — voltage and current — and we sampled them from Gaussian distributions based on the instrument specifications. The output distribution was nearly Gaussian because the function was nearly linear. In Week 9, we apply the same conceptual tool to a more complex aerospace system, and three things change. First, there are more input variables — perhaps stiffness, damping, and applied force. Second, some inputs may be correlated — for example, if both stiffness and damping come from the same material property measurement, their uncertainties are not

for example, if both stiffness and damping come from the same material property measurement, their uncertainties are not independent. Third, the governing equation is nonlinear — the output distribution will not be Gaussian even if all the inputs are Gaussian. The MATLAB structure is almost identical to what you did in Week 2: sample each input, evaluate the model for each sample set, collect the output distribution, compute the standard deviation and percentiles. What changes is the interpretation — the non-Gaussian output requires you to report percentiles rather than assuming a symmetric uncertainty interval, and the correlated inputs require a joint sampling strategy rather than independent draws. slide 5: Correlated Inputs — Advanced Case (Note) (Image: A simple note card layout on

gy rather than independent draws. slide 5: Correlated Inputs — Advanced Case (Note) (Image: A simple note card layout on the slide. Gold border around a card-shaped area with the header: "Advanced case — beyond the scope of this course." Body text: "Most groups in E2 have independent inputs and should use standard randn(N,1) sampling for each input. If two or more of your inputs come from the same source (e.g., same calibration procedure or same material batch), their uncertainties are correlated — independent sampling will underestimate worst-case outcomes. See W9 Reading 2 for the Cholesky approach and MATLAB implementation." At the bottom of the card: a small arrow icon pointing to "R2: Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case." The rest of the slide is dark background with

con pointing to "R2: Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case." The rest of the slide is dark background with minimal text, emphasizing this is a brief informational note rather than lecture content.) Talking Point: I want to flag one case that is beyond the scope of the standard E2 workflow — I am mentioning it here for completeness, not because most groups will need it. If two or more of your input uncertainties come from the same source — the same calibration procedure, the same material property measurement, the same batch test — those inputs are correlated, and sampling them independently in Monte Carlo will underestimate the worst-case output uncertainty. The standard fix uses a Cholesky decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. This is an

inty. The standard fix uses a Cholesky decomposition to generate samples with the correct joint distribution. This is an advanced topic. The full explanation, geometric intuition, and MATLAB code are in Reading 2 this week. If you have genuinely independent inputs — which most of you do — use the standard randn approach from the previous slide and do not worry about this. If you have reason to believe two of your inputs are correlated, read R2 before implementing your Monte Carlo. slide 6: MATLAB Implementation — Multi-Variable Monte Carlo (Image: A MATLAB code excerpt with results, styled consistently with the previous lecture's code slides. Code: "% Multi-variable Monte Carlo for peak deflection"; "% NOTE: This example assumes independent inputs (standard case)."; "% For correlated

Monte Carlo for peak deflection"; "% NOTE: This example assumes independent inputs (standard case)."; "% For correlated inputs, see slide 5 and W9 Reading 2."; "N = 10000; % Number of trials"; "% Input distributions (mean, std)"; "k = 1200 + 50*randn(N,1); % Stiffness [N/m]"; "c = 0.15 + 0.01*randn(N,1); % Damping ratio [-]"; "F = 25.0 + 0.5*randn(N,1); % Applied force [N]"; "% Evaluate model"; "x_peak = F ./ k .* (1 ./ sqrt(1 - c.^2)); % Approx. peak response"; "% Statistics"; "mean_x = mean(x_peak);"; "std_x = std(x_peak);"; "CI_95 = prctile(x_peak, [2.5 97.5]); % 95% CI from percentiles" Results panel: "mean_x = 0.02087 m" | "std_x = 0.00094 m" | "CI_95 = [0.01903, 0.02271] m." A histogram of x_peak is shown to the right, slightly right-skewed. A MATLAB-style caption: "Peak

[0.01903, 0.02271] m." A histogram of x_peak is shown to the right, slightly right-skewed. A MATLAB-style caption: "Peak deflection: 0.0209 m, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m. Non-Gaussian distribution — reported from percentiles.") Talking Point: Here is the MATLAB implementation for a three-variable Monte Carlo analysis of a structural system. The inputs are stiffness, damping ratio, and applied force, each sampled from Gaussian distributions based on the uncertainty estimates from the sensor specifications and material characterization. The model is a simple approximate expression for peak dynamic response. After 10,000 evaluations, we extract the mean and standard deviation of the output distribution, but notice that we report the 95% confidence interval using the percentile function rather

n of the output distribution, but notice that we report the 95% confidence interval using the percentile function rather than by computing mean plus-or-minus two standard deviations. This is important: for a non-Gaussian output distribution, the symmetric interval computed from the standard deviation may not correspond to the correct 95% coverage. The percentile-based interval is distribution-free and always gives the correct coverage. In this example the distribution is slightly right-skewed — the upper tail is longer than the lower tail — which is physically meaningful: there is more room for the system to exceed the nominal response than to fall below it. Report both the mean and the percentile-based interval in the Tier 2 uncertainty analysis section. slide 7: Comparing Monte Carlo to

the mean and the percentile-based interval in the Tier 2 uncertainty analysis section. slide 7: Comparing Monte Carlo to Partial Derivative Propagation — When They Agree and When They Do Not (Image: A comparison table with three rows. Column headers: "Method," "E1 Power example result," "E2 structural example result." Row 1 — "Partial derivative propagation": "P = 12.3 ± 0.21 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209 ± 0.00096 m (symmetric, Gaussian assumed)." Row 2 — "Monte Carlo (10,000 trials)": "P = 12.3 ± 0.19 W" | "x_peak = 0.0209, 95% CI [0.0190, 0.0227] m (non-symmetric, from percentiles)." Row 3 — "Agreement assessment": "Agree closely — power equation nearly linear, Gaussian inputs, symmetric output" | "Agree closely in mean; CI slightly asymmetric in Monte Carlo — nonlinearity causes minor

an inputs, symmetric output" | "Agree closely in mean; CI slightly asymmetric in Monte Carlo — nonlinearity causes minor departure from Gaussian." A caption: "When the model is linear and inputs are Gaussian, both methods agree. When the model is nonlinear, Monte Carlo captures asymmetric distributions that partial derivatives cannot." A note: "When the two methods give substantially different results, the Monte Carlo result should be used — it makes fewer assumptions about the model's linearity.") Talking Point: This comparison table connects back to the Week 2 activity explicitly, as the blueprint asks us to do. In the power equation example, partial derivative propagation and Monte Carlo give almost identical results, because the power equation is nearly linear and the Gaussian input

gation and Monte Carlo give almost identical results, because the power equation is nearly linear and the Gaussian input assumption is good. In the structural dynamics example, they agree on the mean but the Monte Carlo confidence interval is slightly asymmetric. The partial derivative method assumes a linear model and symmetric Gaussian output — it cannot capture the right-skewed distribution we saw in the previous slide. The Monte Carlo result is more accurate when the model is nonlinear because it makes fewer assumptions about the output distribution shape. When the two methods agree closely, either can be used, but for your Tier 2 report, we ask you to report both and compare them — that comparison is evidence that you understand when each method is appropriate and where they diverge.

d compare them — that comparison is evidence that you understand when each method is appropriate and where they diverge. If they give substantially different results, use the Monte Carlo result and explain why the partial derivative method underestimates the uncertainty. slide 8: Residual Analysis Across a Full Dataset — Applying Regression to Validate Model Adequacy (Image: A two-panel MATLAB figure. Top panel: Scatter data plotted against the independent variable (applied load, x-axis) with three overlaid lines: (1) the physical model prediction (dashed gold), (2) a linear regression fit to the data (solid white), and (3) the ±σ uncertainty band around the physical model (shaded region). Data points are gray circles. The physical model is slightly below the data in the middle range.

model (shaded region). Data points are gray circles. The physical model is slightly below the data in the middle range. Bottom panel: Residual plot (model minus data) vs. the same x-axis. Residuals are shown as scatter points with the ±σ uncertainty band shown as dashed lines. The residuals in the middle range are consistently positive, indicating systematic over-prediction. A diagonal trend line through the residuals is labeled "Systematic trend — possible model-form issue at mid-range conditions." Caption: "Regression is not the model. The physical model prediction and the regression line diverge at mid-range — the residual analysis reveals the discrepancy.") Talking Point: Here is how regression connects to the model adequacy question. In the top panel, you see the physical model

king Point: Here is how regression connects to the model adequacy question. In the top panel, you see the physical model prediction, a linear regression fit to the data, and the experimental scatter. They look similar at a glance. But the bottom panel — the residual plot of the physical model versus the data — shows a systematic positive trend in the middle range: the physical model consistently over-predicts the measured deflection at intermediate loads. This is a model-form error signal: the linear regression tracks the data well because it has a free slope parameter that adjusts to fit, but the physical model is constrained by its governing equation and cannot adjust. The systematic residual tells you the physical model is missing something in the mid-range behavior — possibly a

adjust. The systematic residual tells you the physical model is missing something in the mid-range behavior — possibly a compliance effect at intermediate loads that the boundary condition assumption does not capture. This is exactly the analysis you did in Week 8 using residuals at individual points, now applied systematically across the full dataset using regression as the reference. The regression is a diagnostic reference, not the answer. slide 9: Applying These Tools to the Tier 2 Lab — What the Section 4 Deliverable Requires (Image: A structured checklist for the Section 4 (uncertainty analysis) deliverable. Five rows: "1. Partial derivative derivation — propagate uncertainty for the primary derived quantity using the governing equation." "2. Dominant error source ranking — which

te uncertainty for the primary derived quantity using the governing equation." "2. Dominant error source ranking — which input contributes most to the total uncertainty, and how does it change with operating conditions?" "3. Monte Carlo simulation — ≥10,000 samples, Gaussian inputs based on sensor specs, output distribution histogram." "4. Compare methods — partial derivative and Monte Carlo results stated together, differences noted and explained." "5. Interpret adequacy — given the total uncertainty, is the experiment sensitive enough to distinguish the model prediction from the data within the acceptance criterion?" Each row has a MATLAB icon on the left and a report section tag on the right. The checklist is clean and formatted as a structured deliverable specification.) Talking

eport section tag on the right. The checklist is clean and formatted as a structured deliverable specification.) Talking Point: Section 4 of the Tier 2 group report is the uncertainty analysis section, and it requires all five of these elements. You already completed the partial derivative derivation in the prediction document — reproduce it and update it with the actual measured values. The dominant error source ranking tells you which input drives the total uncertainty, and you should check whether that ranking changes across the operating conditions you tested — sometimes a different input dominates at high versus low values of the independent variable. The Monte Carlo simulation uses 10,000 samples at minimum, draws inputs from Gaussian distributions based on the actual sensor

nte Carlo simulation uses 10,000 samples at minimum, draws inputs from Gaussian distributions based on the actual sensor specifications, and outputs a distribution histogram with the mean and 95% CI reported from percentiles. Then you compare the partial derivative result to the Monte Carlo result and note the agreement or disagreement. And finally — the hardest part — you interpret whether the experiment is sensitive enough. If the total uncertainty is comparable to the model-data discrepancy you found, you cannot distinguish whether the discrepancy is real or within measurement noise. That is an important finding: it means a more precise measurement is needed to resolve the model adequacy question at the required confidence level. The ultimate question for Section 3B is whether the

esolve the model adequacy question at the required confidence level. The ultimate question for Section 3B is whether the total uncertainty bound you compute here allows you to evaluate your committed Week 6 acceptance criterion. If the propagated uncertainty is comparable to the model-data discrepancy, you cannot resolve whether your acceptance criterion is met or not — and that finding belongs in your conclusion. slide 10: The Improvement Proposal — Grounding in Statistical Analysis (Image: A worked example of an improvement proposal entry, formatted as a short structured paragraph. Header: "Section 6 — Improvement Proposal, Category (c): Experimental Design." Body: "The current test matrix includes 8 repeated trials at each of 3 operating conditions. The 95% confidence interval for the

he current test matrix includes 8 repeated trials at each of 3 operating conditions. The 95% confidence interval for the mean at each condition is ±1.8 N (from standard error analysis). The model-data discrepancy at the critical operating condition (Condition 2) is 2.1 N — just outside the confidence interval at the current sample size. To resolve the model adequacy question at this condition with 95% confidence, we estimate a sample size of approximately 14 trials per condition would be required (based on the observed standard deviation of 4.2 N and the target half-CI of 1.1 N). An additional 6 trials per condition would close the gap. Alternatively, reducing the random measurement noise through improved signal conditioning (DAQ improvement category b) would achieve the same sensitivity

m measurement noise through improved signal conditioning (DAQ improvement category b) would achieve the same sensitivity improvement without additional trials." A gold border frames this example and a label reads: "This is the quality of improvement proposal that earns full credit — specific, grounded in data, actionable.") Talking Point: This is what a full-quality improvement proposal looks like when it is grounded in the statistical analysis. The group identified that their confidence interval width of 1.8 newtons is just barely larger than the model-data discrepancy of 2.1 newtons — close enough that they cannot definitively classify the discrepancy as either real or within noise. They then computed the sample size that would be needed to narrow the confidence interval sufficiently to

within noise. They then computed the sample size that would be needed to narrow the confidence interval sufficiently to resolve the question. They arrived at a specific number of additional trials — six more per condition. And they noted an alternative path: improving measurement precision through signal conditioning would achieve the same sensitivity without requiring additional trials. Both paths are specific, quantified, and grounded in the analysis results. That is the standard. The improvement proposal is not a reflection on what went well — it is a forward-looking engineering recommendation, derived from your data, that would make the experiment more informative. That is the skill E3 is going to ask you to demonstrate independently for an experiment you design yourself. slide 11:

. That is the skill E3 is going to ask you to demonstrate independently for an experiment you design yourself. slide 11: Closure Regression is a diagnostic tool for evaluating model adequacy across a full dataset — not a substitute for a physical model, and not a validation verdict. R-squared tells you about variance explained; the residual structure tells you about model form adequacy, and you must look at both. Monte Carlo simulation, which we first applied to electrical power in Week 2, extends to multi-variable aerospace systems with correlated inputs and nonlinear models, where partial derivative propagation underestimates uncertainty due to its linearity and Gaussian output assumptions. When both methods agree, either may be reported; when they disagree, use the Monte Carlo result

sian output assumptions. When both methods agree, either may be reported; when they disagree, use the Monte Carlo result and explain why. These tools together — confidence interval comparison, regression, and Monte Carlo — complete the E2 statistical analysis toolkit. Section 4 of your Tier 2 group report requires all three, and the improvement proposal in Section 6 must be grounded in what the analysis revealed about measurement sensitivity. In the lab session today, you apply all of this to your own Tier 2 dataset. The tools are not abstract — they are the specific instruments for finishing your group report, and the quality of how you apply and interpret them is what distinguishes a good experimental engineer from one who can only follow a procedure. ## Agent Notes - Content is

is what distinguishes a good experimental engineer from one who can only follow a procedure. ## Agent Notes - Content is grounded in the W9 L2 blueprint purpose statement: regression, goodness-of-fit, Monte Carlo for multi-variable systems, explicit connection to E1 W2 Monte Carlo activity. - The connection to E1 W2 Monte Carlo is made explicit in slides 1 and 4, per blueprint requirement: "The connection to E1 Week 2 is made explicit — the conceptual tool is the same, the domain is now more complex." - Correlated inputs (slide 5) are included because the blueprint specifies "several inputs have correlated uncertainty." - Slide 7 provides the method comparison (partial derivative vs. Monte Carlo) required by the Tier 2 Section 4 deliverable description. - The improvement proposal example

derivative vs. Monte Carlo) required by the Tier 2 Section 4 deliverable description. - The improvement proposal example (slide 10) demonstrates the quality of Section 6 grounding in statistical analysis, per instructor_clarification_notes.md items on critique and improvement. - No CLO 4 or Phase III content introduced. CLO 2, CLO 3 primary treatment.

pages.title: R01
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Reading R01 # From One Point to Many — Regression, Confidence & What They Don't Tell You ## Three statistical ideas that engineers consistently misapply — and what they actually mean A high R² does not mean your model is right. A... From One Point to Many — Regression, Confidence & What They Don't Tell You Three statistical ideas that engineers consistently misapply — and what they actually mean A high R² does not mean your model is right. A 95% confidence interval does not mean there is a 95% probability the true value is inside it. A statistically significant result does not mean the result is important. If any of those three statements surprised you, read carefully: each represents a misuse so common that it appears routinely in published engineering research. In E1, the statistical work focused on a single operating point: measure a quantity, propagate uncertainty through a formula, report a result with bounds. The comparison was straightforward — does the predicted value lie within the measurement's uncertainty interval? Week 9 extends this to a harder problem: comparing a model to experimental data not at one point but across a range of conditions, and doing so with statistical rigor that says something truthful about whether the model is adequate. Three tools dominate this work. Each is powerful. Each is routinely misapplied. This reading is specifically about where the misapplications happen and what correct interpretation looks like. Regression: What R² Actually Measures Regression is the process of fitting a mathematical relationship to a set of data points — finding the line, curve, or model form that minimizes some measure of the distance between the model and the data. The most familiar result of a regression is the coefficient of determination, R² , which reports what fraction of the variance in the observed data is explained by the model. R² = 0.97 sounds excellent. But here is what R² cannot tell you: It cannot tell you whether the model is physically correct. A cubic polynomial fitted to data from a system that follows simple linear physics will have high R² — the polynomial is flexible enough to follow any smooth curve. That does not mean cubic dynamics are real. Goodness of fit measures how well the model matches the data it was fitted to. It says nothing about whether the model would match data from a different experiment, a different operating range, or a different system. Look at the residuals. A model with R² = 0.97 but systematic trends in the residuals (positive at low loads, negative at high loads) is missing structural physics despite the high R². A model with R² = 0.82 but purely random residuals may be a better physical model, capturing the right functional form even if parameter uncertainty prevents a tighter fit. The reading from W8 established this framework; Week 9 applies it across a full dataset rather than a single comparison point. The correct practice: report R² alongside a residual plot, and let the residual plot carry the interpretive weight. Confidence Intervals: What "95%" Actually Means Practical rule: Use confidence intervals for comparison (do these two results overlap?) rather than for declaring probability about a single result. A confidence interval is produced by a statistical estimation procedure, not by a single experiment. The statement "95% confidence interval" means: if you repeated this experiment many times, each time constructing an interval using the same procedure, 95% of those intervals would contain the true value. It does not mean that there is a 95% probability the true value lies within this particular interval. Once the interval is calculated, the true value either is or isn't inside it — probability no longer applies. This distinction matters in engineering practice in the following way: you cannot look at a 95% confidence interval and declare that you are "95% confident" the true mean is inside it. You can say that your estimation procedure is calibrated to contain the true value 95% of the time over many repetitions. For a single experiment, the interval is either right or wrong, and you don't know which. Think of it this way: imagine you used this same estimation procedure on 100 different experiments. In 95 of them, the CI you calculated would contain the true value. In 5 of them, it would not, and you wouldn't know which 5. Your specific CI either captured the true value or it didn't. The "95%" describes your method's long-run reliability, not this particular result. What this means practically is that confidence intervals must be interpreted in terms of the estimation process, not the individual result. When comparing two results (say, the mean drag coefficient measured at two different angles of attack), the comparison of confidence intervals tells you whether the two results are statistically distinguishable. If the intervals overlap substantially, you cannot confidently claim the results differ. If they do not overlap, the difference is unlikely to be sampling noise. This distinction between statistical significance (results are distinguishable given the noise level of the measurement) and engineering significance (the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use) is where the third common misapplication lives. Statistical vs. Engineering Significance A result can be statistically significant and engineering-irrelevant. If your uncertainty is small enough, you can detect a difference in mean structural stiffness of 0.01% between two configurations. That difference is real — the statistical machinery confirmed it is not sampling noise. But if your structural model requires agreement to within 5% for its intended use, a 0.01% discrepancy is well within acceptance, and the statistical significance of the difference is beside the point. Conversely, a result can be statistically non-significant and engineering-critical. If your experiment has high noise and your confidence intervals are wide, you may be unable to distinguish a 15% stiffness difference from sampling variation. The two means are statistically indistinguishable — but a 15% stiffness difference in a load-bearing structure is an engineering problem regardless of what the statistics say. The experiment was not sensitive enough to detect the relevant difference. This is a measurement design failure, not a physical finding. Effect size, the magnitude of the difference relative to the variability in the data, is the quantitative bridge between statistical significance and engineering significance. A small effect size with a large sample may be statistically significant but engineering-irrelevant; a large effect size with a small sample may not reach statistical significance but be clearly engineering-critical. The connection back to Week 6 is direct: the acceptance criterion you wrote in your pre-lab prediction is the engineering significance threshold. When you compare your Tier 2 results to your committed prediction, that criterion is the frame. Statistical distinguishability is a prerequisite (you need to know whether the discrepancy is real or just noise), but passing the statistical test is not the same as passing the engineering test. Both questions must be answered, and both must be answered against the criteria written before the data was collected. The Takeaway: Regression, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing are powerful tools with specific and limited meanings — applying them correctly means knowing what each one cannot tell you, and connecting statistical results back to the intended-use criteria that were written before the experiment began.

A small effect size with a large sample may be statistically significant but engineering-irrelevant; a large effect size with a small sample may not reach statistical significance but be clearly engineering-critical. The connection back to Week 6 is direct: the acceptance criterion you wrote in your

but be clearly engineering-critical. The connection back to Week 6 is direct: the acceptance criterion you wrote in your pre-lab prediction is the engineering significance threshold. When you compare your Tier 2 results to your committed prediction, that criterion is the frame. Statistical distinguishability is a prerequisite (you need to know whether the discrepancy is real or just noise), but passing the statistical test is not the same as passing the engineering test. Both questions must be answered, and both must be answered against the criteria written before the data was collected. The Takeaway: Regression, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing are powerful tools with specific and limited meanings — applying them correctly means knowing what each one cannot tell you, and

rful tools with specific and limited meanings — applying them correctly means knowing what each one cannot tell you, and connecting statistical results back to the intended-use criteria that were written before the experiment began.

pages.title: R02
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Reading R02 # Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case ## When your uncertainties are not independent, and what to do about it Most Monte Carlo simulations you will run in this course draw each uncertain input independ... Correlated Inputs in Monte Carlo — An Advanced Case When your uncertainties are not independent, and what to do about it Most Monte Carlo simulations you will run in this course draw each uncertain input independently — and that assumption is correct for most of the experiments you will encounter in E2. This reading is for the cases where it is not. This reading is an advanced topic and is beyond the scope of the standard E2 workflow. Most groups in ASEN 3501 have input uncertainties that are genuinely independent — each sensor was calibrated separately, each material property was measured from a different source, each environmental factor varies for different reasons. If that describes your experiment, you do not need the material in this reading. Standard Monte Carlo, with independent draws for each input, gives you the correct result. Read this if, and only if, two or more of your input uncertainties come from the same source. The most common cases: two sensor outputs that were both derived from the same calibration procedure, or two model parameters that were both estimated from the same batch of material tests. In those situations, the uncertainties on the two inputs are not independent: when one is higher than its nominal value, the other tends to be too. Sampling them independently ignores that structure. The result is a Monte Carlo distribution that is too narrow: it includes combinations (input A high, input B low simultaneously) that are physically improbable, and those combinations dilute the worst-case tail of the output distribution. The correction requires two pieces: a correlation matrix and a Cholesky decomposition . The correlation matrix encodes the statistical relationships between your inputs. For two inputs with a correlation coefficient ρ, the matrix is simply [[1, ρ], [ρ, 1]]. A correlation of +1 means the two inputs move together perfectly; a correlation of 0 means they are independent; a correlation of −1 means when one is high, the other is low. You estimate ρ from the physical relationship between the two sources, or from the calibration records if both sensors were calibrated against the same reference artifact. The Cholesky decomposition is the matrix equivalent of taking a square root. Applied to the correlation matrix, it produces a lower triangular matrix L such that L × Lᵀ = R. Multiplying a set of independent standard normal draws by L transforms them into draws with the correct correlated structure. In MATLAB: R = [1 0.7; 0.7 1]; % Correlation matrix (ρ = 0.7) L = chol(R, 'lower'); % Cholesky decomposition X = randn(N, 2) * L'; % N correlated sample pairs The rows of X are sample pairs with the correct joint distribution: they form an elongated ellipse in two dimensions, not a circular cloud. Each row is then scaled by the appropriate standard uncertainty for each input before being passed into the model. Geometrically, the Cholesky factor is the matrix that stretches and rotates the independent standard normal cloud into the correct correlated shape. It does not change the marginal distributions — each input still has its correct individual uncertainty — but it links the samples so that the joint behavior matches the physical relationship between the sources. The Takeaway: Correlated inputs require correlated sampling, and the Cholesky decomposition is the standard mechanism for generating that structure in MATLAB. For most E2 experiments, independent sampling is appropriate and correct. If you have reason to believe two of your inputs are correlated — and you can quantify the correlation — apply this approach, document the correlation coefficient, and verify that the resulting output distribution is wider than the independent-sampling result in the direction of the correlated worst case.

pages.title: R03
pages.text: E2 Week 4 (Course Week 9) Reading R03 # Evaluating a Validation Report — A Checklist for the Comparative Analysis Discussion ## What a credible validation report does that an inadequate one does not Before you can evaluate someone else's validation report... Evaluating a Validation Report — A Checklist for the Comparative Analysis Discussion What a credible validation report does that an inadequate one does not Before you can evaluate someone else's validation report, you need a checklist grounded in the framework you have been building since Week 6. This reading organizes that checklist. It is written from the perspective of an evaluator — someone reading the report to determine whether its validation claim is credible — not from the perspective of the author defending it. This is the reference document for the Week 9 in-class comparative analysis discussion. The sample report contains deliberate weaknesses. Your job is to identify them using the categories below, name the specific failure each represents, and propose what a corrected version would show. Category 1: The Prediction Commitment A credible validation report documents that the model was committed before the data was collected. The key questions an evaluator asks: Is there a pre-experiment prediction with a specific numerical value or range? Is there a committed acceptance criterion with a stated threshold? Is the prediction dated or timestamped before data collection? Failure mode to look for: The prediction is present but the acceptance criterion appears in the discussion section — after the data is analyzed. This is a signal that the criterion was chosen to match the outcome rather than to define what would constitute success in advance. The cure is a committed criterion in the pre-lab prediction document, signed before data collection begins. Also watch for: The prediction matches the data suspiciously well across all conditions. A model calibrated to a dataset will fit it well. Without evidence that the model was committed before the data was collected, a good fit is evidence only of curve-fitting skill. Category 2: Residual Analysis A credible validation report presents residuals explicitly, not just the overlay of prediction and data. Are residuals plotted versus the independent variable (not just shown as a percentage error)? Is the shape of the residual pattern described (random vs. systematic)? Is the residual magnitude compared to the propagated uncertainty band? Failure mode to look for: R² reported as the primary validation metric without a residual plot. A high R² means the model explains variance in the data. It does not mean the residuals are random, and it does not mean the model is adequate for the intended use. A polynomial of sufficient degree achieves R² = 1 on any dataset. The diagnostic content is in the residuals, not the R² value. Also watch for: Residuals described as "within scatter" or "within experimental error" without showing the uncertainty band. This qualitative assessment cannot be evaluated — the reader cannot tell whether the author's definition of "acceptable scatter" matches the propagated uncertainty or was chosen after examining the data. Category 3: Statistical vs. Engineering Significance A credible validation report distinguishes between these two independent criteria. Is the acceptance criterion stated in engineering terms (a specific threshold with physical units and a stated intended use)? Is statistical significance tested separately from engineering significance? Does the report acknowledge cases where the two diverge? Failure mode to look for: Statistical significance treated as validation. A p-value below 0.05 tells you the difference between two means is unlikely to be random chance. It says nothing about whether the difference is large enough to matter for the intended use. A 0.3 N difference may be highly statistically significant with a large enough sample, while being irrelevant for a system designed with a 2.0 N safety margin. Also watch for: Engineering significance asserted without a stated acceptance criterion. "The model agrees reasonably well" is not an engineering statement. "The model agrees within ±5%, and the design requirement is ±10%" is an engineering statement. Category 4: Uncertainty Budget and Calibration Status A credible validation report documents the dominant uncertainty sources with a propagated budget. Are the dominant uncertainty sources identified and ranked? Is calibration uncertainty included in the budget (not just random measurement noise)? Is the calibration status of each primary sensor stated — date, interval, compliance? Failure mode to look for: The uncertainty budget includes only Type A (random) uncertainty. Systematic sources — calibration uncertainty, model input uncertainty, environmental drift — are absent. This produces an artificially narrow uncertainty band that makes the model look better than the measurement system can actually support. Also watch for: Calibration status listed as "calibrated" without documentation of when, by whom, and against what standard. "Calibrated" is not a calibration record. The record should include the calibration date, calibration interval, and the resulting uncertainty in the measurand. Category 5: Confidence Intervals A credible report uses confidence intervals correctly and interprets them precisely. Are CIs computed from the standard error of the mean (not the standard deviation of individual measurements)? Is the confidence level stated explicitly? Are CIs interpreted as statements about the estimation procedure, not about the probability that the true value is inside a specific interval? Failure mode to look for: "There is a 95% probability that the true value is between X and Y." This is the Bayesian posterior interpretation, not the frequentist confidence interval. The frequentist interpretation is: "If we used this procedure on 100 experiments, 95 of the resulting intervals would contain the true value." These are not the same statement. The frequentist CI is a statement about the procedure, not about any specific interval. Also watch for: Reporting the standard deviation of repeated measurements and calling it the confidence interval. The SD describes individual measurement scatter. The CI of the mean narrows as n increases, because more measurements improve your estimate of the mean. Category 6: Improvement Proposal Quality A credible improvement proposal is grounded in the data and uncertainty analysis, not generic. Does the proposal name a specific sensor, parameter, model term, or test condition? Is the proposal motivated by something in the data: a residual trend, a dominant uncertainty source, a calibration gap? Does the proposal estimate the improvement quantitatively (even roughly)? Failure mode to look for: "More trials would reduce uncertainty." This is always true and says nothing. A credible proposal is: "The load cell contributes 68% of the total uncertainty budget. Replacing the LCL-010 (u_cal = ±0.12 N) with a higher-precision alternative (estimated u_cal = ±0.03 N) would reduce the combined uncertainty by approximately 40%, which is sufficient to resolve the acceptance criterion at the current model-data discrepancy of 0.18 N." Summary: What Separates Credible from Inadequate Dimension Inadequate Credible Prediction commitment Present but criterion appears post-hoc Committed criterion with date, before data Residual analysis R² only, or percent error only Residual plot vs. independent variable, compared to uncertainty band Statistical vs. engineering significance p < 0.05 treated as validation Both tested separately; divergence acknowledged Uncertainty budget Random noise only All sources: random, calibration, model input Calibration status "Calibrated" with no documentation Date, interval, compliance, propagated u_cal CI interpretation "95% probability true value is in range" Correct frequentist statement Improvement proposal Generic ("more trials") Specific, grounded in data, with rough quantitative estimate For the discussion: For each identified failure, state (1) the specific text or figure that shows the problem, (2) which category it falls into from the table above, and (3) what a corrected version would show. Observations without category names and proposed corrections do not count as analysis. The Takeaway: A validation report is a credibility argument, not a results summary. Every claim in it is subject to the question: how do you know? The framework you have built in Weeks 6–9 — prediction commitment, residual analysis, uncertainty propagation, statistical comparison, calibration status — is the complete toolkit for asking that question systematically.
